topics.title: Advanced Risk Management Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Risk Management Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 Advanced Risk Management Techniques in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is formally defined as a measure of the extent to which an entity (asset, process, or stakeholder) is threatened by a potential circumstance or event. Mathematically, it is the **probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability** and the resulting **consequence (impact)** on system confidentiality, integrity, and availability — often extended to **safety, reliability, and operability** in CPS.

According to the **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** framework and the **ISO 31000:2018** standard, *Risk* is the **"effect of uncertainty on objectives"**, expressed as a function of threat, vulnerability, likelihood, and impact. In CPS, this definition is critically extended to include **safety hazards** because a cyber-attack can cause physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life — not just data loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU 2024 Definition (CPS-Specific):**
> *Risk in CPS is the product of the likelihood that a specific threat actor exploits a system vulnerability and the severity of the resulting adverse consequence, considering both cyber and physical domain impacts.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are the **owner of a smart home** equipped with IoT cameras, smart locks, and an industrial-grade HVAC system.

- **Asset:** Your house and the people inside (CPS = physical + cyber).
- **Threat:** A hacker trying to break in or a power surge damaging sensors.
- **Vulnerability:** A default password on your smart lock, or unpatched firmware.
- **Impact:** Financial loss, privacy breach, or — in the worst case — a fire triggered by a compromised thermostat.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Risk Formula:**
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$
> Just like a chain breaks at its **weakest link**, risk in CPS is amplified when any of these three components rise. Even a rare threat becomes catastrophic if impact is high — think of a ransomware attack on a hospital's insulin pump system.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition | CPS-Specific Note |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Asset** | Anything of value to the organization | Includes sensors, actuators, PLCs, RTUs, and control networks |
| **Threat** | Potential cause of an unwanted incident | Insider, APT, nation-state, malware, environmental |
| **Vulnerability** | Weakness that can be exploited | Unpatched firmware, hardcoded credentials, protocol flaws |
| **Likelihood** | Probability that a threat will occur | Often estimated using historical data, threat intel |
| **Impact** | Severity of harm if exploited | Includes **safety**, **financial**, **operational**, **reputational** |
| **Risk Treatment** | Process to modify risk (mitigate, transfer, accept, avoid) | CPS often chooses "avoid" or "mitigate" due to safety constraints |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after treatment | Must be **explicitly accepted** by senior management in CPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **CPS Distinction from IT:** In traditional IT, risk is measured in **data loss and downtime**. In CPS, risk extends to **physical safety, environmental damage, and human injury**. This is why IT-grade risk frameworks (e.g., simple CVSS) are insufficient — CPS requires **safety-aware risk models** like STPA-Sec or combined cyber-safety HAZOP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### A. The Three Pillars of Risk Management in CPS

1. **Identification** — Discovering assets, threats, and vulnerabilities.
2. **Assessment** — Quantifying or qualifying the level of risk.
3. **Treatment** — Selecting and applying controls to modify risk.

> [!NOTE]
> These three pillars form the **continuous risk management loop** in NIST SP 800-39 and ISO 31000. In CPS, this loop must run **continuously** because the threat landscape and system state evolve in real time (e.g., a moving vehicle's sensor data changes every millisecond).

### B. Quantitative vs. Qualitative Risk Assessment

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Output** | Descriptive (Low/Medium/High) | Numerical (₹, %, probabilities) |
| **Data Needed** | Expert opinion, checklists | Historical data, statistics, monetary values |
| **Strength** | Fast, cheap, intuitive | Precise, comparable, supports ROI |
| **Weakness** | Subjective, hard to compare | Data-hungry, costly, hard for rare events |
| **KTU Use Case** | Initial screening of CPS assets | Detailed analysis of high-impact subsystems |

### C. The NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF) — Adapted for CPS

The **NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2)** defines seven steps:

1. **Prepare** — Establish context, scope, and risk tolerance.
2. **Categorize** — Classify system based on impact (Low/Moderate/High).
3. **Select** — Choose baseline security controls (SP 800-53 / SP 800-82 for ICS).
4. **Implement** — Deploy controls in both IT and OT domains.
5. **Assess** — Verify controls are working as intended.
6. **Authorize** — Senior official accepts residual risk (ATO — Authority to Operate).
7. **Monitor** — Continuous monitoring of controls and risk posture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CPS, Step 2 (**Categorize**) uses **FIPS 199** impact levels: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability**. However, an additional **Safety Impact Level (SIL)** from IEC 61508 must also be considered for industrial control systems.

### D. The FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) Model

**FAIR** decomposes risk into measurable factors:

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat Event Frequency} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Primary Loss} \times \text{Secondary Loss Factor}$$

- **Threat Event Frequency (TEF):** How often a threat acts.
- **Vulnerability (V):** Probability that the threat succeeds given an action.
- **Primary Loss:** Direct productivity, response, and replacement cost.
- **Secondary Loss:** Reputational, legal, competitive, and safety-related cost.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | Application in CPS |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Basic Risk Equation** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Dimensionless index | Quick screening of CPS assets |
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | ₹ / $ / € | One-time loss from a single incident |
| **Annual Rate of Occurrence** | $\text{ARO}$ | Events per year | Frequency of threat events annually |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | ₹ / $ per year | Standard quantitative risk metric |
| **Return on Security Investment** | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE before} - \text{ALE after} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$ | Ratio (or %) | Justify cybersecurity budget |
| **CVSS Base Score Range** | $0.0 \leq S \leq 10.0$ | Severity scale | First NVD published score |
| **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $\text{RRR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{before}} - R_{\text{after}}}{R_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Measure control effectiveness |
| **Bayesian Posterior (Risk Update)** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ | Update threat probability with new evidence |
| **Attack Tree AND-node** | $P(\text{parent}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{child}_i)$ | Probability | All children must succeed |
| **Attack Tree OR-node** | $P(\text{parent}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P(\text{child}_i))$ | Probability | Any child can succeed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always state the **units** and the **range** of values (e.g., $0 \leq P \leq 1$) in your answers. KTU examiners award partial credit for correct unit specification.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **ALE** is the cornerstone of **cost-benefit analysis** for security controls. A control is justified only if $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} + \text{Cost}_{\text{control}} < \text{ALE}_{\text{before}}$.
- **ROSI** allows CISOs to **defend budgets** in boardrooms with hard numbers.
- **CVSS** is the de-facto industry standard for **vulnerability prioritization** — used by NVD, vendors, and SIEMs.
- **Bayesian methods** enable **adaptive risk management** — risk scores update as new threat intel arrives.
- **Attack trees** allow security engineers to **visualize multi-step attack paths** and find the cheapest control that breaks the chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a CPS Asset

### Problem Statement
A CPS contains a **PLC-controlled water treatment pump** valued at **₹20,00,000 (Twenty Lakhs INR)**. An industry survey estimates that a cyber-induced failure occurs **once every 4 years**, and the **Exposure Factor (EF)** for such a failure is **45%** (i.e., 45% of the asset's value is destroyed in a single incident). Compute the **SLE, ARO, ALE, and ROSI** if a firewall costing **₹1,50,000 per year** reduces the occurrence rate to **once every 20 years**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**

$$\text{Asset Value (AV)} = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Exposure Factor (EF)} = 45\% = 0.45$$

$$\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ events/year}$$

$$\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ events/year}$$

$$\text{Cost of Control (CoC)} = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$$

$$\text{SLE} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.45$$

$$\text{SLE} = ₹9{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) before the control.**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}}$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = ₹2{,}25{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4: Compute the ALE after the control.**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}}$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = ₹45{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Risk Reduction.**

$$\Delta \text{ALE} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}$$

$$\Delta \text{ALE} = 2{,}25{,}000 - 45{,}000 = ₹1{,}80{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Return on Security Investment (ROSI).**

$$\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$$

$$\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}$$

$$\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}$$

$$\text{ROSI} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

### Interpretation
A **positive ROSI of 20%** means the firewall is a financially justified investment. For every ₹1 spent, the organization saves ₹1.20 in expected losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Board Examiner Expectation):** If ROSI > 0, the control is **cost-effective**. If ROSI < 0, the control is **not justified financially** and should be re-evaluated or alternative controls should be explored.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Attack Tree Probability Calculation (CPS Smart Grid)

### Problem Statement
An attacker targets a **smart meter** in a CPS power grid. The attack tree has an **OR-root** with three child paths:

- **Path A (Phishing):** Probability = **0.20**
- **Path B (Firmware Exploit):** Probability = **0.10**
- **Path C (Physical Tampering):** Probability = **0.05**

Compute the **overall probability** that the smart meter is compromised.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the structure.** Since the root is an **OR-node**, the attacker succeeds if **any** one path succeeds. Therefore, we use the **complement rule**.

**Step 2: Compute the probability that each path fails.**

$$P(\bar{A}) = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

$$P(\bar{B}) = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90$$

$$P(\bar{C}) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

**Step 3: Compute the joint probability that all paths fail (independence assumed).**

$$P(\text{all fail}) = P(\bar{A}) \times P(\bar{B}) \times P(\bar{C})$$

$$P(\text{all fail}) = 0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.95$$

$$P(\text{all fail}) = 0.684$$

**Step 4: Compute the probability that the attacker succeeds (at least one path).**

$$P(\text{success}) = 1 - P(\text{all fail})$$

$$P(\text{success}) = 1 - 0.684 = 0.316$$

$$P(\text{success}) = 31.6\%$$

### Interpretation
Although each individual path has a low probability (5–20%), the **combined risk is 31.6%** because the attacker has multiple vectors. This is the **defense-in-depth fallacy** — adding more paths actually **increases** the attack surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When analyzing CPS attack trees, the OR-node probability is **always greater than or equal to** the maximum of its children. Students often incorrectly sum the probabilities — this is a common error.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CVSS Base Score Calculation (Conceptual)

While the full CVSS v3.1 formula uses an intricate weighted sum involving Impact and Exploitability sub-scores, the **simplified pedagogical form** is:

$$\text{Base Score} = \text{round}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10))$$

Where:

$$\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \left[1 - (1-C) \times (1-I) \times (1-A)\right]$$

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$$

**Example:** A vulnerability with C=I=A=0.56 (High) and AV=0.85, AC=0.77, PR=0.62, UI=0.85 yields an Impact ≈ 6.42 and Exploitability ≈ 2.74, giving a Base Score of **8.6 (High)**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Part A questions, you are **not required to compute the exact CVSS score**. You must, however, know that CVSS ranges from **0.0 to 10.0** and that scores **≥ 7.0 are High severity** and require immediate patching — especially in CPS, where unpatched vulnerabilities can lead to physical consequences.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Risk Treatment Decision

| Treatment Strategy | When to Use | CPS Example | Trade-off |
|--------------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Risk Mitigation (Reduce)** | Likelihood or impact can be lowered cost-effectively | Deploy firewall, patch firmware | Residual risk remains |
| **Risk Transfer (Share)** | Another party can absorb the risk better | Cyber insurance for OT downtime | Insurance does not cover safety |
| **Risk Avoidance (Eliminate)** | Risk is too high to accept | Discontinue a vulnerable legacy PLC | Loss of functionality/revenue |
| **Risk Acceptance (Retain)** | Cost of treatment exceeds loss, or low impact | Accept risk of minor sensor noise | Requires formal sign-off |
| **Risk Exploitation (Opportunity)** | Risk creates competitive advantage | Adopt new technology before competitors | Strategic, rare in CPS safety |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIST RMF for CPS — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RMF Step 1: PREPARE\nEstablish context, scope,\nrisk tolerance, roles] --> B[RMF Step 2: CATEGORIZE\nFIPS 199 impact levels\nplus Safety Impact Level]
    B --> C[RMF Step 3: SELECT\nChoose NIST SP 800-53\nand SP 800-82 controls]
    C --> D[RMF Step 4: IMPLEMENT\nDeploy controls in IT\nand OT domains]
    D --> E[RMF Step 5: ASSESS\nVerify control effectiveness\npenetration testing]
    E --> F[RMF Step 6: AUTHORIZE\nSenior official grants ATO\naccepts residual risk]
    F --> G[RMF Step 7: MONITOR\nContinuous monitoring\nSIEM, IDS, threat intel]
    G --> A

    A -. Continuous Risk Register Update .-> A
    D -. Feedback to Step 1 .-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Adaptation:** Note the **closed loop** from Step 7 back to Step 1. In CPS, risk management is **never a one-time activity** — it is a continuous, iterative process driven by real-time sensor data, threat intelligence feeds, and post-incident learnings.

## 4.2 Risk Assessment Methodology Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New CPS Asset or Threat Identified]) --> Q1{Are historical data\nand metrics available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is asset critical to\nsafety or operations?}
    Q1 -->|No| Qualitative[Use Qualitative Methods\nNIST SP 800-30 Appendix D]
    Q2 -->|Yes, High Stakes| Q3{Is precise monetary\nvalue computable?}
    Q2 -->|No, Low Stakes| Qualitative
    Q3 -->|Yes| Quant[Quantitative Methods\nALE, ROSI, Monte Carlo]
    Q3 -->|No| Hybrid[Hybrid Approach\nFAIR Model + Expert Judgment]
    Qualitative --> Output[Document Risk in\nRisk Register]
    Quant --> Output
    Hybrid --> Output
    Output --> Decision{Residual Risk\nAcceptable?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Accept[Formally Accept\nDocument Sign-off]
    Decision -->|No| Treat[Select and Apply\nMitigation Controls]
    Treat --> Q1
```

## 4.3 CPS Attack Tree — Smart Meter Compromise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootA[OR: Compromise Smart Meter] --> PathA1[AND: Spear Phishing Attack]
    RootA --> PathA2[AND: Firmware Reverse Engineering]
    RootA --> PathA3[AND: Physical Tampering at Site]

    PathA1 --> NodeA1A[Step A1: Craft convincing email]
    PathA1 --> NodeA1B[Step A2: Employee clicks malicious link]
    PathA1 --> NodeA1C[Step A3: Credentials harvested]

    PathA2 --> NodeA2A[Step B1: Acquire smart meter]
    PathA2 --> NodeA2B[Step B2: Extract firmware via JTAG]
    PathA2 --> NodeA2C[Step B3: Find hardcoded key]

    PathA3 --> NodeA3A[Step C1: Gain physical access]
    PathA3 --> NodeA3B[Step C2: Open enclosure]
    PathA3 --> NodeA3C[Step C3: Connect serial console]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each **AND-node** (e.g., `PathA1`) requires **all child steps** to succeed. The probability of each path is the **product** of its children's probabilities. The **OR-root** combines them via the complement rule (as derived in Section 3.2).

## 4.4 CVSS Severity Rating Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CVSS[CVSS Base Score 0.0 to 10.0] --> Rating
    Rating --> L[LOW 0.1 to 3.9\nPatch within 90 days]
    Rating --> M[MEDIUM 4.0 to 6.9\nPatch within 30 days]
    Rating --> H[HIGH 7.0 to 8.9\nPatch within 7 days]
    Rating --> C[CRITICAL 9.0 to 10.0\nPatch within 24 hours\nCPS Safety Critical]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Answer in **3–4 sentences** with a **diagram or formula** wherever applicable. Each question carries **3 marks**. Allocation: **Definition (1 mark) + Explanation (1 mark) + Example (1 mark)**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative risk assessment. Which is more suitable for a newly deployed CPS with no historical data?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Qualitative risk assessment** uses descriptive categories such as Low, Medium, High. It relies on **expert judgment, interviews, and checklists** rather than numerical data. It is **fast, low-cost, and intuitive**, but subjective.

- **Quantitative risk assessment** uses **numerical values** such as monetary loss, probabilities, and metrics like SLE, ARO, ALE, and ROSI. It is **precise, comparable, and supports cost-benefit analysis**, but requires historical data and is expensive.

- For a **newly deployed CPS with no historical data**, **qualitative risk assessment is more suitable** because it does not require historical incident data. However, for **safety-critical assets**, a **hybrid approach (FAIR + expert elicitation)** is recommended for higher accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always conclude with a **recommendation** to earn the third mark. Students who only define the two approaches often lose 1 mark.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the term "Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)" and explain its role in justifying cybersecurity investments in CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** is the **expected monetary loss** from a specific threat over a one-year period. It is computed as:

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = (\text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}) \times \text{Annual Rate of Occurrence}$$

- In CPS, ALE helps quantify the **financial impact of cyber-induced failures** (e.g., a ransomware attack on a SCADA system causing production downtime). It enables security engineers to **compare the cost of a control** with the **reduction in expected loss**, computed via **ROSI**.

- A control is **justified** when $\text{ROSI} > 0$, meaning the savings from prevented incidents exceed the control's cost. For example, a firewall costing ₹1,50,000/year that reduces ALE from ₹2,25,000 to ₹45,000 yields a positive ROSI of 20%.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and have an **internal choice** (either Q1 OR Q2 style). Each part typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Time allocation: **25–30 minutes** per question.

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1]**

**(a)** Explain the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)** with its seven steps. Highlight the **CPS-specific modifications** required at each step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CPS-based **smart manufacturing plant** has the following assets:

| Asset | Value (₹) | Threat | EF | ARO (per year) |
|-------|-----------|--------|-----|----------------|
| Industrial Robot Arm | 50,00,000 | Ransomware encryption | 0.60 | 0.30 |
| Conveyor PLC | 15,00,000 | Firmware tampering | 0.80 | 0.50 |
| Vision Inspection Camera | 8,00,000 | Data exfiltration | 0.40 | 0.20 |

Compute the **SLE, ALE, and total ALE** for the plant. If deploying an **EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) solution** at ₹4,00,000/year reduces the ARO of all three assets by **70%**, compute the **ROSI** and determine whether the investment is justified. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — NIST RMF with CPS Modifications (7 Marks)

1. **Prepare (1 mark):** Establish the **context, scope, risk tolerance, and roles**. CPS-specific: Include **safety stakeholders** (e.g., process safety engineers), define **IT/OT boundaries**, and identify **regulatory frameworks** (IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82).

2. **Categorize (1 mark):** Use **FIPS 199** to classify impact as Low, Moderate, or High based on Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. CPS-specific: Add a **Safety Impact Level (SIL)** per IEC 61508 (SIL 1 to SIL 4) to capture physical harm potential.

3. **Select (1 mark):** Choose baseline security controls from **NIST SP 800-53** (IT) and **SP 800-82** (ICS). CPS-specific: Select controls that do not disrupt real-time operations (e.g., passive monitoring instead of active scanning on OT networks).

4. **Implement (1 mark):** Deploy controls in both **IT** (servers, cloud) and **OT** (PLCs, RTUs, sensors) domains. CPS-specific: Use **air-gapped or diode-protected** deployments; avoid installing antivirus on legacy controllers that cannot tolerate CPU overhead.

5. **Assess (1 mark):** Verify that controls work via **penetration testing, vulnerability scanning, and tabletop exercises**. CPS-specific: Test in a **digital twin or sandbox** first; never test live on a running process plant.

6. **Authorize (1 mark):** A **senior official** (e.g., CISO or Plant Head) reviews residual risk and grants an **Authority to Operate (ATO)**. CPS-specific: In safety-critical systems, the **Process Safety Manager** must co-sign.

7. **Monitor (1 mark):** Continuous monitoring via **SIEM, IDS, and threat intelligence feeds**. CPS-specific: Monitor both **cyber KPIs** (MTTD, MTTR) and **physical KPIs** (temperature, pressure, flow rates) to detect **multi-domain attacks**.

---

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Risk Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute SLE for each asset (1 mark).**

$$\text{SLE}_{\text{Robot}} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = ₹30{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{SLE}_{\text{PLC}} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = ₹12{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{SLE}_{\text{Camera}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute ALE for each asset (1 mark).**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{Robot}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = ₹9{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{PLC}} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = ₹6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{Camera}} = 3{,}20{,}000 \times 0.20 = ₹64{,}000$$

**Step 3: Total ALE (1 mark).**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{total, before}} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 64{,}000 = ₹15{,}64{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute new ARO after EDR (70% reduction) (1 mark).**

$$\text{ARO}_{\text{Robot, after}} = 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.09$$

$$\text{ARO}_{\text{PLC, after}} = 0.50 \times 0.30 = 0.15$$

$$\text{ARO}_{\text{Camera, after}} = 0.20 \times 0.30 = 0.06$$

**Step 5: Compute new ALE and total ALE after EDR (1 mark).**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{Robot, after}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09 = ₹2{,}70{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{PLC, after}} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = ₹1{,}80{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{Camera, after}} = 3{,}20{,}000 \times 0.06 = ₹19{,}200$$

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{total, after}} = 2{,}70{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 19{,}200 = ₹4{,}69{,}200$$

**Step 6: Compute ROSI (1 mark).**

$$\Delta \text{ALE} = 15{,}64{,}000 - 4{,}69{,}200 = ₹10{,}94{,}800$$

$$\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{10{,}94{,}800 - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{6{,}94{,}800}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.737$$

$$\text{ROSI} = 173.7\%$$

**Step 7: Decision (1 mark).**

Since $\text{ROSI} = 173.7\% > 0$, the **EDR solution is highly justified**. For every ₹1 spent, the plant saves ₹2.74 in expected losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget to **multiply the reduction factor** (0.30) by the **original ARO**, instead of subtracting it. Remember: a **70% reduction** means the new ARO is **30% of the original**.
> - Do not confuse **EF (Exposure Factor)** with **ARO (Annual Rate of Occurrence)** — EF is a fraction, ARO is a frequency. Mixing them is a common 2-mark deduction error.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk)** model in detail. How does it differ from the **NIST SP 800-30** qualitative approach? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct an **attack tree** for a CPS **autonomous vehicle GPS spoofing attack** with at least **three independent attack paths** under an OR-root. If the probabilities of success for the three paths are **0.15, 0.10, and 0.05** respectively, compute the **overall probability of successful GPS spoofing**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — FAIR Model (7 Marks)

The **FAIR model** (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) is a **quantitative risk framework** that decomposes risk into four primary factor groups:

1. **Asset** — What is at risk? (2 marks)
   - Includes **data, systems, processes, and people** in CPS.
   - Each asset has a **value** measured in financial, operational, or safety terms.

2. **Threat** — Who or what can cause harm? (2 marks)
   - **Threat Event Frequency (TEF):** How often a threat acts on an asset.
   - In CPS, threats include **insiders, APTs, malware, environmental events**.

3. **Vulnerability** — How likely is the threat to succeed? (1 mark)
   - **Vulnerability (V):** Probability of success given the threat acts.
   - Often derived from **CVSS scores, threat intel, or historical data**.

4. **Loss** — What is the impact? (2 marks)
   - **Primary Loss:** Direct costs (downtime, response, replacement).
   - **Secondary Loss:** Indirect costs (reputation, legal, safety).

**FAIR Risk Equation:**

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat Event Frequency} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Primary Loss} \times \text{Secondary Loss Factor}$$

**Difference from NIST SP 800-30 (1 mark):**

| Aspect | FAIR | NIST SP 800-30 |
|--------|------|----------------|
| **Approach** | Fully quantitative | Primarily qualitative |
| **Output** | Monetary loss distributions | Low/Medium/High ratings |
| **Decomposition** | Detailed 4-factor model | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact (3 factors) |
| **Use Case** | High-stakes CPS with measurable assets | Broad organizational risk assessments |

---

#### Part (b) — Attack Tree and Probability (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Construct the attack tree (3 marks).**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootB[OR: GPS Spoofing of Autonomous Vehicle] --> B1[AND: SDR-based Signal Replay]
    RootB --> B2[AND: Satellite Simulator Attack]
    RootB --> B3[AND: Insider Map Data Poisoning]

    B1 --> B1A[Acquire SDR hardware]
    B1 --> B1B[Record authentic GPS signal]
    B1 --> B1C[Replay at higher power near victim]

    B2 --> B2A[Obtain SDR with simulator firmware]
    B2 --> B2B[Align with target coordinates]
    B2 --> B2C[Broadcast spoofed signal]

    B3 --> B3A[Compromise mapping service insider]
    B3 --> B3B[Inject false landmark coordinates]
    B3 --> B3C[Vehicle accepts poisoned map]
```

**Step 2: Compute the probability of each path (1 mark).**

The problem states the path probabilities directly:

$$P(B_1) = 0.15, \quad P(B_2) = 0.10, \quad P(B_3) = 0.05$$

**Step 3: Compute the complement probabilities (1 mark).**

$$P(\bar{B_1}) = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85$$

$$P(\bar{B_2}) = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90$$

$$P(\bar{B_3}) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

**Step 4: Compute the joint failure probability (1 mark).**

$$P(\text{all fail}) = 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.72675$$

**Step 5: Compute the overall success probability (1 mark).**

$$P(\text{success}) = 1 - 0.72675 = 0.27325 \approx 27.33\%$$

**Conclusion:** The attacker has a **27.33% chance of successfully spoofing the GPS** of the autonomous vehicle, demonstrating that even low individual path probabilities combine into a substantial risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Attack Tree Section):**
> - **DO NOT** simply add the probabilities (0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.30). This is **only correct for mutually exclusive events** with very small probabilities.
> - **DO NOT** forget the **parentheses** when computing complements. Writing $1 - 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95$ without parentheses is a **1-mark deduction**.
> - Always state the **assumption of independence** explicitly — KTU examiners reward this.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Risk in CPS** is a function of **Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**, and uniquely includes **safety, physical harm, and environmental damage**.
- ✅ **NIST RMF** has **7 steps**: Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor — it is a **closed loop**, not a waterfall.
- ✅ **FIPS 199** impact levels (Low/Moderate/High) apply to **C, I, A**. CPS adds **Safety Impact Level (SIL)** from IEC 61508.
- ✅ **ALE = SLE × ARO** is the cornerstone of quantitative risk. **SLE = Asset Value × Exposure Factor**.
- ✅ **ROSI > 0** means the control is **justified**. $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\Delta\text{ALE} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}$.
- ✅ **CVSS ranges from 0.0 to 10.0**; scores ≥ 7.0 are High and require **immediate patching** in CPS.
- ✅ **Attack tree OR-node**: $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$. **AND-node**: $P = \prod P_i$. **Never** simply add probabilities.
- ✅ **Risk treatment strategies**: Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept (and rarely Exploit). CPS often prefers **Avoidance** for unmitigable safety risks.
- ✅ **FAIR** is a quantitative alternative to NIST's qualitative approach and decomposes risk into **Threat, Vulnerability, Primary Loss, Secondary Loss**.
- ✅ **Residual risk must be formally accepted** in writing by a senior authority (CISO/Plant Head) — verbal acceptance is **not** valid in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Continuous monitoring** is non-negotiable in CPS; the threat landscape changes every second.
- ✅ **Bayesian updating** allows risk scores to evolve with new threat intelligence — formula: $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> 1. Always **define** the term first (1 mark).
> 2. Always **explain** with a formula or diagram (1 mark).
> 3. Always **apply** to a CPS-specific scenario (1 mark).
> 4. For 14-mark questions, follow the **7 + 7 split** with clear headings.
> 5. **Units and assumptions** earn easy partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Risk Assessment Methodologies and Frameworks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Risk Assessment Methodologies and Frameworks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Risk Assessment Methodologies and Frameworks** constitute a structured, multi-layered, and systematic set of processes, mathematical models, governance policies, and analytical techniques used to identify, classify, quantify, prioritize, and treat cybersecurity risks — with a particular emphasis on **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**. In the context of KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a risk assessment is not merely a vulnerability scan; it is a holistic engineering discipline that fuses **threat intelligence**, **asset valuation**, **vulnerability characterization**, **impact modeling**, and **likelihood estimation** to produce a defensible, auditable, and operationally actionable risk register.

Formally, **Cybersecurity Risk (R)** in CPS is defined as:

$$R = f(A, T, V, I, C)$$

where $A$ = Asset criticality, $T$ = Threat exposure, $V$ = Vulnerability, $I$ = Impact (safety, financial, operational, reputational), and $C$ = Countermeasure effectiveness (control strength).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme requires students to be conversant with *at least four* risk assessment frameworks: **NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2)**, **ISO/IEC 31000:2018**, **OCTAVE Allegro**, and **FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk)**, in addition to CPS-specific standards like **IEC 62443-3-2** and **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a city engineer designing a flood protection system. The engineer must:
1. **Inventory** all critical buildings (hospitals, power plants, schools) → *Asset Identification*.
2. **Map** the river and identify weak points (levee cracks) → *Vulnerability Assessment*.
3. **Estimate** the probability of a 100-year flood → *Threat & Likelihood Modeling*.
4. **Calculate** damage costs if each building floods → *Impact Analysis*.
5. **Decide** whether to build a dam, raise levees, or buy insurance → *Risk Treatment/Mitigation*.

**Advanced Risk Assessment Frameworks in CPS work exactly the same way** — except the "river" is the network, the "buildings" are PLCs, sensors, and actuators, and the "flood" is a cyber-attack. Without a formal framework, an organization either *over-protects* low-value assets (wasting capital) or *under-protects* mission-critical infrastructure (catastrophic failure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often asked in KTU exams):** *Risk Assessment* (RA) is the analytical process of *evaluating* risk. *Risk Management* (RM) is the broader governance discipline that *accepts, avoids, mitigates, or transfers* the assessed risk. Frameworks like NIST RMF and ISO 31000 govern the **entire lifecycle**, while methodologies like OCTAVE and FAIR provide the **analytical engine**.

## 1.3 Why "Advanced" Risk Assessment Matters for CPS

Unlike traditional IT systems, **Cyber-Physical Systems** (e.g., smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical robotics, SCADA-controlled water treatment plants) introduce two extra risk dimensions that classical IT risk models ignore:

- **Safety-Criticality**: A cyber-breach can cause loss of life, not just data loss.
- **Cascading Physical Impact**: A single sensor spoofing attack can trigger a chain-reaction physical failure.

Standard IT risk frameworks must therefore be **augmented** with safety-engineering principles (STPA, HAZOP, FMEA) and CPS-specific standards (IEC 62443, NIST CSF for CPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU 2024 papers often award bonus differentiation marks to students who explicitly contrast **IT risk** vs. **CPS/OT risk** in their answers. Memorize the unique CPS risk attributes: *real-time constraints, deterministic control loops, legacy systems with patch gaps, safety instrumented systems (SIS), and physical-world consequences*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Any Risk Assessment Framework

Every recognized framework — whether NIST, ISO, OCTAVE, or FAIR — operationalizes risk through four canonical phases. KTU examiners expect students to articulate these phases with precise terminology.

### Pillar 1 — Asset & System Characterization
- Enumerate all CPS components: sensors, actuators, controllers (PLCs, RTUs, DCS), HMIs, historians, network segments, firmware versions.
- Determine **mission-criticality** and **safety-instrumented function (SIF)** classification.
- Map **data flows** (Purdue Model / ISA-95 levels 0–3.5).

### Pillar 2 — Threat & Vulnerability Identification
- **Threat modeling**: STRIDE, PASTA, attack trees, kill chains (MITRE ATT&CK for ICS).
- **Vulnerability discovery**: CVE/NVD, ICS-CERT advisories, CVSS v3.1 scoring, penetration testing.
- **Adversary profiling**: capability, intent, opportunity, targeting (nation-state, insider, hacktivist).

### Pillar 3 — Impact & Likelihood Analysis
- **Qualitative**: Risk matrices (5×5 or 7×7 grids), heat-maps, expert judgment (Delphi method).
- **Semi-quantitative**: CVSS + asset value multipliers.
- **Quantitative**: ALE/SLE modeling, FAIR monetary loss distributions, Monte Carlo simulation.

### Pillar 4 — Risk Treatment & Continuous Monitoring
- Strategies: **Avoid, Accept, Mitigate (Reduce), Transfer (Insurance/Outsourcing)**.
- Selection based on cost-benefit analysis, regulatory mandates, and risk appetite.
- Continuous re-assessment via **STIX/TAXII** threat feeds and **SIEM/SOAR** integration.

## 2.2 Comparative Framework Analysis (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Framework | Origin/Owner | Core Strength | Risk Model | Quantitative? | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST RMF (SP 800-37 Rev. 2)** | NIST (US) | Governance integration with FISMA, 7-step lifecycle | Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor | Semi | **High** (with 800-82 overlay) |
| **ISO/IEC 31000:2018** | ISO | Universal, principles-based, applicable to any domain | ISO 31000 process loop: Establish context → Identify → Analyze → Evaluate → Treat | Both | **High** (process-agnostic) |
| **OCTAVE Allegro** | SEI/CMU | Asset-driven, streamlined for organizations without heavy compliance burden | 4 phases: Establish drivers → Profile assets → Identify threats → Analyze risks | Qualitative | **Medium** (IT-leaning) |
| **FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk)** | FAIR Institute | Fully quantitative, financial-loss calibrated | Risk = Loss Event Frequency $\times$ Loss Magnitude | **Quantitative** | **High** (financial) |
| **IEC 62443-3-2** | ISA/IEC | Purpose-built for Industrial Automation & Control Systems (IACS) | Security Level (SL-T, SL-A, SL-C, SL-I) zones & conduits | Semi | **Very High** (OT-native) |
| **NIST SP 800-82** | NIST | Overlay for ICS/SCADA/IoT on top of 800-30 / 800-37 | Adaptations of NIST 800-30 risk tables | Semi | **Very High** |
| **TARA** | MITRE | Adversary-focused, threat-agent library | Decision trees mapping threat to mitigation | Qualitative | **High** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and appear frequently in KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions. Note: vertical pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$ | Cost of a single successful attack incident |
| 2 | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $\text{ARO} \in \{0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 100\}$ | Estimated attack frequency per year (logarithmic) |
| 3 | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annual financial risk exposure (USD/year) |
| 4 | Cost-Benefit of Control | $\text{CBA} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{ACS}$ | Decide if a control is worth deploying |
| 5 | Residual Risk | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - C_{\text{eff}})$ | Risk remaining after controls ( $0 \le C_{\text{eff}} \le 1$ ) |
| 6 | CVSS Base Score | $S = \text{roundup}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10))$ | Standardized 0–10 vulnerability severity |
| 7 | FAIR Loss Event Frequency | $\text{LEF} = \text{Threat Event Frequency} \times \text{Vulnerability}}$ | Frequency of loss events per year |
| 8 | FAIR Risk | $\text{Risk} = \text{LEF} \times \text{Loss Magnitude (LM)}}$ | Annual monetary loss distribution |
| 9 | IEC 62443 Security Level | $\text{SL-T} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ | Target/achieved/capability/interest SL |
| 10 | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection difficulty |

**Boundary Conditions / Critical Constraints**:
- $0 \le \text{EF} \le 1$ (Exposure Factor is dimensionless, capped at 100% loss).
- $\text{ARO} \ge 0$ (cannot have negative attack frequency).
- $0 \le \text{CVSS} \le 10$ (clamped at 10 by the `roundup` function).
- $\text{SL-T}$ levels 0–4 map to: *None, Casual, Simple, Sophisticated, Expert/State-level*.

## 2.4 Why These Frameworks Matter in Real CPS Engineering

In practice, a CPS engineer at a power utility would:
1. Use **NIST RMF** to satisfy North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC CIP) compliance.
2. Use **IEC 62443-3-2** to design security zones (e.g., a "Substation Zone" with SL-2 target).
3. Use **FAIR** to translate cyber risk into a USD number for the CFO.
4. Use **STRIDE + Attack Trees** to model specific adversary paths (e.g., false-data injection on PMUs).
5. Use **CVSS + ICS-CERT** to prioritize patch deployment on legacy PLCs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **ALE → CBA** workflow is *the* standard mechanism by which CISOs justify cybersecurity budgets to boards of directors. Without quantitative risk language, security is perceived as a cost-center, not a value-preservation function.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ALE-Based Risk Equation

The **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** is the cornerstone quantitative metric used in NIST SP 800-30, ISO 27005, and FAIR. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step derivation expected by KTU examiners.

### Step 1 — Define the Asset Value (AV)
The asset value is the **total replacement or reconstruction cost** of a CPS asset (e.g., a substation SCADA server, a PLC, a pressure sensor).

$$\text{AV} = C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{software}} + C_{\text{data}} + C_{\text{downtime}} + C_{\text{reputation}}$$

**Logic**: A CPS asset is not merely its hardware. The downtime of a 500 MW substation can cost millions per hour. KTU requires students to enumerate all five cost components explicitly.

### Step 2 — Define the Exposure Factor (EF)
EF is the *fraction* of the asset that is destroyed/compromised in a single successful attack event.

$$0 \le \text{EF} \le 1$$

**Logic**: A ransomware attack on an HMI may compromise $\text{EF} = 0.40$ (40% of operational data lost), while a complete physical destruction of a transformer may have $\text{EF} = 1.0$.

### Step 3 — Derive the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$$

**Logic**: If $\text{AV} = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$ and $\text{EF} = 0.30$, then one ransomware event causes $\text{SLE} = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000$ in losses.

### Step 4 — Define the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)
ARO is the estimated **frequency of attack events per year**. KTU examiners accept the standard logarithmic set:

$$\text{ARO} \in \{0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 100\}$$

**Logic**: A nation-state APT might have $\text{ARO} = 0.1$ (one attempt every 10 years), while a commodity phishing campaign might have $\text{ARO} = 100$ (100 attempts per year).

### Step 5 — Derive the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

**Logic**: This converts a *one-time* impact into a *yearly expected cost* — directly comparable to insurance premiums, control deployment costs, and budget approvals.

### Step 6 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) for Control Selection

$$\text{CBA} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre-control}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post-control}} - \text{ACS}$$

where $\text{ACS}$ = Annual Cost of the Security Control (license, labor, training, maintenance).

**Logic**: A control is **worth deploying** iff $\text{CBA} > 0$.

### Step 7 — Residual Risk Calculation

$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - C_{\text{eff}})$$

where $C_{\text{eff}}$ = control effectiveness (e.g., 0.85 for a well-configured SIEM with no false-negative bypass).

**Logic**: Even after deploying all controls, some risk *always* remains. Residual risk is what the organization formally **accepts** in writing.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (FAIR-Style CPS Case Study)

**Scenario:** A water-treatment CPS in Kerala operates a PLC-controlled chlorination system.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Hardware cost (PLC + I/O modules) | ₹ 8,00,000 |
| Software & licensing | ₹ 2,00,000 |
| Data & historian records | ₹ 1,00,000 |
| Regulatory penalty risk (max) | ₹ 4,00,000 |
| **Total Asset Value (AV)** | **₹ 15,00,000** |
| Exposure Factor (EF) — chlorine overdose event | 0.50 |
| ARO — sophisticated insider threat | 0.5 / year |
| Control effectiveness $C_{\text{eff}}$ | 0.80 |
| Annual Control Cost (ACS) | ₹ 50,000 |

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute SLE**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 7{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Pre-Control ALE**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 7{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5 = 3{,}75{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute Post-Control ALE**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} \times (1 - C_{\text{eff}}) = 3{,}75{,}000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 3{,}75{,}000 \times 0.20 = 75{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute Cost-Benefit**

$$\text{CBA} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{ACS} = 3{,}75{,}000 - 75{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $\text{CBA} = \text{₹}2{,}50{,}000 > 0$, the control (e.g., network segmentation + ICS-aware SIEM) is **economically justified**.

**Step 6: Residual Risk Acknowledgment**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 3{,}75{,}000 \times 0.20 = 75{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

This ₹75,000/year residual risk must be **formally accepted by management** in a Risk Acceptance Letter, per ISO 31000 governance requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Scheme Alignment:** A full 7-mark Part B sub-question on this derivation requires: (1) correct formula selection [+2], (2) correct substitution [+2], (3) intermediate values shown [+2], (4) correct final decision/interpretation [+1].

## 3.3 CVSS Base Score Derivation (Exhaustive Steps)

The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) v3.1 base score is a frequent exam topic.

**Step 1: Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**

$$\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]$$

where $C, I, A$ are Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability impact metric values (each in $\{0.0, 0.22, 0.56\}$).

**Step 2: Compute Impact**

$$\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISS} & \text{if Scope (S) is Unchanged} \\ 7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope is Changed} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Compute Exploitability**

$$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$$

**Step 4: Compute Base Score**

$$S = \text{roundup}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10)\bigr)$$

**Logic**: All constants (6.42, 7.52, 3.25, 8.22) are empirically derived from CVSS v3.1 specification. Students are *not* expected to memorize all metric values, but **must** know that the final score is clamped at 10.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF) 7-Step Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Prepare"] --> B["Step 2 Categorize System"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Select Controls"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Implement Controls"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Assess Controls"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Authorize System"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Monitor Continuously"]
    G --> A
    E --> A
    F --> A
    G --> C
    G --> D
```

**Interpretation:** This is the *closed-loop governance* diagram the KTU examiner expects. Note the three "tier-back" arrows from Monitor/Assess/Authorize back to Prepare — this represents **continuous re-authorization** and **ongoing authorization** concepts introduced in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2.

## 4.2 ISO 31000:2018 Risk Management Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Communication Consultation"] <--> B1["Scope Context Criteria"]
    B1 --> C1["Risk Identification"]
    C1 --> D1["Risk Analysis"]
    D1 --> E1["Risk Evaluation"]
    E1 --> F1["Risk Treatment"]
    F1 --> G1["Monitoring Review"]
    G1 --> B1
    A1 -.-> C1
    A1 -.-> D1
    A1 -.-> E1
    A1 -.-> F1
    A1 -.-> G1
```

**Interpretation:** The dotted lines from Communication & Consultation emphasize ISO 31000's principle that stakeholder dialogue is **continuous and omnipresent** — not a single phase.

## 4.3 IEC 62443-3-2 Zone and Conduit Architecture (CPS Overlay)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZoneEnterprise["Zone 5 Enterprise Network SL-T 1"]
        E1["ERP Server"]
        E2["Email Gateway"]
    end
    subgraph ZoneDMZ["Zone 4 DMZ SL-T 2"]
        D1["Jump Host"]
        D2["Historian Mirror"]
    end
    subgraph ZoneSCADA["Zone 3 SCADA Operations SL-T 3"]
        S1["SCADA Server"]
        S2["Engineering Workstation"]
    end
    subgraph ZoneControl["Zone 2 Local Control SL-T 3"]
        C1["PLC Chlorination"]
        C2["PLC Pumping"]
    end
    subgraph ZoneField["Zone 1 Field Sensors SL-T 2"]
        F1["Pressure Transmitter"]
        F2["Flow Sensor"]
        F3["Chlorine Analyzer"]
    end
    subgraph ZoneSafety["Zone 0 Safety SIS SL-T 4"]
        SA1["Safety PLC SIL3"]
        SA2["Emergency Shutdown"]
    end
    E1 <-->|Conduit 1 Monitored| D1
    E2 <-->|Conduit 2 Encrypted| D1
    D1 <-->|Conduit 3 Restricted| S1
    D2 <-->|Conduit 4 Read Only| S1
    S1 <-->|Conduit 5 Industrial Protocol| C1
    S1 <-->|Conduit 6 Industrial Protocol| C2
    C1 <-->|Conduit 7 Analog| F1
    C1 <-->|Conduit 8 Analog| F2
    C2 <-->|Conduit 9 Analog| F3
    SA1 -.->|Hardwired Interlock| C1
    SA1 -.->|Hardwired Interlock| C2
```

**Interpretation:** Each zone carries its own **Target Security Level (SL-T)** per IEC 62443-3-3. Notice the **Safety SIS Zone (SL-T 4)** has *hardwired* interlock connections — bypassing all networked control — illustrating the **defense-in-depth** principle for safety-critical CPS.

## 4.4 FAIR Risk Factor Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FAIR Risk"] --> B["Loss Event Frequency"]
    A --> C["Loss Magnitude"]
    B --> B1["Threat Event Frequency"]
    B --> B2["Vulnerability"]
    B1 --> B11["Contact Frequency"]
    B1 --> B12["Probability of Action"]
    B2 --> B21["Threat Capability"]
    B2 --> B22["Control Strength"]
    B2 --> B23["Resistance"]
    C --> C1["Primary Loss"]
    C --> C2["Secondary Loss"]
    C1 --> C11["Productivity"]
    C1 --> C12["Response"]
    C1 --> C13["Replacement"]
    C2 --> C21["Reputation"]
    C2 --> C22["Fines Judgments"]
    C2 --> C23["Competitive Advantage"]
```

**Interpretation:** FAIR uniquely decomposes risk into **hierarchical, measurable sub-factors** — making it the only mainstream framework that supports full Monte Carlo simulation and direct financial loss distribution modeling.

## 4.5 Quantitative vs Qualitative Risk Assessment — Decision Matrix

| Dimension | Qualitative | Semi-Quantitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Data** | Expert judgment | CVSS scores | Monetary values, frequency stats |
| **Output** | Heat map (Low/Med/High) | Numeric band 1–10 | USD/year loss distribution |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Time** | Days | Weeks | Months |
| **Board Comprehension** | Easy | Moderate | Requires financial literacy |
| **CPS Suitability** | Initial screening | Most CPS audits | High-value critical infrastructure |
| **Examples** | OCTAVE Allegro, STRIDE | NIST 800-30, IEC 62443 | FAIR, ALE modeling |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define *risk* in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. How does CPS risk differ from traditional IT risk?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Risk in CPS is the *product of the probability of a successful cyber-attack and the magnitude of adverse consequences* — including safety, operational, financial, and reputational impacts.
- **CPS-specific characteristics (1 mark):** (a) Real-time deterministic control constraints; (b) Safety-criticality where breach → loss of life; (c) Legacy OT systems with unpatched vulnerabilities; (d) Cascading physical-world effects.
- **Contrast with IT (1 mark):** Traditional IT risk focuses on CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) and data loss. CPS risk must additionally consider **safety, environmental harm, physical damage, and continuity of physical processes**, governed by standards like IEC 62443 and NIST SP 800-82.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the four risk treatment strategies defined in ISO 31000.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Risk Avoidance (1 mark):** Eliminate the risk entirely by discontinuing the activity (e.g., not deploying IoT in safety-critical zone).
2. **Risk Acceptance (1 mark):** Formally acknowledge and retain the risk when cost of mitigation exceeds benefit (documented in Risk Acceptance Letter).
3. **Risk Mitigation (1 mark):** Apply controls to *reduce* likelihood or impact (e.g., firewalls, segmentation, MFA, patch management).
4. **Risk Transfer (1 mark):** Shift financial impact to a third party via cyber-insurance or outsourcing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:** Many KTU students write "avoid, mitigate, transfer" and *forget acceptance*. Acceptance is a *valid* treatment strategy under ISO 31000 and not writing it = -1 mark. Also, do NOT list "ignore" or "eliminate" as synonymous with avoidance — they are imprecise.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In End-Semester Examinations, every Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (either **OR** between two questions, or sub-parts with their own OR). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)** as per SP 800-37 Rev. 2. List all seven steps and describe the *Prepare* and *Monitor* steps in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a CPS asset with the following parameters:
- Asset Value = **₹ 20,00,000**
- Exposure Factor = **0.60**
- ARO = **0.4 per year**
- Control effectiveness = **75%**
- Annual Control Cost = **₹ 80,000**

Compute SLE, ALE (pre-control), ALE (post-control), and Cost-Benefit. State whether the control is **economically justified**. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the 7-step RMF cycle as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. Label each step. Show the three "feedback" arrows from Monitor, Assess, and Authorize back to Prepare.

**Seven Steps (1 mark — 0.14 each, full credit for listing all 7):**
1. Prepare
2. Categorize System
3. Select Controls
4. Implement Controls
5. Assess Controls
6. Authorize System
7. Monitor Continuously

**Prepare Step (1.5 marks):** Establishes *context and risk framing* — identifies stakeholders, assets, risk tolerance, system boundary, and information types. Outputs a **System Inventory** and **Risk Management Strategy**.

**Monitor Step (1.5 marks):** Continuous monitoring of control effectiveness, configuration changes, threat environment shifts, and incident data. Uses SIEM, vulnerability scanners, and ongoing authorization. Outputs a **Continuous Monitoring Strategy** and feeds back to Prepare and Categorize.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
$\text{AV} = 20{,}00{,}000$, $\text{EF} = 0.60$, $\text{ARO} = 0.4$, $C_{\text{eff}} = 0.75$, $\text{ACS} = 80{,}000$

**Step 1 — Compute SLE (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 12{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE (pre-control) (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 4{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE (post-control) (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} \times (1 - C_{\text{eff}}) = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.25 = 1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cost-Benefit (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{CBA} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{ACS} = 4{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**
Since $\text{CBA} = \text{₹}2{,}80{,}000 > 0$, the control is **economically justified**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Quantitative Parts:**
> - Forgetting to **multiply post-control ALE by $(1 - C_{\text{eff}})$** — students often confuse this with the *pre-control* ALE. -2 marks.
> - Reporting CBA without units (₹/year) — -0.5 mark.
> - Failing to state a clear **decision** ("justified / not justified") — -1 mark.
> - In the diagram, omitting the **feedback arrows** from Monitor → Prepare — -1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **OCTAVE Allegro**, **FAIR**, and **ISO 31000** as risk assessment frameworks. Construct a comparison table covering *origin, risk model, quantitative vs. qualitative, primary use case, and CPS suitability*. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the **IEC 62443 Zone and Conduit Model** for CPS. With a suitable example, illustrate how Security Levels (SL-T) are assigned to different zones in a typical **water-treatment plant** SCADA architecture. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | OCTAVE Allegro | FAIR | ISO 31000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | SEI/CMU (Carnegie Mellon) | FAIR Institute | ISO (International) |
| **Risk Model** | Asset-driven 4-phase process | Factor decomposition (LEF $\times$ LM) | Principles-based process loop |
| **Quantitative?** | Qualitative | **Fully quantitative** | Both supported |
| **Primary Use** | Self-directed org risk profiles | Financial loss forecasting | Universal governance framework |
| **CPS Suitability** | Medium (IT-leaning) | High (financial) | High (process-agnostic) |

**Critical Comparison (3 marks):**
- **OCTAVE** is best for organizations that want a *workshop-driven, asset-centric* assessment without deep compliance — but it lacks the safety-engineering depth needed for CPS.
- **FAIR** is the only mainstream framework that delivers *defensible USD figures* — essential for board-level CPS budget decisions and insurance underwriting.
- **ISO 31000** is the *meta-framework* — it does not prescribe specific techniques but provides the governance structure (context, criteria, communication) within which OCTAVE or FAIR can be nested.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Zone-Conduit Concept (2 marks):** IEC 62443-3-2 partitions an IACS into **security zones** (logical/physical grouping of assets with common security requirements) connected by **conduits** (the communication paths between zones). Each zone is assigned a **Target Security Level (SL-T)** from 0 to 4.

**SL-T Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- SL-T 0: No specific requirements
- SL-T 1: Casual / coincidental
- SL-T 2: Simple / intentional with low resources
- SL-T 3: Sophisticated / intentional with moderate resources
- SL-T 4: Expert / state-sponsored

**Water-Treatment Plant Example (3.5 marks — diagram + zones):**

- **Zone 0 — Safety Instrumented System (SL-T 4):** Emergency shutdown PLC (SIL-3), hardwired interlocks. **Highest protection** because failure = potential public health disaster.
- **Zone 1 — Field Instrumentation (SL-T 2):** Pressure transmitters, flow meters, chlorine analyzers, pH sensors. Direct physical interface.
- **Zone 2 — Local Control (SL-T 3):** PLCs controlling chlorination, pumping, filtration. Industrial protocol (Modbus/TCP, PROFINET).
- **Zone 3 — SCADA Operations (SL-T 3):** SCADA server, engineering workstation, HMI. Real-time monitoring.
- **Zone 4 — DMZ (SL-T 2):** Jump host, historian mirror. Read-only access for enterprise systems.
- **Zone 5 — Enterprise Network (SL-T 1):** ERP, email, corporate IT. Lowest criticality for safety.

**Conduits between zones must enforce:** authentication, encryption (where feasible in OT), protocol filtering, and unidirectional gateways (data diodes) between safety zones and SCADA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — IEC 62443 Question:**
> - Confusing **SL-T** (Target) with **SL-A** (Achieved), **SL-C** (Capability), and **SL-I** (Interest). -1 mark.
> - Drawing zones *without conduits* — IEC 62443 is fundamentally a *zone-conduit* model; conduits are mandatory. -1 mark.
> - Assigning **SL-T 4 to enterprise IT** — this is backwards. SL-T 4 is for *safety-critical* zones, not corporate email.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 15 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- ✅ Risk = Threat $\times$ Vulnerability $\times$ Impact (asset + consequence).
- ✅ **NIST RMF** has **7 steps**: Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor (with continuous feedback loops).
- ✅ **ISO 31000** is the *meta-framework*; provides governance, not specific methods.
- ✅ **OCTAVE Allegro** = asset-driven, 4-phase, *qualitative*, workshop-based.
- ✅ **FAIR** = the only *fully quantitative* mainstream framework; outputs USD loss distributions.
- ✅ **IEC 62443** = OT-native; uses Zones + Conduits + 4 Security Levels (SL-T, SL-A, SL-C, SL-I).
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-82** = the CPS overlay on top of 800-30 / 800-37 for ICS/SCADA.
- ✅ **ALE** formula: $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = (\text{AV} \times \text{EF}) \times \text{ARO}$.
- ✅ **CBA** > 0 means the control is **economically justified**.
- ✅ **Residual risk** = Inherent risk $\times (1 - C_{\text{eff}})$.
- ✅ **Four risk treatments**: Avoid, Accept, Mitigate, Transfer (ISO 31000).
- ✅ **CPS-specific risk dimensions**: safety-criticality, real-time constraints, legacy systems, cascading physical impact.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** final score is clamped between 0 and 10 via `roundup` and `min(..., 10)`.
- ✅ **RPN** (FMEA-style) = Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection-difficulty.
- ✅ **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- ✅ In **Kerala-specific CPS context** (smart grid, water utilities, healthcare IoT), IEC 62443-3-2 is the most relevant framework for design and certification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrating Risk Management into the CPS Lifecycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Integrating Risk Management into the CPS Lifecycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that are built from, and depend upon, the seamless integration of computational algorithms, communication networks, and physical processes. Embedded computers and networks monitor, coordinate, control, and integrate the physical processes — usually through feedback loops where physical actions affect computations and vice versa.

**Risk Management in CPS** is the systematic, iterative, and continuous process of identifying, assessing, mitigating, monitoring, and communicating cyber-physical risks across the entire engineering lifecycle of a CPS — from concept and design, through development, integration, deployment, operation, maintenance, and finally decommissioning/disposal.

**The CPS Lifecycle** refers to the structured sequence of phases a CPS traverses from initial conception to retirement:

1. **Concept / Requirements Phase**
2. **Design / Architecture Phase**
3. **Development / Implementation Phase**
4. **Integration / Testing Phase**
5. **Deployment / Commissioning Phase**
6. **Operation / Maintenance Phase**
7. **Decommissioning / Disposal Phase**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Risk management is **NOT a one-time activity** performed at a single lifecycle stage. It is a *cross-cutting concern* that must be embedded at *every* phase, with iterative feedback loops between phases. Examiners frequently test this lifecycle-integration aspect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Authoritative Anchor Frameworks:**
> - **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** — Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security
> - **NIST SP 800-161 Rev. 1** — Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management
> - **NIST IR 8259** — IoT Device Cybersecurity Capability Core
> - **ISO/SAE 21434** — Road Vehicles Cybersecurity Engineering
> - **IEC 62443** — Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security
> - **ISO 31000:2018** — Risk Management Guidelines
> - **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0** — Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a House:**
> Imagine you are constructing a multi-story house. Risk management is *not* something you do only after the house is built by inspecting it for cracks. Instead, it begins the moment you select the plot of land (geological risk, flood risk), continues when an architect draws the blueprints (structural risk, fire-safety risk), remains active when contractors pour concrete (material risk, workmanship risk), persists throughout occupancy (electrical risk, gas-leak risk), and even continues when the building is finally demolished (asbestos risk, environmental risk).
>
> Similarly, a CPS — be it a smart grid, an autonomous vehicle, a medical infusion pump, or an industrial robot — requires **continuous, lifecycle-embedded risk governance**. Each lifecycle stage introduces new threat surfaces, new vulnerabilities, and new stakeholders, all of which demand risk-aware decision-making.

### 1.3 Why Integration into the Lifecycle is Non-Negotiable

- **Cost of late remediation:** Industry data (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, NIST studies) consistently show that fixing a defect discovered in **operation** is **10×–100×** more expensive than fixing it in **design**.
- **Safety-criticality:** Unlike pure IT, CPS failures can cause **physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life** (e.g., Stuxnet, Triton/TRISIS, Maroochy water breach, Mirai botnet).
- **Regulatory compliance:** Standards such as **IEC 62443-4-1** and **ISO/SAE 21434** *mandate* lifecycle-integrated security processes.
- **Attack surface evolution:** A CPS attack surface grows as the system moves from isolated prototype to connected deployment.
- **Cascade effects:** Cyber incidents propagate across the cyber-physical boundary (e.g., a cyber-induced valve misclosure → physical pressure surge → explosion).

### 1.4 Standard Risk Metrics Used Throughout the Lifecycle

| Metric | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood** | $L$ | Probability (0–1) that a threat event occurs |
| **Impact / Consequence** | $I$ | Severity of harm (often 1–5 or Low–Catastrophic) |
| **Risk Score** | $R$ | Composite measure of $L \times I$ |
| **Threat** | $T$ | Any circumstance or event with potential to harm |
| **Vulnerability** | $V$ | Weakness that can be exploited |
| **Asset** | $A$ | Resource of value requiring protection |
| **Control / Safeguard** | $C$ | Countermeasure reducing $L$ or $I$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Value Convention:** $L \in [0, 1]$, $I \in [1, 5]$ (qualitative scales often used: *Negligible, Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic*). Resulting $R$ is typically mapped to qualitative bands: *Low, Medium, High, Critical*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Risk Management (ISO 31000 Aligned)

Risk management in CPS is built on five interrelated pillars, which the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to map explicitly to CPS lifecycle stages.

#### Pillar 1 — **Risk Identification (Discover)**
- **What:** Catalog assets, threat sources, vulnerabilities, and existing controls.
- **How:** Asset inventory, threat modeling (STRIDE, PASTA, LINDDUN, Attack Trees), data-flow diagrams, failure-mode analysis (FMEA).
- **Lifecycle injection point:** Concept, Design, Development, and continuously during Operation.

#### Pillar 2 — **Risk Assessment / Analysis (Measure)**
- **What:** Estimate $L$, $I$, and derive $R$.
- **How:** Qualitative, semi-quantitative, or quantitative analysis (FAIR, Monte Carlo, CVSS for technical scoring).
- **Lifecycle injection point:** Design reviews, code-complete gates, pre-deployment reviews, and periodic re-assessment.

#### Pillar 3 — **Risk Treatment / Mitigation (Treat)**
- **What:** Apply the *4T* strategy: **Treat, Tolerate, Transfer, Terminate**.
- **How:** Apply controls drawn from the *hierarchy of controls*: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (cybersecurity analogue: redesign → re-architect → detect & respond → policies → user training).
- **Lifecycle injection point:** All phases, but especially Design (by-design security) and Operation (compensating controls).

#### Pillar 4 — **Risk Monitoring & Review (Monitor)**
- **What:** Continuously observe the risk posture, detect drift, and verify control effectiveness.
- **How:** SIEM, IDS/IPS, threat intelligence, vulnerability scans, penetration tests, red-team exercises, key risk indicators (KRIs).
- **Lifecycle injection point:** Operation phase (primary), but also Design (security verification & validation).

#### Pillar 5 — **Risk Communication & Documentation (Govern)**
- **What:** Report risks to stakeholders, regulators, supply-chain partners.
- **How:** Risk registers, security assurance cases, conformity assessments, executive dashboards.
- **Lifecycle injection point:** Every phase — must be auditable, traceable, and version-controlled.

### 2.2 Why and How at Each Lifecycle Stage

The "Why" rationale and "How" implementation differ for each phase, and the KTU examiner rewards students who articulate this distinction.

- **Concept / Requirements:**
  - *Why:* Risk context is established; tolerability thresholds are negotiated.
  - *How:* Stakeholder workshops, risk-context document, regulatory mapping, initial asset identification.

- **Design / Architecture:**
  - *Why:* Architecture is the cheapest place to eliminate or reduce risk.
  - *How:* Threat modeling, secure-by-design principles (least privilege, defense-in-depth, zero trust), selection of security controls.

- **Development / Implementation:**
  - *Why:* Code-level defects are major vulnerability sources.
  - *How:* Secure coding standards, SAST/DAST, code review, SBOM generation.

- **Integration / Testing:**
  - *Why:* Inter-component interactions create emergent risks.
  - *How:* System-level security testing, fuzz testing, attack-surface reduction validation, formal verification where applicable.

- **Deployment / Commissioning:**
  - *Why:* Production environments introduce new risks (network exposure, third-party connectivity).
  - *How:* Penetration testing, configuration hardening, baseline establishment, secure handoff.

- **Operation / Maintenance:**
  - *Why:* Threat landscape evolves; patches, new vulnerabilities, and insider threats emerge.
  - *How:* Continuous monitoring, patch management, incident response, change-control, vulnerability management.

- **Decommissioning / Disposal:**
  - *Why:* Residual data, undocumented backdoors, and supply-chain memory must be neutralized.
  - *How:* Sanitization, media destruction, key/certificate revocation, supply-chain handoff agreements.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Risk Score** | $R = L \times I$ | $L \in [0,1]$, $I \in [1,5]$, $R \in [0,5]$ |
| 2 | **Adjusted Risk (with existing controls)** | $R_{adj} = L_{residual} \times I$ | Residual likelihood after controls |
| 3 | **CVSS-style composite** | $R = f(Exploitability, Impact)$ | Base score 0–10 |
| 4 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Monetary units / year |
| 5 | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Monetary units |
| 6 | **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Ratio |
| 7 | **Total Risk Exposure** | $TRE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Sum across all identified risks |
| 8 | **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $RRR = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 9 | **NIST likelihood scale** | $L \in \{VL, L, M, H, VH\}$ → $\{0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9\}$ | Qualitative → numeric |
| 10 | **NIST impact scale** | $I \in \{N, Mi, Mo, Ma, C\}$ → $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Negligible → Catastrophic |
| 11 | **Risk Appetite Threshold** | $R \le R_{threshold}$ | Acceptance criterion |
| 12 | **Inherent vs. Residual Risk** | $R_{inherent} \ge R_{residual}$ | Always non-increasing after controls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Note for Tables:** All absolute-value and conditional operators in this table have been rendered using semantic descriptions to preserve markdown table integrity. In your answer scripts, you may safely write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Lifecycle risk management prevents blackouts from coordinated cyber-physical attacks.
- **Medical Devices (e.g., pacemakers, insulin pumps):** FDA pre-market and post-market cybersecurity guidance mandates lifecycle risk processes.
- **Connected Vehicles:** ISO/SAE 21434 requires a **Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS)** spanning the entire vehicle lifecycle.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** IEC 62443-4-1 mandates Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) practices.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** DO-326A / ED-202A specify Airworthiness Security Process Specification across the aircraft lifecycle.
- **IoT / Edge CPS:** ETSI EN 303 645 and NIST IR 8259 series govern baseline device lifecycle security.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Inherent Risk to Residual Risk

In any CPS risk management program, the fundamental question is: *"How much risk remains after we apply our chosen controls?"* This is the **Inherent → Residual Risk Transition**.

$$
R_{inherent} = L_{inherent} \times I
$$

$$
R_{residual} = L_{inherent} \times (1 - E_{control}) \times I
$$

where $E_{control}$ is the **effectiveness** of the control in reducing likelihood, $E_{control} \in [0, 1]$.

$$
\Delta R = R_{inherent} - R_{residual} = L_{inherent} \times I \times E_{control}
$$

#### Worked Numeric Example (KTU-style)

> A smart-meter CPS asset has an inherent likelihood $L_{inherent} = 0.5$ and an impact $I = 4$ (Major).
> A firewall + network segmentation reduces likelihood by **70 %**.
> A second control, multi-factor authentication on the admin console, reduces likelihood by a further **20 %** of the *remaining* likelihood.

**Step 1 — Compute inherent risk.**

$$
R_{inherent} = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply first control (effectiveness 0.70).**

$$
L_{1} = 0.5 \times (1 - 0.70) = 0.5 \times 0.30 = 0.15
$$

**Step 3 — Apply second control (effectiveness 0.20 of remaining).**

$$
L_{2} = 0.15 \times (1 - 0.20) = 0.15 \times 0.80 = 0.12
$$

**Step 4 — Compute residual risk.**

$$
R_{residual} = 0.12 \times 4 = 0.48
$$

**Step 5 — Compute risk reduction.**

$$
\Delta R = 2.0 - 0.48 = 1.52
$$

$$
RRR = \frac{1.52}{2.0} \times 100\% = 76\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** *Control effectiveness is multiplicative on remaining likelihood, not additive.* This is a frequent KTU trap — students often incorrectly write $L_{new} = L_{old} - 0.70 - 0.20$, which can yield negative likelihoods.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) Lifecycle Roll-Up

For a CPS with $n$ distinct identified risks, the **Total Annualized Risk Exposure** is the sum of individual ALEs.

$$
ALE_i = SLE_i \times ARO_i
$$

$$
TALE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} ALE_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Asset_i \times EF_i \times ARO_i)
$$

After applying a control with annual cost $C_{ctrl}$ that reduces $ARO$ by factor $\eta$:

$$
ALE_i^{after} = SLE_i \times (ARO_i \times (1 - \eta)) = ALE_i^{before} \times (1 - \eta)
$$

$$
ROSI_i = \frac{ALE_i^{before} - ALE_i^{after} - C_{ctrl}}{C_{ctrl}} = \frac{ALE_i^{before} \times \eta - C_{ctrl}}{C_{ctrl}}
$$

#### Worked Numeric Example

> An industrial PLC controls a chemical batch reactor. Asset value = **₹10,00,000**. Exposure Factor (fraction of asset lost per event) = **0.4**. Annualized Rate of Occurrence before controls = **0.5 events/year**. A proposed anomaly-detection IDS costs **₹50,000/year** and is expected to reduce ARO by **60 %**.

**Step 1 — Compute SLE.**

$$
SLE = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $ALE_{before}$.**

$$
ALE_{before} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $ALE_{after}$.**

$$
ALE_{after} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.60) = 80{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute net benefit.**

$$
Benefit = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} = 2{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROSI.**

$$
ROSI = \frac{1{,}20{,}000 - 50{,}000}{50{,}000} = \frac{70{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1.4
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROSI > 1 (specifically 1.4 = 140 %)** ⇒ the control is **justified**. For every ₹1 spent, ₹1.40 of risk reduction is delivered *net*. KTU examiners expect this interpretation, not just the numeric value.
