### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lifecycle × Risk Pillar Mapping

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU visualization tool**. Examiners often present a similar matrix in ESE Part B (14-mark) questions and ask students to elaborate or critique the mapping.

| Lifecycle Phase ↓ / Risk Pillar → | Identify | Assess | Treat | Monitor | Communicate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Concept / Requirements** | Risk-context, asset classes, regulatory mapping | Initial qualitative risk profile | Decide overall risk approach (avoid/accept/transfer) | Establish risk-appetite thresholds | Stakeholder brief, governance charter |
| **Design / Architecture** | Threat modeling (STRIDE/PASTA), attack trees, asset & data-flow diagrams | CVSS, attack-tree analysis, design FMEA | Security controls selection, secure-by-design | Security review board, design-time verification | Security assumptions document, traceability matrix |
| **Development** | SBOM, code-level threat surface, dependency risk | SAST/DAST, code review severity | Hardening, parameterized interfaces, secure defaults | Continuous integration security gates | Developer security training records |
| **Integration / Testing** | Emergent interface risks, third-party component risks | System-level test risk scoring | Pen-test remediation, compensating controls | Verification & validation (V&V) reports | Test-summary reports, security assurance cases |
| **Deployment** | Configuration risk, supply-chain risk, environment risk | Site-specific risk assessment | Hardened baseline, secure handoff | First-day monitoring, golden-image comparison | Asset register update, operations briefing |
| **Operation / Maintenance** | New vulnerabilities (CVE feeds), insider threats, drift | Re-assessment on change, periodic review | Patch management, configuration drift correction | SIEM, SOC, KRIs, red-team exercises | Risk dashboards, regulatory filings, board reporting |
| **Decommissioning** | Residual data, forgotten credentials, hardware implants | Final risk-acceptance memo | Sanitization, media destruction, certificate revocation | Last verification scan | Audit closure, chain-of-custody records |

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Risk-Score Aggregation Across the Lifecycle

Suppose a CPS undergoes $m$ lifecycle phase transitions, and at each transition $j$ a risk score $R_j$ is computed. The **Lifecycle-Aggregated Risk Index (LARI)** is a weighted sum that rewards *early* risk reduction (lower-weight early risk is cheaper to fix).

$$
LARI = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \times R_j
$$

Typical weights (based on industry cost-of-defect data):

$$
w_{concept} = 0.5,\ w_{design} = 1,\ w_{dev} = 3,\ w_{integ} = 6,\ w_{deploy} = 10,\ w_{operate} = 15,\ w_{decom} = 5
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A risk of magnitude 5 discovered at the design stage contributes $1 \times 5 = 5$ to the LARI. The *same* risk discovered in operation contributes $15 \times 5 = 75$. This visually justifies the "shift-left" philosophy central to KTU Module-1 learning outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — CPS Lifecycle with Embedded Risk-Management Feedback Loops

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the seven-phase CPS lifecycle with risk management as a continuous, cross-cutting ring that injects risk activities into every phase and feeds lessons learned back into the next iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA([Start: CPS Initiative]) --> phase1
    phase1["Phase 1: Concept and Requirements"]
    phase2["Phase 2: Design and Architecture"]
    phase3["Phase 3: Development and Implementation"]
    phase4["Phase 4: Integration and Testing"]
    phase5["Phase 5: Deployment and Commissioning"]
    phase6["Phase 6: Operation and Maintenance"]
    phase7["Phase 7: Decommissioning and Disposal"]
    endX([End: Asset Retired Securely])

    phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4 --> phase5 --> phase6 --> phase7 --> endX

    rmCore["Risk Management Core: Identify Assess Treat Monitor Communicate"]
    rmGovern["Governance and Risk Context"]

    rmCore -.-> phase1
    rmCore -.-> phase2
    rmCore -.-> phase3
    rmCore -.-> phase4
    rmCore -.-> phase5
    rmCore -.-> phase6
    rmCore -.-> phase7

    rmGovern -.-> rmCore

    fb1[/Lessons Learned Loop/] -.-> phase1
    fb2[/Vulnerability Intelligence Loop/] -.-> phase6
    fb3[/Change-Triggered Re-assessment Loop/] -.-> phase2

    style phase1 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase2 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase3 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase4 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase5 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase6 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style phase7 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style rmCore fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600
    style rmGovern fill:#d5f0d5,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **horizontal blue chain** is the sequential CPS lifecycle.
- The **orange node** (Risk Management Core) connects via *dotted lines* to every phase, indicating that each phase receives risk inputs.
- The **green node** (Governance) sits above the core, representing the *risk-context* setting that precedes all risk activity.
- The three **italicized feedback loops** capture the iterative nature: lessons learned, vulnerability intelligence, and change-triggered re-assessment.

### 4.2 Figure 2 — The Five-Pillar Risk Workflow inside a Single Lifecycle Phase

This diagram drills into one phase (Design) to show how the five pillars interlock.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Asset and Threat Identification"] --> a2["Likelihood and Impact Estimation"]
    a2 --> a3["Risk Score Computation: R = L x I"]
    a3 --> a4{"Risk vs. Appetite Threshold"}
    a4 -- Below Threshold --> a5["Tolerate or Accept: Document in Risk Register"]
    a4 -- Above Threshold --> a6["Apply the 4T Strategy: Treat Transfer Avoid Terminate"]
    a6 --> a7["Control Implementation and Verification"]
    a7 --> a8["Continuous Monitoring: SIEM, KRIs, Pen Tests"]
    a8 --> a9["Communicate: Risk Register, Dashboards, Reports"]
    a9 -.Feedback.-> a1

    style a1 fill:#fff3cd
    style a2 fill:#fff3cd
    style a3 fill:#fff3cd
    style a4 fill:#ffcccb
    style a6 fill:#d4edda
    style a7 fill:#d4edda
    style a8 fill:#cfe2ff
    style a9 fill:#e2d5f0
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Risk-Decision Hierarchy Showing the 4T Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top["Identified Cyber-Physical Risk R above Threshold"]
    top --> q1{"Is the Risk Source Eliminated by Architecture Change?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> t1["TERMINATE: Redesign to Remove the Asset or Pathway"]
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Can the Risk be Avoided by Substitution?"}
    q2 -- Yes --> t2["AVOID: Replace with a Lower-Risk Alternative"]
    q2 -- No --> q3{"Can the Risk be Transferred via Insurance or Contract?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> t3["TRANSFER: Cyber Insurance or SLA-driven Transfer"]
    q3 -- No --> t4["TREAT: Apply Layered Controls: Eliminate Substitute Engineer Administer Detect"]
    t4 --> q4{"Is Residual Risk Now Acceptable?"}
    q4 -- Yes --> t5["ACCEPT: Document and Monitor Continuously"]
    q4 -- No --> t2

    style t1 fill:#ffb3b3
    style t2 fill:#ffe7b3
    style t3 fill:#b3d9ff
    style t4 fill:#c2f0c2
    style t5 fill:#d9d9d9
```

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Shift-Left Defect-Cost Visualization (Quantitative Justification)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Relative Cost of Fixing a CPS Defect by Lifecycle Phase"
    x-axis ["Concept", "Design", "Development", "Integration", "Deployment", "Operation", "Decommission"]
    y-axis "Relative Cost (x times base)" 0 --> 100
    bar [1, 5, 10, 30, 60, 100, 50]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for the Examiner:** The shift-left principle is the *single most important pedagogical anchor* of KTU Module 1. The above cost curve, derived from the Systems Sciences Institute and NIST 800-30 evidence base, is precisely why risk management must be embedded *early* in the CPS lifecycle — not bolted on later.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, May 2024]`
**Define "inherent risk" and "residual risk" in the context of CPS risk management. Why is the gap between them a key management metric?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Inherent Risk (1 mark):** The level of cyber-physical risk that exists *before* any controls or safeguards are applied. It reflects pure exposure to threats and vulnerabilities of the unmitigated system. Mathematically, $R_{inherent} = L_{inherent} \times I$.
- **Residual Risk (1 mark):** The level of risk that *remains* after controls have been designed, implemented, and verified. $R_{residual} = L_{residual} \times I$ where $L_{residual} = L_{inherent} \times (1 - E_{control})$.
- **Why the gap matters (1 mark):** The gap $R_{inherent} - R_{residual}$ quantifies the *effectiveness of the security program*. It is used to compute Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR) and Return on Security Investment (ROSI), and is the primary input to risk-acceptance decisions by governance bodies.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**List the seven phases of the CPS lifecycle and identify the phase in which threat modeling (e.g., STRIDE) is most effective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The seven phases are: **(i)** Concept/Requirements, **(ii)** Design/Architecture, **(iii)** Development, **(iv)** Integration/Testing, **(v)** Deployment, **(vi)** Operation/Maintenance, **(vii)** Decommissioning/Disposal. **(1.5 marks)**
- STRIDE threat modeling is most effective in the **Design / Architecture phase**, where the system data flows, trust boundaries, and component interactions are still malleable. **(1 mark)**
- However, threat modeling should be *revisited* at major change points (e.g., design changes, integration of new components, configuration changes in operation). **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Skipping between the two choices forfeits marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how risk management is integrated into the seven phases of the CPS lifecycle. Discuss the cross-cutting nature of the risk-management process and justify why a "shift-left" approach is economically and technically superior to a "test-and-fix" approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital infusion-pump CPS has been identified with the following characteristics:

- Asset Value = ₹20,00,000
- Exposure Factor per event = 0.5
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) *before* controls = 0.4 events/year
- Proposed control cost = ₹1,20,000 per year
- Expected ARO reduction = 65 %

Compute the **SLE, ALE (before and after),** and **ROSI** for the proposed control. Interpret the ROSI value and recommend whether the control should be implemented. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Diagrammatic representation (2 marks):**
Draw the seven-phase lifecycle (Concept → Design → Development → Integration → Deployment → Operation → Decommission) and overlay the five risk-management pillars (Identify, Assess, Treat, Monitor, Communicate) as a continuous band crossing all phases. Reference Figure 1 from Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Step 2 — Lifecycle integration (2 marks):**

- *Concept:* Establish risk context, asset classes, regulatory mapping, and risk-appetite thresholds.
- *Design:* Apply STRIDE / PASTA threat modeling; select security controls; document design-time assumptions.
- *Development:* Secure coding, SAST, dependency scanning, SBOM.
- *Integration:* System-level security testing, interface risk assessment, attack-surface reduction.
- *Deployment:* Hardened baseline, pen-test, secure configuration, operational handoff.
- *Operation:* Continuous monitoring (SIEM), patch management, incident response, KRIs.
- *Decommission:* Sanitization, key/certificate revocation, chain-of-custody.

**Step 3 — Cross-cutting nature (1.5 marks):**
Risk management is **not a sequential sub-phase** of the lifecycle; it is a *horizontal activity* present at every vertical stage. Outputs of one stage (e.g., vulnerabilities found in operation) feed back as inputs to the next iteration of design.

**Step 4 — Shift-left justification (1.5 marks):**
Industry defect-cost data show that fixing a defect in operation costs **60–100×** more than fixing it at the design stage. Therefore, the earlier risk is identified, the cheaper and safer the remediation. The "shift-left" mantra is the practical embodiment of this economic reality.

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE (1 mark).**

$$
SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $ALE_{before}$ (1 mark).**

$$
ALE_{before} = SLE \times ARO_{before} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $ALE_{after}$ (1.5 marks).**

$$
ARO_{after} = 0.4 \times (1 - 0.65) = 0.4 \times 0.35 = 0.14
$$

$$
ALE_{after} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.14 = 1{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Benefit and ROSI (2 marks).**

$$
Net\ Benefit = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = 2{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
ROSI = \frac{2{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} \approx 1.167
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and Recommendation (1.5 marks):**

- ROSI ≈ **1.167 (116.7 %)** is greater than **1.0**, meaning the control delivers **₹1.17 in net risk reduction for every ₹1 spent**.
- **Recommendation:** The control **should be implemented**, provided that the underlying assumptions (asset value, EF, ARO) are well-validated and that qualitative factors (patient safety, regulatory compliance) also support the decision. Note that for safety-critical medical CPS, *qualitative factors* can override purely financial ROSI calculations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set B]`

**(a)** Describe the **five pillars of CPS risk management** (Identify, Assess, Treat, Monitor, Communicate). For each pillar, give *one concrete technique* and *the specific CPS lifecycle phase* in which it is most impactful. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart-grid SCADA system has identified the following risk:

- Likelihood (L) = **0.6** (occasional, semi-quantitative mapping)
- Impact (I) = **5** (Catastrophic — city-wide outage)
- **Existing controls** are estimated to be **40 %** effective in reducing likelihood.
- **Proposed additional control** is estimated to be **30 %** effective on the *remaining* likelihood.
- Cost of the new control = ₹2,00,000 per year. Risk-acceptance threshold $R_{threshold} = 2.0$.

Compute $R_{inherent}$, $R_{residual}$ (after both control layers), the **Risk Reduction Ratio**, and the **Lifecycle-Aggregated Risk Index (LARI)** assuming the design-phase weight is **w = 1** and operation-phase weight is **w = 15**. Comment on whether the residual risk is acceptable and whether the additional control is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

| Pillar (1 mark each) | Concrete Technique (0.2) | Lifecycle Phase (0.2) |
|---|---|---|
| **Identify** — discover assets, threats, vulnerabilities | STRIDE threat modeling; FMEA; SBOM generation; asset inventory | Design / Architecture (primary) and Operation (continuous) |
| **Assess** — estimate L, I, R | CVSS scoring; qualitative risk matrix; FAIR; attack-tree quantification | Design (initial) and Pre-deployment gate |
| **Treat** — apply the 4T strategy | Multi-layered controls: network segmentation (engineering), MFA (admin), secure-by-default configuration | All phases; the most decisive interventions occur in Design |
| **Monitor** — continuous observation | SIEM, IDS/IPS, KRIs, periodic pen-testing, red-team exercises | Operation (primary) and change-triggered in any phase |
| **Communicate** — stakeholder reporting | Risk register, security assurance cases, executive dashboards, regulatory filings | Every phase; formal reports at every gate review |

*(Award 7 marks distributed as 5 × 1.0 for pillar + 0.4 for technique/phase per pillar; examiner can accept alternative valid mappings.)*

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{inherent}$ (1 mark).**

$$
R_{inherent} = L_{inherent} \times I = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply existing 40 % control (1 mark).**

$$
L_{1} = 0.6 \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.6 \times 0.60 = 0.36
$$

**Step 3 — Apply new 30 % control on residual (1.5 marks).**

$$
L_{2} = 0.36 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.36 \times 0.70 = 0.252
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_{residual}$ (0.5 mark).**

$$
R_{residual} = 0.252 \times 5 = 1.26
$$

**Step 5 — Compute RRR (1 mark).**

$$
RRR = \frac{3.0 - 1.26}{3.0} \times 100\% = \frac{1.74}{3.0} \times 100\% = 58\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute LARI for design + operation phases (1 mark).**

$$
LARI = w_{design} \times R_{design} + w_{oper} \times R_{oper}
$$

Assuming the design-time risk score (after modeling) is $R_{design} = 1.26$ and the operation-time score is also $R_{oper} = 1.26$:

$$
LARI = 1 \times 1.26 + 15 \times 1.26 = 16 \times 1.26 = 20.16
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Flexibility:** If the question is interpreted as having *different* design-time and operation-time scores, the student should clearly state the assumption and proceed. Full marks are awarded for **transparent, well-justified assumptions**.

**Step 7 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

- $R_{residual} = 1.26$ is **less than** $R_{threshold} = 2.0$, so the residual risk is **acceptable** in pure numeric terms.
- RRR of 58 % combined with the LARI dominance of the operation phase underscores that *most of the lifecycle cost of this risk lies in operation*; therefore, even though numeric acceptance is met, the control is **strongly justified** for both financial and safety-of-supply reasons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing risk mitigation with risk elimination.** Many students write *"the firewall removes the risk"* — it does not. It reduces **likelihood**. Full credit requires explicit use of $L$, $I$, $R$ variables.
> 2. **Additive instead of multiplicative control effectiveness.** The 4T and layered-control calculations are *multiplicative on remaining likelihood*, not additive. Showing $L_{new} = 0.6 - 0.40 - 0.30$ is a **common, fatal error** that yields negative values.
> 3. **Treating risk management as a single lifecycle activity.** Examiners penalize answers that place risk management only in the "operation" phase. Always present it as **cross-cutting, with feedback loops**.
> 4. **Forgetting qualitative factors in financial decisions.** For safety-critical CPS (medical, automotive, energy), ROSI is a *necessary but not sufficient* decision metric. A safe answer always includes a sentence on safety, regulatory, and reputational considerations.
> 5. **Failing to convert qualitative scales to numeric.** When the question gives "High" likelihood, the student must explicitly state the numeric mapping (e.g., $L = 0.7$ per NIST 800-30) before computing.
> 6. **In LARI-style questions, omitting weight assumptions.** State every assumption explicitly to avoid penalization.
> 7. **Writing ROSI without units or interpretation.** Always finish with *"ROSI > 1 ⇒ control justified"* or the equivalent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **CPS Lifecycle = 7 phases:** Concept, Design, Development, Integration, Deployment, Operation, Decommission.
- ✅ **Risk Management = 5 pillars:** Identify, Assess, Treat, Monitor, Communicate (ISO 31000-aligned).
- ✅ Risk management is **cross-cutting**, not sequential; outputs of one phase feed the next.
- ✅ Core risk equation: $R = L \times I$, with $L \in [0,1]$, $I \in [1,5]$.
- ✅ **Inherent Risk** = before controls; **Residual Risk** = after controls; **Gap** = program effectiveness.
- ✅ **4T Strategy:** Treat, Tolerate, Transfer, Terminate.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls (Cybersecurity analogue):** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → Detect/Train.
- ✅ **SLE = Asset Value × Exposure Factor; ALE = SLE × ARO; TALE = Σ ALE_i.**
- ✅ **ROSI = (ALE_before − ALE_after − Cost) / Cost**; ROSI > 1 ⇒ justified.
- ✅ **Control effectiveness compounds multiplicatively on residual likelihood** — never additively.
- ✅ **Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR) = (R_before − R_after) / R_before × 100%.**
- ✅ **Shift-left principle:** fixing in Design is 10×–100× cheaper than fixing in Operation; hence risk activities must begin in Concept and intensify at Design.
- ✅ **Threat modeling techniques:** STRIDE (Microsoft), PASTA, LINDDUN, Attack Trees; applied primarily in **Design**.
- ✅ **Lifecycle risk artifacts:** Risk register, Security assurance case, SBOM, threat model, vulnerability assessments, audit closure report.
- ✅ **Authoritative frameworks:** NIST CSF 2.0, NIST SP 800-82 R3, IEC 62443 (ICS), ISO/SAE 21434 (automotive), DO-326A (aerospace), FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance (medical), NIST IR 8259 (IoT).
- ✅ **Qualitative → numeric mapping (NIST 800-30):** VL=0.1, L=0.3, M=0.5, H=0.7, VH=0.9; N=1, Mi=2, Mo=3, Ma=4, C=5.
- ✅ **Total Risk Exposure:** $TRE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — useful for portfolio-level CPS risk aggregation.
- ✅ **Safety-criticality:** For CPS, residual risk acceptance must consider **safety, regulatory, and reputational** dimensions *in addition to* numeric thresholds.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** additive effectiveness; single-phase risk; missing assumptions in LARI; qualitative-only answers without numeric translation; no interpretation of ROSI.
- ✅ **Use semantic delimiters for absolute value in tables** (write $\vert x \vert$, not $\vert x \vert$ with bare pipes) to preserve markdown integrity.
- ✅ **Diagram emphasis:** Always include the **lifecycle diagram** in 14-mark answers; it alone can secure 1.5–2 marks.
- ✅ **Final defensive practice:** For every numeric answer, write the **assumption set, the formula, the substitution, the arithmetic, and the interpretation** — five lines minimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Threat Modeling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Threat Modeling

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Threat Modeling** is a systematic, structured, and proactive engineering discipline used to identify, enumerate, classify, and prioritize potential cybersecurity threats targeting a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, and to define appropriate mitigations against them. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, threat modeling extends beyond traditional IT systems to encompass the tight coupling between computational algorithms, communication networks, and physical processes that define a CPS (e.g., Industrial Control Systems, SCADA, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, and IoT-enabled medical devices).

According to the **NIST SP 800-154 (Draft)** and **IEC 62443-3-3** standards, threat modeling in CPS is not a one-time activity but a continuous lifecycle integrated into the **System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**, where threats to **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA)** are mapped alongside **Safety, Reliability, and Real-time constraints** unique to physical processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS-Specific Triad:** Unlike pure IT (CIA triad), CPS security expands to the **CIAS Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, **Availability**, and **Safety**. A breach in a CPS can lead to physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an architect designing a **skyscraper**. Before laying the first brick, you do not just draw the rooms — you also ask:

- *"Where can a burglar enter?"* (Threat identification)
- *"How strong is the lock on the basement door?"* (Vulnerability analysis)
- *"What is the worst that happens if the water pipe bursts on the 20th floor?"* (Impact / risk assessment)
- *"Where should we place the fire exits?"* (Mitigation planning)

**Advanced Threat Modeling is the architectural blueprint for cybersecurity of CPS.** Instead of buildings, we model the system as **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** showing how data, control signals, and physical measurements move between sensors, controllers, actuators, and cloud services. Then we ask the *adversary's* questions before attackers ask them for us.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mnemonic:** A threat model is a *living document* — like a building's fire-evacuation plan, it must be updated whenever a new floor, sensor, or wing is added to the system.

## 1.3 Why Threat Modeling is Critical for CPS

| Dimension | Traditional IT | Cyber-Physical System (CPS) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Asset | Data | Physical process + Data |
| Consequence of breach | Data theft / financial loss | Physical damage, injury, death |
| Time-criticality | Seconds–minutes | **Milliseconds** (real-time control) |
| Patchability | Frequent, remote | Limited, requires downtime |
| Safety coupling | Weak | **Strong** (cyber → physical impact) |
| Standards | ISO 27001, NIST CSF | **IEC 62443, NIST 800-82, ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat Modeling Lifecycle (Engineering Pipeline)

The advanced threat modeling process for CPS follows a **six-phase iterative pipeline** (per Microsoft SDL + IEC 62443 harmonization):

1. **System Definition & Scope** — Identify assets, trust boundaries, and CPS subsystems (e.g., PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, edge gateways, cloud).
2. **System Decomposition** — Construct layered Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) across **OT (Levels 0–2)** and **IT (Levels 3–5)** per the **Purdue Model**.
3. **Threat Identification** — Apply structured methodologies (STRIDE, PASTA, VAST, TARA).
4. **Vulnerability Mapping** — Cross-reference against CVE databases, CWE catalogs, ICS-CERT advisories.
5. **Risk Quantification & Prioritization** — Compute risk scores (DREAD, CVSS, attack potential).
6. **Mitigation & Validation** — Design countermeasures, run penetration tests, red-team exercises.

## 2.2 Core Threat Modeling Methodologies

### 2.2.1 STRIDE (Microsoft, 1999)

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIDE** is the most widely taught acronym in KTU 2024 Scheme cybersecurity modules. It is a mnemonic for **six categories of threats**, each mapped to a security property violation.

| Letter | Threat Category | Security Property Violated | CPS-Specific Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **S**poofing | Authentication | Attacker spoofs a Modbus/TCP packet from a legitimate PLC |
| **T** | **T**ampering | Integrity | Malicious modification of a control setpoint causing overspeed in a turbine |
| **R** | **R**epudiation | Non-repudiation | Operator deletes HMI audit logs after triggering unsafe shutdown |
| **I** | **I**nformation Disclosure | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping on CAN bus traffic in a connected vehicle |
| **D** | **D**enial of Service | Availability | Jamming wireless sensor network → process becomes blind to pressure |
| **E** | **E**levation of Privilege | Authorization | Exploiting a vulnerable web HMI to gain root on the engineering workstation |

### 2.2.2 PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)

A **risk-centric, seven-stage methodology** that aligns business objectives with technical threats. It is heavily used in **finance and critical infrastructure** sectors and is well-suited for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

**Seven stages of PASTA:**

1. Define Business Objectives
2. Define Technical Scope
3. Application Decomposition
4. Threat Analysis
5. Vulnerability & Weakness Analysis
6. Attack Modeling (Tree/Graph)
7. Risk & Impact Analysis

### 2.2.3 VAST (Visual, Agile, and Simple Threat modeling)

A **scalable, agile-friendly** methodology based on two threat models:
- **Process Flow Diagram** (operational view)
- **Application Data Flow Diagram** (architectural view)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask students to *compare* STRIDE vs PASTA. Remember: **STRIDE = threat-centric, PASTA = risk-centric, VAST = scale-centric**.

### 2.2.4 TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)

Per **MITRE's TARA methodology**, threats are categorized into:
- **Adversary Actions** (e.g., reverse engineering firmware)
- **Malicious Actions** (e.g., ransomware)
- **Accidental Actions** (e.g., operator misconfiguration)
- **Environmental / Failure Actions** (e.g., sensor drift, EMI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TARA is mandatory knowledge for CPS** because it explicitly models *non-malicious* threats (failures, environmental) — a defining feature of physical systems.

### 2.2.5 Attack Trees & Attack Graphs

- **Attack Tree** — A root-node ("attacker goal") decomposition into AND/OR sub-goals.
- **Attack Graph** — A state-transition model showing how a system moves from *safe state* to *compromised state* through chained vulnerabilities.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Risk Quantification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-know** for any KTU Part A/B numerical or descriptive question on threat modeling.

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** | Classic NIST risk equation | Dimensionless score |
| 2 | **DREAD Score** = (D + R + E + A + R) / 5 | D=Damage, R=Reproducibility, E=Exploitability, A=Affected users, D=Discoverability | 1 – 10 |
| 3 | **CVSS Base Score** = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A) | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | 0.0 – 10.0 |
| 4 | **Attack Potential** = (Elapsed Time + Expertise + Knowledge + Opportunity + Equipment) | Used in IEC 62443 / Common Criteria | Categorical (Low → High) |
| 5 | **Likelihood (L)** = f(Threat Capability, Vulnerability Exposure) | PASTA Stage 6 | 0.0 – 1.0 |
| 6 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** = S × O × D | FMEA-derived, used in OT risk | 1 – 1000 |
| 7 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** = SLE × ARO | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annual Rate of Occurrence | Monetary / year |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In DREAD, students often compute (D+R+E+A+R)/**10** instead of /5. The correct denominator is **5** since five sub-scores are summed.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Grid:** Threat modeling of IEC 61850 substation communication prevents false-trip attacks.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Models CAN/Ethernet bus threats to prevent malicious brake commands.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Models infusion pump firmware threats per FDA pre-market cybersecurity guidance.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Identifies lateral movement paths from IT (Level 4) to OT (Level 1) in the **Purdue Reference Model**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: DREAD Risk Scoring for a CPS Asset

**Scenario:** A programmable logic controller (PLC) controlling a chemical reactor's temperature is exposed to the corporate network. An unpatched buffer-overflow vulnerability exists in its web management interface.

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation walks through the **DREAD** computation *exactly* as a KTU examiner expects to see it in a 14-mark answer. The same structure applies to RPN and CVSS.

### Step 1 — Define the Threat

A remote attacker exploits the buffer overflow to gain unauthorized control of the reactor temperature setpoint, potentially causing an exothermic runaway reaction.

### Step 2 — Score Each DREAD Component (1–10 scale)

| Component | Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **D** — Damage Potential | 9 | Could cause explosion, environmental contamination, fatalities |
| **R** — Reproducibility | 8 | Reliable public PoC; deterministic exploit |
| **E** — Exploitability | 7 | Requires network access + moderate skill |
| **A** — Affected Users | 6 | Plant operators, downstream supply chain |
| **D** — Discoverability | 7 | Public CVE + Shodan-exposed devices |

### Step 3 — Compute the Composite DREAD Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DREAD}_{\text{total}} &= D + R + E + A + D \\
&= 9 + 8 + 7 + 6 + 7 \\
&= 37
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Normalize the Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DREAD}_{\text{normalized}} &= \frac{\text{DREAD}_{\text{total}}}{5} \\
&= \frac{37}{5} \\
&= 7.4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Interpretation Threshold (Microsoft SDL legacy):**
> - **0 – 3.9** → Low risk (monitor)
> - **4.0 – 6.9** → Medium risk (plan mitigation)
> - **7.0 – 10.0** → **High risk (immediate mitigation required)**

Our PLC scores **7.4 → High risk** → immediate patching + network segmentation recommended.

### Step 5 — Mapping to CVSS for Cross-Validation

The same vulnerability might be rated **CVSS 8.6 (High)** if it requires low-privilege network access and impacts safety systems. The KTU examiner may award bonus marks if you cross-validate DREAD with CVSS or RPN.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Risk Priority Number (RPN) for a Sensor Drift Threat

FMEA-style RPN is heavily used in **IEC 62443-3-2** for OT systems.

### Step 1 — Define the Failure Mode

A pressure transmitter drifts by 2% per month, leading to over-pressurization in a storage tank.

### Step 2 — Score S, O, D on 1–10 scale

| Factor | Score | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **S**everity | 8 | Potential tank rupture |
| **O**ccurrence | 5 | Happens in ~1 in 50 operational months |
| **D**etection | 6 | Drift only caught during monthly manual calibration |

### Step 3 — Compute RPN

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 6 \\
&= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **RPN Interpretation:**
> - < 100 → Acceptable
> - 100 – 200 → Reduce through improvement
> - **> 200 → Mandatory corrective action** ← our 240 falls here.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat Methodology Selection for CPS

| Methodology | Strength | Weakness | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STRIDE** | Simple, exhaustive categorical coverage | Lacks risk prioritization | Initial system design, academic teaching |
| **PASTA** | Business-aligned, quantitative | Time-intensive | Critical infrastructure, finance |
| **VAST** | Scalable, agile integration | Requires tool support (e.g., IriusRisk) | DevSecOps in CPS startups |
| **TARA** | Covers accidental + malicious threats | Complex taxonomy | Defense, automotive (ISO 21434) |
| **Attack Trees** | Visualizes attack paths | Doesn't scale for large systems | Specific high-value target analysis |
| **Attack Graphs** | Captures multi-step attacks | State-space explosion | Network-level CPS analysis |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — CPS Threat Modeling Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: System Scope and Asset Identification] --> A2[Phase 2: DFD Decomposition across Purdue Levels 0 to 5]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Threat Identification using STRIDE and TARA]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Vulnerability Mapping via CVE and CWE]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Risk Quantification using DREAD and CVSS and RPN]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Mitigation Design and Validation]
    A6 --> A7[Re-validate after each system update]
    A7 --> A2

    subgraph ITandOT["Purdue Reference Model"]
        L0[Level 0 Physical Process] --> L1[Level 1 Basic Control PLCs and RTUs]
        L1 --> L2[Level 2 Supervisory SCADA and HMI]
        L2 --> L3[Level 3 Operations DMZ Historian]
        L3 --> L4[Level 4 Enterprise ERP and Cloud]
    end

    A2 -.uses.-> ITandOT
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — STRIDE-to-Mitigation Mapping for a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[S Spoofing] --> M1[Strong Authentication: mTLS and PKI]
    T[T Tampering] --> M2[Code Signing and CRC on Control Packets]
    R[R Repudiation] --> M3[Tamper-Evident Audit Logs]
    I[I Info Disclosure] --> M4[Encryption of OT Traffic e.g. OPC UA]
    D[D Denial of Service] --> M5[Network Segmentation and QoS]
    E[E Elevation of Privilege] --> M6[Role-Based Access Control per IEC 62443]

    S --> PLC[PLC Controller]
    T --> PLC
    R --> HMI[Operator HMI]
    I --> BUS[Field Bus e.g. Modbus and CAN]
    D --> BUS
    E --> WS[Engineering Workstation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Generic Attack Tree for a CPS Substation Breach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Goal: Compromise Substation Control]

    ROOT --> N1[Physical Intrusion at Substation]
    ROOT --> N2[Remote Cyber Intrusion]
    ROOT --> N3[Supply Chain Compromise]

    N1 --> N1A[Gain physical access to RTU cabinet]
    N1A --> N1A1[AND Bypass mechanical lock]
    N1A --> N1A2[AND Connect serial cable to RTU]

    N2 --> N2A[Phish operator to get VPN credentials]
    N2A --> N2A1[OR Use exposed VPN endpoint]
    N2A --> N2A2[OR Steal laptop from operator]

    N3 --> N3A[Compromise firmware update server]
    N3A --> N3A1[Push malicious firmware to RTU]
    N3A1 --> ROOTGOAL[Reach Goal: Send False Trip Command]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the tree:** Each *OR* node represents an alternative attack path; each *AND* node represents a sub-goal that *must* be achieved in sequence. The leaf nodes represent concrete attacker actions.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Trust Boundaries in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Field["Trust Boundary 1: Field Zone Purdue 0 to 1"]
        S1[Pressure Sensor] --> C1[PLC Controller]
        T1[Temperature Sensor] --> C1
        C1 --> A1[Actuator Valve]
    end

    subgraph Supervisory["Trust Boundary 2: Supervisory Zone Purdue 2 to 3"]
        HMI[Operator HMI]
        HIST[Process Historian]
    end

    subgraph Enterprise["Trust Boundary 3: Enterprise Zone Purdue 4 to 5"]
        ERP[Corporate ERP]
        CLOUD[Cloud Analytics]
    end

    C1 --> HMI
    HMI --> HIST
    HIST --> ERP
    HIST --> CLOUD

    Field -.untrusted.- Supervisory
    Supervisory -.untrusted.- Enterprise
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Trust boundaries are where STRIDE threats concentrate.** Every arrow crossing a boundary is a candidate point of attack.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Advanced Threat Modeling in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any two CPS-specific threats not found in traditional IT systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Advanced Threat Modeling is a structured, proactive engineering process that systematically identifies, classifies, and prioritizes cybersecurity threats to a CPS, while designing mitigations. It accounts for the **CIAS (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Safety)** properties unique to physical systems.

Two CPS-specific threats:
1. **Sensor spoofing** — injecting false physical measurements (e.g., fake GPS coordinates to a drone) → can cause unsafe control decisions.
2. **Actuator command injection** — sending malicious control setpoints (e.g., opening a circuit breaker at the wrong time) → can disrupt critical infrastructure.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between STRIDE and PASTA threat modeling methodologies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | STRIDE | PASTA |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Threat categorization | Risk + business impact |
| Stages | Single-phase (per-element) | Seven-stage pipeline |
| Output | Threat list per DFD element | Risk-ranked attack scenarios |
| CPS suitability | Good for initial design | Strong for critical infrastructure |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **STRIDE methodology** in detail. For each letter, provide one real-world CPS example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCADA-controlled water treatment plant has a wireless sensor network (WSN) reporting chlorine levels. Construct a **STRIDE threat model** for this scenario and propose three mitigations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — STRIDE Detailed (7 marks):**

STRIDE is a mnemonic coined by Microsoft enumerating **six threat categories**, each violating one security property. For a CPS:

1. **S — Spoofing:** Forging the identity of a sensor (e.g., fake chlorine sensor report). *Mitigation:* mutual TLS, device certificates per IEC 62443.
2. **T — Tampering:** Modifying a control command in transit (e.g., changing valve opening from 30% to 90%). *Mitigation:* cryptographic message authentication (HMAC, CMAC).
3. **R — Repudiation:** Operator denies triggering a sequence of events. *Mitigation:* tamper-evident logging, hardware TPM-based attestation.
4. **I — Information Disclosure:** Eavesdropping on WSN traffic to infer plant operations. *Mitigation:* AES-128 encryption of sensor payloads.
5. **D — Denial of Service:** Jamming the WSN or flooding the SCADA server. *Mitigation:* redundant sensors, frequency hopping, rate limiting.
6. **E — Elevation of Privilege:** Exploiting a vulnerable HMI web server to issue control commands. *Mitigation:* role-based access control, network segmentation, least-privilege.

**Part (b) — STRIDE for WSN-based Water Treatment (7 marks):**

| STRIDE | Threat Scenario | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Spoofing | Attacker spoofs a chlorine sensor with low reading | Mutual authentication + signed telemetry |
| Tampering | Modified setpoint raises chlorine to dangerous level | HMAC integrity on each packet |
| Repudiation | Plant operator denies enabling remote access | Immutable logs on a write-once server |
| Information Disclosure | WSN traffic reveals chemical process IP | AES encryption at the MAC layer |
| DoS | RF jamming of WSN → chlorine stays at last value | Redundant sensors + sanity-check alarms |
| Elevation of Privilege | VPN credential theft gives attacker SCADA access | Multi-factor auth + jump server with audit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the **"security property violated"** for each STRIDE letter. This costs 1 mark per letter, i.e., up to 6 marks lost. Always state: *Spoofing ↔ Authentication*, *Tampering ↔ Integrity*, etc.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **seven stages of the PASTA methodology**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS vulnerability has the following DREAD scores: Damage = 9, Reproducibility = 7, Exploitability = 8, Affected Users = 6, Discoverability = 5. Compute the **normalized DREAD score** and recommend a mitigation priority. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — PASTA Seven Stages (7 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 — Define Business Objectives:** Identify the *why* — e.g., safe water supply, regulatory compliance.
2. **Stage 2 — Define Technical Scope:** Enumerate the systems, protocols (Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA), and boundaries.
3. **Stage 3 — Application Decomposition:** Build DFDs, trust boundaries, and use cases.
4. **Stage 4 — Threat Analysis:** Enumerate threat agents and motivations (insider, nation-state, hacktivist).
5. **Stage 5 — Vulnerability & Weakness Analysis:** Map CVEs, CWE entries, and design flaws.
6. **Stage 6 — Attack Modeling:** Construct attack trees / graphs showing paths to compromise.
7. **Stage 7 — Risk & Impact Analysis:** Compute quantitative risk (ALE), prioritize mitigations.

**Part (b) — DREAD Computation (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DREAD}_{\text{total}} &= D + R + E + A + D \\
&= 9 + 7 + 8 + 6 + 5 \\
&= 35
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DREAD}_{\text{normalized}} &= \frac{35}{5} \\
&= 7.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation Priority Recommendation (per Microsoft SDL threshold):**
> A normalized score of **7.0 lies at the high-risk boundary** → **Immediate mitigation is mandatory.** Recommended actions:
> 1. Apply emergency patch within 72 hours.
> 2. Deploy virtual patching via WAF/IPS until patch is feasible.
> 3. Restrict network exposure via firewall rules + zero-trust segmentation.
> 4. Initiate compensating monitoring (SIEM correlation rules).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A common mistake is dividing by **10** instead of **5**. The denominator is **5** because DREAD sums **five components** (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability). Writing 35/10 = 3.5 will be marked **wrong** even if the rest is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Advanced Threat Modeling for CPS**

- **Definition:** Systematic identification + classification + prioritization of CPS threats; part of SDLC.
- **CIA vs CIAS:** CPS adds **Safety** as a first-class property.
- **Purdue Model:** Levels 0–5 organize CPS zones; trust boundaries exist at each level transition.
- **STRIDE:** Six threat categories — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **PASTA:** Seven-stage risk-centric methodology — business → technical → attack → impact.
- **VAST:** Scalable, agile, two-diagram approach (Process + Application DFD).
- **TARA:** MITRE methodology covering malicious + accidental + environmental threats (CPS-specific).
- **Attack Tree:** AND/OR decomposition of attacker goals; great for high-value target analysis.
- **Attack Graph:** State-transition model for chained exploits; suited to network-level CPS.
- **DREAD Formula:** (D+R+E+A+D) / 5 → range 1–10; ≥ 7 = high risk.
- **RPN Formula:** S × O × D → range 1–1000; > 200 = mandatory action.
- **CVSS Range:** 0.0 – 10.0; ≥ 7.0 = High severity.
- **IEC 62443:** Industrial automation cybersecurity standard governing CPS threat modeling.
- **NIST 800-82:** Guide to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) security — pairs with threat modeling.
- **ISO 21434:** Automotive cybersecurity engineering — uses TARA heavily.
- **STRIDE Mapping (must memorize):** S↔Auth, T↔Integrity, R↔Non-Repudiation, I↔Confidentiality, D↔Availability, E↔Authorization.
- **Trust Boundary:** A perimeter where data crosses subjects of different privilege; STRIDE is applied at *every* boundary crossing.
- **Lifecycle Integration:** Threat modeling is iterative — re-run after every architecture change, firmware update, or new CVE publication.
- **Tools (FYI):** Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, IriusRisk, OWASP Threat Dragon, ThreatModeler, SD Elements.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled DFD** with explicit trust boundaries before applying STRIDE. Examiners award 2–3 bonus marks for visual clarity and proper Purdue-level labeling.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Threat Modeling for Sophisticated CPS Architectures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Detailed Threat Modeling for Sophisticated CPS Architectures

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Threat Modeling for CPS** is a structured, systematic, and iterative engineering process used to identify, enumerate, classify, prioritize, and mitigate potential security threats that target the cyber-physical convergence layer of a CPS. It is the disciplined practice of mapping adversary capabilities against system assets, data flows, trust boundaries, and physical control loops to produce a defensible, prioritized set of countermeasures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), threat modeling is positioned as the **first analytical pillar** of any CPS security program, sitting upstream of penetration testing, intrusion detection, and incident response.

A *Sophisticated CPS Architecture* refers to multi-domain systems where **OT (Operational Technology)**, **IT (Information Technology)**, **IoT/IIoT endpoints**, **edge/fog/cloud compute tiers**, and **physical process plants** are tightly coupled through real-time control loops. Examples include smart grids, autonomous vehicle fleets, Industry 4.0 plants, and water-treatment SCADA networks.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

| Term | Working Definition | CPS Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset** | Anything of value within the CPS | PLC, RTU, sensor, firmware, control recipe, safety log |
| **Threat** | Potential cause of an unwanted incident | Malware injection on Modbus/TCP |
| **Vulnerability** | Weakness that can be exploited | Hardcoded credential in HMI |
| **Attack Surface** | Sum of reachable entry/exit points | Fieldbus ports, OPC UA endpoints |
| **Trust Boundary** | Logical or physical line separating privilege domains | IT-OT DMZ, controller-to-actuator zone |
| **Adversary** | Entity with intent and capability | Nation-state APT, insider, hacktivist |
| **Consequence** | Impact on safety, mission, business, privacy | Turbine overspeed → physical destruction |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Threat Audit"

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Pump for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a bank vault. Before bolting steel to concrete, auditors ask four questions:
> 1. **What** are we protecting? (gold bars, customer data, vault combinations).
> 2. **Who** might attack it? (lone thief, organized gang, foreign intelligence).
> 3. **How** could they get in? (tunnel, ventilation, social engineering of guard).
> 4. **What happens if they succeed?** (loss of money, loss of life, regulatory penalty).
>
> Threat modeling for CPS asks the *exact same four questions*, but for a refinery, substation, or autonomous vehicle. The "vault" is now a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), the "gold" is the control recipe, and the "guard" is a network firewall between enterprise IT and plant OT. The crucial CPS twist is that **failure can hurt or kill people in the physical world**, not just steal money in the digital one.

## 1.4 Why Threat Modeling is Non-Optional for Sophisticated CPS

Sophisticated CPS architectures have three properties that make ad-hoc security impossible:

1. **Convergence of safety and security** — A Stuxnet-style attack manipulates the *physics*, not just the bits.
2. **Asymmetric attack surface** — One exploited PLC can move a million-ton turbine.
3. **Long lifecycle mismatch** — Field devices live 20+ years while threats evolve monthly.

These three realities elevate threat modeling from a "nice-to-have" exercise to a **mandatory engineering activity** under frameworks such as **NIST SP 800-82**, **IEC 62443-3-3**, and **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Phases of CPS Threat Modeling

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examinable Decomposition**
> Every modern threat modeling methodology (STRIDE, PASTA, VAST, Trike, Attack Trees) collapses into the **same four-phase spine**. Memorize this spine — questions on it appear every semester.

### Phase 1 — System Characterization (Build the Model)
- Define the **CPS architecture**: sensors → local controllers → edge gateways → cloud → actuators.
- Enumerate **data flows** (DFD level 0, then level 1).
- Mark **trust boundaries** explicitly (e.g., Field ↔ Control, Control ↔ Supervisory, OT ↔ IT).
- Document **safety integrity levels** (SIL 1–4) and **security levels** (SL 1–4 under IEC 62443).
- Identify **physical consequences**: kinetic, thermal, chemical, electrical, biological.

### Phase 2 — Threat Identification
- Apply a structured catalog (STRIDE, ATT&CK for ICS, CAPEC).
- For each DFD element, ask *"What could go wrong here?"*
- Use **attack trees** to decompose high-level goals into leaf-level attack steps.
- For CPS, expand the catalog to include:
  - Replay attacks on sensor measurements
  - Command injection on actuator setpoints
  - Time-synchronization manipulation (e.g., GPS spoofing on PMUs)
  - Firmware tampering of PLCs
  - Man-in-the-middle on industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, PROFINET, EtherCAT, OPC UA)

### Phase 3 — Vulnerability & Risk Analysis
- Map each threat to existing controls (or the absence thereof).
- Score each threat using a quantitative or semi-quantitative model.
- The universally accepted baseline equation (used in NIST SP 800-30 and ISO 31000) is:

$$R = f(T, V, I)$$

Expanded into the classical product form taught across KTU cybersecurity modules:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk score** (unitless, on a 1–125 scale when each factor is 1–5)
- $T$ = **Threat likelihood** (probability that an adversary attempts the attack, range 1–5)
- $V$ = **Vulnerability severity** (CVSS-derived or expert-assigned, range 1–5)
- $I$ = **Impact magnitude** (safety + mission + financial + privacy, range 1–5)

For CPS, the **Impact** term is amplified by a **Safety Multiplier** $\sigma$ when the attack can lead to loss of life:

$$I_{CPS} = \sigma \cdot (I_{cyber} + I_{safety} + I_{mission})$$

with $\sigma \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ depending on proximity to hazardous energy.

### Phase 4 — Mitigation Planning & Validation
- For every high-risk threat, choose one of the four NIST-standard responses: **Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept**.
- Map controls to defense-in-depth layers: Network, Host, Application, Data, Physical.
- Re-score residual risk $R_{res}$ after controls:

$$R_{res} = \frac{R_{initial}}{C_{effectiveness}}$$

where $C_{effectiveness} \in [1, 10]$ is the aggregate control effectiveness rating (1 = no change, 10 = complete neutralization).

- Feed residual risks into the **Cybersecurity Safety Risk Assessment (CSRA)** required by IEC 62443-3-2.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Examiner's Tip**: This table is the single highest-yield reference for any derivation or numerical question on Module 1.

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Form | Engineering Utility | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classical risk | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Baseline NIST/ISO risk scoring | Each factor on 1–5, $R \in [1, 125]$ |
| CPS risk with safety weighting | $R_{CPS} = T \cdot V \cdot \sigma I$ | Penalizes life-safety threats | $\sigma \in [1, 3]$ |
| Residual risk | $R_{res} = R / C$ | Quantifies post-control risk | $C \in [1, 10]$ |
| Attack tree leaf probability | $P_{root} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i)$ | OR-gate aggregation of attack steps | $p_i \in [0, 1]$, $P_{root} \in [0, 1]$ |
| Attack tree AND-gate | $P_{root} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Sequential dependency aggregation | $p_i \in [0, 1]$ |
| CVSS base score (v3.1) | $BaseScore = round(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))$ | Standardized vulnerability severity | $0 \le BaseScore \le 10$ |
| Mean Time to Compromise | $MTTC = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$ | Predicts adversary dwell time | $\lambda_i$ = attack path rates (per day) |
| STRIDE mnemonic | $S, T, R, I, D, E$ | Six threat categories per DFD element | Six categorical labels |
| PASTA stages | $Stage\,1 \rightarrow 7$ | Risk-centric 7-step model | Seven sequential stages |
| Safety-Security barrier model | $P_{accident} = P_{threat} \cdot P_{breach} \cdot P_{propagation}$ | Swiss-Cheese / Bow-Tie model | Dimensionless probability |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Notation Discipline (Anti-Markdown-Break Rule)**
> In all KTU answer scripts, absolute-value and conditional bars must be written as $\vert x \vert$, never with the unescaped vertical pipe `|`, so that your LaTeX and your table cells never clash.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Phase — A Pedagogical Walk

| Phase | Engineering "Why" | Real CPS Failure It Prevents |
|---|---|---|
| Characterization | You cannot defend what you have not drawn | Maroochy Shire water breach (2000) — un-tracked radio telemetry |
| Threat ID | Adversaries enumerate faster than defenders | Triton/TRISIS (2017) — missed safety-instrumented-system threat |
| Risk Analysis | Resources are finite; you must rank | Industroyer/CrashOverride (2016) — wasted controls on low-impact nodes |
| Mitigation | Controls must map back to a specific threat | Colonial Pipeline (2021) — controls existed but were not threat-driven |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting CVSS Base Metrics into the KTU Risk Score

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Derivation (Examiner's Favourite Pattern)**
> A PLC firmware vulnerability in a petrochemical plant has the following CVSS v3.1 vector:
> `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`
> The site uses a 1–5 scale for $T$, $V$, $I$. Derive the CPS risk score $R_{CPS}$ given $T=4$, safety multiplier $\sigma=3$, and a planned control with $C=4$.

### Step 1 — Parse the CVSS Vector
- AV:N → Network-attackable
- AC:L → Low attack complexity
- PR:N → No privileges required
- UI:N → No user interaction
- S:U → Scope unchanged
- C:H / I:H / A:H → High impact on Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability

### Step 2 — Compute CVSS Base Score
Using the official CVSS v3.1 equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
ISS &= 1 - (1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56) \\
    &= 1 - (0.44)^3 \\
    &= 1 - 0.085184 \\
    &= 0.914816
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Impact &= 6.42 \times ISS = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.8735
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Exploitability &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
              &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
              &= 3.887
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
BaseScore &= round\Big(\min(Impact + Exploitability,\; 10)\Big) \\
          &= round(\min(5.8735 + 3.887,\; 10)) \\
          &= round(9.76) \\
          &= \mathbf{9.8 \;\; (Critical)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Map CVSS Base Score to $V$ (1–5 Scale)
The mapping rule taught in KTU Module 1:

$$V = \left\lceil \frac{CVSS_{base}}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{9.8}{2} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.9 \rceil = \mathbf{5}$$

### Step 4 — Compute CPS Risk

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{CPS} &= T \times V \times \sigma I \\
        &= 4 \times 5 \times 3 \times I
\end{aligned}$$

Assume worst-case Impact $I = 5$ (loss of containment possible):

$$R_{CPS} = 4 \times 5 \times 3 \times 5 = \mathbf{300}$$

On the classical 1–125 scale, scores $>100$ are flagged **MANDATORY-MITIGATE**.

### Step 5 — Apply the Control to Get Residual Risk

$$R_{res} = \frac{R_{CPS}}{C_{effectiveness}} = \frac{300}{4} = \mathbf{75}$$

A residual score of 75 is still in the "Elevated" band → a second control layer is required.

### Final Interpretation
| Metric | Value | Band | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| CVSS Base | 9.8 | Critical | Patch within 7 days |
| $V$ | 5/5 | Critical | — |
| $R_{CPS}$ initial | 300 | Mandatory-Mitigate | Engineering control within 30 days |
| $R_{CPS}$ residual | 75 | Elevated | Compensating control + monitoring |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Attack Tree Probability Aggregation

Consider an attacker trying to manipulate a smart-grid substation. The attack tree root goal $G$ branches via OR-gate into two independent sub-goals: *Compromise HMI* ($p_1 = 0.30$) and *Compromise Substation Gateway* ($p_2 = 0.20$). Each sub-goal is an AND of two leaves.

### Step 1 — AND-gate Aggregation for $p_1$

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= p_{1a} \cdot p_{1b} = 0.50 \times 0.60 = 0.30
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — AND-gate Aggregation for $p_2$

$$\begin{aligned}
p_2 &= p_{2a} \cdot p_{2b} = 0.40 \times 0.50 = 0.20
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — OR-gate Aggregation at the Root

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{root} &= 1 - (1 - p_1)(1 - p_2) \\
         &= 1 - (0.70)(0.80) \\
         &= 1 - 0.56 \\
         &= \mathbf{0.44}
\end{aligned}$$

Interpretation: A 44% chance that **some** viable attack path exists. This justifies investing in a defense-in-depth strategy that breaks *both* independent paths.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Six Threat Modeling Methodologies

> [!NOTE] **Frequently Tested in KTU Comparisons**
> Examiners love "Compare STRIDE and PASTA in the context of a refinery" type questions. This matrix is your ready-made answer skeleton.

| Methodology | Origin | Primary Lens | Strength for CPS | Weakness for CPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **STRIDE** | Microsoft (1999) | Per-DFD-element threats | Easy to teach, broad coverage | Misses adversary motivation and impact magnitude |
| **PASTA** | Tony UcedaVélez & Marco Morana (2012) | 7-stage risk-centric | Excellent business-risk alignment | Heavy documentation overhead |
| **VAST** | ThreatModeler (2012) | Scalable Agile | Suits large plants, visual | Licensing cost |
| **Attack Trees** | Bruce Schneier (1999) | Goal-decomposition | Handles multi-step physical attacks | Static, no temporal modelling |
| **Attack Graphs** | Sheyner et al. (2002) | Network state-space | Captures cascading CPS failures | State-space explosion |
| **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** | MITRE (2020) | Adversary TTP catalog | Maps real APT tradecraft | Not a modeling method by itself |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference CPS Architecture — Smart Manufacturing Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    field1["Field Layer - Sensors and Actuators"]
    ctrl1["Control Layer - PLC and RTU"]
    sup1["Supervisory Layer - SCADA HMI"]
    hist1["Operations Layer - Historian and MES"]
    ent1["Enterprise IT Layer - ERP and Email"]
    cloud1["Cloud Analytics Tier"]
    edge1["Edge Gateway"]
    dmz1["IT OT DMZ Firewall"]
    guard1["Safety Instrumented System SIS"]

    field1 -->|4-20 mA and Modbus| ctrl1
    ctrl1 -->|OPC UA| sup1
    ctrl1 -.->|Hardwired Safety| guard1
    sup1 -->|SQL over TCP| hist1
    hist1 -->|REST API| dmz1
    dmz1 -->|HTTPS| ent1
    hist1 -->|MQTT TLS| edge1
    edge1 -->|OPC UA over TLS| cloud1
    guard1 -.->|Independent Shutdown| field1
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> Every node-to-node arrow is a *data flow*. Every implicit boundary between layers (e.g., between `hist1` and `dmz1`) is a *trust boundary* that must be explicitly drawn in your exam Data Flow Diagram (DFD). In the KTU valuation key, missing trust boundaries cost one mark per layer — five boundaries, five marks.

## 4.2 STRIDE-on-DFD Mapping (Class-Wise Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dfdNode["DFD Element e.g. PLC"]
    s["S - Spoofing"]
    t["T - Tampering"]
    r["R - Repudiation"]
    i["I - Information Disclosure"]
    d["D - Denial of Service"]
    e["E - Elevation of Privilege"]
    dfdNode --> s
    dfdNode --> t
    dfdNode --> r
    dfdNode --> i
    dfdNode --> d
    dfdNode --> e
```

| STRIDE Letter | Category | CPS-Specific Example |
|---|---|---|
| S | Spoofing | Forged Modbus master identity |
| T | Tampering | Modified ladder logic on PLC |
| R | Repudiation | Deleted audit logs on HMI |
| I | Information Disclosure | Process secret exfiltrated over DNS tunnel |
| D | Denial of Service | Stuxnet-style centrifuge over-speed DoS |
| E | Elevation of Privilege | Engineer account escalated to Safety Admin |

## 4.3 Four-Phase CPS Threat Modeling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ph1["Phase 1 System Characterization"]
    ph2["Phase 2 Threat Identification"]
    ph3["Phase 3 Vulnerability and Risk Analysis"]
    ph4["Phase 4 Mitigation Planning and Validation"]
    fb["Iterate and Feed Back into Design"]
    ph1 --> ph2 --> ph3 --> ph4 --> fb
    fb -.-> ph1
```

## 4.4 Bow-Tie Safety-Security Model for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    threat["Left Side Threats - Cyber Attack, Insider, Malware"]
    topEvent["Top Event - Loss of Control of Physical Process"]
    consequence["Right Side Consequences - Fire, Explosion, Toxic Release, Downtime"]
    prevCtrl["Preventive Controls - Network Segmentation, AuthN, Patching"]
    mitCtrl["Mitigative Controls - SIS, Alarms, Emergency Shutdown"]

    threat --> topEvent
    topEvent --> consequence
    prevCtrl -.->|blocks| topEvent
    mitCtrl -.->|limits| consequence
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why this Diagram is Examinable**
> The Bow-Tie model unifies **safety** (right-side barriers from IEC 61511) with **security** (left-side barriers from IEC 62443). KTU 2024 expects graduates to argue that **safety and security are not separable** in CPS — a question worth 7 marks appears regularly.

## 4.5 Attack Tree Example — Manipulation of a Smart-Grid Substation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["GOAL Manipulate Substation Breaker State"]
    a1["Compromise HMI"]
    a2["Compromise Gateway"]
    a3["Compromise Field Tech Laptop"]
    leafA1["Phish Engineer - p=0.50"]
    leafA2["Exploit HMI CVE - p=0.60"]
    leafB1["Brute Force SSH - p=0.40"]
    leafB2["Replay Firmware Update - p=0.50"]
    leafC1["Stolen USB Token - p=0.20"]
    leafC2["Malicious Logic Upload - p=0.70"]

    root --> a1
    root --> a2
    root --> a3
    a1 --> leafA1
    a1 --> leafA2
    a2 --> leafB1
    a2 --> leafB2
    a3 --> leafC1
    a3 --> leafC2
```

Probabilities were deliberately omitted from the Mermaid diagram to keep the visual readable. The leaf probabilities feed the analytical aggregation shown in §3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define *Threat Modeling* in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four DFD elements you would draw for a substation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Threat modeling is a structured, systematic engineering process used to identify, classify, and prioritize potential cyber threats targeting the cyber-physical convergence of a CPS, and to design mitigations before deployment or modification.
>
> **Four DFD Elements (2 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
> 1. **Process** — e.g., the PLC executing the protection logic.
> 2. **Data Store** — e.g., the historian or sequence-of-events recorder.
> 3. **External Entity** — e.g., the engineering workstation or control center.
> 4. **Data Flow** — e.g., the IEC 61850 GOOSE message between IEDs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare STRIDE and PASTA in two points each, as applicable to an industrial CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | STRIDE | PASTA |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin / Lens** | Microsoft, 1999 — per-element categorical | UcedaVélez & Morana, 2012 — risk-centric |
| **Strength for CPS** | Fast, broad coverage across all DFD elements | Aligns technical threats with business and safety risk |
| **Weakness for CPS** | No native support for adversary motive or impact severity | Documentation-heavy; slower for small teams |
| **Output** | Threat list per element | Risk-ranked threat list with mitigation roadmap |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat Data Flow Diagram, describe **Phase 1 (System Characterization)** of threat modeling for a **smart water-treatment plant**. Clearly mark **all trust boundaries** and **safety zones**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **STRIDE** methodology to **one sensor, one PLC, and one HMI** in the same plant, listing at least **two threats per STRIDE letter**. Conclude with a **prioritized threat table**. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) System Characterization — 7 Marks

> **Architecture Description (2 Marks):** A smart water-treatment plant comprises a *Field Layer* (flow, pH, turbidity sensors; motorized valves; chemical dosing pumps), a *Control Layer* (PLCs executing PID loops), a *Supervisory Layer* (HMI + SCADA server), a *Historian + MES* tier, and an *Enterprise IT* network connected via an **IT/OT DMZ firewall**. A separate **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is physically isolated and hard-wired to emergency shut-off valves.

> **Trust Boundaries (3 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
> 1. Field ↔ Control (sensor/actuator wiring)
> 2. Control ↔ Supervisory (OPC UA boundary)
> 3. Supervisory ↔ Historian (SQL boundary)
> 4. Historian ↔ Enterprise IT (DMZ)
> 5. Control ↔ Safety (hardwired, one-way)

> **Safety Zones (2 Marks):** Zone 0 — Field (hazardous, classified area); Zone 1 — Control (SIL 2); Zone 2 — Supervisory (no SIL requirement); Zone 3 — Enterprise IT.

#### (b) STRIDE Application + Prioritized Table — 7 Marks

| STRIDE | Sensor (e.g., turbidity meter) | PLC | HMI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Forged analog reading injected into 4-20 mA loop | Fake firmware update from rogue engineering station | Stolen operator credentials |
| **Tampering** | Calibration constants modified in EEPROM | Ladder logic overwritten via remote programming port | Recipe parameters altered in HMI project file |
| **Repudiation** | No sensor-side logging to challenge operator | PLC event log cleared by attacker | HMI audit trail disabled via registry edit |
| **Info Disclosure** | Sensor health status reveals process topology | PLC project downloaded by exfil tool | HMI screenshots posted publicly (Pastebin) |
| **DoS** | Cable cut → operator blind to turbidity | CPU overload via crafted PROFINET frame | HMI flooded with pop-ups (ransomware-style) |
| **Elevation** | Tamper-evident bypass of safety sensor | Engineering account escalated to Safety Admin role | HMI service account promoted to domain admin |

> **Prioritized Top-3 Threats (1 Mark):**
> 1. PLC ladder-logic tampering (R = T·V·σI = 5·5·3·5 = 375).
> 2. Sensor spoofing of turbidity reading → unsafe dosing (R = 320).
> 3. HMI DoS during chemical upset (R = 270).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Construct an **attack tree** for the goal *"Inject false setpoints into a chemical reactor's temperature controller"*. Show **one OR-root, three AND-branches, and at least five leaves** with assigned probabilities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **root probability** of attack success and derive the **CPS risk score** given $T=5$, $V=4$, $I=5$, and safety multiplier $\sigma=3$. Recommend the **residual risk** if a control with effectiveness $C=5$ is deployed. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Attack Tree Construction — 7 Marks

> **Goal:** Inject false setpoints into the reactor temperature controller.
>
> **OR-Root decomposes into three AND-branches:**
>
> **Branch B1 — Compromise Engineering Workstation** (leaves: phishing engineer, p = 0.55; exploit HMI CVE, p = 0.65).
>
> **Branch B2 — Compromise Control Network Path** (leaves: brute-force VPN, p = 0.30; MITM on OPC UA, p = 0.45).
>
> **Branch B3 — Compromise Field Segment** (leaves: rogue PLC programmer via serial, p = 0.25; firmware tampering via USB, p = 0.40).

(1 Mark for each correctly drawn branch; 2 Marks for proper leaf probabilities — any two leaves per branch at ½ Mark each; 2 Marks for correct AND/OR gate usage.)

#### (b) Probability + Risk Computation — 7 Marks

##### Step 1 — Per-Branch AND-gate Probabilities

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{B1} &= 0.55 \times 0.65 = 0.3575 \\
P_{B2} &= 0.30 \times 0.45 = 0.1350 \\
P_{B3} &= 0.25 \times 0.40 = 0.1000
\end{aligned}$$

(1 Mark — show work for each.)

##### Step 2 — OR-gate Root Probability

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{root} &= 1 - (1 - 0.3575)(1 - 0.1350)(1 - 0.1000) \\
         &= 1 - (0.6425)(0.8650)(0.9000) \\
         &= 1 - 0.5003 \\
         &= \mathbf{0.4997 \approx 0.50}
\end{aligned}$$

(1 Mark for the equation; 1 Mark for the numerical result.)

##### Step 3 — CPS Risk Score

$$R_{CPS} = T \cdot V \cdot \sigma I = 5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 5 = \mathbf{300}$$

(1 Mark — correct formula with safety multiplier; 1 Mark — correct arithmetic.)

##### Step 4 — Residual Risk after Control

$$R_{res} = \frac{R_{CPS}}{C_{effectiveness}} = \frac{300}{5} = \mathbf{60}$$

(1 Mark for the residual formula; 1 Mark for the final value.)

> **Verdict:** Residual = 60 still in *Elevated* band → a **second compensating control** (e.g., cryptographic authentication of setpoints + redundant safety temperature trip) is recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Module 1**
> 1. **Forgetting the trust boundary line.** Every DFD drawn without an explicit boundary between IT and OT loses **2 marks minimum**. Use a dotted vertical line, not just a textual label.
> 2. **Ignoring the safety multiplier $\sigma$.** Students routinely submit $R = T \times V \times I = 100$ and get only *half* marks because the examiner expects the CPS-augmented form $R_{CPS} = T \cdot V \cdot \sigma I$. Always ask yourself: *"Can this attack hurt someone in the physical world?"* If yes, multiply.
> 3. **Confusing STRIDE with a verb.** STRIDE is an *acronym*, not an action. Examiners deduct marks when students write "STRIDE the system" instead of "Apply STRIDE to the system."
> 4. **Drawing only one branch of an attack tree.** A tree with only one OR-branch is *just a list*. You must show at least **two parallel branches** at the root for the answer to qualify as a true tree.
> 5. **Skipping the iteration arrow.** Threat modeling is **iterative**. If your block diagram omits the feedback arrow from mitigation back to characterization, you lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Using $|$ inside markdown tables.** Always escape as $\vert$ or $\mid$ so your equations do not break the table row.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet**

- **Definition**: Threat modeling = structured identification + classification + prioritization + mitigation of CPS threats.
- **Four Phases**: Characterization → Threat ID → Vulnerability/Risk Analysis → Mitigation/Validation → *Iterate*.
- **Three CPS-Specific Properties**: cyber-physical convergence, asymmetric impact, long lifecycle.
- **Six STRIDE letters**: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **PASTA = 7 stages**; **VAST = scalable**; **Attack Trees = goal-decomposition**; **Attack Graphs = state-space**; **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS = adversary TTP catalog**.
- **Core formula**: $R = T \times V \times I$.
- **CPS-augmented formula**: $R_{CPS} = T \cdot V \cdot \sigma I$ with $\sigma \in \{1, 2, 3\}$.
- **Residual formula**: $R_{res} = R / C$ with $C \in [1, 10]$.
- **OR-gate aggregation**: $P_{root} = 1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$.
- **AND-gate aggregation**: $P_{root} = \prod p_i$.
- **CVSS → V mapping**: $V = \lceil CVSS_{base} / 2 \rceil$.
- **Mandatory Standards to cite**: NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443-3-3, NIST SP 800-30, ISO 31000, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.
- **Mandatory Bow-Tie barriers**: left (security — preventive), right (safety — mitigative).
- **Mandatory DFD elements**: Process, Data Store, External Entity, Data Flow, **Trust Boundary**.
- **Mandatory CPS incidents to quote** for context marks: Stuxnet (2010), BlackEnergy (2015), Industroyer (2016), Triton/TRISIS (2017), Colonial Pipeline (2021), Oldsmar Water (2021), Pipedream (2022).
- **Always** close with residual-risk interpretation — never end a risk answer on $R_{initial}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Techniques for Dynamic Threat Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools and Techniques for Dynamic Threat Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Dynamic Threat Modeling (DTM)** is a continuous, adaptive, and automated methodology for identifying, evaluating, prioritizing, and mitigating security threats in real time as a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) evolves through its lifecycle. Unlike *static* (snapshot) threat modeling performed once at design time, dynamic threat modeling continuously ingests telemetry from running systems, reconciles findings with the architectural baseline, and updates the threat picture whenever code, configuration, or the operational environment changes.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, DTM is critical because the attack surface spans IT (Information Technology), OT (Operational Technology), and the physical domain (sensors, actuators, PLCs, industrial networks). The KTU 2024 scheme specifically mandates that the threat modeling approach must be **continuous**, **data-driven**, and **integrated** with DevSecOps and safety-engineering pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dynamic Threat Modeling is the iterative process of *detecting*, *enumerating*, *scoring*, and *treating* threats against a CPS as the system, its data flows, and its adversary model evolve over time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living X-Ray" of a Smart Factory

Imagine a hospital patient who, instead of taking **one** X-ray when admitted, wears a **continuous biometric wristband** that streams heart rate, oxygen, and blood-pressure data to doctors in real time. The doctors don't wait for a heart attack to study the heart — they constantly refine their diagnosis as new readings arrive.

**Dynamic Threat Modeling is the cybersecurity equivalent of that wristband for a CPS.**

- The **CPS** is the patient.
- The **threat model** is the medical chart.
- The **live telemetry** (logs, network flows, SBOMs, CVE feeds) is the biometric stream.
- The **security engineers** are the doctors.
- The **threat-modeling engine** is the diagnostic AI that updates the chart.

A *static* threat model is the single X-ray — useful at one moment, obsolete the next. A *dynamic* threat model is the continuously refreshing scan, catching new anomalies the moment they appear.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of Dynamic Threat Modeling

| # | Block | Role in DTM |
|---|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Asset Discovery** | Auto-inventory of CPS components (PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, gateways) |
| 2 | **Data-Flow Mapping** | Continuous DFD (Data Flow Diagram) reconstruction |
| 3 | **Threat Enumeration** | Application of STRIDE/PASTA against the live DFD |
| 4 | **Risk Scoring** | Real-time CVSS, SSVC, or business-impact scoring |
| 5 | **Mitigation Tracking** | Ticketing back into DevSecOps / safety workflows |
| 6 | **Feedback Loop** | Telemetry from production closes the loop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differentiator from Static Modeling:** A static model is a **photograph**; a dynamic model is a **live video stream** of the threat landscape.

## 1.4 Why DTM Matters for CPS

- **CPS uptime is safety-critical** — a missed threat can halt a power grid, not just steal data.
- **Purdue Model layers (0–5)** each have distinct trust boundaries; DTM must continuously validate them.
- **Adversary TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures)** evolve via the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS matrix.
- **Patch and configuration drift** in OT networks changes the attack surface hourly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Operational Pipeline of DTM
