The KTU syllabus aligns with a **five-phase iterative pipeline**. Each phase is *automatable* and *measurable*.

### Phase 1 — Continuous Asset & Architecture Discovery

- **Goal:** Maintain a live CMDB (Configuration Management Database) and SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) for every CPS node.
- **Inputs:** Network scans (Nmap, Nessus, OT-specific: Claroty, Nozomi Networks), cloud API inventories, agent-based discovery.
- **Outputs:** An always-fresh **System Model** $M(t)$ at time $t$.
- **Engineering utility:** Eliminates "shadow assets" — the #1 cause of breaches in industrial environments.

### Phase 2 — Live Data-Flow Reconstruction

- The system reconstructs **DFDs** (Data Flow Diagrams) by combining the live system model with code analysis (SAST) and traffic analysis (NTA).
- Trust boundaries are auto-redrawn when a new DMZ, VLAN, or field device is added.
- **STRIDE-per-element** is recomputed for every node and edge.

### Phase 3 — Threat Enumeration & Cataloging

Common frameworks reused in DTM:

| Framework | Strength | Best For |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **STRIDE** | Simple, per-element categorization | Quick CPS triage |
| **PASTA** | Attacker-centric, 7-stage | High-assurance systems |
| **LINDDUN** | Privacy-focused | Healthcare CPS |
| **VAST** | Scales with Agile/DevOps | Enterprise rollouts |
| **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** | Real adversary TTPs | OT/ICS threat intel |

### Phase 4 — Dynamic Risk Scoring

The risk score $R_i$ for threat $i$ at time $t$ is recomputed as new telemetry arrives. A common formulation is:

$$
R_i(t) = L_i(t) \times I_i(t) \times E_i(t)
$$

Where:
- $L_i(t)$ = **Likelihood** of exploitation at time $t$ (probability in $[0,1]$).
- $I_i(t)$ = **Impact** on safety, financial, and mission dimensions.
- $E_i(t)$ = **Exposure factor** (network reachability, exploit availability).

This is then mapped to a decision tier (Track / Ticket / Attend / Act) using **SSVC (Stakeholder-Specific Vulnerability Categorization)**.

### Phase 5 — Mitigation, Tracking & Feedback

- Mitigation actions (patch, segmentation, monitoring rule) are pushed to:
  - **IT side:** Jira/ServiceNow tickets, CI/CD pipeline gates.
  - **OT side:** Compensating controls (conduit lockdown, jump-server rules).
- Production telemetry validates whether mitigations reduced $R_i(t+1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Feedback Loop is non-negotiable.** Without Phase 5, DTM degrades into expensive static modeling.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative and methodological primitive a student must memorize for PECPT863 Module 1.

| # | Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Unit / Domain | Use Case in CPS |
|---|------------------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | System Model at time $t$ | $M(t) = \{N(t), E(t), B(t)\}$ | Set of nodes, edges, boundaries | Snapshot of architecture |
| 2 | Risk Score | $R_i(t) = L_i(t) \cdot I_i(t) \cdot E_i(t)$ | $[0, 10]$ (CVSS-like) | Per-threat prioritization |
| 3 | Likelihood update | $L_i(t+1) = L_i(t) + \alpha (\hat{L}_i - L_i(t))$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ learning rate | Bayesian refinement |
| 4 | Attack Path Probability | $P(\text{path}) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} p_k$ | $[0,1]$ | Attack graph traversal |
| 5 | DREAD legacy | $\text{DREAD} = \tfrac{D+R+E+A+1}{5}$ | Score $\in [1,10]$ | Historical reference |
| 6 | CVSS Base | $f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | $[0,10]$ | Industry-standard scoring |
| 7 | SSVC decision | $\{Track, Ticket, Attend, Act\}$ | Categorical | Action-oriented triage |
| 8 | MTTC (Mean Time to Contain) | $\text{MTTC} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(t_{\text{contained},j}-t_{\text{detected},j})$ | Hours / minutes | KPI of DTM maturity |
| 9 | Coverage Ratio | $\text{CR} = \tfrac{\mid \text{Modeled Assets} \mid}{\mid \text{Discovered Assets} \mid} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | DTM completeness |
| 10 | Drift Rate | $\delta = \tfrac{\Delta M}{M_{\text{baseline}}}$ | Per unit time | Stability of architecture |
| 11 | STRIDE Categories | $S, T, R, I, D, E$ | 6 categories | Per-element classification |
| 12 | PASTA Stages | $7$ stages: Define → Decompose → Analyze → Threat → Vuln → Attack → Risk | Sequential | Attacker-centric modeling |

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in CPS Engineering

- **Risk Score $R_i(t)$** drives engineering decisions in **IEC 62443**-compliant zones.
- **MTTC** is the key safety KPI in industrial environments — a slow containment can cause physical damage.
- **Drift Rate $\delta$** quantifies how often the OT network changes; high $\delta$ mandates more frequent DTM cycles.
- **Coverage Ratio** is the auditor's favorite metric — KTU exam questions frequently use it.

| Dimension | Static Modeling | Dynamic Threat Modeling |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Trigger | Design phase only | Continuous (event-driven) |
| Input | Architecture documents | Live telemetry + docs |
| Update Frequency | Once / quarter | Real-time / per commit |

| CPS Fit | Weak (OT changes) | Strong (adapts to drift) |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although threat modeling is largely methodological, KTU examiners reward students who can **derive**, **quantify**, and **operationalize** the underlying metrics. Below are the complete, exhaustive derivations of the four high-yield DTM primitives.

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Likelihood Update for a Single Threat

**Premise:** A threat $i$ has an initial likelihood estimate $L_i(0)$ from design-time analysis. As production telemetry flows in, the estimate should evolve.

**Step 1 — Initial Likelihood from Static Model**
At time $t=0$, the static model assigns a likelihood based on expert elicitation or STRIDE heuristics:

$$
L_i(0) = P(\text{exploit} \mid \text{expert elicitation})
$$

**Step 2 — Observation from Telemetry**
Let $\hat{L}_i$ be the empirical likelihood observed from honeypots, IDS alerts, and vulnerability scanners during interval $\Delta t$:

$$
\hat{L}_i = \frac{\text{Number of observed exploitation attempts for threat } i}{\text{Total monitored traffic for that control path}}
$$

**Step 3 — Exponential Moving Average (EMA) Update**
The new likelihood is a weighted blend of the prior and the new observation, with learning rate $\alpha \in (0,1)$:

$$
L_i(t+1) = L_i(t) + \alpha \left( \hat{L}_i - L_i(t) \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Expansion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i(t+1) &= L_i(t) + \alpha\,\hat{L}_i - \alpha\,L_i(t) \\
         &= (1 - \alpha)\,L_i(t) + \alpha\,\hat{L}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-State Behavior**
If $\hat{L}_i$ remains constant for many cycles, the sequence converges to $\hat{L}_i$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} L_i(n) = \hat{L}_i
$$

This proves the EMA model **adapts** to ground truth — the core property that makes threat modeling *dynamic*.

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**
- $\alpha \to 0$ → **conservative**; old estimates persist (useful in safety-critical OT).
- $\alpha \to 1$ → **reactive**; model chases the latest telemetry (useful in IT with rapid CVE churn).

> [!NOTE]
> The same recurrence underpins **Kalman filters** used in industrial anomaly detection — a favorite viva link question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Attack-Graph Path Probability

**Premise:** An attacker must traverse $n$ sequential sub-goals to compromise a critical asset.

**Step 1 — Independence Assumption (Conservative)**
Assume sub-goals are independent — a standard simplification in attack-graph theory.

**Step 2 — Per-Node Probability**
Let $p_k$ be the probability the attacker succeeds at sub-goal $k$.

**Step 3 — Joint Probability (Chain Rule under Independence)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{path}) &= P(\text{sub-goal 1}) \cdot P(\text{sub-goal 2}) \cdot \ldots \cdot P(\text{sub-goal } n) \\
              &= \prod_{k=1}^{n} p_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked CPS Example (3-Stage Attack on a Substation)**
Stage 1: Phish an HMI engineer → $p_1 = 0.20$
Stage 2: Pivot to engineering workstation → $p_2 = 0.35$
Stage 3: Push malicious setpoint to PLC → $p_3 = 0.15$

$$
P(\text{path}) = 0.20 \times 0.35 \times 0.15 = 0.0105 = 1.05\%
$$

**Step 5 — Risk Recomputation**
With impact $I = 9$ (safety-critical) and exposure $E = 0.8$:

$$
R = 0.0105 \times 9 \times 0.8 \approx 0.076
$$

Mapped to CVSS-style scale (×10) → **0.76 (Low)**. Engineering action: *Track* via SSVC.

## 3.3 Derivation: Coverage Ratio (CR) and Drift Rate ($\delta$)

**Coverage Ratio:**

$$
\text{CR}(t) = \frac{\mid A_{\text{modeled}}(t) \mid}{\mid A_{\text{discovered}}(t) \mid} \times 100\%
$$

- $A_{\text{modeled}}$: assets with up-to-date STRIDE annotations.
- $A_{\text{discovered}}$: assets the discovery engine can see.
- **Target for IEC 62443 conformance: $\text{CR} \geq 95\%$** in safety zones.

**Drift Rate:**

$$
\delta = \frac{\Delta M}{M_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{\mid M(t) \setminus M(t_0) \mid + \mid M(t_0) \setminus M(t) \mid}{\mid M(t_0) \mid}
$$

- A $\delta > 0.10$ per week signals **uncontrolled architectural churn** — DTM cycles must be shortened.

## 3.4 Derivation: MTTC as a Maturity Indicator

$$
\text{MTTC} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left( t_{\text{contained},j} - t_{\text{detected},j} \right)
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{detected},j}$ = time the SOC identified threat $j$.
- $t_{\text{contained},j}$ = time the OT/IR team isolated the affected zone.

**Maturity Tiers (KTU reference scale):**

| MTTC | Tier | KTU Board Expectation |
|------|------|------------------------|
| $\le 1$ hr | Elite | Exemplary DTM automation |
| $1$–$8$ hr | Mature | Industry standard |
| $8$–$24$ hr | Developing | Manual bottlenecks |
| $> 24$ hr | Ad-hoc | Failing DTM program |

## 3.5 The DTM Master Equation

Combining all primitives, the *complete* DTM risk vector at time $t$ is:

$$
\mathbf{R}(t) = \begin{bmatrix}
L_1(t) \cdot I_1 \cdot E_1 \\
L_2(t) \cdot I_2 \cdot E_2 \\
\vdots \\
L_m(t) \cdot I_m \cdot E_m
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{R}(t+1) = \text{SSVC}\big(\mathbf{R}(t) \mid \text{Telemetry}(t)\big)
$$

The operator $\text{SSVC}(\cdot)$ encapsulates the *dynamic* part: it consumes telemetry, updates $L_i$, and assigns an action tier to each threat.

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Distinguish between Static and Dynamic Threat Modeling. Why is Dynamic Threat Modeling critical for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)?**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

| Aspect | Static Threat Modeling | Dynamic Threat Modeling |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Frequency | One-time / periodic | Continuous, event-driven |
| Inputs | Architecture docs | Docs + live telemetry |
| Output | Snapshot report | Living dashboard, tickets |
| Update | Manual | Automated, CI/CD-integrated |
| CPS Fit | Poor (drift ignored) | Strong (adapts to OT changes) |

**CPS Criticality (Key Points):**
1. **CPS is safety-critical** — a missed threat can cause physical harm, not just data loss.
2. **OT networks drift** (firmware updates, new PLCs) — static models go stale fast.
3. **Adversary TTPs evolve** — dynamic models consume live intel (MITRE ATT&CK for ICS).
4. **Purdue Model layers** each carry distinct trust boundaries needing constant re-validation.
5. **IEC 62443 / NERC-CIP** compliance demands continuous monitoring, not annual audits.

*(Word count: ~115 — within the 3-mark answer envelope.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Explain the STRIDE model. List any three categories with one example threat each relevant to a CPS.**

**Model Answer:**

**STRIDE** is a mnemonic introduced by Microsoft for categorizing threats:

| Letter | Category | CPS Example |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Forging an engineering workstation's IP to push malicious setpoints to a PLC |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Modifying ladder logic in a PLC over the OT network |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Operator disables an alarm with no audit trail |
| **I** | **Info Disclosure** | Sniffing Modbus/TCP traffic to learn process parameters |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Flooding an HMI server to blind the operator |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Exploiting a vendor backdoor to gain Level 2 access |

*(Any three suffice; the above provides full coverage for examiner satisfaction.)*

## Part B — Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Describe the five phases of a Dynamic Threat Modeling pipeline for a Cyber-Physical System. Illustrate with a block diagram.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Continuous Asset & Architecture Discovery**
- Automated scanning of IT, OT, and IIoT layers.
- Tools: Nessus (IT), Claroty / Nozomi (OT), cloud APIs (cloud-native).
- Output: $M(t) = \{N(t), E(t), B(t)\}$ — live nodes, edges, trust boundaries.

**Phase 2 — Live Data-Flow Reconstruction**
- Combines SAST (e.g., Checkmarx) with Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) to rebuild DFDs.
- Trust boundaries auto-redrawn on topology changes.

**Phase 3 — Threat Enumeration**
- Apply STRIDE, PASTA, or MITRE ATT&CK for ICS against the live DFD.
- Example: A new Modbus gateway triggers an automatic *Tampering* threat on the engineering workstation.

**Phase 4 — Dynamic Risk Scoring**
- Compute $R_i(t) = L_i(t) \cdot I_i \cdot E_i$ per threat.
- Map to SSVC tiers (Track / Ticket / Attend / Act).

**Phase 5 — Mitigation, Tracking & Feedback**
- Push tickets to DevSecOps (Jira) and OT-IR playbooks.
- Production telemetry validates whether $R_i$ decreased in the next cycle.

**Block Diagram (textual representation for the answer sheet):**

```
[Telemetry] → [Asset Discovery] → [DFD Reconstruction]
            → [Threat Enumeration] → [Risk Scoring]
            → [SSVC Triage] → [Mitigation] → [Feedback]
            ↺ (loops back to Telemetry)
```

---

**(b) A CPS substation has three sequential attack stages with success probabilities 0.25, 0.40, and 0.20 respectively. The impact factor is 8 and the exposure factor is 0.75. Compute (i) the path probability, (ii) the risk score, and (iii) the SSVC decision with justification.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List Given Data**

$$
p_1 = 0.25,\quad p_2 = 0.40,\quad p_3 = 0.20,\quad I = 8,\quad E = 0.75
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Path Probability**

$$
P(\text{path}) = p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 = 0.25 \times 0.40 \times 0.20
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{path}) &= 0.25 \times 0.40 = 0.10 \\
               &= 0.10 \times 0.20 = 0.02
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(\text{path}) = 0.02 = 2\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Risk Score**

$$
R = P(\text{path}) \times I \times E = 0.02 \times 8 \times 0.75
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.02 \times 8 = 0.16 \\
  &= 0.16 \times 0.75 = 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to CVSS-like Scale (×10)**

$$
R_{\text{scaled}} = 0.12 \times 10 = 1.2 \;\; (\text{Low})
$$

**Step 5 — SSVC Decision Justification**

- $R_{\text{scaled}} = 1.2$ falls in the **Low** band.
- However, $I = 8$ (safety-critical, substation outage) elevates attention.
- **Decision: ATTEND** — assign to incident-response for active monitoring; not yet *Act* because exposure is partial.

$$
\boxed{\text{SSVC Decision} = \textbf{ATTEND}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the Bayesian / EMA-based likelihood update mechanism used in Dynamic Threat Modeling. Derive the steady-state value of the likelihood estimate.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Recurrence**

$$
L_i(t+1) = L_i(t) + \alpha \left( \hat{L}_i - L_i(t) \right), \quad \alpha \in (0,1)
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic Simplification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i(t+1) &= L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i - \alpha L_i(t) \\
         &= (1-\alpha)L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand for Two Iterations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i(t+1) &= (1-\alpha)L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i \\
L_i(t+2) &= (1-\alpha)L_i(t+1) + \alpha\hat{L}_i \\
         &= (1-\alpha)^2 L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i\big[1 + (1-\alpha)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — General Form After $n$ Iterations**

$$
L_i(t+n) = (1-\alpha)^n L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1-\alpha)^k
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Geometric Series Identity**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1-\alpha)^k = \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^n}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^n}{\alpha}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify**

$$
L_i(t+n) = (1-\alpha)^n L_i(t) + \hat{L}_i \big[1 - (1-\alpha)^n\big]
$$

**Step 7 — Take the Limit as $n \to \infty$**

Since $0 < (1-\alpha) < 1$, the term $(1-\alpha)^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} L_i(t+n) = \hat{L}_i
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Steady-state likelihood} = \hat{L}_i}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The dynamic model *forgets* the initial design-time estimate exponentially fast (rate $\alpha$) and converges to the *observed* empirical likelihood — the mathematical heart of DTM.

---

**(b) Compare STRIDE, PASTA, and VAST methodologies. Recommend the most suitable one for a large CPS-integrated manufacturing plant with 50+ PLCs and discuss the role of the Purdue Model in threat-model segmentation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | STRIDE | PASTA | VAST |
|---------|--------|-------|------|
| Origin | Microsoft | Tony UcedaVélez | ThreatModeler |
| Stages | 6 categories | 7 stages | 2 views (app + infra) |
| Focus | Per-element taxonomy | Attacker-centric, risk-driven | Agile/scalable |
| CPS Fit | Good for triage | Excellent for high-assurance | Excellent for enterprise scale |
| Tooling | MS TMT, Threat Dragon | IriusRisk, Foreseeti | ThreatModeler |
| Effort | Low | High | Medium |

**Recommendation for the 50+ PLC Plant:**

- **VAST** is recommended because it scales across:
  - **Application view** (per application security model).
  - **Infrastructure view** (per Purdue level segmentation).
- PASTA is the academic gold standard but operationally heavy; STRIDE is too lightweight for 50+ PLCs.

**Role of the Purdue Model in Threat Segmentation:**

1. **Layer 0–1 (Process & Basic Control):** Apply *safety-integrity* threats (e.g., rogue setpoints) with VAST infra view.
2. **Layer 2 (Supervisory):** Engineer-workstation threats, supply-chain.
3. **Layer 3–3.5 (Operations & IDMZ):** Pivot attacks, lateral movement.
4. **Layer 4–5 (Enterprise):** Phishing, ransomware.

Each layer receives a **dedicated sub-threat-model** that is then aggregated by the DTM engine into a plant-wide view.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (lose 2–4 marks each):**
> 1. **Writing "STRIDE is a tool"** — it is a *model/methodology*, not a tool. Tools *implement* STRIDE.
> 2. **Forgetting the dynamic feedback loop** — a "threat model" without telemetry ingestion is *static*; examiners will explicitly deduct marks.
> 3. **Confusing CVSS with DREAD** — DREAD is legacy; CVSS v3.1 + SSVC is the modern stack.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly penalizes missing units/percentages.
> 5. **Not mentioning CPS-specific concerns** (safety, IEC 62443, Purdue Model) when the question is about CPS — generic IT answers lose marks.
> 6. **In the EMA derivation, forgetting the boundary condition** that $0 < (1-\alpha) < 1$ — the convergence proof collapses without it.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — KTU PECPT863 Module 1**

- [ ] **Dynamic Threat Modeling (DTM)** = continuous, telemetry-driven, automated threat identification & treatment.
- [ ] **Five Phases:** Discovery → DFD → Enumeration → Scoring → Mitigation+Feedback.
- [ ] **STRIDE:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- [ ] **PASTA:** 7-stage attacker-centric methodology.
- [ ] **VAST:** Visual, Agile, Simple Threat modeling — best for enterprise-scale CPS.
- [ ] **Risk Score:** $R_i(t) = L_i(t) \cdot I_i \cdot E_i$.
- [ ] **EMA Update:** $L_i(t+1) = (1-\alpha)L_i(t) + \alpha\hat{L}_i$, converges to $\hat{L}_i$.
- [ ] **Attack-Path Probability:** $P(\text{path}) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} p_k$ (independence assumption).
- [ ] **Coverage Ratio:** $\text{CR} = \mid A_{\text{modeled}} \mid / \mid A_{\text{discovered}} \mid \times 100\%$; target $\geq 95\%$.
- [ ] **Drift Rate:** $\delta = \Delta M / M_{\text{baseline}}$; if $\delta > 0.10$/week, shorten DTM cycles.
- [ ] **MTTC:** Mean Time to Contain; $\leq 1$ hr is elite.
- [ ] **SSVC Tiers:** Track / Ticket / Attend / Act.
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** is the canonical adversary-TTP catalog for CPS.
- [ ] **Purdue Model (L0–L5):** DTM must segment by layer; IDMZ (3.5) is a critical trust boundary.
- [ ] **Tools to memorize:** Microsoft TMT, OWASP Threat Dragon, IriusRisk, ThreatModeler, pytm.
- [ ] **Frameworks:** STRIDE, PASTA, VAST, LINDDUN, MITRE ATT&CK.
- [ ] **Static ≠ Dynamic** — KTU examiners love this comparison; always quantify the difference.
- [ ] **CPS-specific risks:** Safety impact, OT availability, supply chain, legacy protocols (Modbus, DNP3).
- [ ] **Standards reference:** IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, NERC-CIP (mention when relevant).
- [ ] **Always end with the feedback loop** — that is what makes it *dynamic*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrative Security Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Integrative Security Strategies # MODULE 1 — INTEGRATIVE SECURITY STRATEGIES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECPT863 / Module 1):** > *Integrative Security Strategies* — the unified, multi-domain orchestration of cyber, physical, operational, and human-centric controls designed to deliver **end-to-end resilience** for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Integrative Security Strategy (ISS)** is a holistic, layered, and risk-driven paradigm in which cybersecurity controls, physical safeguards, operational technology (OT) protections, and human-factor policies are architected as a **single, mutually reinforcing defense fabric** rather than as isolated silos. It is governed by the principle that *a compromise in one domain (e.g., cyber) must not cascade unchecked into another (e.g., physical)*, and is operationalized through frameworks such as **NIST CSF 2.0**, **ISA/IEC 62443**, and the **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** matrix. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ISS is the *strategic blueprint* that binds **People, Process, and Technology (PPT)** with **Detect, Defend, Respond, and Recover (PDRR)** capabilities across the **cyber-physical continuum**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Fortress" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Builder — The Airport Security Analogy** > > Imagine a high-value airport protecting a terminal. You do **not** rely on a single metal detector at the entrance. Instead, you layer: > > 1. **Outer Perimeter** (fences, patrols) → *Physical Security* > 2. **Identity Verification** (ID badges, biometrics) → *Authentication & Zero Trust* > 3. **Baggage Scanning** (X-ray, CT) → *Intrusion Detection Systems* > 4. **Behavioral Monitoring** (CCTV with AI) → *Anomaly Detection / SIEM* > 5. **Armed Response** (quick-reaction teams) → *Incident Response* > 6. **Backup Operations** (alternate runways) → *Resilience & Recovery* > > If a terrorist bypasses one layer, the next layer must **catch, contain, or mitigate** the threat. This is the *core philosophy* of Integrative Security — **defense-in-depth** is the architectural pattern, while *integration* is the orchestration glue. ### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Integrative Security | # | Pillar | Core Mandate | KTU 2024 Tag | |---|--------|--------------|--------------| | **P1** | **Cyber Layer** | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, encryption, Zero-Trust | CO1 / CO2 | | **P2** | **Physical Layer** | Tamper-proofing, access control, environmental sensors | CO1 | | **P3** | **OT/ICS Layer** | Protocol whitelisting, Purdue Model segmentation | CO2 / CO3 | | **P4** | **Human Layer** | Awareness, training, insider-threat mitigation | CO3 | | **P5** | **Governance Layer** | Risk frameworks, compliance, audit trails | CO4 / CO5 | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Highlight Constants) > [!NOTE] > - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Industry benchmark **≤ 24 hours** for critical CPS assets. > - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Target **≤ 1 hour** for safety-critical incidents. > - **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** Typically **≤ 4 hours** for Tier-1 SCADA systems. > - **Defense-in-Depth Layers:** Recommended minimum **5–7** independent layers (per DoD & ISA/IEC 62443-3-3). > - **Zero-Trust Verification Frequency:** **Continuous** (never trust, always verify). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of Integrative Security Integrative Security is not a product — it is an **architectural philosophy**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate its five operating principles: ### 2.1.1 Principle 1 — **Defense in Depth (DiD)** A security model where multiple independent layers of controls are placed throughout an IT/OT system so that the failure of one control does not lead to system compromise. - **How it works:** Each layer has its own threat model and detection capability. - **Why it works:** Increases attacker cost, dwell time, and probability of detection. - **Real-world utility:** Used by **Stuxnet-era Iran Natanz facility** post-incident — Siemens PCS 7 was segmented from corporate IT, but Stuxnet still traversed 4 layers via removable media. Post-mortem led to adoption of **6-layer DiD**. ### 2.1.2 Principle 2 — **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** Originated by John Kindervag (Forrester, 2010), formalized in **NIST SP 800-207 (2020)**. - **Core Tenets:** 1. Never trust, always verify. 2. Assume breach. 3. Least-privilege access. 4. Micro-segmentation. 5. Continuous authentication & validation. ### 2.1.3 Principle 3 — **Security by Design (SbD)** Embedding security controls during the **requirements and design phase** rather than retrofitting post-deployment. The cost-of-fix rises **6× to 100×** at each stage of the SDLC (NIST estimates). ### 2.1.4 Principle 4 — **Risk-Based Adaptive Security** Dynamic adjustment of security posture based on **real-time risk scoring** using: $$R = f(T, V, I, C)$$ where: - $R$ = Risk score - $T$ = Threat likelihood - $V$ = Vulnerability exposure - $I$ = Asset criticality / Impact - $C$ = Existing Control effectiveness ### 2.1.5 Principle 5 — **Cross-Domain Orchestration** Unified **Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR)** across IT, OT, IoT, and Cloud. This is the **integrative** element — it prevents **domain-silo blind spots**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet | Concept / Equation | Expression | Domain of Use | Boundary / Note | |--------------------|------------|---------------|-----------------| | **Risk Score** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Quantitative risk assessment | Each factor normalized 0–1; $R \in [0,1]$ | | **Composite Risk** | $R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i$ | Multi-asset CPS risk | $w_i$ = criticality weight, $\sum w_i = 1$ | | **Defense Effectiveness** | $E = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - e_i)$ | Layered defense | $e_i$ = per-layer effectiveness, $L$ = layers | | **Zero-Trust Trust Score** | $TS = f(\text{identity, device, context, behavior})$ | Access decisions | Continuous, real-time | | **MTTD** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})}{N}$ | SOC operations | Lower is better | | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum (t_{resolve} - t_{detect})}{N}$ | Incident response | Lower is better | | **Attack Surface** | $AS = \vert \text{Inputs} \vert + \vert \text{Outputs} \vert + \vert \text{Channels} \vert$ | Threat modeling | Minimization goal | | **Cost of Fix Ratio** | $C_{fix} = C_{req} \times 6^{n}$ | SDLC economics | $n$ = stages later than requirements | | **Availability (CPS)** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Safety-critical CPS | Often targets $A \geq 0.99999$ (5-nines) | | **Redundancy Gain** | $RG = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - r_i)$ | Resilient design | $r_i$ = per-component reliability | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write the **unit** alongside any numerical answer. For example, *"MTTR = 45 minutes"* is worth more marks than *"MTTR is low"*. The 2024 scheme rubric explicitly awards marks for **dimensional clarity**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Sector | Integrative Strategy Used | Outcome | |-----------------|---------------------------|---------| | **Smart Grid (Power)** | ISA/IEC 62443 + NERC CIP | Prevented 2015 Ukraine-style attacks on substations | | **Healthcare CPS** | NIST CSF + FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance | Secured pacemakers, insulin pumps | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | ISO/SAE 21434 + UNECE WP.29 | End-to-end CAN-bus & V2X protection | | **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Purdue Model + Zero Trust | Halt of LockerGaga-style ransomware in plants | | **Aerospace (Avionics)** | DO-326A Airworthiness Security | Protection of ADS-B, FMS, and ground links | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Defense-in-Depth Effectiveness (Cascading Probability) **Problem Context (KTU 2024 Style):** A CPS is protected by $L = 4$ independent security layers. Each layer has a per-layer attack-blocking effectiveness of $e_1 = 0.85$, $e_2 = 0.80$, $e_3 = 0.75$, $e_4 = 0.70$. Compute the **overall defense effectiveness** $E$ and the **residual compromise probability**. ### Step 1 — Define Per-Layer Failure Probability The probability that layer $i$ **fails to block** an attack is: $$p_i = 1 - e_i$$ For Layer 1: $p_1 = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$ For Layer 2: $p_2 = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$ For Layer 3: $p_3 = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$ For Layer 4: $p_4 = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Independence Assumption Since the layers are independent (a key DiD assumption), the probability that **all** layers fail simultaneously is the product of individual failure probabilities: $$P_{all-fail} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} p_i$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values $$ \begin{aligned} P_{all-fail} &= 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.25 \times 0.30 \\ &= (0.15 \times 0.20) \times (0.25 \times 0.30) \\ &= 0.03 \times 0.075 \\ &= 0.00225 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Overall Defense Effectiveness $$E = 1 - P_{all-fail} = 1 - 0.00225 = 0.99775$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Percentage and Interpret $$E = 99.775\%$$ **Interpretation:** Even though no single layer exceeds 85% effectiveness, the **integrative multi-layer strategy** blocks 99.775% of attacks — a textbook demonstration of why *integration* matters. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** This is a classic "show the cascading reasoning" question worth **7 marks**. Marks are awarded for: (1) writing the formula, (2) computing each $p_i$, (3) applying independence, (4) numerical substitution, and (5) final percentage with units. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Risk Scoring for a CPS Asset **Problem Context:** A smart-grid SCADA substation has three threat vectors: **Network Intrusion (N)**, **Physical Tampering (P)**, and **Insider Misuse (I)**. Their likelihoods, vulnerabilities, and impacts (each on a 0–10 scale) are: | Vector | Likelihood ($T$) | Vulnerability ($V$) | Impact ($I$) | Criticality Weight ($w$) | |--------|------------------|----------------------|--------------|--------------------------| | N | 7 | 8 | 9 | 0.50 | | P | 4 | 5 | 10 | 0.30 | | I | 3 | 6 | 8 | 0.20 | Compute the **composite risk** using $R_i = T_i \cdot V_i \cdot I_i$ and $R_{total} = \sum w_i R_i$. ### Step 1 — Compute Individual Risk Scores **For Network Intrusion (N):** $$ R_N = T_N \cdot V_N \cdot I_N = 7 \times 8 \times 9 = 504 $$ **For Physical Tampering (P):** $$ R_P = T_P \cdot V_P \cdot I_P = 4 \times 5 \times 10 = 200 $$ **For Insider Misuse (I):** $$ R_I = T_I \cdot V_I \cdot I_I = 3 \times 6 \times 8 = 144 $$ ### Step 2 — Verify Weight Normalization $$ \sum w_i = 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Composite Risk $$ \begin{aligned} R_{total} &= w_N \cdot R_N + w_P \cdot R_P + w_I \cdot R_I \\ &= (0.50 \times 504) + (0.30 \times 200) + (0.20 \times 144) \\ &= 252 + 60 + 28.8 \\ &= 340.8 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Normalize to a 0–1000 Scale (Optional KTU Enhancement) Since the maximum possible $R_i = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$ and maximum weighted sum = 1000, the normalized risk is: $$R_{norm} = \frac{R_{total}}{1000} = 0.3408 \quad \text{or} \quad 34.08\%$$ ### Step 5 — Classify the Risk | Range | Classification | |-------|----------------| | $0 - 200$ | Low | | $201 - 500$ | **Moderate** ← *This case* | | $501 - 800$ | High | | $801 - 1000$ | Critical | **Conclusion:** The substation is in the **Moderate** risk band — an integrative security upgrade (network segmentation + insider DLP + physical hardening) is recommended. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Integrative Security Models | Attribute | Defense in Depth | Zero Trust | Security by Design | Adaptive (Risk-Based) | Cross-Domain SOAR | |-----------|------------------|------------|-------------------|-----------------------|-------------------| | **Primary Focus** | Layered redundancy | Identity-centric | Lifecycle embedding | Dynamic posture | Unified response | | **Best For** | Air-gapped OT | Cloud-native IT | New CPS products | Evolving threats | Large enterprises | | **Limitation** | Complexity overhead | Cultural resistance | High upfront cost | False positives | Tool integration | | **KTU 2024 CO** | CO1, CO2 | CO2 | CO1, CO3 | CO4 | CO5 | | **Example Framework** | ISA/IEC 62443 | NIST SP 800-207 | NIST SP 800-160 | NIST CSF 2.0 | MITRE ATT&CK + SOAR | | **Cost of Implementation** | Medium | Medium-High | High (early) | Medium | High | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Integrative Security Architecture (CPS Reference Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1[Layer1 Physical Security
Fences Sensors Guards] --> node2[Layer2 Perimeter Network
Firewall DMZ IDS] node2 --> node3[Layer3 Identity and Access
Zero Trust MFA RBAC] node3 --> node4[Layer4 OT Control Zone
Purdue Level 1 to 3] node4 --> node5[Layer5 Application and Data
Encryption EDR SIEM] node5 --> node6[Layer6 Governance and Audit
NIST CSF IEC 62443] node6 --> node7[Central SOAR Orchestrator
Incident Response Recovery] node4 --> node7 node3 --> node7 node2 --> node7 node1 --> node7 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Zero-Trust Decision Flow in CPS ```mermaid flowchart LR reqA[Subject Access Request] --> check1{Identity Verified} check1 -- No --> denyA[Access Denied Log Event] check1 -- Yes --> check2{Device Posture Trust} check2 -- No --> denyA check2 -- Yes --> check3{Context Location Time} check3 -- No --> denyA check3 -- Yes --> check4{Behavioral Anomaly} check4 -- Yes --> denyA check4 -- No --> grantA[Least Privilege Micro Segment Access Granted] grantA --> monitorA[Continuous Monitoring SOC] monitorA --> check4 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Risk-Based Adaptive Security Loop ```mermaid flowchart TB phaseA[Asset Identification] --> phaseB[Vulnerability Scanning] phaseB --> phaseC[Threat Intelligence Ingestion] phaseC --> phaseD[Risk Score Computation R equals f of T V I C] phaseD --> phaseE{R above Threshold} phaseE -- Yes --> phaseF[Dynamic Policy Enforcement] phaseF --> phaseG[Real Time Monitoring] phaseE -- No --> phaseG phaseG --> phaseH[Feedback Loop] phaseH --> phaseB ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Cross-Domain SOAR Orchestration ```mermaid flowchart TB src1[IT Domain Alerts] --> soara[SOAR Engine] src2[OT Domain Alerts] --> soara src3[IoT Edge Alerts] --> soara src4[Physical Sensor Alerts] --> soara soara --> enrich[Threat Intelligence Enrichment MITRE] enrich --> triage{Auto Triage Possible} triage -- Yes --> play[Execute Playbook Automated] triage -- No --> analyst[SOC Analyst Review] analyst --> play play --> contain[Containment Quarantine Patch] contain --> report[Post Incident Report and Lessons Learned] report --> improve[Update Risk Register] improve --> soara ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** These schematics map the **block-level functional architecture** of integrative security. In the KTU 2024 ESE, you are expected to draw **labeled layered diagrams** — these Mermaid flows serve as the *canonical reference* for hand-drawing in the answer sheet. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]** ### **Q1. Define Integrative Security Strategy (ISS) in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). Mention any two of its core pillars.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Integrative Security Strategy (ISS) is a holistic, multi-layered approach that unifies **cyber, physical, operational, and human-centric security controls** into a single coordinated defense framework, ensuring that compromise in one domain does not cascade to another within a CPS. **Two Core Pillars (any two):** 1. **Cyber Layer** — Firewalls, IDS/IPS, encryption, Zero-Trust access control. 2. **Physical Layer** — Tamper-proof enclosures, biometric access, environmental sensors. 3. **OT/ICS Layer** — Purdue Model segmentation, protocol whitelisting. 4. **Human Layer** — Awareness training, insider-threat mitigation. 5. **Governance Layer** — Risk frameworks (NIST CSF, IEC 62443), audit, compliance. *(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 2 pillars + 0.5 for relevance = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. State the NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust core principles. Why is "assume breach" critical for CPS?** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Core Principles of Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207):** 1. **Never trust, always verify** — Every access request is authenticated and authorized. 2. **Assume breach** — Operate as if the network is already compromised. 3. **Least-privilege access** — Minimum required permissions, just-in-time. 4. **Micro-segmentation** — Granular network zones to limit lateral movement. 5. **Continuous validation** — Real-time trust-score evaluation. **Why "Assume Breach" is Critical for CPS:** CPS environments (e.g., power grids, medical devices, industrial control systems) have **long asset lifecycles (20–30 years)**, legacy unpatched firmware, and **safety-critical** consequences. Assuming an attacker is already inside forces the design to focus on **containment, segmentation, and rapid detection** rather than perimeter-only defense — essential when a Stuxnet-style or ransomware attack can cause physical harm, not just data loss. *(1 mark for 2 principles + 1 mark for the third + 1 mark for "why" = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Model Paper Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **(a)** Explain the **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** strategy with reference to a Cyber-Physical System. Illustrate with a **6-layer model** and describe the role of each layer. *(7 Marks)* > **(b)** A CPS asset is protected by **5 independent security layers** with individual effectivenesses $e_1 = 0.90$, $e_2 = 0.85$, $e_3 = 0.80$, $e_4 = 0.75$, $e_5 = 0.70$. Compute the **overall defense effectiveness** and the **residual compromise probability**. Comment on the result. *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** is a security strategy that places **multiple independent layers of controls** across a CPS such that no single failure leads to full system compromise. Each layer operates on a **different attack vector** and uses a **different detection/defense mechanism**, making it exponentially harder for an adversary to traverse the entire system. **6-Layer DiD Model for CPS:** | Layer | Name | Example Controls | CPS Asset Protected | |-------|------|------------------|---------------------| | **L1** | **Physical Security** | Fencing, biometric access, tamper sensors, CCTV | Field devices, RTUs, PLCs | | **L2** | **Perimeter Network** | Firewalls, DMZ, IDS/IPS | IT-OT boundary | | **L3** | **Network Segmentation** | VLANs, Purdue Model, micro-segmentation | Internal OT zones (Levels 0–3) | | **L4** | **Identity & Access** | MFA, RBAC, Zero-Trust, PAM | HMI, Engineering Workstations | | **L5** | **Host & Application** | EDR, antivirus, application whitelisting, secure boot | SCADA servers, historians | | **L6** | **Data & Governance** | Encryption (at-rest, in-transit), SIEM, logging, audit | Telemetry, control commands | **Role of Integration:** Each layer is **independent** but **coordinated** via a central **SOAR/SIEM platform** (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel). If L2 is bypassed via a phishing email, L4 (Identity) must catch it. If L4 fails, L5 (Application Whitelisting) blocks the malware from executing. *(Marking scheme: 2 marks for DiD definition + 4 marks for the 6-layer table + 1 mark for integration explanation = 7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** $L = 5$, $e_1 = 0.90$, $e_2 = 0.85$, $e_3 = 0.80$, $e_4 = 0.75$, $e_5 = 0.70$ **Step 1 — Compute per-layer failure probabilities $p_i = 1 - e_i$:** $$ \begin{aligned} p_1 &= 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 \\ p_2 &= 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \\ p_3 &= 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 \\ p_4 &= 1 - 0.75 = 0.25 \\ p_5 &= 1 - 0.70 = 0.30 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the DiD overall effectiveness formula (independence assumption):** $$E = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{5} p_i$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and compute:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 1 - (0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.25 \times 0.30) \\ &= 1 - \left( \frac{1}{10} \times \frac{15}{100} \times \frac{2}{10} \times \frac{25}{100} \times \frac{3}{10} \right) \\ &= 1 - \left( \frac{1 \times 15 \times 2 \times 25 \times 3}{10 \times 100 \times 10 \times 100 \times 10} \right) \\ &= 1 - \left( \frac{2250}{10{,}000{,}000} \right) \\ &= 1 - 0.000225 \\ &= 0.999775 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percentage and compute residual risk:** $$E = 99.9775\% \quad ; \quad P_{residual} = 0.0225\%$$ **Step 5 — Comment:** Even though the **weakest layer (L5)** has only 70% effectiveness, the integrative 5-layer strategy delivers **99.9775% defense effectiveness**. This is a **2,250-fold improvement** over the best single layer (90%) and demonstrates the **non-linear compounding** of layered defense. The residual 0.0225% risk is addressed via **incident response, recovery, and insurance** — completing the integrative loop. *(Marking scheme: 1 mark for formula + 2 marks for $p_i$ computation + 2 marks for product + 1 mark for final E + 1 mark for comment = 7 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0** functions: **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**. How does this align with integrative security in CPS? *(7 Marks)* > **(b)** A CPS asset portfolio has 3 threat vectors with the following data: > > | Vector | T (0–1) | V (0–1) | I (0–1) | Weight w | > |--------|---------|---------|---------|----------| > | Network Attack | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 0.5 | > | Physical Breach | 0.3 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 0.3 | > | Insider Threat | 0.4 | 0.7 | 0.8 | 0.2 | > > Compute the **composite risk score** and classify the risk level. Suggest one integrative mitigation per vector. *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution to (a):** **NIST CSF 2.0** (released Feb 2024) organizes cybersecurity into **6 functions**, the latest adding **Govern** as a unifying function. **The 6 Functions:** 1. **GOVERN (GV)** — *New in v2.0.* Establishes the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, policies, roles, and supply-chain risk. It is the *overarching umbrella* that informs all other functions. 2. **IDENTIFY (ID)** — Asset inventory, business environment, governance, risk assessment. *(Know what you must protect.)* 3. **PROTECT (PR)** — Access control, awareness training, data security, protective technology. *(Put the right safeguards in place.)* 4. **DETECT (DE)** — Anomalies and events, security monitoring, detection processes. *(Find incidents quickly.)* 5. **RESPOND (RS)** — Response planning, communications, analysis, mitigation. *(Contain the impact.)* 6. **RECOVER (RC)** — Recovery planning, improvements, communications. *(Restore capabilities and learn.)* **Alignment with Integrative CPS Security:** | CSF Function | Integrative CPS Counterpart | |--------------|----------------------------| | **Govern** | OT/IT security policy unification, regulatory mapping (NERC CIP, IEC 62443) | | **Identify** | CPS asset discovery (passive OT monitoring, e.g., Claroty, Nozomi) | | **Protect** | Purdue segmentation, Zero-Trust, encryption, MFA | | **Detect** | OT-aware IDS, anomaly detection, SIEM with ICS rules | | **Respond** | SOAR playbooks for safety-critical systems, isolation procedures | | **Recover** | Cold/hot spares, firmware restoration, black-start procedures for power grids | **Integration Logic:** NIST CSF 2.0 is *itself* integrative — Govern binds the remaining 5 functions into a single risk-driven loop, mirroring the integrative philosophy in CPS. For CPS, this framework is often combined with **ISA/IEC 62443** for OT-specific controls. *(Marking scheme: 3 marks for explaining 6 functions + 2 marks for diagram/table + 2 marks for CPS alignment = 7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Compute individual risk scores using $R_i = T_i \times V_i \times I_i$:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{Network} &= 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.378 \\ R_{Physical} &= 0.3 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.150 \\ R_{Insider} &= 0.4 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.224 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Verify weight normalization:** $$\sum w_i = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Compute composite risk:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{total} &= (0.5 \times 0.378) + (0.3 \times 0.150) + (0.2 \times 0.224) \\ &= 0.189 + 0.045 + 0.0448 \\ &= 0.2788 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Classify the risk:** | Range | Classification | |-------|----------------| | 0.00 – 0.20 | Low | | 0.21 – 0.50 | **Moderate** ← *This case* | | 0.51 – 0.75 | High | | 0.76 – 1.00 | Critical | **Step 5 — Suggest integrative mitigations:** | Vector | Suggested Integrative Mitigation | |--------|----------------------------------| | **Network Attack** | Deploy **network segmentation** (Purdue Model) + **Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA)** for all OT remote sessions. | | **Physical Breach** | Install **tamper-evident enclosures**, **biometric access** to PLC cabinets, and **environmental sensors** (vibration, temperature) feeding the SIEM. | | **Insider Threat** | Enforce **separation of duties**, **privileged access management (PAM)**, and **user-behavior analytics (UBA)** on engineering workstations. | **Conclusion:** With $R_{total} = 0.2788$ (Moderate), the asset requires **targeted hardening** of the network vector (highest contribution) and **continuous monitoring** of insider activity. *(Marking scheme: 1 mark for formula + 1.5 marks for individual $R_i$ + 1 mark for weights + 1.5 marks for composite + 1 mark for classification + 1 mark for mitigations = 7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PECPT863 / Module 1 — Integrative Security:** > > 1. **Forgetting units / scale** — KTU 2024 deducts 0.5 marks if you write $E = 0.997$ without specifying *percentage* or *probability*. > 2. **Using the wrong formula variant** — Confusing $R = T + V + I$ (additive, deprecated) with $R = T \times V \times I$ (multiplicative, NIST standard). Always use **multiplicative**. > 3. **Independence assumption omitted** — DiD formulas assume **layer independence**. If you write the product without stating this assumption, expect 1 mark deduction. > 4. **No diagram in Part B** — A neat, labeled layered diagram in Q1(a) or Q2(a) fetches **1–2 extra marks**. Mermaid-style boxes are acceptable, but **hand-drawn is preferred** in the answer sheet. > 5. **Ignoring the "Govern" function** — Many students forget the **NIST CSF 2.0 Govern** pillar. Listing only 5 functions = -1 mark. > 6. **Generic answers** — Mentioning *"use firewalls"* alone is worth 0.5 marks. Specify *"Purdue Level 3.5 DMZ firewall with deep-packet-inspection for Modbus/TCP"* to score full marks. > 7. **No real-world example** — Citing **Stuxnet (2010), BlackEnergy (Ukraine 2015), Triton/TRISIS (2017), or Colonial Pipeline (2021)** adds 0.5–1 mark bonus per question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PECPT863 / Module 1 — Integrative Security Strategies: 60-Second Recap** > > - **Definition:** Holistic, multi-layered coordination of cyber + physical + OT + human + governance controls. > - **5 Pillars:** Cyber, Physical, OT/ICS, Human, Governance. > - **5 Principles:** Defense-in-Depth, Zero Trust, Security by Design, Risk-Based Adaptive, Cross-Domain SOAR. > - **Key Formula (DiD Effectiveness):** $E = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - e_i)$ > - **Key Formula (Risk):** $R = T \times V \times I$ ; Composite: $R_{total} = \sum w_i R_i$ > - **Key Formula (Availability):** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ > - **NIST CSF 2.0 Functions (6):** **G**overn, **I**dentify, **P**rotect, **D**etect, **R**espond, **R**ecover — mnemonic: **"Govern + I P D R R"** or *"Good Identification Prevents Damage, Restores Reliability"*. > - **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207):** Never trust, always verify; assume breach; least privilege; micro-segmentation; continuous validation. > - **Frameworks to remember:** NIST CSF 2.0, NIST SP 800-207, ISA/IEC 62443, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, ISO/SAE 21434 (automotive), NERC CIP (power). > - **Real-world case studies (must-know for ESE):** Stuxnet (Iran 2010), BlackEnergy (Ukraine 2015), Triton (Saudi Arabia 2017), Colonial Pipeline (USA 2021), Oldsmar Water Treatment (USA 2021). > - **Cost of late fix (NIST):** 1× at requirements, 6× at design, 30× at coding, 100× at post-deployment. > - **5-Nine Availability Target:** $A \geq 0.99999$ for safety-critical CPS. > - **Exam weightage (typical):** Module 1 carries **15–20%** of the 100-mark ESE; expect 1 Part A (3 marks) and 1 Part B sub-question (7 marks) on Integrative Security. > - **Always draw** at least **one diagram** (layered architecture, NIST CSF wheel, or Zero-Trust flow) for full marks in Part B. > - **Always state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming layer independence", "assuming weights sum to 1"). ---

topics.title: Combining Risk Management and Threat Modeling Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combining Risk Management and Threat Modeling Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Risk Management (RM)** is a continuous, systematic process of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, treating, and monitoring information security risks to organizational assets, ensuring that risks are reduced to an acceptable level aligned with the organization's risk appetite and business objectives.

**Threat Modeling (TM)** is a structured, engineering-grade analytical process used to identify, enumerate, prioritize, and mitigate potential threats and vulnerabilities affecting a system, typically expressed through decomposition of the system, threat identification, and countermeasure definition.

**Combining Risk Management and Threat Modeling Strategies** refers to the **integrated security engineering paradigm** wherein the qualitative/quantitative outputs of threat modeling (e.g., attacker-centric threat trees) are systematically fed as inputs into the risk management lifecycle (asset → threat → vulnerability → impact → control), creating a closed-loop, defense-in-depth governance model for **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the **foundation stone** of Module 1. Almost every CPS security architecture question in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) will expect you to demonstrate how threat modeling outputs *operationalize* risk management decisions. Never treat RM and TM as independent silos.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are building a **new house** in Kerala during monsoon season.

- **Risk Management** is like a *holistic insurance and planning office* that asks: "What can go wrong? (floods, fire, theft, earthquakes), how bad will it be? (financial loss), and what is my budget to fix it? (risk appetite)?"
- **Threat Modeling** is like a *detailed architectural audit* that walks through every room, every window, every power socket, and asks: "If a burglar enters through this window, what can he reach? If water enters through this plug, what shorts? If a hacker breaks into my smart lock, which rooms does he control?"

**The combination** is the act of using the architectural audit (TM) to feed the insurance office (RM) — so that the insurance premiums (risk scores) are based on *real architectural weaknesses*, not on guesswork.

In a **CPS context** (e.g., a smart grid, autonomous vehicle, or medical infusion pump), this combination ensures that a logical cyber-threat (e.g., a man-in-the-middle attack on a sensor) is correlated with its *physical consequence* (e.g., overdose, voltage collapse, brake failure), and then both are prioritized on the same risk register.

### 1.3 Key Industry-Standard Frameworks (KTU Must-Know)

| Framework | Origin | Focus | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST SP 800-30** | NIST (USA) | Quantitative + Qualitative Risk Assessment | High |
| **ISO 31000** | ISO | Generic Enterprise Risk Management | High |
| **OCTAVE** | SEI / CMU | Asset-Centric Operational Risk | High |
| **STRIDE** | Microsoft | Threat Categorization (6 categories) | High |
| **PASTA** | Tony UcedaVélez | Attacker-Centric, Risk-Centric | Very High |
| **VAST** | ThreatModeler | Scalable, Agile | High |
| **MITRE ATT&CK** | MITRE | Adversary TTPs | High |
| **FAIR** | FAIR Institute | Factor Analysis of Information Risk | Very High |
| **TARA** | MITRE | Threat Assessment & Remediation Analysis (for vehicles) | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When answering in the ESE, always **name-drop the framework** in your opening line. A sentence starting with *"According to NIST SP 800-30, risk is the..."* immediately earns the examiner's goodwill and partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Risk Management Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-30 Based)

The risk management process is **iterative**, not linear. Each iteration refines the previous one's outputs.

1. **Asset Identification** — Catalog every CPS component (PLCs, RTUs, HMI, sensors, actuators, gateways, cloud connectors). Assign **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA)** weights based on safety criticality.
2. **Threat Identification** — Enumerate potential threat sources (nation-state APTs, insider threats, natural disasters, malware, ransomware). Use **threat catalogs** like CAPEC, ATT&CK, or ICS-CERT advisories.
3. **Vulnerability Identification** — Map vulnerabilities to assets (CVEs, misconfigurations, weak authentication, unpatched firmware).
4. **Likelihood Determination** — Estimate the probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. Use **L = f(Threat Capability, Control Strength)**.
5. **Impact Determination** — Estimate the consequence: financial, operational, safety, reputational, regulatory. For CPS, **safety impact is paramount** (e.g., loss of life).
6. **Risk Determination** — Combine likelihood and impact: **Risk = f(Likelihood, Impact)**.
7. **Risk Treatment** — Choose one of: **Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept**.
8. **Risk Monitoring & Communication** — Continuous re-assessment, KPI dashboards, board-level reporting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why combine with Threat Modeling?** Pure RM without TM produces *abstract* risk registers with no specific attack paths. Pure TM without RM produces *academic* threat lists with no business prioritization. The combination gives **actionable, prioritized, cost-justified controls**.

### 2.2 The Threat Modeling Methodology (Microsoft STRIDE + PASTA Hybrid)

The most pedagogically rich and KTU-aligned approach is the **7-stage PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)**:

1. **Define Business Objectives** — Why does the CPS exist? (e.g., "deliver 11kV power with 99.99% uptime").
2. **Define Technical Scope** — Which systems, protocols (Modbus, DNP3, OPC-UA, MQTT, IEC 61850), and trust boundaries?
3. **Application Decomposition** — Data Flow Diagrams (DFD), sequence diagrams, trust boundaries.
4. **Threat Analysis** — Catalog threats via STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege).
5. **Vulnerability & Weakness Analysis** — Map to CWE/CVE databases.
6. **Attack Modeling** — Build attack trees, kill chains, MITRE ATT&CK mappings.
7. **Risk & Impact Analysis** — Feed back into RM: likelihood × impact → residual risk.

### 2.3 The Combined Framework (Core of This Topic)

The integration point is **Stage 6 → Stage 7 of PASTA feeding into Step 6 of NIST RM**. The workflow is:

```
CPS Architecture  →  Decomposition (DFD)  →  Threat Enumeration (STRIDE)
       ↓
Attack Trees (TM Output)  →  Likelihood & Impact Scoring (RM Input)
       ↓
Risk Register Entry  →  Treatment Decision  →  Security Control Selection
       ↓
Residual Risk Re-evaluation  →  Loop back to TM (validate mitigations)
```

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Mathematical Form | Units / Scale | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$ | Currency (₹, $) | Loss from a single occurrence |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)}$ | Currency / year | Annualized expected loss |
| **Risk Score (FAIR model)** | $R = \text{Threat Event Frequency} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Primary Loss} \times \text{Secondary Loss}$ | Scaled (0–100) | Quantitative risk |
| **Qualitative Risk** | $R = L \times I$ (Likelihood × Impact) | Score 1–25 | 5×5 matrix |
| **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{CVSS} = f(\text{AV},\text{AC},\text{PR},\text{UI},\text{S},\text{C},\text{I},\text{A})$ | 0.0 – 10.0 | Standardized vuln severity |
| **Risk Reduction (Cost-Benefit)** | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Annual Control Cost}}{\text{Annual Control Cost}}$ | Ratio (decimal) | Justify control spending |
| **Residual Risk** | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - \text{Control Effectiveness})$ | Scaled 0–100 | Post-control risk |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** | $\text{ARO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Incidents}}{\text{Time Period (years)}}$ | Events/year | Frequency metric |
| **Exposure Factor** | $\text{EF} = \dfrac{\text{Loss from Single Event}}{\text{Total Asset Value}}$ | Fraction (0–1) | % of asset lost |
| **Defense-in-Depth Index** | $\text{DiDI} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - e_i)$ | Fraction (0–1) | Layered control efficacy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **ALE = SLE × ARO** formula and the **ROSI** formula. These are the two most frequently tested in the Quantitative Risk Analysis 14-mark question. The CVSS formula structure (8 input metrics) is also a favorite 7-mark sub-part.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **power grid** domain, the combination helps utilities comply with **NERC CIP** standards. In **automotive**, the ISO/SAE 21434 standard mandates TARA (Threat Assessment and Remediation Analysis) — which is exactly this combined approach. In **medical devices**, FDA pre-market cybersecurity guidance requires threat modeling + risk management per **ISO 14971**. In **OT/SCADA**, NIST SP 800-82 explicitly prescribes combining threat modeling with the risk management process.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Qualitative 5×5 Matrix to Quantitative ALE

**Step 1: Map Qualitative Likelihood to Quantitative ARO**

The 5×5 qualitative risk matrix uses linguistic scales. We must convert them into a numerical ARO for use in ALE calculations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verbal Likelihood (L)} &: \text{Very Low} \to \text{Low} \to \text{Medium} \to \text{High} \to \text{Very High} \\
\text{Quantitative ARO} &: 0.01 \to 0.1 \to 0.5 \to 2.0 \to 10.0 \;\; \text{(events / year)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Map Verbal Impact to Asset Value & Exposure Factor**

For a CPS, impact has four dimensions: **Safety, Financial, Operational, Reputational**. We aggregate them via a weighted formula:

$$
I_{\text{agg}} = w_1 \cdot I_{\text{safety}} + w_2 \cdot I_{\text{financial}} + w_3 \cdot I_{\text{operational}} + w_4 \cdot I_{\text{reputational}}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and $w_1 \geq 0.4$ for CPS (safety is king).

**Step 3: Compute SLE**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}
$$

where AV = replacement cost + revenue loss + regulatory fine, and EF = the fraction of AV destroyed in a single event.

**Step 4: Compute ALE**

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

This is the **annualized expected loss** in monetary terms. It is the language executives understand.

**Step 5: Compute ROSI for a Proposed Control**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - C_{\text{control}}}{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}}
$$

If ROSI > 0, the control is financially justified. If ROSI < 0, accept the risk instead.

**Step 6: Compute Residual Risk**

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - e_i)
$$

where $e_i$ is the effectiveness of the $i$-th control (0 to 1). For perfect controls, $R_{\text{residual}} \to 0$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (CPS Smart Meter Scenario)

**Scenario:** A regional electricity distributor has **50,000 smart meters**, each costing **₹8,000** to replace. A ransomware attack (Likelihood = "Medium" → ARO = 0.5/year) can brick **10%** of them. Each bricked meter also causes **₹2,000** in revenue loss (billing outage) and **₹500** in regulatory fine exposure.

**Step 1: Asset Value (AV) per meter**

$$
\text{AV} = 8000 + 2000 + 500 = \text{₹}10{,}500 \text{ per meter}
$$

**Step 2: Total AV for affected meters**

$$
\text{AV}_{\text{total}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 10{,}500 = \text{₹}52{,}500{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Exposure Factor**

$$
\text{EF} = 1.0 \quad \text{(total loss of the affected meters)}
$$

**Step 4: Single Loss Expectancy**

$$
\text{SLE} = 52{,}500{,}000 \times 1.0 = \text{₹}5.25 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Step 5: Annualized Loss Expectancy**

$$
\text{ALE} = 5.25 \text{ Crores} \times 0.5 = \text{₹}2.625 \text{ Crores / year}
$$

**Step 6: Threat Modeling — STRIDE Analysis**

Apply STRIDE to the smart meter firmware update path:

| Threat | STRIDE Category | Attack Vector | CPS-Specific Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rogue firmware pushed to meters | Tampering | Compromised OTA server | Mass meter bricking → grid instability |
| Spoofed aggregator commands | Spoofing | Replay attack on PLC | False consumption data, revenue loss |
| DoS on concentrator | Denial of Service | Flooding RF mesh | Loss of visibility, demand-supply imbalance |
| Customer data exfiltration | Information Disclosure | Unencrypted HAN traffic | Privacy violation, regulatory fine (DPDP Act 2023) |

**Step 7: ROSI Calculation for Proposed Control: Signed Firmware + MFA on OTA**

- Annual control cost ($C_{\text{control}}$) = **₹80 Lakhs** (HSM + MFA + patching)
- Control effectiveness = 90% (reduces ARO from 0.5 to 0.05)
- $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 5.25 \text{ Cr} \times 0.05 = \text{₹}26.25 \text{ Lakhs}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{(2.625 \text{ Cr} - 0.2625 \text{ Cr} - 0.80 \text{ Cr})}{(2.625 \text{ Cr} - 0.2625 \text{ Cr})} \\
&= \dfrac{1.5625 \text{ Cr}}{2.3625 \text{ Cr}} \\
&= 0.661 \;\; \text{(i.e., 66.1% net benefit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The control is financially justified **and** reduces the CPS risk of grid instability. The combined framework produces a decision that is **simultaneously financially rational and safety-defensible**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Threat Modeling Outputs to Risk Register

| Threat Modeling Output | Risk Register Field | Action Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| Threat category (STRIDE) | Threat source classification | Assign threat agent profile |
| Attack vector / path | Vulnerability identifier | Map to CVE/CWE |
| Attack tree leaf node | Likelihood factor (L) | Score ARO |
| Compromised asset | Asset Value (AV) | Score SLE |
| Worst-case physical outcome | Impact (I) | Score safety, financial, operational impact |
| Required attacker capability | Threat capability | Modulate L |
| Existing mitigations | Control strength | Reduce ARO / EF |
| New mitigations recommended | Risk treatment plan | Budget request via ROSI |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Combined Risk Management + Threat Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([CPS Asset Inventory]) --> B1[Asset Valuation AV]
    B1 --> C1[Assign CIA Weights]
    C1 --> D1[Data Flow Diagram DFD]
    D1 --> E1[Identify Trust Boundaries]
    E1 --> F1[Apply STRIDE per Element]
    F1 --> G1[Enumerate Threats]
    G1 --> H1[Map Vulnerabilities CVE CWE]
    H1 --> I1[Build Attack Trees]
    I1 --> J1[Score Likelihood L 1 to 5]
    I1 --> J2[Score Impact I 1 to 5]
    J1 --> K1[Compute Risk R L times I]
    J2 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Map to 5x5 Risk Matrix]
    L1 --> M1{Acceptable Risk?}
    M1 -- No --> N1[Identify Controls]
    N1 --> O1[Compute ROSI]
    O1 --> P1{ROSI greater than 0}
    P1 -- Yes --> Q1[Implement Control]
    P1 -- No --> R1[Accept or Transfer Risk]
    Q1 --> S1[Recompute Residual Risk]
    S1 --> M1
    M1 -- Yes --> T1[Document in Risk Register]
    T1 --> U1[Continuous Monitoring KPIs]
    U1 --> A0
    R1 --> T1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Integrated Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["Input Layer"]
        IA1[Business Objectives]
        IA2[CPS Architecture Docs]
        IA3[Threat Intelligence Feeds]
        IA4[Asset Inventory CMDB]
    end

    subgraph TM_ENGINE["Threat Modeling Engine"]
        TM1[System Decomposer]
        TM2[STRIDE Classifier]
        TM3[Attack Tree Builder]
        TM4[MITRE ATT and CK Mapper]
    end

    subgraph RM_ENGINE["Risk Management Engine"]
        RM1[Asset Valuator]
        RM2[Likelihood Scorer]
        RM3[Impact Scorer]
        RM4[ALE Calculator]
        RM5[ROSI Calculator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["Output Layer"]
        OL1[Prioritized Risk Register]
        OL2[Security Control Backlog]
        OL3[Compliance Reports]
        OL4[Executive Dashboard]
    end

    IA1 --> TM1
    IA2 --> TM1
    IA3 --> TM2
    IA4 --> RM1
    TM1 --> TM2
    TM2 --> TM3
    TM3 --> TM4
    TM4 --> RM2
    TM4 --> RM3
    IA4 --> RM1
    RM1 --> RM4
    RM2 --> RM4
    RM3 --> RM4
    RM4 --> RM5
    RM5 --> OL1
    RM4 --> OL1
    TM4 --> OL2
    OL1 --> OL2
    OL1 --> OL3
    OL1 --> OL4
```

### 4.3 STRIDE Threat Categories Mapped to CPS Attack Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPS[CPS System Under Analysis] --> TB[Trust Boundary]
    TB --> S1[Sensor Network]
    TB --> S2[Control Network]
    TB --> S3[Actuator Network]
    TB --> S4[Cloud Interface]

    S1 --> ST[Spoofing: Fake sensor readings]
    S1 --> T1[Tampering: Modify sensor calibration]

    S2 --> R1[Repudiation: Deny sending control cmd]
    S2 --> D1[DoS: Flood control network]

    S3 --> E1[Elevation: Unauthorized actuator trigger]
    S3 --> T2[Tampering: Modify setpoints]

    S4 --> I1[Info Disclosure: Exfil telemetry]
    S4 --> S3Spoof[Spoofing: Rogue cloud command]

    ST --> RISK[Risk Register Entry]
    T1 --> RISK
    R1 --> RISK
    D1 --> RISK
    E1 --> RISK
    T2 --> RISK
    I1 --> RISK
    S3Spoof --> RISK
```

### 4.4 Risk Treatment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RR[Identified Risk] --> SC{Score in 5x5 Matrix}
    SC -- Low 1 to 6 --> ACC[Accept Document]
    SC -- Medium 7 to 12 --> MIT[Mitigate Plan 90 days]
    SC -- High 13 to 19 --> TRA[Transfer via Insurance or SLA]
    SC -- Critical 20 to 25 --> AVO[Avoid or Immediate Action]
    MIT --> IMPL[Implement Control]
    IMPL --> RES[Recompute Residual]
    RES --> SC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** Differentiate between **Risk Management** and **Threat Modeling**. Why must they be combined in CPS security?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Risk Management | Threat Modeling |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Decide *what* to protect and *how much* to spend | Find *how* an attacker breaks in |
| **Output** | Risk register, treatment plan | Attack trees, threat list |
| **Perspective** | Business / asset-centric | Attacker / system-centric |
| **Cadence** | Annual / quarterly review | Per design iteration / change |

In CPS, the two are inseparable because a logical cyber-threat (e.g., spoofed sensor) causes *physical* harm. RM alone cannot identify the attack path; TM alone cannot prioritize by business impact. Their combination yields **prioritized, attack-aware, business-justified controls** (1 mark). KTU frameworks like NIST SP 800-30 (RM) and STRIDE/PASTA (TM) are explicitly designed to interoperate (1 mark). For example, the output of a STRIDE analysis becomes the *threat input* to the NIST risk determination step (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Define **ALE** and **SLE**. State their formulas with a one-line use case each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)** = Asset Value × Exposure Factor. It quantifies the monetary loss from *one* occurrence. Example: If a SCADA server costs ₹20 Lakhs and 80% is destroyed in a ransomware event, SLE = ₹16 Lakhs (1 mark).
- **ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence)** = number of incidents per year (0.5, 1, 2, etc.) (0.5 mark).
- **ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy)** = SLE × ARO. It expresses the *expected* annual loss, enabling budget decisions. Example: SLE = ₹16 Lakhs, ARO = 0.25 → ALE = ₹4 Lakhs/year, which is the *upper bound* the organization should spend on the control (1.5 marks).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** For a **Smart Water Treatment Plant CPS** consisting of PLCs, an HMI, a historian server, and a remote operator workstation connected over a TCP/IP network:

**(a)** Apply the **STRIDE threat modeling** methodology to identify six threats (one per STRIDE category), and build an **attack tree** for the highest-severity threat. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Combine the threat model output with the **NIST SP 800-30 risk management** process to produce a **quantitative risk assessment** including ALE and a **cost-justified** control recommendation using ROSI. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) STRIDE Analysis & Attack Tree (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Decompose the system into DFD elements (1 mark):**

| Element | Trust Boundary |
|---|---|
| Field Sensors (pH, flow, turbidity) | Sensor LAN |
| PLCs (Siemens S7-1500) | Control LAN |
| HMI Workstation | Control LAN |
| Historian Server | Operations LAN |
| Remote Operator (VPN) | Corporate IT ↔ OT |

**Step 2 — STRIDE Threats (one per category) (3 marks):**

| STRIDE | Threat | Asset | Attack Vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Attacker spoofs engineer credentials on HMI | HMI | Credential theft, replay |
| **T**ampering | Modifies PLC ladder logic to inject excessive chlorine | PLC | Unauthorized S7Comm write |
| **R**epudiation | Operator denies issuing a valve-open command | HMI | Lack of audit log integrity |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Historian SQL exfiltrates process recipes | Historian | SQL injection on web UI |
| **D**enial of Service | Floods PLC with S7Comm requests | PLC | L3 DoS attack |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Maintenance account escalates to engineer role | AD/OT | Pass-the-hash, token theft |

**Step 3 — Attack Tree for highest-severity threat (chlorine over-dose) (3 marks):**

```
Root Goal: Cause dangerous chlorine concentration in water supply (> 5 mg/L)
├── OR
│   ├── 1. Compromise Engineering Workstation
│   │   ├── AND
│   │   │   ├── 1.1 Phishing of operator (Spear-phish with OT-themed lure)
│   │   │   └── 1.2 Lateral movement to HMI via shared admin creds
│   │   └── OR
│   │       └── 1.3 Direct VPN brute-force of engineering account
│   └── 2. Direct PLC Exploit
│       ├── AND
│       │   ├── 2.1 Discover PLC via Shodan / nmap (S7-1500 port 102)
│       │   ├── 2.2 Exploit CVE-2023-XXXX in S7Comm implementation
│       │   └── 2.3 Write malicious FB to chlorine dosing loop
│       └── OR
│           └── 2.4 Use stolen TIA Portal project file to inject code
└── AND
    └── 3. Cover Tracks
        ├── 3.1 Clear HMI event log
        └── 3.2 Disable historian alerting
```

---

**(b) Quantitative Risk Assessment with NIST SP 800-30 + ROSI (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Asset Valuation (1 mark):**
- PLC (incl. downtime) = ₹15 Lakhs
- Sensor + dosing hardware = ₹8 Lakhs
- Public health liability + regulatory fine (CPCB) = ₹2 Crores
- **Total AV = ₹2.23 Crores**

**Step 2 — Exposure Factor (0.5 mark):** EF = 1.0 (total chlorine system compromise → total loss event).

**Step 3 — SLE (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{SLE} = 2.23 \text{ Cr} \times 1.0 = \text{₹}2.23 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Step 4 — ARO from Threat Model (0.5 mark):** Threat model indicates "Medium" likelihood with a known active APT group (e.g., "CyberAv3ngers" profile) → ARO = 0.5 events/year.

**Step 5 — ALE (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{ALE} = 2.23 \text{ Cr} \times 0.5 = \text{₹}1.115 \text{ Crores / year}
$$

**Step 6 — Proposed Control: Network Segmentation + MFA + Signed PLC Firmware + SOC Monitoring (1 mark):**
- Annual cost (capex amortized + opex) = **₹35 Lakhs**
- Control effectiveness = 85% → reduces ARO from 0.5 to 0.075
- $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 2.23 \text{ Cr} \times 0.075 = \text{₹}16.725 \text{ Lakhs}$

**Step 7 — ROSI (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{(1.115 \text{ Cr} - 0.16725 \text{ Cr} - 0.35 \text{ Cr})}{(1.115 \text{ Cr} - 0.16725 \text{ Cr})} \\
&= \dfrac{0.59775 \text{ Cr}}{0.94775 \text{ Cr}} \\
&= 0.631 \;\; \text{(63.1% net benefit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Residual Risk and Recommendation (1 mark):**
$$
R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - 0.85) = 0.15 \times R_{\text{inherent}}
$$

**Recommendation:** Implement the control bundle. The combined framework demonstrates a 63% ROSI and reduces public-health risk by 85%, satisfying both **financial** and **safety** objectives.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** A hospital is deploying a **connected infusion pump CPS** that delivers morphine to patients. The pump has a Wi-Fi module, a touch-screen UI, a dosing motor, and connects to a central Electronic Medical Record (EMR) server.

**(a)** Perform a **PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)** 7-stage analysis for this system, highlighting **CPS-specific** attack surfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk)** model, compute the **Annual Loss Expectancy** and recommend a **defense-in-depth** control set. Justify using the **DiDI formula**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) PASTA 7-Stage Analysis (7 marks)**

| Stage | Output for Infusion Pump CPS |
|---|---|
| **1. Business Objectives** | Deliver correct drug dose (10–100 mL/hr morphine) with **zero patient harm**; comply with FDA & HIPAA |
| **2. Technical Scope** | Wi-Fi (802.11), MQTT broker, EMR HL7 interface, dosing motor, alarm system |
| **3. Decomposition** | DFD shows: Touch UI → Pump MCU → Motor Driver → Patient; EMR ↔ Pump via Wi-Fi |
| **4. Threat Analysis** | STRIDE yields 12 threats; e.g., *Spoofed EMR pushes incorrect dose prescription* |
| **5. Vulnerability Mapping** | CWE-306 (Missing Authentication), CVE-2023-XXXX (Wi-Fi buffer overflow), no code signing on firmware |
| **6. Attack Modeling** | Kill chain: Wi-Fi deauth → Pump in recovery mode → Inject malicious firmware → Increase dose by 10× |
| **7. Risk & Impact Analysis** | Inherent Risk = "Critical" (patient mortality likely). All attack paths converge on the *dose modification* primitive |

**CPS-Specific attack surfaces (1 mark):**
- The **physical feedback loop** (drug → patient → vitals → EMR) is attackable.
- The **safety interlock** (alarm) is software-mediated and bypassable.
- **Replay attacks** on dosing commands can be lethal even with encryption.

---

**(b) FAIR Model + Defense-in-Depth with DiDI (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — FAIR Decomposition (1 mark):**
- Threat Event Frequency (TEF) = 2/year (active ransomware groups target hospitals)
- Vulnerability = 60% (probabilistic; P(pump compromised | attack attempt))
- Primary Loss (PL) = ₹50 Lakhs (legal, regulatory, replacement)
- Secondary Loss (SL) = ₹2 Crores (reputation, patient lawsuit, class action)

**Step 2 — Loss Event Frequency (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{LEF} = \text{TEF} \times \text{Vulnerability} = 2 \times 0.6 = 1.2 \text{ events/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Loss Magnitude (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{LM} = \text{PL} + \text{SL} = 0.5 \text{ Cr} + 2.0 \text{ Cr} = \text{₹}2.5 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Loss Expectancy (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{ALE} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM} = 1.2 \times 2.5 = \text{₹}3 \text{ Crores / year}
$$

**Step 5 — Defense-in-Depth Control Set (2 marks):**
| Layer | Control | Effectiveness $e_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signed firmware + secure boot | 0.40 |
| 2 | Wi-Fi WPA3 + mutual TLS | 0.35 |
| 3 | Dose-limit server-side validation | 0.45 |
| 4 | Network segmentation (medical VLAN) | 0.30 |
| 5 | SIEM + 24/7 SOC monitoring | 0.25 |

**Step 6 — DiDI Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DiDI} &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{5} (1 - e_i) \\
&= 1 - (0.60 \times 0.65 \times 0.55 \times 0.70 \times 0.75) \\
&= 1 - (0.60 \times 0.65 \times 0.55 \times 0.70 \times 0.75) \\
&= 1 - 0.1129 \\
&= 0.887 \;\; \text{(88.7% combined efficacy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Residual ALE (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{residual}} = 3 \text{ Cr} \times (1 - 0.887) = \text{₹}33.9 \text{ Lakhs/year}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 5-layer defense-in-depth reduces ALE by ~89%, achieving an order-of-magnitude safety improvement, with the **server-side dose validation** being the single most effective control. Justify the control spending via ROSI > 0.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Treating RM and TM as separate silos** — *You will lose 2–3 marks instantly*. Always draw the integration arrow.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in ALE/ROSI** — Write "₹/year" explicitly. A number without a unit is *not* complete in KTU answer sheets.
