| **Industrial ICS** | Ransomware, Stuxnet | Application whitelisting, DMZ | PLC golden-image rollback |
| **Connected Vehicles** | CAN bus injection | Message authentication (AUTOSAR SecOC) | Fail-safe limp mode |
| **Healthcare IoMT** | Device hijack | Device identity, network segmentation | Patient safety override |
| **Smart Buildings** | BMS tampering | BACnet Secure Connect | HVAC fail-open to safety |
| **Drone Swarms** | GPS spoofing | Multi-source navigation fusion | Geofence enforcement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Intrusion Prevention & Mitigation Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prepare: Asset Inventory, Baselines, IR Playbooks] --> B[Detect: SIEM, NIDS, Anomaly Engine]
    B --> C{Is it a true intrusion?}
    C -- No --> D[Log as benign, refine rules]
    C -- Yes --> E[Classify: IT or OT domain?]
    E --> F[Contain: Isolate zone, block C2]
    F --> G[Eradicate: Remove malware, revoke creds]
    G --> H[Recover: Restore from golden image, failback]
    H --> I[Post-Incident: RCA, Lessons, Control Update]
    I --> A
    F --> J[Deception: Honeypot, Canary token]
    J --> K[Threat Intel Enrichment]
    K --> I
```

## 4.2 CPS Layered Defense Architecture (Purdue + Mitigation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph L5["L5: Enterprise Network (IT)"]
        ERP[ERP, Email, Cloud]
    end
    subgraph L4["L4: Site Business Planning"]
        MES[MES, Historians]
    end
    subgraph L3_5["L3.5: IDMZ - Industrial DMZ"]
        FW1[Industrial Firewall]
        JUMP[Jump Server / Data Diode]
    end
    subgraph L3["L3: Site Operations (SCADA)"]
        HMI[HMI, Engineering WS]
        NIDS[NIDS / NIPS]
    end
    subgraph L2["L2: Area Supervisory Control"]
        SCADA[SCADA Server, Alarms]
    end
    subgraph L1["L1: Basic Control"]
        PLC[PLC, RTU, DCS Controller]
    end
    subgraph L0["L0: Physical Process"]
        SENS[Sensors, Actuators]
    end
    ERP --> FW1
    FW1 --> MES
    MES --> JUMP
    JUMP --> HMI
    HMI --> SCADA
    SCADA --> PLC
    PLC --> SENS
    NIPS_Mon[NIPS Monitoring] -.-> HMI
    NIDS -.-> SCADA
    WAF[Whitelisting on HMI] -.-> HMI
    HONEYPOT[OT Honeypot] -.-> L3
```

## 4.3 Mitigation Decision Tree (Triage Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Intrusion Detected]) --> Q1{Severity Level?}
    Q1 -- Critical --> C1[Immediate Isolation + Failover]
    Q1 -- High --> C2[Quarantine Zone + Manual Review]
    Q1 -- Medium --> C3[Enhanced Monitoring + Patch]
    Q1 -- Low --> C4[Log + Defer to Maintenance Window]
    C1 --> R1[Activate Redundant Controller]
    C2 --> R2[Capture Forensics Snapshot]
    C3 --> R3[Update IDS Signatures]
    C4 --> R4[Schedule Hardening]
    R1 --> Post[Post-Incident Review]
    R2 --> Post
    R3 --> Post
    R4 --> Post
```

## 4.4 Strategy Inter-Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    Prev[Prevention] --- Det[Detection]
    Det --- Mit[Mitigation]
    Mit --- Rec[Recovery]
    Rec --- Prev
    Det --- Dec[Deception]
    Dec --- Int[Threat Intelligence]
    Int --- Prev
    Prev --- Seg[Network Segmentation]
    Prev --- Auth[Zero Trust Auth]
    Prev --- Hard[System Hardening]
    Mit --- Auto[SOAR Automation]
    Mit --- Hon[Honeypots]
    Rec --- Red[Redundancy]
    Rec --- BC[Business Continuity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). How does it differ from an Intrusion Detection System (IDS)?

**Model Answer:**
An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is an active security control that monitors network or system traffic for malicious activity and **automatically takes action to block** the offending traffic in real time. In contrast, an **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a **passive** monitoring system that only **alerts** administrators about suspected intrusions without blocking them.

| Feature | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Passive (sniffer) | Active (inline) |
| Action | Alert only | Alert + block/drop/reset |
| Latency impact | None | Adds ~1–10 ms |
| Failure consequence | Silent miss | Self-induced DoS if misfires |

IPS is preferred for CPS edge protection where human response time is too slow.

---

### Question 2: List any three CPS-specific challenges in implementing intrusion prevention strategies.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Legacy OT Protocols:** Protocols like Modbus, DNP3, and PROFIBUS lack authentication, making deep inspection difficult without breaking compatibility.
2. **High Availability Requirement:** CPS systems cannot tolerate downtime for patching or security updates; preventive measures must be non-disruptive.
3. **IT-OT Convergence Risk:** Connecting OT to IT networks introduces attack surfaces (e.g., Stuxnet propagated via USB into an air-gapped network).
4. **Safety Interlock Conflict:** Aggressive blocking may override safety-critical fail-safe modes.
5. **Resource-Constrained Devices:** Many RTUs/PLCs cannot host traditional anti-malware due to CPU/memory limits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Defense-in-Depth** strategy for Cyber-Physical Systems. Illustrate with a layered architecture diagram and discuss how each layer contributes to intrusion prevention.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the joint breach probability for an $n$-layer independent defense system. Show mathematically how adding layers exponentially reduces breach probability. Use a numerical example with $n = 4$, each $P_i = 0.15$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Defense-in-Depth for CPS (7 marks):**

Defense-in-Depth (DiD) is a security strategy that employs **multiple, overlapping, and independent layers of controls** so that the failure of any single layer does not result in compromise of the system. For CPS, the strategy is adapted from the **Purdue Model** (ISA-99/IEC 62443) which divides the enterprise into hierarchical zones (Levels 0–5).

**Key Layers (each contributing uniquely):**

- **L0–L1 (Physical Process & Basic Control):** Hardened PLC firmware, tamper-evident enclosures, physical access logs.
- **L2 (Supervisory Control):** Application whitelisting on HMI/SCADA, host-based intrusion detection.
- **L3 (Operations):** Industrial firewalls, unidirectional gateways (data diodes) to enforce one-way data flow.
- **L3.5 (Industrial DMZ):** Jump servers, malware inspection, broker between IT and OT.
- **L4–L5 (Enterprise/Cloud):** Standard IT controls — NIPS, EDR, identity governance.

**Contribution of each layer:**
- Prevention: Stops 70–80% of attacks at perimeter (firewall, DMZ).
- Detection: Catches anomalies missed by prevention (NIDS, SIEM).
- Mitigation: Limits blast radius (segmentation, isolation).
- Recovery: Ensures business continuity (golden images, redundancy).

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 for the visual architecture.)

**Board-Standard Pointers to Mention:**
- DiD follows the **CIA Triad** and **Parkerian Hexad**.
- Aligned with **NIST SP 800-82** (Guide to ICS Security).
- Compensating controls replace single points of failure.

---

**(b) Joint Breach Probability Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define $P_i$ = probability that layer $i$ is breached. Probability of layer $i$ *preventing* the breach = $1 - P_i$.

**Step 2:** For an attacker to breach the system, **all** $n$ independent layers must fail. Joint probability:

$$
P(\text{full breach}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

**Step 3:** For **identical** layers with $P_i = p$:

$$
P(\text{breach}) = p^n
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example — $n = 4$, $p = 0.15$:

$$
P(\text{breach}) = (0.15)^4 = 0.15 \times 0.15 \times 0.15 \times 0.15
$$

$$
= 0.0225 \times 0.0225 = 0.00050625
$$

$$
\approx 5.06 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0506\%
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with single-layer ($n = 1$): $P = 15\%$. Improvement:

$$
\text{Improvement Factor} = \frac{0.15}{5.06 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 296\times
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion — Adding independent layers yields **exponential** security improvement, justifying defense-in-depth investment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** principle. How does it apply to CPS environments, and what are its key components?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain how **Honeypots and Deception Technology** can be used as an active mitigation strategy in Industrial Control Systems (ICS).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Zero Trust Architecture (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Zero Trust is a security model that assumes **no implicit trust** for any entity (user, device, application) regardless of its location (inside or outside the network perimeter). Every access request must be **authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated**.

**Core Principle:** *"Never trust, always verify."*

**Key Components (NIST SP 800-207):**

1. **Policy Engine (PE):** The decision-maker that evaluates access requests against policy rules.
2. **Policy Administrator (PA):** Executes the PE's decisions (allow/deny).
3. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP):** The gateway that controls access (e.g., NGFW, NAC).
4. **Continuous Diagnostics & Mitigation (CDM):** Real-time device posture assessment.
5. **Microsegmentation:** Granular per-workload access control.
6. **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** Strong identity verification.
7. **Least Privilege Access:** Just-in-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA).

**Application to CPS:**
- **Device Identity:** Every PLC, RTU, and sensor has a unique cryptographic identity (x.509 cert).
- **Mutual TLS (mTLS):** Authenticated communication between OT devices.
- **Software-Defined Perimeter (SDP):** Replaces static network zones with dynamic, identity-based access.
- **Continuous Monitoring:** Behavioral baselining detects deviations (e.g., a PLC suddenly communicating with an external IP).

**Example:** A maintenance engineer connecting to a SCADA HMI is granted access **only** to the specific tag group required, **only** during the scheduled maintenance window, **only** from an authorized engineering laptop with current AV signatures — and the session is **continuously re-evaluated**.

---

**(b) Honeypots and Deception Technology in ICS (7 marks):**

**Definition:** A **honeypot** is a decoy system that mimics legitimate CPS components (e.g., a fake PLC or HMI) to attract, detect, and study attackers. A **honeypot network (honeynet)** is a collection of such decoys.

**Types:**
- **Low-interaction:** Simulates services (e.g., a fake Modbus server on port 502). Easy to deploy, low risk.
- **High-interaction:** Fully functional simulated PLC (e.g., using Conpot, GasPot). Higher fidelity, higher risk of compromise.

**How it works as mitigation:**

1. **Deployment:** Place honeypots in **uninstrumented** zones of the OT network (e.g., a fake engineering workstation in the L3 zone).
2. **Detection:** Any contact with the honeypot is **suspicious by definition** — legitimate users have no reason to access it. Triggers an immediate alert.
3. **Diversion:** Attackers waste time probing decoys, slowing down the attack chain.
4. **Intelligence Gathering:** Logs reveal TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) of the attacker.
5. **Forensic Evidence:** Provides detailed activity records for post-incident analysis.

**Example:** The **Conpot** honeypot mimics a Siemens S7-1200 PLC. When deployed in a water utility's network, it successfully detected credential-stuffing attempts within 24 hours of deployment (case study from the 2014 SANS ICS honeypot project).

**Limitations in CPS:**
- Risk of honeypot compromise providing attackers a pivot point.
- May not replicate proprietary protocols perfectly.
- Must be isolated from real control networks.

**Integration with SOAR:** Honeypot alerts are automatically correlated with SIEM events and trigger isolation playbooks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — Many students incorrectly state that an IDS "blocks" traffic. IDS = passive; IPS = active. This is a **2-mark deduction** on direct questions.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why" in layered defense** — Don't just list layers; explain **what each prevents** and **what residual risk remains**.
> 3. **Math errors in probability derivation** — Watch out for the difference between $1 - (1-p)^n$ and $p^n$. The latter is joint **failure** probability; the former is defense **success**.
> 4. **Ignoring CPS-specific context** — Generic IT security answers (e.g., "use antivirus") will be marked down. Always mention OT protocols, availability, and safety.
> 5. **No diagrams in Part B** — KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for clear, labeled diagrams. Always include a Purdue/zoning diagram.
> 6. **Omitting standards references** — Citing **NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443, or ISA-99** adds academic weight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **IPS = Active, IDS = Passive** — know the distinction cold.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** = independent, overlapping layers; breach probability = $\prod P_i = p^n$ (exponential decay).
- ✅ **Purdue Model** has 6 levels (0–5); **L3.5 IDMZ** is the critical IT/OT boundary.
- ✅ **Zero Trust** = "Never trust, always verify" + microsegmentation + continuous validation.
- ✅ **F1-Score** is the balanced metric for IDS evaluation; in CPS, **prioritize Recall over Precision** (avoid false negatives).
- ✅ **Honeypots** are detection-by-deception tools; any access is suspicious by design.
- ✅ **MTTD/MTTR** are key incident response KPIs; combined they drive the **availability metric $A$**.
- ✅ **OT-specific challenges:** legacy protocols (Modbus/DNP3), no patching windows, safety interlocks, resource-constrained devices.
- ✅ **Standards to cite:** NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443, ISA-99, NIST SP 800-61 (IR), NIST SP 800-207 (Zero Trust).
- ✅ **Safety-Security Co-Design:** Security must never override safety; both are co-equal in CPS.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** is the modern TTP framework for OT-specific threat modeling.
- ✅ **Network Segmentation** (zone & conduit) is the single most effective CPS mitigation — limits lateral movement.
- ✅ **Golden Images** + **Configuration Baselines** enable fast recovery from compromise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Intrusion Prevention Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Intrusion Prevention Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is a network security apparatus that goes beyond the passive monitoring capability of an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) by actively analyzing network traffic and system behaviour in real time, and upon detection of malicious activity, automatically executing pre-defined preventive actions such as packet dropping, session termination, traffic re-routing, quarantine of compromised nodes, or reconfiguration of firewalls.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, an Advanced IPS must operate under strict constraints of **real-time latency**, **deterministic response**, and **safety-critical reliability** because the system under protection interfaces directly with physical processes (e.g., power grids, autonomous vehicles, medical devices, industrial control loops).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT863):**
> *An Advanced Intrusion Prevention Technique is a proactive, automated cyber-defence mechanism that combines deep traffic inspection, behavioural analytics, machine learning inference, and policy enforcement to block, deflect, or neutralize adversarial actions before they compromise Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability of the CPS triad (the CIA-plus-Safety model).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security Checkpoint"

Imagine a modern international airport:

| Airport Component | CPS Intrusion Prevention Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Passenger boarding pass + ID scan (signature verification) | **Signature-based IPS** matching known attack patterns |
| Behavioural officers watching for nervous travellers (suspicious gait, repeated loitering) | **Anomaly-based IPS** powered by ML/AI inference |
| Metal detectors + body scanners (deep physical inspection) | **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** of payload bytes |
| Armed response team neutralizing threats instantly | **Inline blocking engine** that drops malicious packets |
| Runway diversions and air-traffic rerouting | **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** flow re-routing |
| Decoy boarding gates that look real | **Honeypots / Honeynets** as tripwires |
| Locked cockpit doors, changeable flight paths | **Moving Target Defense (MTD)** with dynamic address mutation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examinations:**
> - **IDS = Alarm system** (detects & alerts, does NOT block).
> - **IPS = Alarm + automatic sprinkler system** (detects AND blocks inline).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard detection benchmark latency for CPS-IPS:** $\le 1\text{ ms}$ for hard real-time loops (e.g., IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging in smart substations).
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** benchmark: $\le 100\text{ ms}$ for critical infrastructure.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** benchmark: $\le 1\text{ s}$ for safety-critical CPS.
- **Default False Positive Rate (FPR) industrial target:** $\le 0.1\%$ to prevent alarm fatigue in SCADA control rooms.

## 1.4 Classification of Advanced IPS (KTU Board Taxonomy)

Advanced Intrusion Prevention Techniques can be classified along three orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — Deployment Location:**
- **Network-based IPS (NIPS):** Inline appliance at choke points.
- **Host-based IPS (HIPS):** Resides on the endpoint / RTU / PLC.
- **Hybrid / Distributed IPS (DIPS):** Sensor mesh across CPS layers (cloud, fog, edge, field).

**Axis 2 — Detection Paradigm:**
- **Signature-based (Misuse Detection).**
- **Anomaly-based (Behavioural Detection).**
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis.**
- **Specification-based Detection** (formal CPS invariants).

**Axis 3 — Response Paradigm:**
- **Reactive / Inline Blocking.**
- **Proactive / Deceptive (honeypots, MTD).**
- **Adaptive / Self-Healing (AI-driven).**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Any answer on "Advanced Intrusion Prevention" that does **not** mention *real-time*, *inline*, and *CPS-specific deterministic response* will be marked incomplete in KTU 2024 valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of an Advanced IPS

A modern Advanced IPS executes a four-stage pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Traffic Acquisition & Normalization
- Packet capture via **SPAN ports**, **TAPs**, or **in-line network interface cards (NICs)**.
- Protocol decoding up to Layer 7 (e.g., Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, OPC-UA).
- Time-stamping with **IEEE 1588 Precision Time Protocol (PTP)** for CPS clock synchronization.

### Stage 2 — Multi-Engine Inspection
- **Engine A — Signature Matcher:** Uses **Aho-Corasick** or **Wu-Manber** string-search algorithms against a rule database (e.g., Snort, Suricata rulesets).
- **Engine B — Anomaly Detector:** Statistical or ML model compares observed features $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ against a learned baseline distribution.
- **Engine C — Protocol State Machine:** Validates the legality of state transitions in CPS-specific protocols.
- **Engine D — Specification Checker:** Verifies physical invariants (e.g., pump pressure $\in [0, 10]$ bar).

### Stage 3 — Decision Fusion
- The four engines emit **soft scores** or **hard verdicts** that are combined via a **Bayesian fusion model** or a **weighted voting ensemble**.

### Stage 4 — Inline Response
- Actions: **DROP**, **REJECT**, **RESET**, **REROUTE**, **QUARANTINE**, **SLOW-RATE**, **ALERT-ONLY**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Mathematical Toolkit)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae form the **evaluation backbone** of every IPS / IDS numerical question in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Positive Rate (Recall / Sensitivity) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | TP=True Positives, FN=False Negatives |
| 2 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | FP=False Positives, TN=True Negatives |
| 3 | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | — |
| 4 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | — |
| 5 | F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$ | — |
| 6 | Accuracy | $ACC = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | — |
| 7 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust for imbalanced data |
| 8 | Shannon Entropy (anomaly scoring) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $p(x_i)$ = probability of symbol/state |
| 9 | Conditional Entropy | $H(Y \vert X) = -\sum_{x}\sum_{y} p(x,y) \log_2 p(y \vert x)$ | Residual uncertainty in $Y$ given $X$ |
| 10 | Information Gain (feature selection) | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | Used in Decision Tree IPS classifiers |
| 11 | Bayes Posterior (Bayesian IPS) | $P(C \vert \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \vert C) \, P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | $C$ = class (attack/normal), $\mathbf{x}$ = feature vector |
| 12 | Naïve Bayes Joint Likelihood | $P(\mathbf{x} \vert C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C)$ | Conditional independence assumption |
| 13 | Mahalanobis Distance (anomaly score) | $D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ = mean, $\Sigma$ = covariance |
| 14 | Z-Score Anomaly Threshold | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}, \quad \vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow \text{anomaly}$ | $\sigma$ = standard deviation |
| 15 | Risk Score (CPS Impact) | $R = T \times V \times I$ | T=Threat, V=Vulnerability, I=Impact |
| 16 | ROC AUC (detector quality) | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(x)) \, dx$ | $\in [0.5, 1.0]$ for usable detectors |
| 17 | Detection Latency | $L_{det} = t_{alert} - t_{attack\_onset}$ | Must be $\le 1\text{ ms}$ for hard real-time CPS |
| 18 | Network Throughput Penalty | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_{with\_IPS}}{T_{baseline}}$ | Performance cost of in-line inspection |
| 19 | Packet Loss Probability (M/M/1) | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{(1-\rho)\rho^N}{1-\rho^{N+1}}, \quad \rho = \lambda/\mu$ | $\lambda$=arrival, $\mu$=service rate, $N$=buffer size |
| 20 | Cryptographic Hash Speed (blockchain IPS audit) | $H = \dfrac{\text{hashes}}{\text{second}}$ | Used in trust-chain verification |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** NIPS protects IEC 61850 traffic between substation IEDs.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Specification-based IPS enforces actuator command limits (e.g., valve must not open $>90\%$ in $<1$ s).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** HIPS detects CAN-bus injection attacks using timing-interval anomaly analysis.
- **Healthcare CPS:** ML-IPS monitors infusion pump telemetry for dose-tampering patterns.
- **Smart Cities:** Distributed IPS using fog computing protects municipal sensor networks from coordinated botnet attacks.

## 2.4 Why Each Stage Matters — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why normalize traffic?** CPS protocols (Modbus, DNP3) lack authentication; decoding reveals semantic intent.
- **Why multi-engine?** A single signature rule cannot catch **zero-day attacks**; statistical models fail on **polymorphic malware**. Fusion gives resilience.
- **Why Bayesian fusion?** It produces *calibrated probabilities* rather than binary outputs, which is essential for graded response in CPS (warn vs. shutdown).
- **Why inline response?** CPS safety demands **deterministic prevention** — not after-the-fact forensics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Bayes Decision Threshold for IPS

**Problem Context (KTU-style):** A CPS intrusion prevention system observes a network flow feature $x$ (e.g., inter-arrival time). The prior probability of attack is $P(C_1) = \pi_1$ and of normal flow is $P(C_0) = \pi_0$. The loss incurred for a *false negative* (missed attack) is $L_{FN}$ and for a *false positive* (blocked legitimate flow) is $L_{FP}$. Derive the optimal decision threshold $x^*$.

### Step 1 — Define the Posterior Odds
From Bayes' theorem:

$$
P(C_1 \vert x) = \frac{P(x \vert C_1) \, \pi_1}{P(x)} \quad \text{and} \quad P(C_0 \vert x) = \frac{P(x \vert C_0) \, \pi_0}{P(x)}
$$

The likelihood ratio is:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{P(x \vert C_1)}{P(x \vert C_0)}
$$

### Step 2 — Expected Loss (Conditional Risk)
The expected loss for deciding $\hat{C} = C_1$ (block) is:

$$
R(C_1 \vert x) = L_{FP} \cdot P(C_0 \vert x)
$$

The expected loss for deciding $\hat{C} = C_0$ (allow) is:

$$
R(C_0 \vert x) = L_{FN} \cdot P(C_1 \vert x)
$$

### Step 3 — Minimum-Risk Decision Rule
The IPS should block ($C_1$) iff $R(C_1 \vert x) \le R(C_0 \vert x)$:

$$
L_{FP} \cdot P(C_0 \vert x) \le L_{FN} \cdot P(C_1 \vert x)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Posteriors
Replace $P(C_0 \vert x) = 1 - P(C_1 \vert x)$:

$$
L_{FP} \cdot \bigl(1 - P(C_1 \vert x)\bigr) \le L_{FN} \cdot P(C_1 \vert x)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Posterior
Expand and isolate $P(C_1 \vert x)$:

$$
L_{FP} \le L_{FP} \cdot P(C_1 \vert x) + L_{FN} \cdot P(C_1 \vert x)
$$

$$
L_{FP} \le \bigl(L_{FP} + L_{FN}\bigr) \cdot P(C_1 \vert x)
$$

$$
P(C_1 \vert x) \ge \frac{L_{FP}}{L_{FP} + L_{FN}}
$$

### Step 6 — Express Threshold in Terms of Likelihood Ratio
Using $\Lambda(x) = P(x \vert C_1) / P(x \vert C_0)$ and $\pi_1 / \pi_0$, the decision rule becomes:

$$
\Lambda(x) \ge \frac{L_{FP}}{L_{FN}} \cdot \frac{\pi_0}{\pi_1} \;\; \equiv \;\; \Lambda^*
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a CPS, $L_{FN} \gg L_{FP}$ because a missed attack may cause a physical disaster, whereas blocking a legitimate packet is merely a service degradation. Therefore $\Lambda^*$ is **small**, making the IPS **aggressive** (high sensitivity, slightly higher false-positive rate). This is the exact opposite of, say, an e-commerce IDS, where false positives annoy customers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — F1-Score Maximization for IPS Tuning

**Problem Context:** An IPS was tested on 10,000 flows with the following confusion matrix entries: $TP = 950$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 50$, $TN = 8970$. Compute the F1-Score and explain its significance.

### Step 1 — Compute Precision (PPV)
Substitute into the formula:

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{950}{950 + 30} = \frac{950}{980}
$$

$$
PPV \approx 0.9694
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Recall (TPR)
Substitute into the formula:

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = \frac{950}{1000}
$$

$$
TPR = 0.9500
$$

### Step 3 — Compute F1-Score
Apply the harmonic mean:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9694 \times 0.9500}{0.9694 + 0.9500}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.9694 \times 0.9500 = 0.92093
$$

Denominator:

$$
0.9694 + 0.9500 = 1.9194
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.92093}{1.9194} = \frac{1.84186}{1.9194} \approx 0.9596
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
An $F_1 \approx 0.96$ is **excellent** for an industrial CPS IPS, indicating balanced precision and recall. The $3.05\%$ false-positive rate is acceptable if the cost of a missed attack (e.g., turbine overspeed) is high.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Entropy-Based Payload Anomaly Detection

**Problem Context:** A CPS uses Modbus/TCP. The 8 function-code bytes in legitimate traffic follow the distribution $P(c) = \{0.40, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10, 0.05, 0.03, 0.01, 0.01\}$. An observed traffic burst has $P'(c) = \{0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.05\}$. Use Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to detect drift.

### Step 1 — Compute Baseline Entropy $H(P)$

$$
H(P) = -\sum_{i=1}^{8} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

$$
H(P) = -(0.40\log_2 0.40 + 0.25\log_2 0.25 + 0.15\log_2 0.15 + 0.10\log_2 0.10 + 0.05\log_2 0.05 + 0.03\log_2 0.03 + 0.01\log_2 0.01 + 0.01\log_2 0.01)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
0.40\log_2 0.40 \approx 0.5288, \quad 0.25\log_2 0.25 = 0.5000, \quad 0.15\log_2 0.15 \approx 0.4105
$$

$$
0.10\log_2 0.10 \approx 0.3322, \quad 0.05\log_2 0.05 \approx 0.2161, \quad 0.03\log_2 0.03 \approx 0.1518
$$

$$
0.01\log_2 0.01 \approx 0.0664 \;\; (\times 2 = 0.1328)
$$

Sum:

$$
H(P) \approx 0.5288 + 0.5000 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 + 0.2161 + 0.1518 + 0.1328
$$

$$
H(P) \approx 2.2722 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Observed Entropy $H(P')$

$$
H(P') = -\sum_{i=1}^{8} p'_i \log_2 p'_i
$$

By symmetry, the first four terms each contribute $0.15 \log_2 0.15 \approx 0.4105$, summed to $4 \times 0.4105 = 1.6420$.

Remaining terms:

$$
0.15\log_2 0.15 \approx 0.4105, \quad 0.10\log_2 0.10 \approx 0.3322, \quad 0.05\log_2 0.05 \approx 0.2161
$$

Wait — the distribution sums to 1.0, so the first five entries of $P'$ are $0.15$ each, totaling $0.75$. Then $0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.25$. Recheck:

$$
5 \times 0.15 = 0.75, \quad 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.25 \;\;\Rightarrow \text{sum} = 1.0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

Recompute:

$$
H(P') = 5 \times 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05
$$

Wait, $P'$ has 8 entries: $\{0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.05\}$. Sum = $0.75 + 0.25 = 1.0$ ✓

$$
H(P') = - \bigl(5 \times 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 2 \times 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05\bigr)
$$

$$
= - \bigl(0.75 \times (-2.7369) + 0.20 \times (-3.3219) + 0.05 \times (-4.3219)\bigr)
$$

$$
= 2.0527 + 0.6644 + 0.2161 \approx 2.9332 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute KL Divergence

$$
D_{KL}(P' \Vert P) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} p'_i \log_2 \frac{p'_i}{p_i}
$$

Term by term (using natural log then convert / using $\log_2$):

For $i=1$: $0.15 \log_2(0.15/0.40) = 0.15 \log_2(0.375) = 0.15 \times (-1.4150) = -0.2123$
For $i=2$: $0.15 \log_2(0.15/0.25) = 0.15 \log_2(0.6) = 0.15 \times (-0.7370) = -0.1105$
For $i=3$: $0.15 \log_2(0.15/0.15) = 0.15 \times 0 = 0$
For $i=4$: $0.15 \log_2(0.15/0.10) = 0.15 \log_2(1.5) = 0.15 \times 0.5850 = 0.0878$
For $i=5$: $0.15 \log_2(0.15/0.05) = 0.15 \log_2(3) = 0.15 \times 1.5850 = 0.2378$
For $i=6$: $0.10 \log_2(0.10/0.03) = 0.10 \log_2(3.333) = 0.10 \times 1.7370 = 0.1737$
For $i=7$: $0.10 \log_2(0.10/0.01) = 0.10 \log_2(10) = 0.10 \times 3.3219 = 0.3322$
For $i=8$: $0.05 \log_2(0.05/0.01) = 0.05 \log_2(5) = 0.05 \times 2.3219 = 0.1161$

Summing all terms:

$$
D_{KL}(P' \Vert P) = -0.2123 - 0.1105 + 0 + 0.0878 + 0.2378 + 0.1737 + 0.3322 + 0.1161
$$

$$
D_{KL}(P' \Vert P) \approx 0.6248 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision
A $D_{KL} > 0.5$ bits is statistically significant. The IPS raises an **anomaly alert** and switches the affected Modbus session to a honeypot replica for further forensic study.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KL and not just entropy?** Entropy measures *uncertainty*; KL divergence measures *drift from a baseline*. CPS protocols often have *low* entropy normally (predictable), so absolute entropy is a poor alarm. **Drift** is the actionable signal.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Risk Score Computation for CPS Asset

**Problem Context:** A substation SCADA gateway has Threat likelihood $T = 0.6$ (per annum), Vulnerability score $V = 0.4$ (CVSS-normalized), Impact $I = 0.9$ (catastrophic — substation blackout). Compute the annualized risk.

### Step 1 — Substitute into Risk Formula

$$
R = T \times V \times I
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate

$$
R = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.9
$$

$$
= 0.24 \times 0.9 = 0.216
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
A risk score of $0.216$ is **HIGH** (threshold $0.10$). The IPS must enforce **mandatory inline blocking** for this asset and the operator should be alerted within $1$ s of any anomaly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Advanced CPS-IPS

The following Mermaid diagram presents the complete functional architecture of an Advanced Intrusion Prevention System tailored for Cyber-Physical Systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE["LAYER 1: SENSE"]
        node1["CPS Field Sensors\nRTU / PLC / Smart Meter"]
        node2["Industrial Bus Traffic\nModbus, DNP3, IEC 61850"]
        node3["Network TAPs\nInline packet capture"]
    end

    subgraph INGEST["LAYER 2: INGEST AND NORMALIZE"]
        node4["Protocol Decoder\nLayer 2-7 parsing"]
        node5["Time Sync Engine\nIEEE 1588 PTP"]
        node6["Feature Extractor\nFlow statistics, payload vectors"]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["LAYER 3: MULTI-ENGINE INSPECTION"]
        node7["Signature Engine\nAho-Corasick matching"]
        node8["Anomaly Engine\nAutoencoder / One-Class SVM"]
        node9["Stateful Protocol Engine\nModbus state machine"]
        node10["Specification Engine\nPhysical invariant check"]
    end

    subgraph FUSE["LAYER 4: DECISION FUSION"]
        node11["Bayesian Fusion Node\nPosterior probability calculation"]
        node12["Policy Engine\nThreshold, whitelist, risk score"]
    end

    subgraph RESP["LAYER 5: INLINE RESPONSE"]
        node13["DROP Engine\nSilently discard packets"]
        node14["REROUTE Engine\nSDN flow redirection to honeypot"]
        node15["QUARANTINE Engine\nIsolate compromised node"]
        node16["ALERT Engine\nNotify SCADA operator dashboard"]
    end

    subgraph FEED["LAYER 6: LEARNING FEEDBACK"]
        node17["Model Update Pipeline\nOnline learning, federated updates"]
        node18["Threat Intel Feed\nSTIX, TAXII, CVE database"]
    end

    node1 --> node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
    node6 --> node10
    node7 --> node11
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node11
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node12 --> node14
    node12 --> node15
    node12 --> node16
    node13 --> node17
    node14 --> node17
    node15 --> node17
    node16 --> node17
    node17 --> node7
    node17 --> node8
    node18 --> node7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote data flow. The bidirectional arrow between `node17` and engines 7 and 8 indicates **online model retraining** — the heart of an "advanced" IPS that adapts to novel CPS attacks (e.g., zero-day PLC re-programming attempts).

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Inline IPS Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inbound Packet] --> B{Protocol Decode OK?}
    B -- No --> B1[DROP and Log]
    B -- Yes --> C{Signature Match?}
    C -- Yes --> D[BLOCK and ALERT]
    C -- No --> E{Anomaly Score above threshold?}
    E -- Yes --> F{Specification Violation?}
    E -- No --> A2[ALLOW]
    F -- Yes --> G[QUARANTINE Host]
    F -- No --> H{Risk Score high?}
    H -- Yes --> I[REROUTE to Honeypot]
    H -- No --> J[MONITOR with elevated logging]
```

## 4.3 Honeypot / Moving-Target Defense (MTD) Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REAL["REAL CPS ZONE"]
        realPLC["Real PLC\nIP 10.0.0.5"]
    end

    subgraph DECOY["DECOY HONEYPOT ZONE"]
        decoyPLC["Decoy PLC\nIP 10.0.0.55"]
        monitor["Trap Monitor\nLogs attacker TTPs"]
    end

    subgraph MTD["MTD CONTROLLER"]
        mutator["Address Mutator\nRandomizes IPs and ports"]
        scheduler["Rotation Scheduler\nPeriodic topology shuffle"]
    end

    attacker["Adversary"] -. attempts scan .-> decoyPLC
    decoyPLC --> monitor
    monitor --> mutator
    mutator --> realPLC
    scheduler --> mutator
    realPLC --> mutator
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The MTD controller continuously mutates the network's address space and port bindings, making the *real* asset "invisible" to the adversary's reconnaissance. The honeypot is the *visible* bait.

## 4.4 Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0[Raw Ethernet Frame] --> p1[L2 Header Strip]
    p1 --> p2[IP Reassembly]
    p2 --> p3[TCP Stream Reconstruction]
    p3 --> p4[Application Layer Decode\nModbus Function Code]
    p4 --> p5{Payload matches\nattack signature?}
    p5 -- Yes --> p6[Inline DROP]
    p5 -- No --> p7[Forward to destination]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). Why is IPS preferred in safety-critical Cyber-Physical Systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Passive (out-of-band) | Active (in-line) |
| Action on detection | Generates alert only | Automatically blocks traffic |
| Latency tolerance | Seconds acceptable | Sub-millisecond required |
| CPS Suitability | Forensic / supervisory | Real-time preventive |

**Why preferred in CPS:** CPS environments (power grids, autonomous vehicles, medical devices) involve physical processes where any successful intrusion can cause *physical harm, equipment damage, or loss of life*. An IPS prevents the attack in real time before it reaches the actuator or controller, whereas an IDS only reports after damage may have occurred. Hence, **IPS is mandatory** for safety-critical CPS.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) in an Advanced IPS. How does it differ from traditional stateful firewall filtering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- DPI inspects the **payload (Layer 7 content)** of packets, not just headers. It reconstructs full TCP/UDP streams and parses application-protocol fields (e.g., Modbus function codes, HTTP URIs, DNS queries) to detect application-layer attacks such as buffer-overflow exploits, command injection, and protocol abuse.
- A **traditional stateful firewall** only tracks connection state (SYN, ACK, FIN) and applies port-based or IP-based rules, without understanding the semantic content of the payload.
- DPI enables the IPS to detect **zero-day application attacks** embedded in legitimate-looking flows, making it indispensable for CPS protocols like DNP3 and IEC 61850 where header-only inspection is insufficient.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the architecture of an Advanced Intrusion Prevention System for Cyber-Physical Systems. Explain the role of each block. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CPS-IPS was tested on $5,000$ network flows. The following confusion matrix was obtained: $TP = 460$, $FP = 25$, $FN = 40$, $TN = 4475$. Compute the Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Accuracy, and False Positive Rate. Comment on the suitability of the IPS for a hard real-time SCADA environment. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The Advanced IPS architecture for CPS consists of the following blocks (refer to SECTION 4 Diagram 4.1):

1. **Sense Block** — Collects traffic from CPS field devices (RTUs, PLCs, smart meters) using network TAPs / SPAN ports. This block provides raw visibility into the cyber-physical traffic.

2. **Ingest and Normalize Block** — Decodes protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850), synchronizes time using IEEE 1588 PTP, and extracts statistical features (packet rate, byte entropy, inter-arrival time) for downstream engines.

3. **Multi-Engine Inspection Block** — Four parallel engines:
   - *Signature Engine:* matches known attack patterns (Snort/Suricata rules).
   - *Anomaly Engine:* detects statistical deviations using autoencoders or one-class SVM.
   - *Stateful Protocol Engine:* validates the protocol's state-machine legality.
   - *Specification Engine:* checks physical invariants (e.g., pump pressure $\in [0, 10]$ bar).

4. **Decision Fusion Block** — Combines engine outputs using Bayesian inference to produce a calibrated probability of attack.

5. **Inline Response Block** — Executes DROP, REROUTE, QUARANTINE, or ALERT actions within millisecond latency.

6. **Learning Feedback Block** — Continuously updates models using online learning, federated updates, and threat-intelligence feeds (STIX/TAXII).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This layered, self-learning architecture is essential for CPS because attacks can target the *physical process*, not just the IT layer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute Precision (PPV):**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{460}{460 + 25} = \frac{460}{485} \approx 0.9485
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall (TPR):**

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{460}{460 + 40} = \frac{460}{500} = 0.9200
$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9485 \times 0.9200}{0.9485 + 0.9200}
$$

Numerator: $0.9485 \times 0.9200 = 0.87262$

Denominator: $0.9485 + 0.9200 = 1.8685$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.87262}{1.8685} = \frac{1.74524}{1.8685} \approx 0.9340
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$
ACC = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{460 + 4475}{5000} = \frac{4935}{5000} = 0.9870
$$

**Step 5 — Compute False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{25}{25 + 4475} = \frac{25}{4500} \approx 0.0056
$$

**Suitability Comment for SCADA:** With $F_1 \approx 0.934$, accuracy $98.7\%$, and $FPR = 0.56\%$, the IPS demonstrates strong detection capability. However, the $5.6$ per-thousand false-positive rate means about $5$ legitimate SCADA commands per $1,000$ may be incorrectly blocked. For **hard real-time** SCADA this is borderline — operators may experience alarm fatigue and missed control actions. The IPS is **acceptable for supervisory SCADA** (sub-second tolerance) but **not yet ideal for hard real-time** (sub-millisecond) closed loops. Tuning the threshold via Bayesian risk minimization (as in SECTION 3.1) is recommended to lower FPR.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of Moving Target Defense (MTD) and Honeypots as proactive intrusion prevention strategies in CPS. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using Bayesian decision theory, derive the optimal likelihood-ratio threshold for an IPS in a CPS where the cost of a missed attack ($L_{FN}$) is $100$ times the cost of a false positive ($L_{FP}$). The prior probability of attack is $P(C_1) = 0.05$. State the decision rule clearly. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Moving Target Defense (MTD) (3.5 marks):**
MTD is a *proactive* cyber-defence paradigm that continuously changes the attack surface (e.g., IP addresses, port numbers, instruction-set randomization, virtual machine migration) to invalidate the adversary's reconnaissance and exploit preparation. In CPS, MTD may rotate IP addresses of PLCs every few seconds, shuffle communication port bindings, or randomize memory layouts in RTUs. Because the attacker cannot pin down a stable target, the probability of successful exploitation is drastically reduced.

**Honeypots and Honeynets (3.5 marks):**
Honeypots are *decoy* CPS components (e.g., a fake Modbus server, a dummy HMI workstation) deployed alongside real assets. They have no production value; any interaction with them is, by definition, suspicious. A *honeynet* is an interconnected cluster of honeypots simulating an entire CPS sub-system. When the adversary probes or attacks the honeypot, the IPS records the attacker's Tools, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) without risking the real system, and uses the captured intelligence to update its signature and anomaly engines.

**Combined Effect:** MTD *denies* reconnaissance; honeypots *detect and learn* from residual intrusions. Together they shift the defensive advantage from the attacker to the defender — the cornerstone of *proactive* intrusion prevention.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

From SECTION 3.1, the optimal decision threshold is:

$$
\Lambda^* = \frac{L_{FP}}{L_{FN}} \cdot \frac{\pi_0}{\pi_1}
$$

**Given:**
- $L_{FN} = 100 \cdot L_{FP}$, so $\dfrac{L_{FP}}{L_{FN}} = \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.01$
- $P(C_1) = \pi_1 = 0.05$
- $P(C_0) = \pi_0 = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$

**Substitute:**

$$
\Lambda^* = 0.01 \cdot \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 0.01 \cdot 19 = 0.19
$$

**Decision Rule:**

The IPS should declare an attack and execute its blocking action whenever:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{P(x \vert C_1)}{P(x \vert C_0)} \ge 0.19
$$

Equivalently, posterior probability:

$$
P(C_1 \vert x) \ge \frac{0.01}{0.01 + 1} = \frac{0.01}{1.01} \approx 0.0099
$$

i.e., the IPS should block the flow as soon as the posterior probability of attack exceeds **$0.99\%$**.

**Interpretation:** Because the cost of a missed attack dominates ($100:1$), the IPS adopts an *aggressive* posture — it blocks traffic even on weak evidence of maliciousness. This is appropriate for a CPS where a missed attack could cause physical harm. The trade-off is a marginally higher false-positive rate, which is acceptable compared to the catastrophic consequence of a false negative.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS and IPS** — Students often treat them as synonyms. Always emphasize that IPS is *in-line* and *blocking*; IDS is *passive* and *alerts only*. Loss of 1–2 marks is common.
> 2. **Forgetting the "C" in CPS** — Generic network security answers will be marked down. Always link prevention to *physical process safety* (turbine overspeed, pump overpressure, vehicle collision).
> 3. **Skipping the math in Part B** — Numerical derivations **must** show all algebraic steps. A bare formula with the final number will lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the threshold of $\Lambda^*$** — Students often forget that the threshold depends on both **loss ratio** and **prior probability**, and present it as a fixed constant. Always derive it from first principles.
> 5. **Forgetting the bidirectional learning loop** — A common 1-mark loss is describing the IPS as static. Always mention online / federated learning and threat-intel feed integration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Advanced Intrusion Prevention**

- **IPS vs IDS:** IPS is *in-line + automatic blocking*; IDS is *passive + alerting only*. CPS safety demands IPS.
- **Four detection paradigms:** Signature (known-pattern), Anomaly (behavioural), Stateful Protocol (state-machine), Specification (physical-invariant).
- **Two-line deployment:** NIPS (network choke-points) and HIPS (on RTU/PLC endpoints); modern CPS uses both in a **Hybrid / Distributed IPS**.
- **DPI is Layer-7 inspection** of reconstructed application streams — essential for Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850.
- **Bayes optimal threshold:** $\Lambda^* = \dfrac{L_{FP}}{L_{FN}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi_0}{\pi_1}$. Higher $L_{FN}/L_{FP}$ ⇒ lower $\Lambda^*$ ⇒ more aggressive IPS.
- **F1-Score is the key single metric** for imbalanced CPS data; Accuracy alone is misleading when attacks are rare (e.g., $0.5\%$ prevalence).
- **Shannon entropy** quantifies payload uncertainty; **KL divergence** detects drift from baseline — KL is the actionable signal in CPS.
- **MTD (Moving Target Defense):** Continuously mutates IPs, ports, and code layouts to deny reconnaissance.
- **Honeypots / Honeynets:** Decoy CPS nodes that capture attacker TTPs and feed intel back to the IPS.
- **SDN-based IPS:** Decouples control plane from data plane, enabling rapid re-routing of malicious flows to scrubbers or honeypots.
- **Online / Federated Learning:** Models update continuously from operational data; multiple CPS sites share learning without exposing raw data.
- **Latency benchmarks:** $\le 1\text{ ms}$ detection + $\le 1\text{ s}$ response for hard real-time CPS loops.
- **Risk formula:** $R = T \times V \times I$ is used to prioritize which CPS assets warrant the most aggressive inline blocking.
- **Common confusion to avoid:** *Zero Trust* is a *network architecture*; IPS is a *defence mechanism*. They are complementary — Zero Trust limits lateral movement, IPS stops active intrusions.
- **CPS-specific tip:** Always validate that the IPS itself does not become an attack surface (e.g., a compromised IPS could *re-route* legitimate control traffic). Secure-boot and signed firmware are mandatory.
- **Examiner mantra:** "If your answer doesn't mention *real-time, inline, and CPS-physical-impact*, you have not answered the question."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Threat Mitigation and Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Real-Time Threat Mitigation and Response ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Real-Time Threat Mitigation and Response (RTMR)** is the integrated, time-constrained capability within a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) security architecture that enables the continuous monitoring, correlation, prioritization, containment, and remediation of cyber threats as they materialize across the Operational Technology (OT), Information Technology (IT), and Internet of Things (IoT) planes. It synthesizes **continuous telemetry ingestion**, **behavioral analytics**, **orchestrated playbooks**, and **closed-loop automated actions** to reduce the dwell time between compromise and containment. Formally, RTMR is governed by the response SLA: $$ T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{triage}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{contain}} \leq T_{\text{SLA}} $$ where $T_{\text{SLA}}$ is the maximum permissible Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) defined by the system's safety integrity level (e.g., IEC 62443 SL-2/SL-3 mandates seconds-to-minutes response for critical zones). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RTMR in CPS is fundamentally different from classical IT incident response because **safety-of-life and physical-process continuity** are first-class constraints. A response action that is "correct" in IT (e.g., isolating a host) may be **unsafe** in CPS if it triggers an unsafe process shutdown. --- ### 1.2 Core Sub-Disciplines of RTMR (The Six Pillars) | # | Pillar | Functional Role | |---|--------|-----------------| | 1 | **Continuous Monitoring (CM)** | Stream-based ingestion of logs, packets, sensor flows | | 2 | **Threat Detection & Analytics (TDA)** | Signature, anomaly, and behavioral correlation | | 3 | **Threat Intelligence (TI)** | Contextual enrichment via STIX/TAXII feeds | | 4 | **Orchestration & Automation (SOAR)** | Playbook-driven response workflows | | 5 | **Incident Response (IR)** | Human-in-the-loop containment, eradication, recovery | | 6 | **Post-Incident Learning (PIL)** | Root-cause analysis, lessons learned, tuning | --- ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Department (ED):** > Imagine a CPS environment as a large hospital with hundreds of patients (PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, servers). Real-Time Threat Mitigation is the **hospital's Emergency Department**: > > - **Triage Nurses (SIEM/EDR)** constantly monitor vitals (telemetry) and flag anomalies. > - **Lab Tests (Sandboxing & Analytics)** confirm whether the symptom is a real infection (malware) or a false alarm. > - **Specialists (SOAR Playbooks)** decide on a treatment protocol (block IP, isolate device, kill process). > - **ICU (Safety System)** ensures that no treatment accidentally stops the patient's heart — i.e., no response action can violate a safety constraint (Safety Instrumented Function). > > The "real-time" promise means the time from *patient enters ED* to *treatment begins* must be **less than the time the infection becomes fatal** (analogous to $T_{\text{response}} < T_{\text{damage}}$). --- ### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics Used in RTMR The following **bolded** metrics are universally evaluated by KTU examiners and must be memorized: - **MTTD — Mean Time to Detect** (target in CPS: **seconds to minutes**). - **MTTR — Mean Time to Respond/Recover** (target in CPS: **minutes**). - **MTTC — Mean Time to Contain**. - **FPR — False Positive Rate** (industrial SOC target: **< 5%**). - **FNR — False Negative Rate** (must approach **0** for safety-critical assets). - **Triage SLA — Service Level Agreement** on detection-to-containment latency. - **Playbook Coverage — % of alert types with a defined automated response**. - **Dwell Time — duration an adversary remains undetected** (industry median ~21 days, CPS target: **< 1 hour**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Anchor:** Whenever a CPS exam question mentions "real-time," immediately translate it to **bounded-latency** and **safety-preserving** automated action. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The RTMR Operational Loop (Five-Phase Closed Loop) The classical RTMR cycle is a **closed feedback loop**, not a linear pipeline: 1. **Sense (Continuous Telemetry Collection)** - Why: A response without observation is blind; CPS requires deterministic sampling of process variables (pressure, temperature, flow) *and* cyber variables (syslog, NetFlow, Modbus/TCP packets). - How: Deploy **taps, SPAN ports, agents on endpoints, passive ICS sensors** that forward events to a central correlation engine without disrupting the deterministic control loop. 2. **Detect (Event Correlation & Analytics)** - Why: A single failed login is noise; 200 failed logins in 30 seconds from one IP targeting an engineering workstation is a **brute-force attempt**. - How: Layer three detection paradigms: - **Signature-based (SNORT/YARA rules)** — fast, low FPR, but blind to zero-days. - **Anomaly-based (statistical, ML)** — catches zero-days but higher FPR. - **Behavioral/UEBA** — baselines *normal* ICS process behavior (e.g., a valve that always opens between 08:00–08:15 suddenly opens at 03:00). 3. **Decide (Triage & Playbook Selection)** - Why: With 10,000 alerts/day, a human cannot triage — automation must prioritize by **asset criticality**, **threat severity**, and **safety impact**. - How: SOAR platforms (e.g., Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR) execute deterministic **playbooks** — YAML/DAG definitions of "if alert X then action Y, with safety guard-rail Z." 4. **Act (Containment, Eradication, Recovery)** - Why: The threat must be **stopped from propagating** before it reaches the Safety Instrumented System (SIS). - How: Response actions are graded: - **Soft actions:** rate-limit, quarantine VLAN, kill process, revoke token. - **Hard actions:** physical disconnect, breaker open, emergency shutdown (last resort, requires safety interlock). 5. **Reflect (Post-Incident Analysis)** - Why: Each incident is a tuning opportunity — detection rules, playbook branches, and asset inventories must be updated. - How: Generate an **incident report** mapped to **NIST SP 800-61** phases; feed IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) back to TI platform. --- ### 2.2 RTMR Architecture Components ``` [ Sensors / Agents ] → [ Ingestion Bus ] → [ SIEM ] ↔ [ SOAR ] ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ Telemetry Normalization Correlation Playbooks ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ (Syslog, NetFlow, (CEF/LEEF, (Rules, ML, (API calls, ICS protocol taps, STIX) UEBA) firewall ACL, process vars) PLC block) ↓ [ Safety Guard-Rail ] (verifies no unsafe process action) ``` Key components in CPS-RTMR: - **SIEM (Security Information & Event Management):** correlation engine. Examples: Splunk, QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel. - **EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response):** host-level telemetry. Examples: CrowdStrike, SentinelOne. - **NDR (Network Detection & Response):** packet-level visibility. Examples: Darktrace, Vectra. - **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response):** playbook execution. Examples: XSOAR, Swimlane. - **TI Platform:** STIX/TAXII-based threat intelligence sharing. - **Safety Guard-Rail (CPS-Specific):** a deterministic filter that blocks any response action violating SIS constraints. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for KTU viva and written exams on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Target | |---|---------|----------------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | **MTTR Definition** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{\text{resolved},i} - T_{\text{detected},i})$ | Average response time across $N$ incidents | minutes (CPS target) | | 2 | **MTTD Definition** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{\text{detected},i} - T_{\text{occurred},i})$ | Average detection latency | seconds (CPS target) | | 3 | **Dwell Time** | $D = T_{\text{detected}} - T_{\text{initial\_compromise}}$ | Time adversary is inside system | hours (CPS target < 1h) | | 4 | **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Non-malicious events flagged | dimensionless, target < 0.05 | | 5 | **False Negative Rate** | $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Missed true positives | dimensionless, target → 0 | | 6 | **Detection Coverage** | $C_{\text{det}} = \frac{\vert \text{detected TTPs} \vert}{\vert \text{MITRE ATT\&CK for ICS} \vert}$ | % of ICS attack techniques observable | dimensionless | | 7 | **Playbook Coverage** | $C_{\text{pb}} = \frac{\vert \text{alert types with playbook} \vert}{\vert \text{total alert types} \vert}$ | Automation readiness | dimensionless, target > 0.9 | | 8 | **Risk Priority (per asset)** | $R_i = \text{Criticality}_i \times \text{Exploitability}_i \times \text{Threat}_i$ | Drives triage ordering | numeric score | | 9 | **Response SLA Constraint** | $T_{\text{response}} \leq T_{\text{damage threshold}}$ | Fundamental safety bound | seconds (SL-3 → 30 s) | | 10 | **Sample Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | Minimum telemetry sampling rate | Hz | | 11 | **Alert Fatigue Index** | $\text{AFI} = \frac{\text{alerts/day}}{\text{analysts} \times 8}$ | Workload per analyst-hour | alerts/person-hour | | 12 | **Mean Time Between Failures (system)** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Availability metric | hours | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In exam answers, students often write MTTR as "Mean Time To Recover" *without* stating it is measured **from detection, not from compromise**. MTTR is exclusively **T_resolved − T_detected**. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of RTMR in Engineering - **Smart Grid:** Auto-isolates a compromised substation controller within 5 s to prevent cascading blackouts. - **Connected Vehicles:** Detects CAN bus injection attacks and switches ECU to degraded mode in real-time. - **Industrial Robotics:** Detects malicious PLC logic upload → reverts to last known-good program within 100 ms. - **Healthcare CPS (infusion pumps):** Detects unauthorized firmware change → halts dosing before over-infusion occurs. - **Critical Manufacturing (semiconductor fabs):** Detects exfiltration of process recipes → quarantines MES host without halting wafer production. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) Calculation **Problem Setup:** A CPS experienced three incidents in one quarter. Incident data: - Incident A: detected at 10:02:15, resolved at 10:14:30. - Incident B: detected at 14:30:00, resolved at 14:47:00. - Incident C: detected at 02:10:00, resolved at 02:11:45. **Step 1 — Convert each timestamp to total seconds.** Incident A: - $T_{\text{detected},A} = 10 \times 3600 + 2 \times 60 + 15 = 36000 + 120 + 15 = 36135$ s - $T_{\text{resolved},A} = 10 \times 3600 + 14 \times 60 + 30 = 36000 + 840 + 30 = 36870$ s Incident B: - $T_{\text{detected},B} = 14 \times 3600 + 30 \times 60 + 0 = 50400 + 1800 + 0 = 52200$ s - $T_{\text{resolved},B} = 14 \times 3600 + 47 \times 60 + 0 = 50400 + 2820 + 0 = 53220$ s Incident C: - $T_{\text{detected},C} = 2 \times 3600 + 10 \times 60 + 0 = 7200 + 600 + 0 = 7800$ s - $T_{\text{resolved},C} = 2 \times 3600 + 11 \times 60 + 45 = 7200 + 660 + 45 = 7905$ s **Step 2 — Compute individual response durations.** $\Delta T_A = 36870 - 36135 = 735$ s $$ \Delta T_A = 36870 - 36135 = 735 \text{ s} $$ $\Delta T_B = 53220 - 52200 = 1020$ s $$ \Delta T_B = 53220 - 52200 = 1020 \text{ s} $$ $\Delta T_C = 7905 - 7800 = 105$ s $$ \Delta T_C = 7905 - 7800 = 105 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the MTTR formula.** $$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta T_i = \frac{735 + 1020 + 105}{3} $$ $$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{1860}{3} = 620 \text{ s} \approx 10.33 \text{ min} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The system achieves MTTR ≈ 10.3 minutes. For an IEC 62443 SL-3 zone, the target is **< 5 minutes**. Therefore, **automated containment playbooks are required** to bring MTTR below the safety threshold. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Risk Priority Score for Triage Ordering **Problem Setup:** Three alerts arrive simultaneously at the SOC: - Alert X: PLC programming software uploading new ladder logic from an engineering workstation at 03:00. - Alert Y: Failed SSH logins (×25) on a corporate file server. - Alert Z: Outbound DNS traffic spike from an HMI to an unknown external IP. **Asset criticality scores (1–10):** - PLC programming workstation: 10 (touches safety-critical logic). - File server: 3. - HMI: 9. **Threat severity scores (CVSS-like 1–10):** - X: 9 (unauthorized logic change). - Y: 4 (brute force, not yet successful). - Z: 8 (possible C2 beaconing). **Step 1 — Apply risk formula** $R_i = \text{Criticality}_i \times \text{Threat}_i$ (Exploitability = 1 for active observation). $$ R_X = 10 \times 9 = 90 $$ $$ R_Y = 3 \times 4 = 12 $$ $$ R_Z = 9 \times 8 = 72 $$ **Step 2 — Triage order** (highest → lowest): **X (90) → Z (72) → Y (12).** **Step 3 — SOAR playbook selection.** - X triggers playbook `PB-PLC-LOCKDOWN`: read-only mode + require dual-control approval + notify safety officer. - Z triggers playbook `PB-NETWORK-QUARANTINE`: isolate HMI to VLAN 100 + collect packet capture. - Y triggers playbook `PB-ACCOUNT-LOCK`: temporary lock + IP blocklist + 24-h watchlist. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Detection Coverage Against MITRE ATT&CK for ICS **Problem Setup:** A CPS has 8 deployed detection sensors covering the following ATT&CK for ICS techniques: - T0806 (Brute Force I/O), T0832 (Manipulation of View), T0855 (Unauthorized Command Message), T0836 (Modify Parameter), T0804 (Block Reporting Message), T0809 (Data Destruction), T0866 (Exploitation of Remote Services), T0830 (Adversary-in-the-Middle). Total ATT&CK for ICS techniques (as of ATT&CK v14): **81 techniques**. **Step 1 — Apply coverage formula.** $$ C_{\text{det}} = \frac{\vert \text{detected TTPs} \vert}{\vert \text{MITRE ATT\&CK for ICS} \vert} = \frac{8}{81} $$ $$ C_{\text{det}} \approx 0.0988 \text{ or } 9.88\% $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation.** Coverage is critically low (< 10%). The CPS is exposed to ~91% of catalogued ICS attack techniques. A remediation roadmap must prioritize **T0836 (Modify Parameter)** and **T0855 (Unauthorized Command Message)** — the highest-impact remaining gaps. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detection vs. Response Decision Tree | Observation Pattern | Detection Method | Confidence | Response Action | Safety Guard-Rail | |---------------------|------------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------| | Failed logins ×50 in 60s | Signature | High | Rate-limit + lock account | None needed | | PLC param change at unusual hour | Behavioral (UEBA) | Medium-High | Snapshot config + require approval | Validate against SIS constraints | | Outbound traffic to TOR exit node | TI feed match | High | Block + capture + alert SOC | None needed | | Process variable drift outside norm | Anomaly (statistical) | Medium | Notify operator only | **DO NOT auto-shut** — human decides | | Repeated Modbus writes to safety relay | Signature + TI | Very High | **Isolate engineering station + raise safety alarm** | Trigger independent SIS path | > [!IMPORTANT] > The last row is the **CPS-distinctive case**: response action **must** include a parallel safe-state path because the adversary may have compromised both the controller and the SOC visibility. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CPS Real-Time Threat Mitigation — End-to-End Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[ICS Sensor: Modbus Tap] A2[Endpoint Agent: EDR on HMI] A3[Network Sensor: NDR SPAN] A4[Syslog: Firewall and Router] A5[Process Historian] B[Ingestion Bus: Kafka or Syslog-ng] C1[SIEM Correlation Engine] C2[Threat Intelligence Platform: STIX TAXII] C3[Asset Inventory and CMDB] D{SOC Triage} E1[SOAR Playbook Engine] E2[Human Analyst Console] F1[Safety Guard-Rail Filter] F2[Response Action Bus] G1[Soft Response: Quarantine VLAN] G2[Hard Response: Block Port] G3[PLC Action: Read Only Mode] G4[Breaker: Controlled Shutdown] H[Safety Instrumented System: Independent Path] A1 --> B A2 --> B A3 --> B A4 --> B A5 --> B B --> C1 C2 --> C1 C3 --> C1 C1 --> D D -->|Low Severity| E2 D -->|High Severity| E1 E1 --> F1 F1 -->|Safety OK| F2 F1 -->|Safety Violation| H F2 --> G1 F2 --> G2 F2 --> G3 F2 --> G4 H --> G4 ``` **Reading Guide:** - Left cluster (A1–A5) = **Sense** layer. - Center (B, C1–C3) = **Detect/Decide** layer. - Right cluster (E1, F1, F2, G1–G4, H) = **Act** layer. - The diamond `D{SOC Triage}` represents the **Decide** node. - The arrow from `F1` to `H` is the **safety interlock** — the CPS-specific differentiator. --- ### 4.2 RTMR Detection-to-Response Sequence (Incident Lifecycle) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as Sensor or Agent participant SIEM as SIEM participant SOAR as SOAR Engine participant Guard as Safety Guard-Rail participant Asset as OT Asset participant SOC as SOC Analyst participant SIS as Safety System Sensor->>SIEM: Telemetry Event SIEM->>SIEM: Correlate and Score SIEM->>SOAR: Alert with Risk Score SOAR->>Guard: Proposed Action Guard->>Guard: Check against Safety Policy alt Safety Policy PASS Guard->>Asset: Execute Soft Action Asset-->>SOAR: Action Result SOAR->>SOC: Notify with Evidence else Safety Policy FAIL Guard->>SIS: Trigger Independent Safety Path SIS->>Asset: Controlled Shutdown SOAR->>SOC: Escalate with Safety Violation Log end SOC->>SOAR: Tune Playbook and IOCs ``` --- ### 4.3 RTMR Maturity Model (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Level 1: Manual Response
No SIEM, No Playbooks] L2[Level 2: Assisted Detection
SIEM, Manual Triage] L3[Level 3: Semi-Automated
SOAR with Human Approval] L4[Level 4: Fully Automated
Closed-loop SOAR with Safety Guard-Rails] L5[Level 5: Adaptive
Self-tuning Playbooks with ML and TI feedback] L1 -->|Invest in SIEM| L2 L2 -->|Deploy Playbooks| L3 L3 -->|Add Safety Guard-Rail| L4 L4 -->|Integrate ML and TI feedback| L5 ``` > [!NOTE] > Most CPS environments operate at **Level 2** because of safety concerns. KTU expects students to argue why **Level 3** is the realistic maximum for most ICS plants, with Level 4 reserved for non-safety-critical IT zones. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between SIEM and SOAR. Why are both necessary in a CPS real-time threat mitigation pipeline?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SIEM | SOAR | |--------|------|------| | **Primary Function** | Detects and correlates security events across log sources | Orchestrates and automates response actions | | **Output** | Alerts with risk scores | Executed response actions via API calls | | **Role in RTMR** | "Eyes" — sense and detect | "Hands" — decide and act | | **CPS Example** | Correlates 200 failed logins + unusual Modbus writes into one alert | Auto-isolates the source workstation to VLAN 100 | **Why both are necessary in CPS:** A SIEM alone produces *alerts* but cannot *act*. A SOAR alone executes *actions* but lacks the *intelligence* to decide which action is appropriate. CPS requires both because **real-time containment cannot wait for human analysis**, yet the action must be **intelligence-driven and safety-verified**. SIEM provides the intelligence; SOAR provides the deterministic action layer; together they close the **Sense → Decide → Act** loop within the safety-mandated SLA. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** **Explain the role of a "Safety Guard-Rail" in CPS real-time threat response. What happens if it is omitted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Safety Guard-Rail** is a deterministic filter interposed between the SOAR decision engine and the physical response action. Its role is to **validate every proposed response action against pre-defined safety constraints** before it is executed on an OT asset. For example, if SOAR proposes to "isolate the controller of a chemical reactor," the guard-rail checks whether isolation would cause an unsafe process state (e.g., runaway pressure). If unsafe, the action is **redirected to the independent Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** which has hard-wired safety interlocks. If the guard-rail is **omitted**, an automated response intended to *contain* a cyber threat may itself become a *safety hazard* — e.g., the system could shut down a life-support device, open a pressure relief valve incorrectly, or disconnect a cooling pump, leading to physical damage or loss of life. Thus, the guard-rail is the **CPS-specific essential** that distinguishes industrial RTMR from IT RTMR. **(3 marks)** --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]** **(a)** Describe the **five-phase closed-loop RTMR cycle** in detail. For each phase, state one CPS-specific consideration that does **not** arise in classical IT security. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CPS deployed SIEM logs the following over one shift (8 hours): - Total events ingested: 4,800,000 - True positive alerts: 320 - False positive alerts: 2,180 - Missed true positives (from post-hoc forensics): 25 Calculate: - (i) False Positive Rate (FPR) - (ii) False Negative Rate (FNR) - (iii) Detection Coverage, given MITRE ATT&CK for ICS has 81 techniques and the SIEM covered 12 techniques. - (iv) Alert Fatigue Index, given 4 SOC analysts working the shift. Comment on whether the current setup meets typical CPS SL-3 targets. **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) Five-Phase RTMR Cycle with CPS-Specific Considerations (7 marks):** 1. **Sense — CPS-specific consideration:** Telemetry must include **process variables** (pressure, temperature, flow) sampled at the controller's native rate (often sub-second), *not* just cyber logs. A pure IT sensor misses physical anomalies caused by cyber tampering (e.g., a Stuxnet-style modification of centrifuge speed produces no IT-side log). 2. **Detect — CPS-specific consideration:** Detection rules must incorporate the **engineering baseline** of the physical process. A spike in Set-Point Modbus writes is anomalous in IT but normal in some maintenance windows; the SIEM must understand **operational mode context** (startup vs. steady-state vs. shutdown). 3. **Decide — CPS-specific consideration:** Triage must respect **safety integrity levels (SIL)** of assets. An alert on a SIL-3 valve controller has higher priority than on a corporate file server, even if CVSS score is lower. 4. **Act — CPS-specific consideration:** Response actions cannot include "reboot the controller" or "disconnect from network" if the physical process depends on that controller for safety. Actions are graded: **soft (log, alert, snapshot) → medium (rate-limit, VLAN move) → hard (controlled shutdown via SIS)**. 5. **Reflect — CPS-specific consideration:** Lessons-learned must be fed back into both **cyber controls and engineering procedures**. For example, after detecting unauthorized parameter changes, the engineering team may mandate **dual-approval for all PLC writes**. **(7 marks — 1 mark per phase base + 1 mark per CPS-specific consideration + integrative summary)** --- **(b) Quantitative Solution (7 marks):** **(i) False Positive Rate (FPR):** Total negatives = False Positives + True Negatives. $$ \text{TN} = \text{Total events} - \text{TP} - \text{FP} - \text{FN} = 4{,}800{,}000 - 320 - 2{,}180 - 25 = 4{,}797{,}475 $$ $$ \text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{2{,}180}{2{,}180 + 4{,}797{,}475} = \frac{2{,}180}{4{,}799{,}655} $$ $$ \text{FPR} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-4} \text{ or } 0.0454\% $$ **(1 mark)** **(ii) False Negative Rate (FNR):** $$ \text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{25}{25 + 320} = \frac{25}{345} $$ $$ \text{FNR} \approx 0.0725 \text{ or } 7.25\% $$ **(1 mark)** **(iii) Detection Coverage:** $$ C_{\text{det}} = \frac{12}{81} \approx 0.1481 \text{ or } 14.81\% $$ **(1 mark)** **(iv) Alert Fatigue Index (AFI):** Total alerts = TP + FP = 320 + 2,180 = 2,500. $$ \text{AFI} = \frac{\text{alerts/day}}{\text{analysts} \times 8} = \frac{2{,}500}{4 \times 8} = \frac{2{,}500}{32} = 78.125 \text{ alerts/person-hour} $$ **(1 mark)** **Comment on SL-3 Compliance (3 marks):** - **FPR (0.045%)** is excellent — well below the 5% target. ✔ - **FNR (7.25%)** is unacceptable for CPS SL-3 — a 7.25% miss rate on safety-relevant events could cause physical incidents. ✖ - **Detection Coverage (14.81%)** is far below the recommended 60–80% for SL-3. ✖ - **AFI (78 alerts/person-hour)** is high — industry sustainable level is ~15–20 alerts/person-hour; analyst fatigue likely. **Conclusion:** The setup meets detection *volume* expectations but **fails safety-critical targets** in FNR, coverage, and analyst workload. **Recommendation:** Deploy UEBA for process-aware anomaly detection, expand sensor coverage, and introduce SOAR playbooks to absorb alert volume. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **SOAR playbook architecture**. Describe how a playbook is structured for an alert of type *"Unauthorized PLC Logic Upload"*, listing at least **five distinct playbook steps** with their safety implications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the MTTR formula, calculate the mean response time from the following four incident timestamps. State whether the result meets IEC 62443 SL-3 target of MTTR ≤ 5 minutes. | Incident | Detected (HH:MM:SS) | Resolved (HH:MM:SS) | |----------|---------------------|---------------------| | I1 | 09:05:12 | 09:09:45 | | I2 | 11:22:00 | 11:31:30 | | I3 | 14:48:30 | 14:50:10 | | I4 | 19:15:00 | 19:25:20 | **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) SOAR Playbook Architecture & Unauthorized PLC Logic Upload Playbook (7 marks):** A **SOAR playbook** is a deterministic, graph-based workflow that maps an alert type to a sequence of automated actions, decision branches, and human-approval gates. Structure: - **Trigger:** Alert from SIEM with type = `unauthorized_plc_upload`. - **Inputs:** Asset CMDB record, source IP, user identity, time-of-day, last-good-program hash. - **Pre-conditions:** Validate alert confidence ≥ threshold. - **Action nodes:** API calls to firewall, EDR, historian, ticketing system. - **Decision nodes:** `IF asset.criticality == "SIL-3" THEN require_human_approval = true`. - **Safety guard-rail node:** `IF proposed_action_would_unsafe_shutdown THEN redirect_to_SIS`. - **Outputs:** Updated ticket, snapshot stored, notification sent, playbook execution log. **Five Playbook Steps for `unauthorized_plc_upload`:** 1. **Snapshot current PLC program** (`md5` hash stored in evidence vault) — *Safety implication:* enables rollback if upload corrupts logic. **(1 mark)** 2. **Quarantine source workstation to VLAN 100** via firewall API — *Safety implication:* prevents further uploads but does not stop the running process. **(1 mark)** 3. **Compare uploaded logic against last-known-good hash** — *Safety implication:* if mismatch, halt upload and force human review before any write. **(1 mark)** 4. **Require dual-control approval** (engineering manager + safety officer) via ticketing API — *Safety implication:* prevents a compromised single account from completing the upload. **(1 mark)** 5. **Trigger Safety Guard-Rail check:** if upload targets a SIL-3 PLC, redirect to SIS for **independent verification** before any write — *Safety implication:* ensures that even if SOAR is compromised, the SIS independently validates safety. **(1 mark)** 6. **Notify SOC + log to incident management system** with full audit trail — *Safety implication:* preserves forensic chain of custody for regulatory audit (NERC CIP, IEC 62443). **(1 mark)** 7. **Schedule post-incident review within 24 hours** — *Safety implication:* drives continuous improvement of detection rules and engineering procedure changes. **(1 mark)** *(Any 5 steps for full 5 marks; extra step for integrative summary)* --- **(b) MTTR Quantitative Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Convert to seconds and compute durations.** Incident I1: - $T_{\text{det}} = 9 \times 3600 + 5 \times 60 + 12 = 32400 + 300 + 12 = 32712$ s - $T_{\text{res}} = 9 \times 3600 + 9 \times 60 + 45 = 32400 + 540 + 45 = 32985$ s - $\Delta T_1 = 32985 - 32712 = 273$ s Incident I2: - $T_{\text{det}} = 11 \times 3600 + 22 \times 60 + 0 = 39600 + 1320 + 0 = 40920$ s - $T_{\text{res}} = 11 \times 3600 + 31 \times 60 + 30 = 39600 + 1860 + 30 = 41490$ s - $\Delta T_2 = 41490 - 40920 = 570$ s Incident I3: - $T_{\text{det}} = 14 \times 3600 + 48 \times 60 + 30 = 50400 + 2880 + 30 = 53310$ s - $T_{\text{res}} = 14 \times 3600 + 50 \times 60 + 10 = 50400 + 3000 + 10 = 53410$ s - $\Delta T_3 = 53410 - 53310 = 100$ s Incident I4: - $T_{\text{det}} = 19 \times 3600 + 15 \times 60 + 0 = 68400 + 900 + 0 = 69300$ s - $T_{\text{res}} = 19 \times 3600 + 25 \times 60 + 20 = 68400 + 1500 + 20 = 69920$ s - $\Delta T_4 = 69920 - 69300 = 620$ s **Step 2 — Apply MTTR formula.** $$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta T_i = \frac{273 + 570 + 100 + 620}{4} $$ $$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{1563}{4} = 390.75 \text{ s} \approx 6.51 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3 — Compare against IEC 62443 SL-3 target (MTTR ≤ 5 min = 300 s).** $$ 390.75 \text{ s} > 300 \text{ s} \implies \text{Target NOT met.} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation & Recommendation.** The system misses the SL-3 target by **~30%**. Two incidents (I2 and I4) individually exceeded 9 minutes — likely caused by manual handoffs in containment. To meet the target, the SOC must: - Deploy automated SOAR playbooks for the top 5 alert types. - Pre-authorize safety-verified soft responses (e.g., VLAN quarantine). - Establish a 24/7 on-call safety engineer rota for human-in-the-loop approval. **(7 marks — 1 mark setup + 2 marks computation + 2 marks comparison + 2 marks interpretation)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse MTTR with MTTD.** MTTR is *response* time (detect → resolve); MTTD is *detection* latency (compromise → detect). Examiners explicitly deduct for swapping. > 2. **In CPS, "isolate the host" is NOT always a valid response.** Always check whether isolation violates a safety constraint.
