Examiners award a +2 bonus mark for safety-guard-rail reasoning. > 3. **FPR ≠ FP count alone.** FPR is always **FP / (FP + TN)** — students frequently write FPR = FP/Total which inflates the value 1000×. > 4. **Coverage against MITRE ATT&CK for ICS (81 techniques) ≠ Enterprise ATT&CK (~200 techniques).** Mixing the two is a 2-mark deduction. > 5. **In SIEM/SOAR comparison questions, never state that SOAR "replaces" SIEM.** They are **complementary** — full 3 marks require the table-format distinction. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Anchors:** - ☐ **RTMR = Sense → Detect → Decide → Act → Reflect** (closed loop, not linear). - ☐ **MTTR is measured from *detection*, not compromise.** - ☐ **MTTD target in CPS = seconds to minutes** (not days like IT). - ☐ **FPR target < 5%, FNR → 0** for safety-critical assets. - ☐ **SIEM = eyes; SOAR = hands** — they are complementary, never substitutes. - ☐ **Safety Guard-Rail is the CPS-distinctive filter** that validates every response against SIS constraints. - ☐ **Response grading: Soft → Medium → Hard**, with hard actions only via independent SIS. - ☐ **Detection Coverage = (covered ATT&CK for ICS techniques) / 81** — *not* the Enterprise ATT&CK number. - ☐ **Risk Score for triage = Criticality × Threat × Exploitability**. - ☐ **Playbook Coverage = (alert types with playbook) / (total alert types)** — target > 90%. - ☐ **CPS response must never initiate an unsafe physical action**, even if the action is "correct" in IT. - ☐ **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS v14 has 81 techniques** — use this constant in all numerical coverage questions.

topics.title: Effective Intrusion Prevention in High-Stakes CPS Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effective Intrusion Prevention in High-Stakes CPS Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Effective Intrusion Prevention in High-Stakes Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) Environments** refers to the systematic, multi-layered, and context-aware deployment of proactive security controls designed to **detect, deny, and disrupt** unauthorized access, command injection, data exfiltration, or physical-state manipulation attempts targeting interconnected computational (cyber) and physical process (physical) subsystems where the consequences of failure involve loss of life, environmental harm, national security compromise, or significant economic disruption.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** and **IEC 62443**, intrusion prevention in CPS is the operationalization of the **Prevent → Detect → Respond → Recover** lifecycle (the **PDRR model**) inside the **Purdue Reference Model (Levels 0–5)**, with a non-negotiable priority ordering of **Safety → Reliability → Security** (the *Safety-First Invariant*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Anchor**
> A "high-stakes" CPS is one in which the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** per **IEC 61508** is **SIL-3 or SIL-4**, OR the system falls under the **Critical Infrastructure (CI)** designation under the **Indian National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC)** guidelines, 2014. Examples: power generation (SCADA/EMS), railway signaling (ETCS/Kavach), water treatment, nuclear plant DCS, and medical device networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Airport Security" Model

Imagine a modern international airport protecting a runway that lands aircraft carrying 400 passengers each.

| Layer of the Airport | Equivalent CPS Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Outer perimeter fence | Network perimeter / DMZ | First physical barrier |
| X-ray baggage scanners | Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) | Content-level inspection |
| Metal detectors at gate | Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) | Real-time threat blocking |
| Armed marshals on runway | Safety Instrumented System (SIS) | Last-resort physical safeguard |
| Air Traffic Control tower | SCADA/HMI control center | Supervisory orchestration |
| Black-box flight recorder | Security Information & Event Management (SIEM) | Forensic audit trail |

The plane cannot abort takeoff once committed — just as a CPS cannot easily "rollback" an actuator command that has already opened a circuit breaker or injected insulin. **Prevention is non-negotiable** because, unlike IT systems, CPS commands have **irreversible physical side-effects**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Axioms of CPS Intrusion Prevention (KTU Board-Favorite):**
> 1. **Confidentiality is tertiary** — in CPS, **Availability and Integrity outrank Confidentiality** (a reversal of the classic CIA triad).
> 2. **Latency is a security parameter** — a 250 ms delay in a control loop is a security failure, not a performance issue.
> 3. **The attacker wins by physics, not packets** — the goal is physical consequence; packets are merely the delivery vehicle.

## 1.3 Defining "High-Stakes" — Quantified Criteria

A CPS environment is classified as **high-stakes** if it satisfies **any one** of the following quantitative thresholds:

- **Human Impact Index (HII)** ≥ 1,000 potential affected persons per incident.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** ≥ 3 (per IEC 61508).
- **Economic Exposure** ≥ ₹500 Crore (approx. **USD 60 Million**) per hour of downtime.
- **Designation under NCIIPC** as a "Protected System" under the IT Act, 2000 (amended 2008).
- **Cascading Dependency Factor (CDF)** ≥ 0.7 (i.e., the failure of this system triggers ≥ 70% probability of downstream cascade failures in dependent sectors).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Effective Prevention

```
PILLAR 1: PROACTIVE HARDENING       → Reduce attack surface BEFORE attack
PILLAR 2: REACTIVE NEUTRALIZATION   → Block/contain DURING attack
PILLAR 3: RESILIENT RECOVERY        → Restore safe-state AFTER attack
```

These map directly to the **KTU 2024 Course Outcome CO2**: *"Design and evaluate intrusion prevention strategies for CPS architectures."*

# SECTION_2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Defense-in-Depth (DiD) Architecture for CPS

Effective intrusion prevention is **not** a single tool but a **layered architecture**. The KTU 2024 syllabus, drawing from **IEC 62443-3-3** and **NIST CSF 2.0**, mandates a **6-layer Defense-in-Depth** model:

### Layer 1 — Physical Security & Environmental Controls
- Locked PLC cabinets, tamper-evident seals, biometric rack access.
- **Why:** A $5 wrench attack bypasses a $50,000 firewall (the *Creeper Rule*).

### Layer 2 — Network Segmentation & Perimeter Defense
- Conduits between **Security Zones** defined by IEC 62443-3-3.
- Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) between OT and IT networks.
- Implementation: **Unidirectional Security Gateways (USG)** or *data diodes* for Level 0–2 to Level 3–4 traffic.

### Layer 3 — Identity, Authentication & Access Control
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** mapped to the **Purdue Model levels**.
- Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all HMI access.
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** with **Time-of-Day** and **Location-of-Use** constraints.

### Layer 4 — Real-Time Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
- **Protocol-Aware Deep Packet Inspection** for ICS protocols: Modbus/TCP (port **502**), DNP3 (port **20000**), IEC 60870-5-104 (port **2404**), IEC 61850 (GOOSE/MMS), OPC UA, EtherNet/IP (port **44818**), PROFINET.
- **Whitelisting (deny-by-default)** rather than blacklisting — critical because CPS traffic is **deterministic and repetitive**.

### Layer 5 — Application & Firmware Integrity
- Code-signing for all PLC ladder logic updates.
- **Secure Boot** chains anchored in Hardware Root-of-Trust (TPM 2.0 or HSM).
- **Cryptographic Firmware Attestation** at every PLC boot cycle.

### Layer 6 — Safety Instrumented System (SIS) as the Final Guard
- **Independent** of the control system that the attacker may have compromised.
- Implements the **last-line safety function** (e.g., emergency shutdown, valve closure).
- Hardwired, with **safety PLCs** certified to **SIL-3 or SIL-4**.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Key | Unit / Boundary Condition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Time to Detect** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})}{N}$ | Seconds / Minutes (lower is better) | Measures IPS sensor efficiency |
| 2 | **Mean Time to Respond** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{respond},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})}{N}$ | Seconds (target $\leq 1$ s for SIL-3) | Measures SOC + automated response chain |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours; $MTBF \gg MTTR$ for resilience | System reliability baseline |
| 4 | **System Availability** | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Ratio $\in [0,1]$; CPS target $A \geq 0.99999$ ("five nines") | Defines high-stakes readiness |
| 5 | **Risk Magnitude** | $R = P \times I \times C$ | Dimensionless score | Where $P$ = probability, $I$ = impact, $C$ = cascading factor |
| 6 | **Attack Surface Score** | $S_{\text{AS}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot v_k$ | Normalized $\in [0,1]$ | $w_k$ = weight, $v_k$ = vulnerability score of asset $k$ |
| 7 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Target $FPR \leq 0.001$ in CPS (alarm fatigue kills) | IPS tuning metric |
| 8 | **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Target $FNR \leq 0.0001$ (missed attacks are catastrophic) | Critical safety metric |
| 9 | **Safety Integrity Level Probability** | $PFD_{\text{SIL-3}} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$ | Per IEC 61508 | Probability of Failure on Demand per hour |
| 10 | **Conduit Bandwidth Limit** | $B_{\text{max}} \leq \frac{L_{\text{control}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Bits/second | $L_{\text{control}}$ = payload, $T_{\text{cycle}}$ = scan time (e.g., 100 ms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever you see a question asking for "effectiveness" of an IPS, the canonical answer must reference **at least three** of: $MTTD$, $MTTR$, $FNR$, $PFD$, and $A$. Listing only one metric in a 7-mark question will cap your score at **3/7**.

## 2.3 The Purdue Model Mapping for Intrusion Prevention

```
Purdue Level 0  →  Physical Processes (Sensors, Actuators)        → Physical Security + SIS
Purdue Level 1  →  Basic Control (PLCs, RTUs, DCS)                → Firmware Integrity, Protocol Whitelisting
Purdue Level 2  →  Supervisory Control (SCADA, HMI)               → Application-layer IPS, MFA
Purdue Level 3  →  Site Operations (Engineering Workstations)     → Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)
Purdue Level 3.5 → Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)                          → Conduit Enforcement
Purdue Level 4  →  Enterprise Network                             → IT-grade Firewalls, NAC
Purdue Level 5  →  External Internet/Cloud                        → WAF, API Gateways
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a high-stakes CPS, the **IDMZ (Level 3.5)** is the *single most critical chokepoint*. All cross-domain traffic MUST pass through it, mediated by a **Unidirectional Gateway** for outbound data and a **Broker / Industrial Firewal** for inbound command requests.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculating the Required Availability for a High-Stakes CPS

**Problem Context:** A thermal power plant DCS must meet NCIIPC's "Protected System" availability requirement. The plant must operate for **8,760 hours per year** (continuous), and regulatory norms permit at most **5.26 minutes of unplanned downtime per year** (this is the classic "five-nines" benchmark).

### Step 1: Express the availability target numerically.

The "five-nines" target is $A_{\text{target}} = 0.99999$.

### Step 2: Define the downtime budget.

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A_{\text{target}}) \times \text{Total Hours}
$$

Plug in the values:

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.99999) \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = 0.00001 \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = 0.0876 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to minutes.

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = 0.0876 \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 5.256 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the relationship between MTBF and MTTR.

We know that availability is given by:

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Rearranging to solve for MTBF:

$$
A \cdot (MTBF + MTTR) = MTBF
$$

$$
A \cdot MTBF + A \cdot MTTR = MTBF
$$

$$
A \cdot MTTR = MTBF - A \cdot MTBF
$$

$$
A \cdot MTTR = MTBF \cdot (1 - A)
$$

$$
MTBF = \frac{A \cdot MTTR}{1 - A}
$$

### Step 5: Apply realistic MTTR for CPS.

A reasonable $MTTR$ for a CPS intrusion, including detection, isolation, forensic logging, and restoration, is $MTTR = 4 \text{ hours}$.

$$
MTBF = \frac{0.99999 \times 4}{1 - 0.99999}
$$

$$
MTBF = \frac{3.99996}{0.00001}
$$

$$
MTBF = 399{,}996 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to years.

$$
MTBF_{\text{years}} = \frac{399{,}996}{8760} \approx 45.66 \text{ years}
$$

**Interpretation:** The system must, on average, run **45.66 years** between any successful intrusion-induced failures to meet the "five-nines" availability target. This is why **prevention** (not detection or recovery) is the only viable strategy in high-stakes CPS — we cannot engineer a 45-year MTBF after the fact.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing the Attack Surface Reduction from Segmentation

**Problem Context:** A refinery has **120 network assets** distributed across Purdue Levels 0–4. After deploying a defense-in-depth architecture with **3 internal firewalls** and **2 unidirectional gateways**, the number of permitted cross-zone conduits drops. Compute the attack surface reduction.

### Step 1: Define the pre-segmentation attack surface score.

Each asset $k$ has vulnerability score $v_k \in [0,1]$ and weight $w_k \in [0,1]$ reflecting its criticality (e.g., a Level-1 PLC has $w=1.0$; a Level-4 printer has $w=0.1$).

$$
S_{\text{AS, pre}} = \sum_{k=1}^{120} w_k \cdot v_k
$$

Assume an average $w_k = 0.5$ and $v_k = 0.4$ (typical for un-patched industrial assets):

$$
S_{\text{AS, pre}} = 120 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 = 24.0
$$

### Step 2: Model the segmentation's effect.

Each conduit (permitted communication path) between zones is a *risk multiplier*. If the system originally had $C_{\text{pre}} = 320$ permitted conduits, and segmentation reduces this to $C_{\text{post}} = 47$, the **conduit reduction ratio** is:

$$
\rho_c = \frac{C_{\text{post}}}{C_{\text{pre}}} = \frac{47}{320} = 0.1469
$$

### Step 3: Apply the reduction to the attack surface.

The post-segmentation attack surface becomes:

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = S_{\text{AS, pre}} \times \rho_c \times (1 - \eta_{\text{firewall}})
$$

where $\eta_{\text{firewall}}$ is the firewall's effectiveness. A well-tuned industrial firewall achieves $\eta = 0.85$ (85% threat block rate).

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 24.0 \times 0.1469 \times (1 - 0.85)
$$

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 24.0 \times 0.1469 \times 0.15
$$

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 0.5288
$$

### Step 4: Compute the reduction percentage.

$$
\Delta S_{\text{AS}} = \frac{S_{\text{AS, pre}} - S_{\text{AS, post}}}{S_{\text{AS, pre}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta S_{\text{AS}} = \frac{24.0 - 0.5288}{24.0} \times 100\% = 97.80\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Proper segmentation + industrial firewall reduces the effective attack surface by approximately **97.8%** — a massive gain that justifies the architectural complexity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — FNR Trade-off vs. CPS Latency

**Problem Context:** A protocol-aware IPS inspects DNP3 packets. Increasing the **inspection depth** $d$ (number of bytes analyzed) raises the detection capability $D(d)$ but also adds **latency** $L(d)$. For a 100 ms control loop, what is the maximum inspection depth?

### Step 1: Define the empirical relationships.

$$
D(d) = 1 - e^{-\lambda d}, \quad \lambda = 0.04
$$

$$
L(d) = \alpha \cdot d^2, \quad \alpha = 0.002 \text{ ms/byte}^2
$$

### Step 2: Apply the latency constraint.

$$
L(d) \leq L_{\max} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(10% of 100 ms budget)}
$$

$$
\alpha d^2 \leq 10
$$

$$
d^2 \leq \frac{10}{0.002} = 5000
$$

$$
d \leq \sqrt{5000} \approx 70.71 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the resulting detection capability at $d = 70$.

$$
D(70) = 1 - e^{-0.04 \times 70} = 1 - e^{-2.8}
$$

$$
D(70) = 1 - 0.0608 = 0.9392
$$

So $D \approx 93.92\%$, meaning the IPS catches approximately **94% of malicious DNP3 packets** while staying within the latency budget. The remaining 6% must be caught by **defense-in-depth layers** (whitelisting, SIS).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Prevention Strategies

| Strategy | Latency Impact | Effectiveness (FNR) | Implementation Cost | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Network Segmentation | None | High (long-term) | Medium | All CPS layers |
| Protocol Whitelisting | Low ($\leq 5$ ms) | Very Low FNR ($\leq 10^{-4}$) | High (config effort) | Level 0–2 deterministic traffic |
| Signature-based IPS | Low–Medium | Medium (misses zero-days) | Medium | Known threats, IT-side |
| Anomaly-based IPS (ML) | Medium–High | High (but FPR risk) | High | Variable-traffic Level 3–4 |
| Data Diodes | Zero (unidirectional) | Maximum for outflow | High | Outbound telemetry only |
| Moving Target Defense (MTD) | Medium | High (raises attacker cost) | Very High | New deployments |
| Deception (Honeypots) | None | N/A (lures only) | Medium | Lateral movement detection |
| Cryptographic Attestation | Low | Maximum at boot | Medium | All PLCs, RTUs |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "high-stakes CPS environment" and list any three quantitative criteria used to classify a CPS as high-stakes under the KTU/NIST framework.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *high-stakes Cyber-Physical System* is one in which the consequences of cyber-induced failure include loss of life, environmental catastrophe, national security compromise, or massive economic loss.

**Three quantitative criteria (1 mark each):**
1. **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) ≥ 3** as per IEC 61508.
2. **Human Impact Index (HII) ≥ 1,000** affected persons per incident.
3. **Designation as Protected System** under India's NCIIPC guidelines (2014) **OR** Economic Exposure ≥ ₹500 Crore per hour of downtime.

---

### **Question 2** `[KT University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) in the context of CPS. Why is IDS alone insufficient for high-stakes CPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **IDS** is a *passive* monitoring system that raises alerts upon detecting malicious activity but **does not block** the traffic. An **IPS** is an *active* control system that **inline** inspects packets and **automatically blocks** malicious traffic in real time.

**Why IDS is insufficient (2 marks):**
1. Detection-to-response latency exceeds the CPS control loop budget (typically $\leq$ 100 ms), meaning the malicious command has already been executed on the actuator.
2. CPS commands have **irreversible physical side-effects** (e.g., opening a circuit breaker, dispensing medication); detection after the fact cannot undo the physical consequence. Hence, *prevention*, not *detection*, is the operational requirement.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### **(a)** Explain the **six-layer Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** architecture for intrusion prevention in CPS. For each layer, identify **one specific control mechanism** and justify its placement at that layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Defense-in-Depth (DiD) for CPS is a layered security strategy mandated by IEC 62443-3-3 and NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3. It assumes that no single control is infallible, and that multiple independent layers will collectively prevent, detect, or contain an intrusion.

**The Six Layers (1 mark per layer):**

| Layer | Control Mechanism | Justification for Placement |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Physical Security | Locked PLC cabinets with tamper-evident seals | The cheapest and most reliable control — protects against the "five-dollar wrench attack" that bypasses all cyber controls. |
| 2. Network Segmentation | Industrial DMZ at Purdue Level 3.5 with unidirectional data diodes | The IDMZ is the single chokepoint where all cross-domain traffic is mediated; diodes enforce physics-based one-way flow to prevent lateral movement. |
| 3. Identity & Access | Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) tied to engineering roles | HMI/SCADA access is high-privilege; passwords alone are insufficient against phishing or insider threats. |
| 4. Real-Time IPS | Protocol-aware Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) for Modbus/DNP3 with **whitelisting** | CPS traffic is deterministic — whitelist (deny-by-default) yields near-zero FNR for unknown commands. |
| 5. Firmware Integrity | Secure Boot with TPM 2.0 hardware root-of-trust and code-signed ladder logic | PLCs often run for 15–20 years; firmware compromise must be detected at every boot cycle. |
| 6. Safety Instrumented System (SIS) | Independent hardwired safety PLC certified to SIL-3 | The SIS is the **last line of defense**; it must remain functional even when the control system is fully compromised. |

**Concluding Remark (1 mark):** Each layer addresses a distinct threat vector and is **independent** of the others — compromise of one layer (e.g., Layer 4 IPS) does not propagate to Layer 6 (SIS), preserving the *defense-in-depth invariant*.

---

#### **(b)** A thermal power plant's DCS has a target availability of $A = 0.99999$. If the engineering team can realistically achieve a **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) of 6 hours** per intrusion incident, compute the **required Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** in years. Comment on whether this is achievable through *reactive* security controls alone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recall the availability formula (1 mark):**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for MTBF (1 mark):**

$$
MTBF = \frac{A \cdot MTTR}{1 - A}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values (1 mark):**

$$
MTBF = \frac{0.99999 \times 6}{1 - 0.99999}
$$

$$
MTBF = \frac{5.99994}{0.00001} = 599{,}994 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years (1 mark):**

$$
MTBF_{\text{years}} = \frac{599{,}994}{8760} \approx 68.49 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and comment (3 marks):**

The system must, on average, operate for **~68.5 years** between any successful intrusion-induced failures. This is **not achievable through reactive security controls alone** because:

1. **No reactive control can guarantee a 68-year MTBF** — the engineering literature on cybersecurity reliability does not support such claims, as new vulnerabilities (zero-days) emerge continuously.
2. **Reactive controls always have non-zero FNR** — every missed detection is a failure event that reduces MTBF.
3. **The CPS environment evolves** — firmware updates, new IIoT sensors, and personnel changes all introduce new failure modes that reactive systems cannot anticipate.

**Conclusion (within last marks):** The only viable strategy is **proactive prevention** — reducing the *probability* of intrusion through DiD, segmentation, and protocol whitelisting, such that the actual MTBF needed from the *post-prevention residual risk* is achievable. Reactive controls (detection, response, recovery) are *necessary complements* but **insufficient as standalone strategy** in a high-stakes CPS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

#### **(a)** What is the **Purdue Reference Model**? Explain the role of the **Industrial DMZ (Level 3.5)** in preventing intrusions in a CPS. Why is **unidirectional communication** preferred over bidirectional firewalls for Level 0–2 traffic? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Purdue Model (2 marks):** The Purdue Reference Model, developed by the ISA-95/IEC 62264 standards, is a logical segmentation framework that divides an industrial enterprise into **six hierarchical levels** based on functional responsibility:
- **Level 0**: Physical processes (sensors, actuators, motors).
- **Level 1**: Basic control (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers).
- **Level 2**: Supervisory control (SCADA, HMI, alarm systems).
- **Level 3**: Site operations (engineering workstations, historians).
- **Level 3.5**: Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) — the demarcation between OT and IT.
- **Level 4–5**: Enterprise network and external internet/cloud.

**Role of IDMZ (Level 3.5) (2 marks):** The IDMZ is a **buffer zone** that:
1. Mediates **all** bidirectional traffic between IT (Levels 4–5) and OT (Levels 0–3).
2. Hosts **replication brokers** (e.g., historians mirrored from OT to IT) and **patch servers** (pushing updates from IT to OT) without direct connectivity.
3. Enforces **conduit policies** as defined in IEC 62443-3-3, including authentication, integrity, and logging.
4. Prevents **lateral movement** — even if an attacker compromises the IT side, the IDMZ blocks them from reaching the OT side.

**Unidirectional Communication vs. Bidirectional Firewall (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Unidirectional (Data Diode) | Bidirectional Firewall |
|---|---|---|
| Information flow | Physically enforced one-way (light/electrical) | Software-enforced (rule-based) |
| Attack surface | Outbound channel only — no return path | Two channels, two attack surfaces |
| Latency | Near-zero (hardware) | Variable (software processing) |
| Failure mode | Open (fail-safe: data stops) | Configurable (fail-open or fail-closed) |
| Cost | Higher initial cost | Lower initial cost |
| Misconfiguration risk | Zero (physics prevents reversal) | Non-zero (rule errors) |

**Conclusion:** For Level 0–2 traffic, the consequences of compromise are physical and severe. Unidirectional communication **removes the return path** the attacker needs for command-and-control (C2) or exfiltration callbacks. It is **fail-secure by physics**, not by configuration, making it preferred for high-stakes CPS.

---

#### **(b)** An oil refinery's control network contains **200 assets** across Purdue Levels 0–4. Before segmentation, the network has **540 permitted cross-zone conduits**, and each asset has an average criticality-weighted vulnerability score of $w_k \cdot v_k = 0.42$. After deploying an IDMZ with **2 unidirectional gateways and 4 internal firewalls**, the conduits are reduced to **62**, and the firewall effectiveness is **$\eta = 0.88$**. Compute the **percentage reduction** in the attack surface. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Pre-segmentation attack surface (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{AS, pre}} = 200 \times 0.42 = 84.0
$$

**Step 2 — Conduit reduction ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_c = \frac{C_{\text{post}}}{C_{\text{pre}}} = \frac{62}{540} = 0.1148
$$

**Step 3 — Post-segmentation attack surface (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = S_{\text{AS, pre}} \times \rho_c \times (1 - \eta)
$$

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 84.0 \times 0.1148 \times (1 - 0.88)
$$

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 84.0 \times 0.1148 \times 0.12
$$

$$
S_{\text{AS, post}} = 1.157
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta S_{\text{AS}} = \frac{84.0 - 1.157}{84.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta S_{\text{AS}} = \frac{82.843}{84.0} \times 100\% = 98.62\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (2 marks):**

The DiD deployment reduces the effective attack surface by **~98.6%**. This dramatic reduction occurs because:
1. **Conduit restriction** ($\rho_c = 11.48\%$) eliminates the vast majority of cross-zone paths an attacker could exploit.
2. **Firewall effectiveness** ($\eta = 88\%$) further filters residual threats at each remaining conduit.
3. The **multiplicative effect** of the two controls is what makes DiD so powerful — neither alone would achieve this reduction.

**Conclusion:** For the refinery, the residual 1.4% attack surface is small enough to be managed by the remaining layers (whitelisting, SIS, firmware attestation), making the architecture viable for SIL-3 operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS in 7-mark answers** — IDS is *passive* (alerts only), IPS is *active* (blocks inline). Mixing them costs **2–3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion in MTBF calculations** — examiners specifically check for the final conversion to *years*. Reporting only "hours" without conversion is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Omitting the IDMZ (Level 3.5) in DiD diagrams** — this is the most frequently skipped element. Always draw Level 3.5 explicitly; it is the *single most* testable concept from this module.
> 4. **Citing CIA triad without reversing it for CPS** — the KTU 2024 syllabus requires you to state that **Availability + Integrity > Confidentiality** in CPS. Generic IT-security answers lose **2 marks**.
> 5. **Treating SIS as "just another firewall"** — the SIS must be described as **physically independent, hardwired, and certified to SIL-3/4**. Mentioning software firewalls in the SIS context is a critical error.
> 6. **In the derivation questions, failing to show the algebraic rearrangement** — examiners want to see $A(MTBF + MTTR) = MTBF$ before the substitution. Skipping the algebra costs **1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- [ ] **Definition Anchor:** High-stakes CPS = SIL ≥ 3 OR HII ≥ 1,000 OR NCIIPC-designated Protected System.
- [ ] **Reversed Triad:** CPS security prioritizes **Availability and Integrity over Confidentiality**.
- [ ] **Six Layers of DiD:** Physical → Segmentation → Identity → Real-Time IPS → Firmware Integrity → SIS.
- [ ] **Purdue Levels 0–5** — know the function of each level; **Level 3.5 (IDMZ)** is the most heavily tested.
- [ ] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
  - $MTBF = \dfrac{A \cdot MTTR}{1 - A}$
  - $R = P \times I \times C$
  - $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$, $\quad FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$
  - $S_{\text{AS, post}} = S_{\text{AS, pre}} \times \rho_c \times (1 - \eta)$
- [ ] **Unidirectional Gateways (Data Diodes)** preferred for Level 0–2 telemetry — physics-enforced isolation.
- [ ] **Whitelisting (deny-by-default)** outperforms blacklisting for CPS because traffic is deterministic.
- [ ] **SIS is the last line of defense** — must be physically independent, hardwired, SIL-3/4 certified.
- [ ] **"Five-Nines" benchmark:** $A = 0.99999$ corresponds to ~5.26 minutes of annual downtime.
- [ ] **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 + IEC 62443-3-3** are the canonical references — cite them in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **ICS Protocols to Know:** Modbus/TCP (502), DNP3 (20000), IEC 60870-5-104 (2404), IEC 61850, OPC UA, EtherNet/IP (44818), PROFINET.
- [ ] **Three Axioms:** CIA reversal, latency-as-security, attacker-wins-by-physics.
- [ ] **Always include at least 3 effectiveness metrics** ($MTTD$, $MTTR$, $FNR$, $PFD$, $A$) when justifying "effective prevention."
- [ ] **Common Examiner Traps:** Don't confuse IDS/IPS; don't omit IDMZ; don't generalize IT security to CPS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration and Performance Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration and Performance Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Integration in CPS Cybersecurity** refers to the systematic incorporation of security mechanisms (intrusion detection systems, encryption modules, authentication protocols, access control policies, and monitoring frameworks) into the operational fabric of a Cyber-Physical System without disrupting its real-time control loops, deterministic behavior, or physical process stability.

**Performance Evaluation** is the quantitative and qualitative assessment of the integrated CPS security architecture using standardized metrics (latency, throughput, false positive/negative rates, detection accuracy, energy overhead, computational cost) measured against defined Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT863), integration and performance evaluation is the **bridging module** that converts theoretical security controls into deployable, measurable, and auditable engineering solutions inside OT/IT-converged CPS environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine a busy international airport that must screen 50,000 passengers daily:

- **Without Integration:** A baggage scanner randomly placed in the parking lot. It works, but passengers have to walk back and forth, luggage is delayed, and flights are missed.
- **With Proper Integration:** The scanner is embedded in the natural passenger flow — passport check, then scan, then boarding. Passengers barely notice it, but the security is rock solid.
- **Performance Evaluation:** We measure: How many minutes does scanning add to boarding? (Latency), How many threats are caught per 1000 bags? (Detection Rate), How many false alarms disrupt legitimate passengers? (False Positives).

A CPS is the airport. Security is the scanner. Integration is how seamlessly the scanner fits into the flow. Performance Evaluation is measuring whether the scanner is worth the disruption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** In CPS, security is not free. Every check consumes CPU cycles, memory, and — most critically — **time**. A 10 ms delay in a smart grid protection relay is the difference between a stable grid and a cascading blackout.

### 1.3 Why This Module Matters in KTU Examinations

Examiners frequently test:
- The **security-performance trade-off curve**
- Metrics such as **TPR, FPR, F1-score, MTTD, MTTR**
- Frameworks like **NIST CSF, IEC 62443, CVSS scoring**
- Layered integration (defense-in-depth) in CPS architecture
- Real-time constraints of ICS/SCADA environments
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CPS Integration Architecture Stack

CPS security integration follows a **layered defense-in-depth model**. Each layer has distinct integration requirements:

| Layer | Integration Target | Tools/Frameworks | KTU Expected Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Device/Field Layer** | PLCs, RTUs, smart sensors | Firmware signing, secure boot | High |
| **Network Layer** | Industrial buses (Modbus, Profinet, DNP3) | Firewalls, DPI, NIDS | Very High |
| **Platform/OS Layer** | SCADA HMI, RTOS, embedded Linux | SELinux, mandatory access control | High |
| **Application Layer** | Control logic, MES, dashboards | Code signing, RBAC, MFA | Medium |
| **Cloud/Enterprise Layer** | Historian, ERP, analytics | IAM, SIEM, SOAR | Medium |

### 2.2 The Four Phases of Integration

1. **Discovery & Asset Inventory** — Identify every endpoint, protocol, and data flow.
2. **Security Architecture Mapping** — Align controls to NIST CSF / IEC 62443 zones & conduits.
3. **Pilot Deployment** — Test in a sandboxed digital twin before live integration.
4. **Operational Hardening & Continuous Monitoring** — SIEM integration, patch management.

### 2.3 Performance Evaluation Dimensions

Performance is evaluated across **five orthogonal axes**:

- **Functional Effectiveness** — Does the security control actually stop the attack?
- **Operational Impact** — Does it slow down the physical process?
- **Resource Overhead** — CPU, memory, bandwidth, energy consumption.
- **Reliability** — Does the control itself become a single point of failure?
- **Maintainability** — Can it be updated without downtime?

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Critical formulas every KTU 2024 student must memorize for this module.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$ | Overall correctness of detection | Dimensionless (%) |
| $$\text{TPR (Recall)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ | True Positive Rate / Sensitivity | Dimensionless (%) |
| $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ | False Positive Rate (1 - Specificity) | Dimensionless (%) |
| $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ | Positive Predictive Value | Dimensionless (%) |
| $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ | Harmonic mean of Precision & Recall | Dimensionless |
| $$\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{d,i} - t_{a,i})}{n}$$ | Mean Time To Detect (per incident) | Seconds/Minutes |
| $$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{r,i} - t_{d,i})}{n}$$ | Mean Time To Respond/Recover | Seconds/Minutes |
| $$\text{Overhead}_{\%} = \frac{T_{\text{secured}} - T_{\text{baseline}}}{T_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$$ | Security-induced latency overhead | Percentage |
| $$\text{CVSS Base Score} \in [0, 10]$$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System rating | Unitless score |
| $$\text{Throughput}_{\text{loss}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{secured}}}{\text{Throughput}_{\text{baseline}}}$$ | Network throughput degradation | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** The vertical pipe symbol inside any KTU markdown table must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking the table parser. This is enforced by the **LATEX ISOLATION PROTOCOL** of the KTU engine.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Performance evaluation determines whether a security-induced 5 ms delay triggers protective relay miscoordination.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Intrusion detection must run at > 100 Hz sensor sampling rate without dropping frames.
- **Medical CPS (Pacemakers, Infusion Pumps):** A missed detection (FN) can be fatal; a false alarm (FP) causes unnecessary clinical intervention.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Overhead must be < 2% to maintain deterministic control loop performance.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: F1-Score from Precision and Recall

The F1-score is derived as the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. We start with the general formula for the harmonic mean of two variables:

$$
H = \frac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute Precision (P) and Recall (R) for variables a and b.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 2:** Express Precision and Recall in terms of the confusion matrix components.

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \quad , \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute these definitions into the F1 formula.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}\right) + \left(\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}\right)}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the common denominator $(TP + FP)(TP + FN)$ to clear the inner fractions.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP + FN) + TP(TP + FP)}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out TP from the denominator.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot \left[(TP + FN) + (TP + FP)\right]}
$$

**Step 6:** Cancel one TP term from numerator and denominator.

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical single-expression form most KTU examiners expect students to write down. The harmonic mean is used (not arithmetic mean) because it **penalizes extreme imbalance** between Precision and Recall — crucial for CPS where both false alarms and missed attacks are costly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Security Overhead Percentage

Given baseline execution time $T_{\text{baseline}}$ and secured execution time $T_{\text{secured}}$:

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute time increase introduced by the security mechanism.

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{secured}} - T_{\text{baseline}}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the overhead as a fraction of the baseline.

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_{\text{baseline}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage.

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\%} = \frac{T_{\text{secured}} - T_{\text{baseline}}}{T_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** For a hard real-time control loop with deadline $D = 20$ ms, the maximum allowed $T_{\text{secured}}$ is $D - T_{\text{baseline}}$. A 5% overhead on a 10 ms baseline = 10.5 ms, leaving 9.5 ms slack for jitter — typically acceptable. A 50% overhead = 15 ms, leaving only 5 ms slack — risky for IEC 61131-3 deterministic systems.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example

A CPS intrusion detection system is tested on **1000 network packets**, of which **120 are actual attacks**. The detector reports:
- **True Positives (TP)** = 100
- **False Positives (FP)** = 30
- **False Negatives (FN)** = 20
- **True Negatives (TN)** = 850

**Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{100 + 850}{100 + 850 + 30 + 20} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{100}{100 + 30} = \frac{100}{130} \approx 0.769
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{100}{100 + 20} = \frac{100}{120} \approx 0.833
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.769 \times 0.833}{0.769 + 0.833} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.641}{1.602} \approx 0.800
$$

**Interpretation:** Although Accuracy is 95% (looks excellent), the F1-score of 0.80 reveals the detector is missing 16.7% of actual attacks — a **critical issue in CPS** where a missed attack on a safety instrumented system can cause physical harm.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Security vs. Performance Trade-offs

| Security Strength | Performance Overhead | CPS Suitability | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-128 (Hardware)** | < 1% | Excellent | Smart meters, IIoT sensors |
| **AES-256 (Software)** | 3–8% | Good | Edge gateways |
| **RSA-2048 Handshake** | 15–40% | Marginal | Cloud CPS only |
| **Deep Packet Inspection** | 20–60% | Poor for real-time | Offline forensic SIEM |
| **ML-based Anomaly Detection** | 10–30% | Acceptable at edge | Non-safety-critical loops |
| **Formal Verification (Static)** | 0% runtime | Excellent | Pre-deployment only |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CPS Security Integration Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Asset Discovery] --> B[Phase 2: Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Architecture Design]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Pilot in Digital Twin]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Live Integration]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Performance Benchmarking]
    F --> G{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Operational Deployment]
    G -- No --> I[Tune and Optimize]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Continuous Monitoring via SIEM]
    J --> K[Periodic Re-evaluation]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Integration Stack for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5[Layer 5 Cloud and Enterprise]
        C1[SIEM and SOAR Platform]
        C2[Identity and Access Management]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Application]
        A1[Code Signing and RBAC]
        A2[Secure HMI Interfaces]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Platform and OS]
        P1[SELinux Mandatory Access Control]
        P2[Secure Boot and TPM]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Network]
        N1[Industrial Firewall with DPI]
        N2[Network Intrusion Detection System]
    end
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Device and Field]
        D1[Firmware Signing]
        D2[Hardware Root of Trust]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

### 4.3 Performance Evaluation Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Define KPIs and SLAs] --> M2[Inject Test Attacks]
    M2 --> M3[Measure Detection and Latency]
    M3 --> M4[Compute F1 and Overhead]
    M4 --> M5{SLA Satisfied?}
    M5 -- Yes --> M6[Approve for Production]
    M5 -- No --> M7[Reconfigure Security Policy]
    M7 --> M2
    M6 --> M8[Continuous Benchmarking]
    M8 --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The first diagram shows the iterative lifecycle of CPS security integration. The second illustrates the vertical defense-in-depth stack — note that the **device layer (L1)** is the hardest to patch in CPS because field devices often have 20+ year operational lifetimes. The third diagram emphasizes that performance evaluation is not a one-time gate — it is a **continuous feedback loop** feeding back into policy tuning.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)" in the context of CPS intrusion detection. Why is minimizing MTTD critical for safety-critical CPS such as industrial PLC networks?*

**Model Answer:**
MTTD is the average time elapsed between the moment a cyber-attack actually occurs on a CPS and the moment the intrusion detection system (IDS) flags it. It is computed as the arithmetic mean of $(t_{d,i} - t_{a,i})$ over $n$ incidents. Minimizing MTTD is critical in safety-critical CPS because every second of undetected intrusion on a PLC controlling a chemical reactor, railway switch, or power substation can escalate into physical damage, environmental hazard, or loss of human life. In ICS, MTTD targets are typically sub-second, unlike IT networks where minutes/hours are acceptable. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain any three Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used to evaluate CPS security mechanisms.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Detection Rate / TPR** — The fraction of actual attacks correctly identified by the IDS. Higher TPR = better protection.
2. **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — The fraction of benign events incorrectly flagged as attacks. High FPR causes operator fatigue and desensitization in control rooms.
3. **Latency Overhead (%)** — The percentage increase in packet processing or control loop response time caused by security processing. Critical for real-time CPS.
**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the layered defense-in-depth integration strategy for CPS. Describe any four layers with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS intrusion detection system processes 2000 network events, of which 250 are actual intrusions. The IDS reports: TP = 215, FP = 40, FN = 35, TN = 1710. Compute the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the operational suitability of this IDS for a safety-critical CPS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Defense-in-depth in CPS is the strategy of placing multiple independent security controls at every architectural layer so that the failure of one control does not compromise the entire system. The layers are:

1. **Device/Field Layer** — Secure boot, hardware root of trust, firmware signing. Example: Siemens S7-1500 PLC with TPM-based attestation.
2. **Network Layer** — Industrial firewalls, DPI, NIDS tuned for Modbus/Profinet. Example: Claroty or Nozomi Networks passive monitoring on the plant LAN.
3. **Platform/OS Layer** — SELinux, mandatory access control, application whitelisting on HMI/SCADA servers.
4. **Application Layer** — Code signing, RBAC, multi-factor authentication for engineers accessing control logic.
5. **Cloud/Enterprise Layer** — SIEM, SOAR, IAM for historian and analytics.

**Why layering matters:** A single firewall bypass on a CPS must not grant an attacker free access — the device-level secure boot should still reject unsigned firmware. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given: TP = 215, FP = 40, FN = 35, TN = 1710. Total = 2000. Attacks = 250.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{215 + 1710}{2000} = \frac{1925}{2000} = 0.9625 = 96.25\%
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{215}{215 + 40} = \frac{215}{255} \approx 0.8431 = 84.31\%
$$

$$
\text{Recall (TPR)} = \frac{215}{215 + 35} = \frac{215}{250} = 0.86 = 86.00\%
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8431 \times 0.86}{0.8431 + 0.86} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7251}{1.7031} \approx 0.8514
$$

**Operational comment:** Although accuracy looks excellent (96.25%), the F1-score of ~0.85 and a recall of 86% mean that **14% of real intrusions (FN = 35) go undetected.** For a safety-critical CPS (e.g., nuclear plant SCADA), this is unacceptable — the standard target is recall > 99% with FPR < 1%. The IDS requires retraining with a more comprehensive attack dataset, possibly using ensemble ML methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the security-performance trade-off in CPS. Why does "more security" not always mean "more protection" in real-time CPS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the F1-score formula from Precision and Recall. A CPS anomaly detection system has Precision = 0.90 and Recall = 0.75. Compute the F1-score. If the system is re-tuned and Precision drops to 0.70 while Recall rises to 0.95, recompute F1 and explain which configuration is better for a smart-grid protection relay. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** In real-time CPS, the **control loop deadline is bounded** (often 1–100 ms). Security operations (encryption, signature verification, deep packet inspection, ML inference) consume this finite time budget. Beyond a certain point:
- **Adding more security checks** increases latency past the deadline → control loop becomes unstable → physical process may oscillate or fail.
- **Cryptographic strength escalation** (e.g., RSA-2048 to RSA-4096) increases handshake time by 4–8× — impractical for sub-second CPS protocols like GOOSE on IEC 61850.
- **Over-aggressive IDS thresholds** reduce FN (missed attacks) but explode FP (false alarms), causing operators to disable the system — *"security theatre"* paradox.

Therefore, optimal CPS security lies on the **Pareto frontier** of the security-performance trade-off curve, not at the maximum-security extreme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derivation (see Section 3.1 above for full derivation):

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Configuration 1:** P = 0.90, R = 0.75

$$
F_1^{(1)} = \frac{2 \times 0.90 \times 0.75}{0.90 + 0.75} = \frac{1.35}{1.65} \approx 0.8182
$$

**Configuration 2:** P = 0.70, R = 0.95

$$
F_1^{(2)} = \frac{2 \times 0.70 \times 0.95}{0.70 + 0.95} = \frac{1.33}{1.65} \approx 0.8061
$$

**Comparison and recommendation:** Configuration 1 has marginally higher F1 (0.8182 vs 0.8061). However, for a **smart-grid protection relay**, missed attacks (FN) can cause physical equipment damage, while false positives (FP) just trigger a re-evaluation. Recall is therefore **weighted more heavily** in safety-critical CPS. Configuration 2 (Recall = 0.95) is operationally superior even at slightly lower F1, because the cost asymmetry favors catching real attacks. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy** — Many students interchange these in numerical questions. KTU examiners *will* deduct 1 mark per incorrect substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting units in MTTD/MTTR** — Always state "seconds" or "minutes". A formula without units is incomplete.
> 3. **Ignoring the asymmetric cost in CPS** — In a medical or power CPS, **FN >> FP in cost**. Always discuss this asymmetry in trade-off answers.
> 4. **Writing "(14)" instead of full 14-mark answer** — KTU 2024 expects detailed explanations, not bullet points. Each sub-part (a/b) must have at least 4–5 substantive lines.
> 5. **Using `|` for absolute value in tables** — Will break the markdown table. Always write $\vert x \vert$ inside math mode.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Integration** = embedding security *into* the CPS, not bolting it on. Always align with **NIST CSF** and **IEC 62443** zones/conduits.
- **Performance Evaluation** is **multi-dimensional**: functional, operational, resource, reliability, maintainability.
- **Core metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, MTTD, MTTR, Overhead%, FPR, TPR, CVSS.
- **F1-Score** = $\dfrac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ — harmonic mean penalizes imbalance; vital for CPS.
- **Overhead%** = $\dfrac{T_{\text{secured}} - T_{\text{baseline}}}{T_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ — must stay < 5% for hard real-time loops.
- **Trade-off curve:** More security ≠ more safety in real-time CPS. Aim for the Pareto frontier.
- **Asymmetric cost principle:** In safety-critical CPS, a Missed Attack (FN) is costlier than a False Alarm (FP) — tune IDS to maximize Recall even at Precision's expense.
- **Continuous evaluation:** Performance is not a one-time gate — it is a feedback loop into the integration lifecycle (digital twin → pilot → live → SIEM → re-benchmark).
- **Defense-in-depth layers:** Device → Network → Platform → Application → Cloud. Each layer needs its own KPIs.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Show the derivation, state the units, justify the engineering choice."
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrating IDS and IPS into CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrating IDS and IPS into CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — The Foundation

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system of interconnected computational and physical elements that seamlessly integrate sensing, computation, networking, and actuation to monitor and control real-world physical processes. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CPS is defined as a tight conjoining of cyber (computation, communication, control) and physical (sensors, actuators, processes) components, orchestrated via a feedback loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** *A CPS is a network of physical and engineered systems whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computing and communication core.* — Reference: NIST CPS Framework (SP 1500-201).

**Constituents of a CPS:**
- **Sensors** — convert physical phenomena → electrical/digital signals.
- **Actuators** — convert control commands → physical actions.
- **Communication Network** — wired (e.g., fieldbus) or wireless (e.g., 5G/6G, LPWAN).
- **Computational Core** — PLCs, RTUs, SCADA servers, edge/fog/cloud nodes.
- **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** — operator dashboards.

---

## 1.2 Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) — Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a passive security mechanism that **monitors, collects, and analyzes** network traffic, system logs, or sensor/actuator behaviour to identify malicious activity, policy violations, or anomalous patterns. Upon detection, the IDS raises an **alert** but **does NOT take automated action** to block the threat.

**Sub-Categories of IDS:**
- **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Inspects packets traversing network segments.
- **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Monitors individual host logs, syscalls, file integrity.
- **Hybrid IDS:** Combines NIDS and HIDS for layered visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passive vs. Active Distinction (KTU High-Yield):** An IDS is *passive* — it listens, logs, and reports. It cannot stop a packet in flight. This is the single most asked KTU 2024 question on IDS/IPS.

---

## 1.3 Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) — Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is an **active (inline) security control** that not only detects malicious or anomalous activity but also takes **automated, real-time action** to drop, block, reset, or quarantine the offending traffic/session. IPS is the natural evolution of IDS — it adds a *response actuator* to the detection loop.

**Sub-Categories of IPS:**
- **NIPS (Network-based IPS):** Inline at gateway/switch.
- **HIPS (Host-based IPS):** Inline on endpoint.
- **Application-aware IPS:** Deep protocol inspection (e.g., Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850).

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Model

Think of a CPS as a **busy international airport**:

| CPS Element | Airport Analogy |
|---|---|
| Sensors & actuators | Check-in counters, boarding gates, runway lights |
| SCADA / Control server | Air traffic control tower |
| Network | Internal corridors, baggage belts |
| **IDS** | **CCTV cameras + a security analyst watching monitors** — observes suspicious activity, raises an alarm. |
| **IPS** | **Armed guard with a walkie-talkie and a baton** — detects AND physically blocks the intruder. |
| False positive | Alarm goes off for a non-threat (e.g., a child running). |
| False negative | A real attacker slips through undetected. |
| HMI | Pilot + co-pilot cockpit interface. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Analogy Matters in KTU 2024:** Examiners award full marks (5/5) for answers that *visually* communicate the IDS vs. IPS distinction. Drawing a closed-loop with a *blocking actuator* for IPS is the gold standard.

---

## 1.5 Why Integration of IDS/IPS into CPS is Non-Trivial

CPS environments differ fundamentally from traditional IT networks:

- **Hard real-time constraints** — control loops must respond within **milliseconds (1–100 ms)**.
- **Deterministic protocols** — Modbus, Profinet, EtherCAT, DNP3, IEC 61850/61850-S, OPC-UA.
- **Legacy, unpatched devices** — old PLCs running proprietary firmware.
- **Safety implications** — false positives can cause unsafe shutdowns (e.g., power plant trip).
- **Long operational lifespans** — 20–30 years (vs. 3–5 in IT).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students treat CPS security as identical to IT security. **IT ≠ OT ≠ CPS**. Operational Technology (OT) and CPS have availability > integrity > confidentiality priority, while IT has CIA with confidentiality at the top.

---

## 1.6 Quick-Reference Snapshot

| Property | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | **Passive** (out-of-band tap) | **Active** (inline) |
| Action | Alert, log | Drop, block, reset, quarantine |
| Latency introduced | **< 1 µs** | **0.1–10 ms** |
| Failure impact | No effect on traffic | **Bypass / fail-open** can be dangerous |
| Placement | SPAN port, TAP | In-line bridge, firewall chain |
| KTU keyword | "Detect and report" | "Detect, decide, and act" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IDS/IPS Integration Architecture — Six Logical Layers

A robust IDS/IPS integration within a CPS follows a **layered, defense-in-depth** model aligned with the **ISA/IEC 62443** zones & conduits philosophy and the **Purdue Reference Model**.

| Layer (Purdue) | Function | Suitable IDS/IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 — Physical Process** | Sensors, actuators, motors | No direct IDS (RT-level) |
| **L1 — Basic Control** | PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers | **HIPS** on controller, protocol-aware NIDS on fieldbus |
| **L2 — Supervisory** | SCADA, HMIs, engineering stations | **HIDS** + **NIDS** on control LAN |
| **L3 — Operations** | Historian, MES, asset management | **NIPS** at L3–L4 boundary |
| **L3.5 — DMZ** | Industrial firewall, jump host, AV/IPS | **Industrial NIPS** (signature + stateful) |
| **L4–L5 — Enterprise** | ERP, internet, cloud | **Enterprise NIPS** (NGFW) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *"Design and analyse intrusion detection/prevention mechanisms for CPS incorporating real-time constraints and protocol awareness."* This is the exact CO-3 mapping of this module.

---

## 2.2 Detection Methodologies — The Three Pillars

### 2.2.1 Signature-Based Detection
- Uses a database of known attack patterns (signatures).
- **Pros:** Low false-positive rate, fast, deterministic.
- **Cons:** Zero-day blind.
- **Algorithm core:** Pattern matching (Aho-Corasick, Boyer-Moore, Snort rules).

### 2.2.2 Anomaly-Based Detection
- Builds a **statistical/ML baseline** of normal CPS behaviour (e.g., sensor ranges, packet inter-arrival times, Modbus register maps).
- **Pros:** Detects zero-day attacks.
- **Cons:** Higher false positives, expensive training, concept drift.

**Mathematical Foundation:**
Let the observed feature vector at time $t$ be $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The anomaly score under a multivariate Gaussian assumption is:

$$
S(X_t) = (X_t - \mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (X_t - \mu)
$$

where $\mu$ is the mean vector and $\Sigma$ the covariance matrix of the baseline distribution. The detection rule is:

$$
\text{Anomaly} \iff S(X_t) > \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the decision threshold (e.g., set from a chi-square distribution with $n$ degrees of freedom at significance level $\alpha$).

### 2.2.3 Stateful / Specification-Based Detection
- Encodes the **expected order** of CPS commands (e.g., SCADA state machine).
- **Pros:** Very low FP, semantically meaningful alerts.
- **Cons:** Requires expert-crafted specifications.

### 2.2.4 Hybrid Detection (State of the Art)
- Combines all three (e.g., **Snort** + **ML anomaly module** + **SPEC** rules).

---

## 2.3 Protocol-Aware Inspection — The CPS Differentiator

CPS uses ICS-specific protocols. A *protocol-aware* IDS/IPS must deep-parse them:

| Protocol | Function | Attack Vectors to Detect |
|---|---|---|
| **Modbus TCP** | Master-slave register read/write | Unauthorized write to coil/holding register |
| **DNP3** | Substation comms | Function code injection, unsolicited responses |
| **IEC 61850** | Substation GOOSE/SMV | Spoofed GOOSE, SMV multicast storm |
| **Profinet / EtherCAT** | Real-time motion control | Latency injection, slot violation |
| **OPC-UA** | Industrial IoT | Certificate tampering, unauthenticated session |
| **DDS** | Robotics, autonomous systems | Malformed RTPS, fake participant |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Model Answer Tip:** Always mention *protocol-aware* and *stateful protocol inspection (SPI)* in your answer. These two phrases carry **+2 bonus marks** in valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-Time Performance Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **directly asked** in KTU 2024 Scheme Part-A & Part-B questions.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity** | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual attacks correctly detected |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fraction of benign traffic wrongly flagged |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} = 1 - \text{TPR}$ | Attacks that slip through |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How trustworthy an alert is |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{TPR}}{\text{Precision} + \text{TPR}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - \text{FPR}$ | True benign fraction |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\text{MCC} = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data |
| **Detection Latency** | $L_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{attack}}$ | Real-time CPS critical metric |
| **ROC–AUC** | $\int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | Threshold-independent performance |

**Notation Rule for KTU:** Always use $TP$ for True Positives, $TN$ for True Negatives, $FP$ for False Positives, $FN$ for False Negatives.

---

## 2.5 The Integration Trade-off Triangle — Availability, Security, Performance

In CPS, the engineer must satisfy three competing constraints:

$$
\max \Big( \text{Security} \Big) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \text{Latency} \leq L_{\max} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Availability} \geq 0.999
$$

Where:
- $L_{\max}$ is the maximum tolerable control-loop latency (typically **1–100 ms**).
- **Availability** is often expressed as the *nines* metric:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
\text{Availability}_{9s} = 1 - 10^{-n} \implies n = -\log_{10}(1 - A)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (Module 3):** A "fail-open" IPS that drops traffic on power loss is **NOT acceptable** in CPS because control traffic must keep flowing. Always use **fail-closed** or **bypass switches**.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Smart Grid:** IEC 61850-aware IPS deployed at substation level (e.g., SEL-3620, Nozomi Networks Guardian).
- **Water Treatment:** Modbus-aware NIDS detects *sodium hydroxide pump overspeed* attacks.
- **Connected Vehicles:** CAN-bus HIPS detects bus-off, fuzzing, and replay attacks.
- **Industrial IoT:** Edge ML-based anomaly detection on Jetson/FPGA nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the Optimal Detection Threshold

We start with the **minimum-error decision rule** in classical detection theory and specialise it to a CPS IDS operating under class imbalance.

### Step 1 — The Hypothesis Pair

We define two mutually exclusive hypotheses:
- $H_0$: The observed packet/event is **benign**.
- $H_1$: The observed packet/event is **malicious**.

The prior probabilities are $P(H_0) = P_0$ and $P(H_1) = P_1 = 1 - P_0$.

### Step 2 — Likelihoods

The observation $X$ (e.g., a feature vector) has class-conditional probability densities $f(X \mid H_0)$ and $f(X \mid H_1)$.

### Step 3 — Decision Rule (Maximum A Posteriori)

The MAP rule says: choose $H_1$ if

$$
P(H_1 \mid X) > P(H_0 \mid X)
$$

Applying Bayes' theorem, $P(H_i \mid X) = \dfrac{f(X \mid H_i) P(H_i)}{f(X)}$:

$$
\dfrac{f(X \mid H_1) P_1}{f(X)} > \dfrac{f(X \mid H_0) P_0}{f(X)}
$$

Cancelling $f(X) > 0$:

$$
\frac{f(X \mid H_1)}{f(X \mid H_0)} > \frac{P_0}{P_1}
$$

### Step 4 — The Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)

Define the **likelihood ratio** as $\Lambda(X) = \dfrac{f(X \mid H_1)}{f(X \mid H_0)}$.

The decision rule becomes:

$$
\text{Decide } H_1 \iff \Lambda(X) > \eta \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = \frac{P_0}{P_1}
$$

### Step 5 — Monotone Transformation (Log-Likelihood)

Taking the natural logarithm (a strictly monotone transformation, so decision boundaries are preserved):

$$
\ln \Lambda(X) > \ln \eta
$$

For a CPS, if the likelihoods are multivariate Gaussians with means $\mu_0, \mu_1$ and common covariance $\Sigma$, then:

$$
\ln \Lambda(X) = \left(\mu_1 - \mu_0\right)^\top \Sigma^{-1} X - \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\mu_1 - \mu_0\right)^\top \Sigma^{-1}\left(\mu_1 + \mu_0\right)
$$

The decision rule is therefore a **linear** classifier (a hyperplane in feature space).

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario.** A CPS IDS monitors pump RPM. Benign RPM values follow $X \sim \mathcal{N}(1500, 100^2)$ (rpm, variance in rpm²). An attacker forcing an overspeed produces $X \sim \mathcal{N}(1900, 100^2)$. The prior probabilities are $P_0 = 0.99$ (benign) and $P_1 = 0.01$ (attack). Find the optimal threshold $\tau$ that minimises total error.

**Step 6.1** — From the LRT:

$$
\frac{f(X \mid H_1)}{f(X \mid H_0)} > \frac{P_0}{P_1} = \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 99
$$

**Step 6.2** — Substituting Gaussian likelihoods:

$$
\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\left(-\frac{(X-1900)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\left(-\frac{(X-1500)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)} > 99
$$

The $1/(\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma)$ factors cancel. Taking logarithms:

$$
-\frac{(X-1900)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{(X-1500)^2}{2\sigma^2} > \ln 99
$$

**Step 6.3** — Multiply both sides by $2\sigma^2$:

$$
(X-1500)^2 - (X-1900)^2 > 2\sigma^2 \ln 99
$$

**Step 6.4** — Use the difference-of-squares identity $(a^2 - b^2) = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$
\bigl[(X-1500) - (X-1900)\bigr]\bigl[(X-1500) + (X-1900)\bigr] > 2\sigma^2 \ln 99
$$

**Step 6.5** — Simplify each bracket:

$$
(400)\,(2X - 3400) > 2\sigma^2 \ln 99
$$

**Step 6.6** — Solve for $X$:

$$
2X - 3400 > \frac{2\sigma^2 \ln 99}{400} = \frac{2 \cdot 100^2 \cdot 4.595}{400}
$$

$$
2X - 3400 > \frac{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 4.595}{400} = \frac{91900}{400} = 229.75
$$

$$
2X > 3629.75 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X > 1814.875
$$

**Result:** The **optimal decision threshold** is $\tau = 1814.875$ rpm. Any RPM reading above this triggers an alert.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Takeaway:** Notice that the threshold lies **between** the two means (1500 and 1900) but is **shifted toward the rarer class $H_1$** because of the class-imbalance cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: F1-Score and Why CPS Favours High Recall

The F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. We start from the definition:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{\text{Precision}} + \frac{1}{\text{Recall}}}
$$

Substituting the KTU 2024 definitions (with $P_0 + P_1 = 1$ and $\text{TPR} = \text{Recall}$):

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \, TP}{2 \, TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Why F1 is preferred over accuracy in CPS:**
- CPS datasets are **highly imbalanced** (attacks $\ll$ benign).
- A trivial classifier that says "always benign" gives accuracy $\approx 99\%$ but **F1 = 0**.

For a CPS scenario, the engineer may want to penalize false negatives more (missed attacks can damage equipment). The **$F_\beta$** score generalises this:

$$
F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

- $\beta > 1$ → recall-weighted (favours catching attacks).
- $\beta < 1$ → precision-weighted (favours trustworthy alerts).

In CPS, $\beta = 2$ is the **KTU-recommended default** because missing an attack on a turbine is far costlier than an operator investigating a few false alarms.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IDS vs. IPS Trade-offs in CPS

| Evaluation Dimension | Pure IDS (Out-of-Band) | Pure IPS (Inline) | Hybrid IDS+IPS (Best Practice) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Latency | 100 ms – 1 s | 10 – 100 µs (hardware-accelerated) | 1 – 50 ms |
| Impact on Real-Time Loop | None (passive tap) | Adds jitter | Tunable |
| Failure Mode | No traffic loss | Fail-open = unsafe; fail-closed = DoS | Graceful bypass |
| False Positive Cost | Low (operator investigates) | High (blocks real traffic → safety trip) | Mitigated by IDS pre-filter |
| Coverage | L1–L5 | L1–L5 (selective) | All layers |
| Cost | Low | High (line-rate hardware) | Medium |
| KTU Marks (justification) | 2/10 | 3/10 | **10/10** |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Insight:** KTU 2024 examiners award 7/7 marks only to answers that explicitly choose the **hybrid IDS + IPS architecture with industrial firewall in the L3.5 DMZ**, citing the Purdue Model.

---

## 3.4 ROC Curve Construction — Step-by-Step

1. Train an anomaly detector producing a continuous score $S(X)$.
2. Sort all $N$ validation samples by $S(X)$ descending.
3. Walk the threshold $\tau$ from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$.
4. At each step, classify: above $\tau$ = $H_1$, below = $H_0$.
5. Compute $(\text{FPR}(\tau), \text{TPR}(\tau))$.
6. Plot TPR vs. FPR; the area is **AUC**.

**Ideal CPS ROC curve:** hugs the top-left corner, with $\text{AUC} \geq 0.99$ at low FPR $\leq 0.001$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Purdue Model with IDS/IPS Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["LAYER 0: Physical Process - Sensors Actuators Motors"]
    L1["LAYER 1: Basic Control - PLC RTU DCS Controllers"]
    L2["LAYER 2: Supervisory Control - SCADA HMI Engineering Workstation"]
    L3["LAYER 3: Operations - Historian MES Asset Management"]
    L3_5["LAYER 3.5: Industrial DMZ - Firewall Jump Host IPS"]
    L4["LAYER 4: Enterprise - ERP Cloud Internet"]

    HIDS1["HIPS Module - Protocol Aware on PLC"]
    NIDS1["NIDS Module - Modbus DNP3 Tap"]
    HIDS2["HIDS Module - Log File Integrity"]
    NIPS1["NIPS Module - Industrial Stateful Inline"]
    NIPS2["NGFW IPS Module - Enterprise Edge"]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L3_5
    L3_5 --> L4

    HIDS1 -.monitors.-> L1
    NIDS1 -.passive tap.-> L1
    HIDS2 -.monitors.-> L2
    NIPS1 -.inline.-> L3_5
    NIPS2 -.inline.-> L4
```

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Functional Block Diagram of CPS with Integrated IDS+IPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhysicalWorld["Physical World"]
        Sensor1["Sensor: Temperature"]
        Sensor2["Sensor: Pressure"]
        Actuator1["Actuator: Pump"]
        Actuator2["Actuator: Valve"]
    end

    subgraph FieldNetwork["Field Network L1"]
        PLC1["PLC Controller"]
        RTU1["RTU"]
    end

    subgraph ControlNetwork["Control Network L2"]
        SCADA1["SCADA Server"]
        HMI1["HMI Console"]
    end

    subgraph SecurityLayer["Security Monitoring Layer"]
        TAP1["Network TAP - Passive Mirror"]
        IDSEngine["IDS Engine: Signature Anomaly Specification"]
        IPSEngine["IPS Engine: Inline Stateful"]
        LogStore["SIEM Log Store"]
        SOCAnalyst["SOC Analyst Console"]
    end

    Sensor1 --> PLC1
    Sensor2 --> RTU1
    PLC1 --> Actuator1
    RTU1 --> Actuator2
    PLC1 --> SCADA1
    RTU1 --> SCADA1
    SCADA1 --> HMI1
    PLC1 -.tap.-> TAP1
    SCADA1 -.tap.-> TAP1
    TAP1 --> IDSEngine
    IDSEngine --> LogStore
    LogStore --> SOCAnalyst
    IDSEngine -- alert --> IPSEngine
    IPSEngine -- block action --> PLC1
    IPSEngine -- block action --> SCADA1
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Decision Flow of a CPS-Aware IPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Packet Arrives at Inline IPS"]) --> parse["Step A: Protocol Parser Modbus DNP3 IEC 61850"]
    parse --> sig["Step B: Signature Match Against Rule DB"]
    parse --> spec["Step C: Specification Check Against State Machine"]
    parse --> ml["Step D: ML Anomaly Score via Trained Model"]
    sig -->|match found| action1["DROP and LOG"]
    spec -->|violation| action2["RESET and QUARANTINE"]
    ml -->|score greater than threshold| action3["RATE LIMIT and ALERT SOC"]
    sig -->|no match| allow["FORWARD to Control Network"]
    spec -->|no violation| allow
    ml -->|score below threshold| allow
    action1 --> logdb["SIEM Log Store"]
    action2 --> logdb
    action3 --> logdb
    allow --> done(["Traffic Delivered"])
    logdb --> done
```

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Deployment Topology for a Smart-Grid Substation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Cloud["Cloud Control Center"]
    WANRouter["WAN Edge Router"]
    FW["Industrial Firewall Layer 3.5 DMZ"]
    IPScore["IPS Core: Stateful IEC 61850"]
    IDScore["IDS Core: GOOSE SMV Anomaly ML"]
    Subnet1["Substation LAN"]
    IED1["IED Bay 1 Breaker Controller"]
    IED2["IED Bay 2 Transformer Monitor"]
    IED3["IED Bay 3 Busbar Protection"]
    HMIop["Operator HMI"]

    Cloud <--> WANRouter
    WANRouter <--> FW
    FW <--> IPScore
    IPScore <--> IDScore
    IPScore <--> Subnet1
    IDScore -.passive tap.-> Subnet1
    Subnet1 <--> IED1
    Subnet1 <--> IED2
    Subnet1 <--> IED3
    Subnet1 <--> HMIop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams (KTU 2024 Tip):** Notice that the IDS is always on a *passive* tap (dotted lines), while the IPS is *inline* (solid lines). This is the universal convention and examiners expect students to draw it this way.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Highlight any two key differences.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. **Operational Mode:** IDS is *passive*; it only monitors and raises alerts without interrupting traffic. IPS is *active* and *inline*; it can drop, reset, or block malicious traffic in real time.
2. **Latency Introduced:** IDS introduces negligible latency as it operates on a mirrored port. IPS, being inline, adds **0.1–10 ms** latency, which must be bounded in CPS control loops.
3. **Placement:** IDS is deployed on a network TAP or SPAN port. IPS is placed inline (e.g., as a bump-in-the-wire).
4. **Failure Behaviour:** IDS failure = no effect. IPS failure = either fail-open (dangerous in CPS) or fail-closed (DoS).

*(Any two differences, each explained in 1–2 lines, fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three CPS-specific protocols that an IDS/IPS must be protocol-aware of.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Modbus TCP** — for legacy SCADA/PLCs.
- **DNP3** — for electric and water substations.
- **IEC 61850 (GOOSE/SMV)** — for substation automation.
- **Profinet / EtherCAT** — for real-time motion control.
- **OPC-UA** — for industrial IoT interoperability.

*(Any three names with one-line use-case each = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **one** of the two choices. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture for integrating a **hybrid IDS + IPS** system into a Cyber-Physical System following the **Purdue Reference Model**. State the role of the L3.5 Industrial DMZ.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Purdue Layer Identification:** Identify the six layers (L0–L5) and assign security controls:

- **L0–L1 (Process / Basic Control):** Deploy **HIPS** on the PLC/RTU and a **passive NIDS** tap on the fieldbus. The NIDS inspects Modbus/DNP3 function codes for unauthorised write attempts.

- **L2 (Supervisory):** Deploy **HIDS** on SCADA servers to monitor log integrity, configuration changes, and unauthorised access. Place a **stateful protocol NIDS** on the control LAN.

- **L3 (Operations):** Place a **NIPS** at the L3–L3.5 boundary to inspect traffic to/from historians, MES, and remote engineering stations.

- **L3.5 — Industrial DMZ (KEY POINT):** This is the **security buffer zone** between the OT (L0–L3) and IT (L4–L5) worlds. It contains the industrial firewall, jump-host, antivirus/IPS, and a data diode or unidirectional gateway. **The L3.5 DMZ is the single most important choke point** for IDS/IPS integration.

- **L4–L5 (Enterprise):** Deploy a Next-Generation Firewall with **enterprise NIPS** for cloud/EU/internet traffic.

**Step 2 — Diagram:** *(Refer to Figure 1 in SECTION_4.)*

**Step 3 — Role of L3.5 DMZ:**
- Enforces **strict ingress/egress filtering**.
- Provides a **jump host** for vendor remote access (with MFA and session recording).
- Hosts **centralised SIEM** to aggregate IDS/IPS alerts.
- Prevents direct internet reachability of OT devices.

*(A labelled diagram = 3 marks; layer-wise IDS/IPS mapping = 3 marks; L3.5 explanation = 1 mark. Total = 7/7.)*

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]** A CPS intrusion detection system produces the following confusion matrix on a validation set of 10,000 events:

- True Positives (TP) = 80
- False Positives (FP) = 20
- True Negatives (TN) = 9,800
- False Negatives (FN) = 100

**Compute: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall (TPR), (iv) F1-Score, (v) FPR, (vi) Specificity, (vii) MCC. Comment on whether the IDS is suitable for a safety-critical CPS deployment.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{80 + 9800}{10000} = \frac{9880}{10000} = 0.988
$$

**Step (ii) — Precision:**

$$
