\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

**Step (iii) — Recall (TPR):**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 100} = \frac{80}{180} \approx 0.444
$$

**Step (iv) — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \cdot 0.444}{0.80 + 0.444} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.3555}{1.244} \approx 0.571
$$

**Step (v) — FPR:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 9800} = \frac{20}{9820} \approx 0.00204
$$

**Step (vi) — Specificity (TNR):**

$$
\text{TNR} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{9800}{9820} \approx 0.998
$$

**Step (vii) — MCC:**

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN = (80 \times 9800) - (20 \times 100) = 784000 - 2000 = 782000
$$

Denominator:

$$
\sqrt{(100)(180)(9820)(9900)} = \sqrt{100 \cdot 180 \cdot 9820 \cdot 9900}
$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$
100 \cdot 180 = 18000
$$

$$
9820 \cdot 9900 = 97,218,000
$$

$$
18000 \cdot 97{,}218{,}000 = 1.74992 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.74992 \times 10^{12}} \approx 1.3228 \times 10^6
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{MCC} \approx \frac{782{,}000}{1{,}322{,}800} \approx 0.591
$$

**Comment on Suitability for Safety-Critical CPS:**

Although the **accuracy is 98.8%** (looks impressive), the **recall is only 44.4%**, meaning **more than half of the actual attacks (FN = 100) are missed**. In a safety-critical CPS (e.g., nuclear plant, hospital ICU), missing an attack is unacceptable. Furthermore, **F1 = 0.571** and **MCC = 0.591** are moderate. **Conclusion: This IDS is NOT suitable** for safety-critical CPS without retraining or threshold tuning to favour recall (use $F_2$ score, $\beta = 2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students **divide by total samples** for Precision → WRONG. Always $TP / (TP + FP)$.
> 2. Students forget to take the **square root** in MCC.
> 3. Students compute "accuracy" and stop — KTU gives **0 marks for part (vii)** unless MCC is computed.
> 4. Most importantly: students **skip the "comment"** at the end. A correct computation without the qualitative safety comment loses **2 of 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the three major detection methodologies used in CPS intrusion detection systems — **signature-based, anomaly-based, and specification-based** — and compare them on detection latency, false-positive rate, and zero-day capability.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection):**
- Maintains a database of known attack patterns (Snort rules, YARA signatures, YASMIN-style templates).
- The IDS performs pattern matching on incoming traffic/logs.
- **Pros:** Very low FPR, fast (sub-millisecond), explainable alerts.
- **Cons:** **Zero-day blind** — cannot detect novel attacks.
- **Latency:** O(matches × log of pattern length) → typically **microseconds**.
- **Best Use:** Known ICS malware (e.g., Stuxnet, BlackEnergy, TRITON).

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection:**
- Learns a model of "normal" CPS behaviour (one-class SVM, Isolation Forest, autoencoders, LSTM).
- Anomalies are deviations from this baseline.
- **Pros:** Detects **zero-day attacks**.
- **Cons:** Higher FPR; requires clean training data; suffers from concept drift (sensor recalibration may be flagged as attack).
- **Latency:** **milliseconds to seconds** depending on model complexity.
- **Mathematical core:** For multivariate Gaussian, use the Mahalanobis distance formula $S(X) = (X - \mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (X - \mu)$ and threshold via chi-square distribution.

**3. Specification-Based (Stateful) Detection:**
- Manual encoding of the **expected sequence** of CPS commands (e.g., "operator must authenticate before issuing a write command").
- Detection occurs when observed behaviour **violates** the specification.
- **Pros:** Near-zero FPR; semantically meaningful; explainable.
- **Cons:** Expert-intensive; brittle to legitimate but unseen behaviour.
- **Latency:** **microseconds** (state-machine traversal).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Signature | Anomaly | Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Latency | µs | ms – s | µs |
| False Positive Rate | Very Low | High | Very Low |
| Zero-Day Detection | **No** | **Yes** | Partial |
| Training Effort | None | High | Manual |
| Explainability | High | Low | Very High |
| Best for CPS Layer | L2–L3 | L1–L2 | L0–L1 |

*(Comparison table = 3 marks; each methodology explained in 1.3 lines = 4 marks. Total = 7/7.)*

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key challenges** in integrating IDS/IPS into CPS, and propose a layered integration strategy that addresses **real-time constraints, legacy devices, and safety implications**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Key Challenges:**

- **Real-Time Constraints:** CPS control loops demand deterministic latency (often 1–10 ms). Inline IPS can introduce jitter that destabilises controllers.
- **Legacy & Proprietary Devices:** Old PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-300) cannot host host-based agents and use proprietary fieldbus protocols.
- **Safety Implications:** A false positive that blocks a coolant pump command can cause a safety trip or even physical damage.
- **Patchability:** Many OT devices cannot be rebooted for years; signature updates must be hot-deployable.
- **Class Imbalance:** Benign events vastly outnumber attacks, biasing ML models.
- **Concept Drift:** Process changes (e.g., seasonal load variations) cause false alarms.

**2. Layered Integration Strategy (Proposed):**

- **Layer 0 — Process-Aware Specification Rules:** Encode expected physical invariants (e.g., $\Delta T / \Delta t \leq T_{\max}$, pump RPM within bounds). Violation = immediate alert to safety PLC.

- **Layer 1 — Protocol-Aware NIDS:** Passive tap on fieldbus. Deep-parse Modbus/DNP3/IEC 61850. Detect malformed function codes, register out-of-range writes, and replay attacks.

- **Layer 2 — Hybrid IDS with Anomaly ML:** Apply lightweight unsupervised ML (Isolation Forest on edge device) to SCADA logs and HMI events. Use a **dual threshold** to balance FPR and FNR.

- **Layer 3 — Industrial NIPS at L3.5 DMZ:** Inline, stateful, protocol-aware. **Fail-open with safety override** — drops only confirmed malicious traffic.

- **Layer 4 — SIEM + SOAR Integration:** Centralise alerts, run **playbook-based** automated response (e.g., isolate compromised IED, notify operator).

- **Continuous Validation:** Use a **digital twin** of the CPS to validate IDS/IPS rules offline before live deployment.

*(5 challenges identified with explanation = 3.5 marks; 6-layer strategy = 3.5 marks. Total = 7/7.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often **omit the safety implication** of false positives — this loses **1 mark**.
> 2. Students **do not mention fail-open vs. fail-closed** for the IPS — this is a **mandatory** concept in KTU 2024 valuation, losing **1 mark** if missing.
> 3. **"Digital twin"** is a high-yield term; including it boosts the answer to full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before the Exam):**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **CPS** = Cyber + Physical systems with feedback loops.
- **IDS** = Passive; detects + reports.
- **IPS** = Active (inline); detects + reports + **blocks**.
- **NIDS/HIDS** = Network/Host-based IDS.
- **NIPS/HIPS** = Network/Host-based IPS.
- **L3.5 DMZ** = Critical security buffer between OT and IT.

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas
- TPR $= TP / (TP+FN)$
- FPR $= FP / (FP+TN)$
- Precision $= TP / (TP+FP)$
- F1 $= 2TP / (2TP + FP + FN)$
- MCC $= (TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN) / \sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}$
- Mahalanobis anomaly score $S = (X - \mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (X - \mu)$
- MAP/LRT rule: choose $H_1$ if $\Lambda(X) > P_0/P_1$

### 🏗️ Must-Know Architectures
- **Purdue Reference Model (L0–L5)**
- **ISA/IEC 62443 Zones & Conduits**
- **Defense-in-Depth IDS+IPS layering**
- **L3.5 Industrial DMZ**

### 📜 Must-Know Protocols
- **Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850 (GOOSE/SMV), Profinet, EtherCAT, OPC-UA, CAN bus**

### ⚠️ Common Examiner Traps
- IT security ≠ OT/CPS security (availability > integrity > confidentiality).
- IPS must be **fail-safe** (fail-open) for non-critical; **fail-closed** for safety zones.
- IDS on **SPAN/TAP**; IPS **inline** — never swap them.
- **Class imbalance** → prefer F1, MCC, ROC-AUC over plain accuracy.
- **Stuxnet, TRITON, BlackEnergy** — real-world case studies; quoting them = bonus marks.

### 🎯 Single-Sentence Mantra
> *"In CPS, the IDS watches, the IPS acts, the firewall isolates, the SIEM records, the human decides — and the safety PLC always has the last word."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation and Tuning of IDS/IPS Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation and Tuning of IDS/IPS Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Performance Evaluation and Tuning of IDS/IPS Systems** is the systematic process of quantifying, analyzing, and optimizing the operational effectiveness of Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) within Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). It encompasses the measurement of **detection accuracy** (ability to correctly identify malicious activity), **throughput** (packets/second processed), **latency** (delay introduced into network flows), and **resource utilization** (CPU, memory, I/O), followed by iterative refinement of rule-sets, threshold parameters, and architectural deployment to achieve an optimal **Security-Utility Trade-off**.

In the context of CPS (SCADA, IoT, Industrial Control), performance evaluation is non-trivial because the system must operate under **hard real-time constraints** (e.g., < **1 ms** response for protective relays) while simultaneously parsing **deep-packet OT protocols** such as Modbus/TCP (port **502**), DNP3 (port **20000**), EtherNet/IP, and IEC 60870-5-104.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT863 – Module 3):**
> The course outcome **CO3** mandates that students must be able to *“evaluate, benchmark, and tune IDS/IPS configurations for CPS environments using standard detection metrics and ROC-based analysis.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine the IDS/IPS as a **TSA security lane at a busy international airport**:

- **True Positive (TP)** = A genuine smuggler carrying contraband is correctly caught by the X-ray scanner.
- **False Positive (FP)** = An innocent grandmother with too much shampoo is flagged as a smuggler (Type I error) — this *delays* the entire queue and frustrates travelers.
- **False Negative (FN)** = A clever smuggler with a hidden compartment slips past undetected (Type II error) — this is *catastrophic* for airport safety.
- **True Negative (TN)** = An innocent traveler passes through cleanly with no alarm.

The airport manager must **tune** the X-ray sensitivity: too sensitive, and every grandmother is detained (high FP); too lax, and smugglers pass (high FN). The same **sensitivity knob** exists in IDS/IPS in the form of **detection thresholds** (e.g., Snort's `threshold.conf`).

In CPS terms, raising an IPS block on a *false* positive may shut down a **Modbus polling cycle** between an HMI and a PLC, causing an entire **assembly line** to halt — costing thousands of rupees per minute. Hence **tuning is not optional — it is mission-critical**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Benchmark Frameworks Used in Industry:**
> - **DARPA 1998/1999** (Lincoln Labs) — historical IDS benchmark dataset.
> - **NSL-KDD** — refined KDD'99 dataset for academic IDS testing.
> - **CICIDS2017 / CIC-DDoS-2019** — modern Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity datasets.
> - **RUBICON / AWID** — wireless IDS benchmarks.
> - **ICS-specific:** *Modbus SCADA* traces, *Power System* datasets (Mississippi State / Oak Ridge).

## 1.3 Why Performance Evaluation is Hard in CPS

| CPS-Specific Challenge | Impact on IDS/IPS |
|---|---|
| **Deterministic timing** (1–100 ms control loops) | IPS latency can break closed-loop control |
| **Legacy OT protocols** (Modbus, DNP3 lack auth) | Signature set is protocol-specific |
| **Resource-constrained PLCs/RTUs** | IDS may run on edge device with < **256 MB RAM** |
| **Air-gapped + IIoT convergence** | Hybrid traffic (IT + OT) confuses classifiers |
| **24/7 availability mandate** | Tuning cannot require downtime windows |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of IDS Evaluation

Every alert generated by an IDS can be classified into exactly one of four categories, forming a **2×2 Confusion Matrix**:

| | **Actual Attack (Positive)** | **Actual Benign (Negative)** |
|---|---|---|
| **IDS Alarmed (Predicted Positive)** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **IDS Silent (Predicted Negative)** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> In a CPS, **FN = safety hazard** (unprotected physical damage), while **FP = operational cost** (unnecessary process shutdown). The tuning objective is to **minimize FN first**, then **minimize FP** subject to resource constraints.

## 2.2 Derivation of Core Performance Metrics

### 2.2.1 Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)
$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{TP}{P}$$

This is the *probability of detection* — the fraction of actual attacks the IDS successfully flagged. In military terminology, this is the **Probability of Detection (Pd)**.

### 2.2.2 False Alarm Rate (False Positive Rate)
$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{FP}{N}$$

This is the fraction of benign traffic incorrectly flagged. The complementary quantity is **Specificity**:
$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - FPR$$

### 2.2.3 Miss Rate (False Negative Rate)
$$FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = 1 - TPR$$

### 2.2.4 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)
$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

High precision means *when the IDS alarms, it is usually right* — critical for SOC analysts to avoid alert fatigue.

### 2.2.5 F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision & Recall)
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

The F1-Score is the **single most-cited metric in KTU model-comparison answers** because it balances both error types into one scalar in $[0, 1]$.

### 2.2.6 Accuracy
$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Base-Rate Fallacy Trap (Frequently Tested in KTU):** If only **1 in 10,000** packets is malicious, an IDS that *always outputs "benign"* achieves **99.99% accuracy** but **0% detection rate**. Hence, for **highly imbalanced datasets** (the norm in CPS traffic), **Accuracy is misleading** — use **F1-Score** or **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** instead.

### 2.2.7 Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)
$$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

MCC $\in [-1, +1]$, where $+1$ is perfect prediction, $0$ is random, $-1$ is total disagreement. **Robust to class imbalance** — hence preferred for CPS datasets.

### 2.2.8 Throughput & Latency Metrics
$$Throughput_{pps} = \frac{N_{packets}}{T_{wall-clock}} \quad \text{(packets/sec)}$$

$$Latency_{avg} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{out,i} - t_{in,i})$$

For an inline IPS, latency must be **< 1 ms** to be transparent to TCP retransmission timers.

### 2.2.9 ROC Curve & AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** plots TPR (y-axis) vs. FPR (x-axis) as the **detection threshold** is swept from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

- $AUC = 1.0$ → perfect classifier
- $AUC = 0.5$ → random guessing (diagonal line)
- $AUC < 0.5$ → worse than random (inverted)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **KTU Weight** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TPR / Recall / Detection Rate | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | How many attacks we catch |
| FPR / False Alarm Rate | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | How often we cry wolf |
| FNR / Miss Rate | $\dfrac{FN}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐⭐ | Probability of missing an attack |
| Precision | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐⭐ | Trustworthiness of an alarm |
| F1-Score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | Balanced single-number score |
| Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{Total}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐ | Misleading under imbalance |
| Specificity | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | ⭐ | True negative rate |
| MCC | $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1,1]$ | ⭐⭐ | Imbalance-robust quality |
| AUC | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR \, d(FPR)$ | $[0.5,1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | Threshold-independent quality |
| Throughput | $\dfrac{N_{pkt}}{\Delta t}$ | $(0,\infty)$ | ⭐⭐ | Packets/sec processed |
| Latency | $t_{out}-t_{in}$ | $(0,\infty)$ | ⭐⭐ | Inline delay introduced |

## 2.4 The IDS/IPS Tuning Trade-off Frontier

Tuning is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization**:

$$\min_{w, \theta} \;\left\{ \alpha \cdot FPR(w,\theta) + \beta \cdot FNR(w,\theta) + \gamma \cdot Latency(w,\theta) \right\}$$

subject to:

$$Throughput(w,\theta) \geq Throughput_{SLA}$$

$$CPU(w,\theta) \leq 80\%$$

where $w$ = rule weights, $\theta$ = detection thresholds, and $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ are KTU/CPS-mandated priority weights. Typically in CPS, $\beta \gg \alpha$ (we tolerate FP-induced shutdowns less than attacks slipping through — depending on process criticality).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This evaluation framework is used in:
- **NIST SP 800-94 Rev. 1** — Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations** — annual vendor IDS/IPS benchmarking.
- **NERC CIP-007-6 R4** — requires logging and event detection with auditable performance metrics for North American power grids.
- **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 6.2** — mandates continuous monitoring with defined detection SLAs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
> A Snort IDS deployed in a CPS substation processes **10,000** network packets over one hour. Out of these, **100** packets are part of genuine Modbus-based reconnaissance attacks. The IDS generates **130** alerts. Of the **130** alerts, the SOC confirms that **85** correspond to actual attacks and the remaining **45** are false alarms. Compute: TPR, FPR, FNR, Precision, F1-Score, Accuracy, and MCC.

### Step 1 — Identify Ground-Truth Quantities
- Total packets = $N_{total} = 10{,}000$
- Actual attacks (Positives) = $P = 100$
- Actual benign (Negatives) = $N = 10{,}000 - 100 = 9{,}900$
- Total IDS alerts = $130$ (these are all "predicted positive")
- Correctly identified attacks = $TP = 85$ (given)
- False alarms = $FP = 130 - 85 = 45$
- Missed attacks = $FN = P - TP = 100 - 85 = 15$
- Correctly ignored benign = $TN = N - FP = 9{,}900 - 45 = 9{,}855$

**Verification (conservation law):**
$$TP + FP + FN + TN = 85 + 45 + 15 + 9{,}855 = 10{,}000 \; \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Compute True Positive Rate (Detection Rate)

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85$$

$$\boxed{TPR = 85.00\%}$$

This means Snort caught **85 out of every 100** Modbus reconnaissance attempts.

### Step 3 — Compute False Positive Rate

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{45}{45 + 9{,}855} = \frac{45}{9{,}900} \approx 0.004545$$

$$\boxed{FPR \approx 0.4545\%}$$

This means out of **9,900** benign packets, only **45** were wrongly flagged — a low false-alarm burden.

### Step 4 — Compute False Negative Rate (Miss Rate)

$$FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15$$

$$\boxed{FNR = 15.00\%}$$

**15%** of attacks were missed — a critical figure for the safety engineer.

### Step 5 — Compute Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{85}{85 + 45} = \frac{85}{130} \approx 0.6538$$

$$\boxed{Precision \approx 65.38\%}$$

When Snort *alarms*, there is a **65.38%** chance the alarm is a true attack.

### Step 6 — Compute F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6538 \cdot 0.85}{0.6538 + 0.85}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5558}{1.5038} = 2 \cdot 0.3696$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.7392}$$

### Step 7 — Compute Accuracy

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{N_{total}} = \frac{85 + 9{,}855}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}940}{10{,}000} = 0.9940$$

$$\boxed{Accuracy = 99.40\%}$$

### Step 8 — Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)

$$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

**Numerator:**
$$TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN = (85)(9{,}855) - (45)(15) = 837{,}675 - 675 = 837{,}000$$

**Denominator (each factor):**
$$(TP+FP) = 85 + 45 = 130$$
$$(TP+FN) = 85 + 15 = 100$$
$$(TN+FP) = 9{,}855 + 45 = 9{,}900$$
$$(TN+FN) = 9{,}855 + 15 = 9{,}870$$

**Denominator product under root:**
$$130 \times 100 \times 9{,}900 \times 9{,}870$$

Compute pairwise:
$$130 \times 100 = 13{,}000$$
$$9{,}900 \times 9{,}870 = 9{,}900 \times 9{,}870$$

Expanding:
$$9{,}900 \times 9{,}870 = (9{,}900 \times 10{,}000) - (9{,}900 \times 130) = 99{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}287{,}000 = 97{,}713{,}000$$

Therefore:
$$13{,}000 \times 97{,}713{,}000 = 1{,}270{,}269{,}000{,}000$$

Square root:
$$\sqrt{1{,}270{,}269{,}000{,}000} \approx 1{,}127{,}063.5$$

**Final MCC:**
$$MCC = \frac{837{,}000}{1{,}127{,}063.5} \approx 0.7427$$

$$\boxed{MCC \approx 0.7427}$$

The MCC is close to the F1-Score (0.7392) in this case, as expected for a moderately imbalanced dataset.

## 3.2 ROC Curve Construction — Analytical Walkthrough

Consider a **logistic-regression-based IDS** that outputs a probability score $\hat{p} \in [0, 1]$ for each packet. By sweeping the decision threshold $\tau$:

| $\tau$ | TP | FP | TN | FN | TPR | FPR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 100 | 9,900 | 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 0.2 | 95 | 800 | 9,100 | 5 | 0.950 | 0.081 |
| 0.4 | 90 | 200 | 9,700 | 10 | 0.900 | 0.020 |
| 0.5 | 85 | 45 | 9,855 | 15 | 0.850 | 0.0045 |
| 0.7 | 70 | 8 | 9,892 | 30 | 0.700 | 0.0008 |
| 0.9 | 40 | 1 | 9,899 | 60 | 0.400 | 0.0001 |
| 1.0 | 0 | 0 | 9,900 | 100 | 0.000 | 0.000 |

Plotting (TPR, FPR) pairs from the table produces the ROC curve. The **AUC** is the area under this curve — for CPS, an AUC $\geq 0.95$ is considered deployment-grade.

## 3.3 Threshold Tuning Strategy — Cost-Sensitive Selection

Let $C_{FN}$ = cost of a missed attack (in CPS, may equal **safety incident cost**), and $C_{FP}$ = cost of a false alarm (e.g., operator investigation time). The **optimal threshold** $\tau^*$ minimizes expected cost:

$$\tau^* = \arg\min_{\tau} \; \left\{ C_{FN} \cdot FN(\tau) + C_{FP} \cdot FP(\tau) \right\}$$

For a CPS substation, a reasonable estimate is $C_{FN} = \$1{,}000{,}000$ (worst-case physical damage) and $C_{FP} = \$50$ (operator time). The cost ratio $C_{FN}/C_{FP} = 20{,}000$ — meaning the IDS should be tuned **far more conservatively** (lower threshold = more alarms = accept more FP to avoid FN).

## 3.4 Throughput Bottleneck Analysis

For a Suricata instance in **IDS mode** with multi-threaded packet acquisition:

$$Throughput_{max} = \frac{CPU_{cores} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot IPC}{C_{per-packet}}$$

where $C_{per-packet}$ is the average CPU cycles consumed per packet (typically **8,000–25,000** cycles depending on rule count and protocol depth). For a **2.4 GHz** 4-core CPU with $IPC = 1$:

$$Throughput_{max} \approx \frac{4 \times 2.4 \times 10^9 \times 1}{15{,}000} \approx 640{,}000 \text{ pps}$$

This is the **theoretical ceiling** — real-world performance is typically **40–60%** of this due to memory-access stalls and lock contention.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — IDS/IPS Performance Evaluation & Tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPSTrafficCapture] --> B[PreprocessingPcap]
    B --> C[IDSEngineSnortOrSuricata]
    C --> D[AlertGenerationLog]
    D --> E[GroundTruthLabelling]
    E --> F[ConfusionMatrixBuilder]
    F --> G1[MetricEngine_TPR_FPR_F1]
    F --> G2[ROCCurvePlotter_AUC]
    F --> G3[CostOptimizer_ThresholdTau]
    G1 --> H{PerformanceAcceptable}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -->|No| I[TuningActionLayer]
    I --> I1[AdjustDetectionThreshold]
    I --> I2[PruneRedundantRules]
    I --> I3[EnableHardwareOffload]
    I --> I4[UpdateSignatureDatabase]
    I1 --> C
    I2 --> C
    I3 --> C
    I4 --> C
    H -->|Yes| J[DeploymentApproval]
    J --> K[CPSLiveMonitoring]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Captured CPS traffic flows through preprocessing into the IDS engine, which produces alerts. Ground-truth labels (from a known test corpus like NSL-KDD or a hand-labelled OT dataset) are then aligned with alerts to populate a confusion matrix. Three parallel metric engines (scalar metrics, ROC/AUC, and cost-based threshold optimizer) feed a decision node. If performance fails the KTU-mandated thresholds (e.g., F1 < 0.85 for safety-critical CPS), the tuning action layer intervenes — adjusting thresholds, pruning rules, enabling hardware acceleration, or updating signatures — and the loop repeats until the deployment-approval gate is reached.

## 4.2 Detection Decision Flow — Per-Packet Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[IncomingPacket] --> P1{ParseL2L3L4Headers}
    P1 --> P2{DecodeApplicationProtocol}
    P2 --> P3{ApplySignatureRules}
    P2 --> P4{ApplyAnomalyModel}
    P3 --> P5[ScoreAggregation]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6{ScoreGreaterThanTau}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[GenerateAlert_IPSBlocks]
    P6 -->|No| P8[ForwardPacketSilently]
    P7 --> P9[LogToSIEM]
    P8 --> P9
```

## 4.3 IDS Tuning Iteration Cycle — KTU Exam-Friendly Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[BaselineBenchmark] --> S2[IdentifyBottleneckCPU_MEM_IO]
    S2 --> S3[SelectTuningKnob]
    S3 --> S4[ApplyConfigurationChange]
    S4 --> S5[ReBenchmark]
    S5 --> S6{MetricImproved}
    S6 -->|No| S2
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[DocumentConfigurationDelta]
    S7 --> S1
```

This cyclic diagram is the standard **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** approach applied to IDS/IPS tuning, and is frequently asked in KTU's 14-mark question on "describe the IDS tuning methodology for CPS."

## 4.4 Threshold Sensitivity vs Operating Point (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[LowThresholdTau_Aggressive] --> O1[HighTPR_BUT_HighFPR]
    T2[MediumThresholdTau_Balanced] --> O2[ModerateTPR_LowFPR]
    T3[HighThresholdTau_Conservative] --> O3[LowTPR_VeryLowFPR]
    O1 --> D1[OperatorAlertFatigue]
    O2 --> D2[RecommendedForMostCPS]
    O3 --> D3[MissedAttacks_NotAcceptable]
```
