# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following IDS performance metrics with formulas: (i) Detection Rate, (ii) False Alarm Rate, (iii) F1-Score.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

**(i) Detection Rate (True Positive Rate):** The probability that an actual attack is correctly flagged by the IDS.
$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**(ii) False Alarm Rate (False Positive Rate):** The fraction of benign traffic incorrectly classified as malicious.
$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

**(iii) F1-Score:** The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a balanced single-number quality measure robust to class imbalance.
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the "Base-Rate Fallacy" in the context of IDS performance evaluation. Why is accuracy a misleading metric for CPS traffic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Base-Rate Fallacy** arises when the a-priori probability of an attack in the monitored stream is extremely low (e.g., **0.01%** in a CPS). Under this imbalance, a trivial classifier that labels every packet as "benign" achieves near-perfect **accuracy** (≈ 99.99%) but a **0% detection rate** — entirely failing its security purpose.

$$Accuracy_{trivial} = \frac{TN}{TP+TN} \approx 1 \text{ when } TP \ll TN$$

In CPS — where attacks are rare but **catastrophic** when they occur — accuracy is therefore a misleading scalar. **F1-Score, MCC, and AUC** are preferred, as they are sensitive to the rare positive class. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for math, 1 mark for CPS-specific justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a CPS-oriented IDS/IPS**, clearly identifying the packet acquisition, decoding, detection, alerting, and tuning feedback components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CPS network, an IDS analyses **5,000** packets of which **200** are actual attacks. The IDS raises **180** alerts, of which **150** are confirmed true attacks. Compute: **TPR, FPR, Precision, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Comment on whether the IDS is suitable for deployment in a **safety-critical** substation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A(a):**

The CPS-oriented IDS/IPS architecture consists of **six functional blocks**:

1. **Packet Acquisition Layer** — Libpcap/PF_RING/DPDK captures from the OT network TAP, decoupled from production traffic to avoid single-point-of-failure.

2. **Pre-processing / Decoder** — Decodes L2 (Ethernet), L3 (IP), L4 (TCP/UDP), and the **OT application layer** (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 104, EtherNet/IP). This block normalizes the byte stream into a structured event tuple.

3. **Detection Engine** — Two parallel paths:
   - **Signature-based** (e.g., Snort VRT rules matching known CVE patterns).
   - **Anomaly-based** (statistical, ML — One-Class SVM, autoencoder, Isolation Forest).

4. **Alert Generator** — Converts matches into structured events (timestamp, src/dst, signature ID, severity).

5. **Output / Action Module** — In **IDS mode**, logs to SIEM; in **IPS mode**, drops/blocks via TCP RST or NFQUEUE.

6. **Tuning Feedback Loop** — Periodically recomputes TPR/FPR/F1 on a labelled validation set, adjusts thresholds and rule-set, and pushes updated configuration back to the detection engine.

```
[ CPS Traffic ] -> [ TAP ] -> [ Packet Capture ] -> [ Decoder ] -> [ Detection Engine ]
                                                                     |
                                                              [ Alert Generator ]
                                                                     |
                                                              [ SIEM / IPS Block ]
                                                                     ^
                                                                     |
                                                          [ Tuning Feedback ]
```

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for each of the 3 core blocks correctly described; 1 mark for the diagram; 1 mark for naming CPS-specific protocols like Modbus/DNP3.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:**
- $N_{total} = 5{,}000$
- $P = 200$ (actual attacks)
- $N = 5{,}000 - 200 = 4{,}800$ (benign)
- Total alerts = $180$, so $TP + FP = 180$
- $TP = 150$ (given)
- $FP = 180 - 150 = 30$
- $FN = P - TP = 200 - 150 = 50$
- $TN = N - FP = 4{,}800 - 30 = 4{,}770$

**Step 2 — TPR:**
$$TPR = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 \quad (75.00\%)$$

**Step 3 — FPR:**
$$FPR = \frac{30}{4{,}800} = 0.00625 \quad (0.625\%)$$

**Step 4 — Precision:**
$$Precision = \frac{150}{180} \approx 0.8333 \quad (83.33\%)$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 150}{2 \cdot 150 + 30 + 50} = \frac{300}{380} \approx 0.7895 \quad (78.95\%)$$

**Step 6 — Accuracy:**
$$Accuracy = \frac{150 + 4{,}770}{5{,}000} = \frac{4{,}920}{5{,}000} = 0.9840 \quad (98.40\%)$$

**Step 7 — Deployment Verdict:**

For a **safety-critical substation** governed by **IEC 62443 SL-2** and **NERC CIP-007**, the typical industrial acceptance threshold is **F1 ≥ 0.90** and **TPR ≥ 0.95**. Here:
- $TPR = 0.75$ → **unacceptable** (25% of attacks slip through).
- $F_1 = 0.7895$ → **below 0.90 threshold**.

**Verdict:** ❌ The IDS is **NOT suitable** for safety-critical deployment. Recommended remediation: (i) add more **Modbus-specific signatures**, (ii) retrain anomaly model on substation-specific baseline, (iii) lower detection threshold to raise TPR, accepting marginally higher FPR as the acceptable trade-off in safety-first systems.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark each for TPR, FPR, Precision, F1, Accuracy; 2 marks for the deployment verdict with threshold justification.)*

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve and the Area Under the Curve (AUC)** in IDS evaluation. With a sketch, describe how the operating point is selected in a CPS environment where missed attacks have very high cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major tuning techniques** for Snort/Suricata IPS in a CPS, covering: (i) threshold.conf tuning, (ii) rule pruning, (iii) preprocessor optimization, and (iv) hardware offload. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B(a):**

The **ROC curve** is a 2-D plot with **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis and **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis, parameterized by the detection threshold $\tau$. As $\tau$ decreases from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$, the curve sweeps from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$.

**Key properties:**
- The **diagonal** $y = x$ represents a **random classifier** (AUC = 0.5).
- A curve **bowing toward the top-left** indicates better discrimination.
- The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** aggregates performance over **all thresholds** into a single number:
$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

**Operating Point Selection in CPS:**

In a CPS where missed attacks have very high cost ($C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$), the **optimal operating point** lies in the **upper-left region** of the ROC curve, i.e., **low FPR, high TPR**. Concretely, the cost-sensitive threshold is:

$$\tau^* = \arg\min_{\tau} \left\{ C_{FN} \cdot FN(\tau) + C_{FP} \cdot FP(\tau) \right\}$$

For example, with $C_{FN}/C_{FP} = 1000$, the chosen operating point may be at $(FPR = 0.02, \; TPR = 0.97)$ — accepting a small false-alarm load to ensure near-zero misses.

**Sketch (described):** A concave curve from $(0,0)$ rising steeply to $(0.02, 0.97)$, then plateauing to $(1,1)$. The chosen operating point is marked near the top-left "elbow."

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for ROC definition + axes, 2 marks for AUC definition, 2 marks for cost-based operating point selection, 1 mark for sketch.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B(b):**

**Tuning Techniques for Snort/Suricata IPS in CPS:**

**(i) `threshold.conf` Tuning:**
Snort's `threshold.conf` (or `threshold.lua` in Snort 3) allows:
- **Limit** alerts per signature (e.g., `threshold gen_id 1, sig_id 2014001, type limit, track by_src, count 1, seconds 60`) — prevents log flooding.
- **Suppression** of low-severity alerts on trusted HMI IPs.
- For CPS, this is critical to avoid alert fatigue on **routine Modbus polls** (Function Code 3 — Read Holding Registers) which can fire thousands of times per hour.

**(ii) Rule Pruning:**
- Disable rules irrelevant to OT (e.g., SMB, NetBIOS, HTTP browser exploits).
- Enable only the **VRT OT category** (`alert industrial-protocol.rules`).
- Use `oinkmaster` or `pulledpork` to manage rule versioning.
- Typically, pruning reduces **active rules from ~50,000 to ~3,000–5,000**, giving **5–8× throughput improvement**.

**(iii) Preprocessor / Decoder Optimization:**
- Disable unused preprocessors (e.g., `dcerpc2`, `dns`, `http_inspect` if not on network).
- For Modbus/DNP3-only CPS segments, enable only the **modbus** and **dnp3** preprocessors.
- Use **Stream5** with appropriate timeout values for TCP reassembly of OT traffic (typically longer timeouts than IT, since polling is slow).

**(iv) Hardware Offload:**
- Use **Intel DPDK** or **Netronome Agilio CX** smartNICs to bypass kernel packet-copy overhead.
- For Suricata, enable **AF_PACKET** with fan-out load balancing across cores.
- Deploy **FPGA-based pattern matching** for signature engines in high-throughput (> 10 Gbps) CPS backbones.
- Offload SSL/TLS termination to a **dedicated crypto accelerator** to free IDS cores.

**Summary Table:**

| Technique | Throughput Gain | Latency Reduction | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold tuning | Low | Low | Missed attacks if over-suppressed |
| Rule pruning | **5–8×** | Moderate | Reduced coverage |
| Preprocessor opt. | **2–3×** | Moderate | Loss of deep-packet inspection |
| Hardware offload | **10–20×** | High | CapEx cost, driver complexity |

*(Marking scheme: ~1.5 marks per technique + 1 mark for summary table.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in PECPT863 — IDS/IPS Performance Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR with Precision** — TPR is "of all real attacks, how many did we catch?" (vertical recall). Precision is "of all alarms we raised, how many were real?" (predictive value). Mixing them costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `+FN` in the denominator of TPR** — students often write $TPR = TP/(TP+FP)$, which is mathematically wrong.
> 3. **Reporting Accuracy without acknowledging base-rate fallacy** — examiners deduct marks if you do not warn about the imbalanced-dataset caveat.
> 4. **Not specifying units of throughput** — write *packets-per-second (pps)*, not just "throughput." Unit literacy is a 1-mark item.
> 5. **Ignoring CPS context** — generic answers that do not mention **Modbus/DNP3/IEC 104** lose CO3 alignment marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "verdict" in numerical problems** — a 7-mark numerical must end with a **deployment recommendation** (acceptable / not acceptable / needs retuning) referencing an industrial standard (IEC 62443, NERC CIP, NIST SP 800-94).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Confusion Matrix (TP, FP, FN, TN)** is the foundation of every IDS metric.
- ✅ **TPR (Recall) = $TP / (TP+FN)$** — detection capability.
- ✅ **FPR = $FP / (FP+TN)$** — false-alarm burden.
- ✅ **Precision = $TP / (TP+FP)$** — alarm trustworthiness.
- ✅ **F1-Score = $2TP / (2TP+FP+FN)$** — balanced single number; **most cited in KTU answers**.
- ✅ **MCC** is the most robust metric under class imbalance.
- ✅ **AUC** aggregates ROC curve performance into one scalar; **AUC ≥ 0.95 = deployment-grade for CPS**.
- ✅ **Base-Rate Fallacy** → accuracy is misleading for imbalanced data.
- ✅ **Cost-sensitive threshold** $\tau^*$ balances $C_{FN}$ vs $C_{FP}$; CPS sets $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$.
- ✅ **Tuning techniques:** `threshold.conf` limits/suppressions, rule pruning, preprocessor opt., DPDK/smartNIC offload.
- ✅ **CPS-specific protocols:** Modbus/TCP (port 502), DNP3 (port 20000), IEC 60870-5-104 (port 2404), EtherNet/IP (port 44818).
- ✅ **Industrial standards:** NIST SP 800-94, IEC 62443-3-3, NERC CIP-007, MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations.
- ✅ **Inline IPS latency budget:** < **1 ms** to be transparent to TCP retransmission.
- ✅ **Benchmark datasets:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, AWID, and OT-specific (Mississippi State SCADA, Oak Ridge power-system).
- ✅ **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** is the standard tuning methodology expected in KTU descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Best Practices for IDS/IPS Integration and Performance Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Best Practices for IDS/IPS Integration and Performance Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a passive security mechanism that monitors network traffic or host activities within a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) to identify malicious behavior, policy violations, or anomalous patterns, generating alerts without actively interrupting traffic flow. An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the active counterpart that not only detects threats but also automatically implements countermeasures (e.g., dropping packets, blocking sessions, or reconfiguring firewall rules) in real-time.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — which integrate computation, networking, and physical processes (e.g., smart grids, SCADA, autonomous vehicles, IIoT) — IDS/IPS integration must satisfy stringent **deterministic latency**, **safety integrity**, and **real-time control-loop** requirements. According to the **IEC 62443** industrial cybersecurity standard, IDS/IPS are classified as **Security Level (SL)** enforcing controls, typically mapped to **SL-2 to SL-4** depending on threat model severity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The integration of IDS/IPS in CPS differs fundamentally from traditional IT environments because misclassification (false positives/negatives) can cause physical-world damage, not just data loss. A false positive in a smart grid IPS may trigger a cascading shutdown.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Security Analogy**
>
> Think of a CPS environment as a **high-security airport**:
> - **IDS** is like the **CCTV cameras and security officers** who watch screens and announce *"Suspicious person at Gate 7"* over the radio. They *observe* but don't physically intervene.
> - **IPS** is the **armed security guard** who, upon hearing the alert, *physically blocks the door, detains the suspect, and seals the terminal*.
> - In CPS, the "airport" is a power plant, water treatment facility, or autonomous vehicle fleet. The "passengers" are Modbus/DNP3/OPC UA packets, and the "weapons" are zero-day exploits, protocol injection attacks, and command spoofing.
> - **Integration best practices** are the **standard operating procedures (SOPs)** — where cameras are placed, how guards are trained, which doors fail-open vs fail-closed, and how alarms are escalated.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in IDS/IPS Performance Evaluation

The following metrics are standardized by **NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems)** and adopted by KTU for evaluation:

- **True Positive (TP)**: Correctly identified attack
- **False Positive (FP)**: Benign traffic flagged as attack
- **True Negative (TN)**: Correctly identified benign traffic
- **False Negative (FN)**: Missed attack
- **Throughput (T)**: **1 Gbps / 10 Gbps / 40 Gbps** (line-rate processing)
- **Latency (L)**: **< 1 ms** (real-time CPS constraint)
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)**: **< 5 seconds** for critical infrastructure

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that performance optimization in CPS must balance the **trade-off triangle**: *Detection Accuracy ↔ Latency ↔ Resource Utilization*. You cannot maximize all three simultaneously — this is formalized through **Pareto-optimal configurations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IDS/IPS Integration Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

**Phase 1 — Asset Discovery & Network Mapping**
- Enumerate all CPS components: PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, sensors, actuators
- Map industrial protocols: **Modbus TCP (port 502)**, **DNP3 (port 20000)**, **IEC 61850 (GOOSE/MMS)**, **OPC UA (port 4840)**, **PROFINET**
- Identify critical control loops whose disruption would cause physical harm

**Phase 2 — Sensor Placement Strategy**
- **Inline (in-band) deployment** for IPS — traffic physically passes through device
- **Passive tap (out-of-band) deployment** for IDS — mirrored traffic via SPAN port or TAP
- **Bypass switches** (fail-open/fail-closed) to maintain availability during IPS failure
- **Recommended placement points in CPS**:
  * Between IT and OT networks (DMZ boundary)
  * Inside OT zones (between cells, Purdue Levels 1–3)
  * At remote field sites (RTU aggregation points)

**Phase 3 — Detection Engine Configuration**
- **Signature-based detection**: Snort, Suricata rules for known CPS exploits
- **Anomaly-based detection**: Statistical baselines, machine learning models (e.g., One-Class SVM, LSTM autoencoders)
- **Protocol-aware deep packet inspection (DPI)**: Parses Modbus function codes, DNP3 objects
- **Stateful protocol analysis**: Tracks session state, detects command injection

**Phase 4 — Tuning & Baselining**
- Run IDS in **monitor-only mode** for 30–90 days
- Capture "normal" operational patterns (e.g., periodic SCADA polls every 1s)
- Apply **allow-lists** for legitimate traffic; reduce FP noise
- Periodic re-tuning (quarterly) as CPS evolves

**Phase 5 — Correlation, Response, and Forensics**
- Forward logs to **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar, ELK)
- Define **SOAR playbooks** for automated response
- Retain logs for **1–3 years** (NERC-CIP, IEC 62443 requirements)

### 2.2 Performance Optimization Pillars

Optimization operates along **four axes**:

1. **Hardware Acceleration** — Use **DPDK (Data Plane Development Kit)**, **FPGA SmartNICs**, or **GPU offload** for packet processing
2. **Software Optimization** — Multi-threaded packet processing (run-to-completion, pipeline models), lock-free data structures
3. **Algorithmic Optimization** — Early packet discard, flow pre-filtering, rule indexing (Aho-Corasick for signatures)
4. **Architectural Optimization** — Distributed IDS sensors with centralized correlation (hierarchical IDS)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| Metric | Formula | Unit / Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Rate (Recall / TPR)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le TPR \le 1$ | Probability of catching an attack |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ | Benign traffic wrongly flagged |
| **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot TPR}{P + TPR}$ | $0 \le F1 \le 1$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $0 \le Acc \le 1$ | Overall correctness (misleading for imbalanced data) |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $-1 \le MCC \le 1$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced datasets |
| **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_{packets}}{\Delta t}$ | pps / Gbps | Packets processed per second |
| **Per-Packet Latency** | $L_p = t_{out} - t_{in}$ | $\mu s$ / $ms$ | Time spent inside IPS |
| **Packet Loss Probability** | $P_{loss} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot L_{service}}$ | dimensionless | M/D/1 queueing approximation |
| **Cost-Weighted Detection Score** | $C_{w} = w_1 \cdot TPR - w_2 \cdot FPR$ | dimensionless | Optimizes combined objective |
| **Pareto Optimality Condition** | $\nabla f_1 = -\mu \nabla f_2$ | scalar $\mu$ | Trade-off gradient equality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in KTU board problems involving **ROC curve analysis**, **IDS benchmarking** (e.g., DARPA 1998/1999, KDD Cup 99, CICIDS 2017, SWaT dataset for CPS), and **capacity planning** for IPS deployment in industrial networks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real **Smart Grid SCADA deployment**, an IPS must process **> 100,000 packets/sec** with **< 500 μs latency** to avoid disrupting the **GOOSE message** timing budgets defined in **IEC 61850-5** (Type 1A messages: 3 ms total). Failure to meet these bounds causes **protection relay misoperation** — a direct physical safety hazard. Therefore, **performance optimization is not optional; it is a safety-critical engineering constraint**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput-Latency Trade-off (M/D/1 Queueing Model)

Assume an IPS behaves as an **M/D/1 queue** (Poisson arrivals, Deterministic service, single server). The **Pollaczek-Khinchine mean-value formula** gives the average waiting time.

**Step 1 — Define arrival and service parameters**
- $\lambda$ = mean arrival rate of packets (packets/second)
- $\mu$ = service rate (packets/second)
- Traffic intensity: $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$, with $0 \le \rho < 1$ for stability

**Step 2 — Mean residual service time for deterministic service**
For deterministic service, the variance of service time is zero, $\sigma^2 = 0$. The Pollaczek-Khinchine formula for mean waiting time in an M/G/1 queue reduces to:

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}[S^2]}{2(1 - \rho)}
$$

where $S$ is service time, $\mathbb{E}[S] = 1/\mu$, and $\mathbb{E}[S^2] = 1/\mu^2$ (deterministic).

**Step 3 — Substitute deterministic second moment**

$$
W_q = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot (1/\mu^2)}{2(1 - \rho)} = \dfrac{\rho^2}{2\lambda(1 - \rho)}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time in system (Little's Law + service time)**

$$
W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho^2}{2\lambda(1 - \rho)} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput at saturation ($\rho \to 1$)**

$$
T_{max} = \lim_{\rho \to 1} \lambda = \mu
$$

This proves the fundamental trade-off: as $\rho \to 1$, waiting time $W_q \to \infty$ (queue overflow). To keep latency bounded, IPS must operate at $\rho \le 0.7$ (engineering rule of thumb: **70% utilization ceiling**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why CPS IPS devices are **over-provisioned** (e.g., a 10 Gbps IPS is deployed on a 1 Gbps link) — to ensure $\rho \le 0.1$, giving latency headroom for attack correlation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — F1-Score from Confusion Matrix

**Step 1 — Given confusion matrix values**: TP = 90, FP = 10, FN = 5, TN = 895

**Step 2 — Compute Precision**

$$
P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 10} = \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.90
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recall (TPR)**

$$
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 5} = \dfrac{90}{95} \approx 0.947
$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score**

$$
F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot TPR}{P + TPR} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.947}{0.90 + 0.947}
$$

$$
F1 = \dfrac{1.7046}{1.847} \approx 0.923
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**: An F1 of **0.923** indicates excellent balance between catching attacks and avoiding false alarms — suitable for CPS critical infrastructure.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pareto-Optimal Trade-off Between Detection and Latency

**Step 1 — Define objective functions**
- $f_1(x) = TPR(x)$ (maximize detection rate)
- $f_2(x) = -L(x)$ (minimize latency, equivalent to maximizing $-L$)
- $x$ = configuration parameter (e.g., rule set size, inspection depth)

**Step 2 — Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) optimality condition for Pareto frontier**

$$
\nabla f_1(x^*) = -\mu \nabla f_2(x^*), \quad \mu > 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute first-order approximations**
- $TPR(x) \approx 1 - e^{-\alpha x}$ (saturating curve with inspection depth $x$)
- $L(x) \approx \beta x$ (linear latency growth with inspection depth)

**Step 4 — Compute gradients**

$$
\nabla f_1 = \dfrac{d(TPR)}{dx} = \alpha e^{-\alpha x}
$$

$$
\nabla f_2 = \dfrac{d(-L)}{dx} = -\beta
$$

**Step 5 — Apply KKT condition**

$$
\alpha e^{-\alpha x^*} = -\mu(-\beta) = \mu\beta
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for optimal $x^*$**

$$
e^{-\alpha x^*} = \dfrac{\mu\beta}{\alpha}
$$

$$
x^* = -\dfrac{1}{\alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{\mu\beta}{\alpha}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**: The optimal inspection depth $x^*$ is determined by the ratio $\beta/\alpha$ (latency-to-detection sensitivity) and the Lagrange multiplier $\mu$ (decision-maker's preference weight). Engineers can tune $x^*$ to operate anywhere on the Pareto frontier.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — IDS vs IPS Deployment Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Question Type:** *"When should you use IDS over IPS in a CPS environment?"*

| Criterion | Use IDS (Passive) | Use IPS (Active) |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency tolerance** | $> 1$ ms acceptable | $< 1$ ms critical |
| **Network availability** | Mission-critical (cannot risk block) | Can sustain brief interruption |
| **False positive tolerance** | Low (alerts can be triaged) | Very low (auto-block is destructive) |
| **Compliance regime** | NERC-CIP-007 logging only | IEC 62443 SL-3 active defense |
| **Typical CPS zone** | Field devices, legacy PLCs | IT-OT DMZ, corporate ingress |
| **Failure mode** | Fail-open acceptable | Requires bypass switch (fail-open) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IDS/IPS Integration Architecture in CPS (Purdue Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Internet and External Networks] --> B[Corporate IT Zone - Level 5]
    B --> C[DMZ with IPS - Inline Active Defense]
    C --> D[Industrial DMZ - Level 3.5]
    D --> E[Site Operations - Level 3]
    E --> F[Supervisory Control SCADA HMI - Level 3]
    F --> G[IDS Sensor - Passive Monitoring]
    G --> H[Area Control - Level 2]
    H --> I[PLCs and RTUs - Level 1]
    I --> J[Sensors and Actuators - Level 0]
    
    K[SIEM Central Correlation Server] -.Logs and Alerts.-> C
    K -.Logs and Alerts.-> G
    K -.Logs and Alerts.-> E
    
    L[Bypass Switch Fail-Open] --- C
    M[Signature Engine Snort Suricata] --- C
    N[Anomaly Engine ML LSTM] --- G
    O[Protocol DPI Modbus DNP3] --- G
    P[SPAN Port Tap] --- G
```

### 4.2 Performance Optimization Pipeline (DPDK-Accelerated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Packet Arrival NIC] --> P2[Ring Buffer DPDK]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 1 Flow Pre-Filter]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 2 Protocol Parser]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 3 Signature Match Aho-Corasick]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 4 Anomaly Score ML Model]
    P6 --> P7{Decision Engine}
    P7 -->|Attack| P8[Action: Drop or Block]
    P7 -->|Benign| P9[Action: Forward]
    P8 --> P10[Log to SIEM]
    P9 --> P10
    
    Q[FPGA SmartNIC Offload] -.Acceleration.-> P3
    R[Multi-Core Pipeline Workers] -.Parallelism.-> P4
    S[Lock-Free Ring Buffers] -.Low Latency.-> P2
```

### 4.3 Detection Decision Flowchart (IDS/IPS Action Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Incoming Packet] --> S1{Protocol Known?}
    S1 -->|No| S2[Anomaly Detection: Protocol Fingerprint]
    S1 -->|Yes| S3{Signature Match?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4{Severity Level?}
    S3 -->|No| S5{Anomaly Score above Threshold?}
    
    S4 -->|Critical| S6[IPS Action: Immediate Block]
    S4 -->|High| S7[IPS Action: Rate Limit]
    S4 -->|Medium| S8[IDS Action: Log and Alert]
    S4 -->|Low| S9[IDS Action: Log Only]
    
    S5 -->|Yes| S10[ML Confidence above 0.95?]
    S5 -->|No| S11[Forward to SIEM as Suspicious]
    
    S10 -->|Yes| S12[IPS Action: Block and Isolate]
    S10 -->|No| S8
    
    S2 --> S5
```

### 4.4 Optimization Trade-off Triangle (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Detection Accuracy] --- B1[Latency Bound]
    B1 --- C1[Resource Utilization]
    C1 --- A1
    
    A2[Improve Accuracy] -->|+ Cost.-> C2[Increase CPU Memory]
    A3[Reduce Latency] -->|+ Cost.-> C3[Add Hardware Accel]
    A4[Lower Resources] -->|- Effect.-> B2[Accept Lower Accuracy]
    
    A1 -.Pareto Frontier.-> P1[Optimal Operating Point]
    B1 -.Pareto Frontier.-> P1
    C1 -.Pareto Frontier.-> P1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). Differentiate between them in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **IDS (1 mark):** A passive security mechanism that monitors network traffic or system activities in a CPS, analyzes events for signs of intrusions, and generates alerts without interfering with traffic flow. Example: Suricata in monitor mode on a SCADA network.

- **IPS (1 mark):** An active security mechanism that extends IDS functionality by automatically blocking, dropping, or rejecting malicious traffic in real-time, in addition to generating alerts. Example: Suricata in IPS mode deployed at the IT/OT DMZ of a smart grid.

- **CPS-Specific Differentiation (1 mark):** In CPS, an IPS introduces latency that may disrupt deterministic control loops (e.g., IEC 61850 GOOSE messages with 3 ms deadlines), so passive IDS is often preferred for in-zone monitoring, while IPS is reserved for perimeter defense. Failure mode handling (fail-open bypass switches) is critical to maintain physical process safety.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any THREE best practices for optimizing the performance of an IPS in an industrial CPS environment.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hardware Acceleration using DPDK/FPGA:** Offload packet parsing and pattern matching to SmartNICs or FPGAs, achieving 10× throughput improvement and reducing per-packet latency from 100 μs to < 10 μs.

2. **Hierarchical Distributed Architecture:** Deploy lightweight IDS sensors at field sites and a central correlation engine. This reduces per-sensor load and enables global threat correlation while preserving local response speed.

3. **Hybrid Signature + Anomaly Detection with Continuous Tuning:** Combine Snort signatures for known CPS exploits with ML-based anomaly detection for zero-day threats. Perform quarterly baselining to minimize false positives in dynamic industrial environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the architecture and integration strategy for deploying IDS/IPS in a Cyber-Physical System following the **Purdue Reference Model**. Discuss sensor placement at each level with justification. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the **F1-Score** for the following confusion matrix obtained from a CPS IDS evaluation:
- TP = 180, FP = 20, FN = 15, TN = 9785
Comment on the suitability of this IDS for deployment in a **smart grid SCADA** environment. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Architecture and Integration Strategy (7 Marks)**

The **Purdue Reference Model (ANSI/ISA-99)** segments industrial networks into 6 levels (0–5). IDS/IPS placement strategy:

**Level 0–1 (Process Control):** Field sensors, actuators, PLCs, RTUs
- **Placement (1 mark):** Passive IDS sensor on the cell-level network segment
- **Justification (1 mark):** These devices operate under strict real-time constraints (1–10 ms cycles). Inline IPS would introduce unacceptable latency. Passive monitoring preserves determinism.

**Level 2 (Area Supervisory Control):** SCADA Masters, HMIs, Engineering Workstations
- **Placement (1 mark):** Hybrid — IDS for protocol anomaly detection (Modbus/DNP3/OPC UA DPI), IPS only if latency budget permits
- **Justification (1 mark):** This is the aggregation point for field data. Compromised HMIs can issue malicious control commands. DPI at this level detects protocol-level attacks (e.g., illegal Modbus function codes 0x5A for unauthorized writes).

**Level 3.5 / DMZ (IT-OT Boundary):**
- **Placement (1 mark):** **Inline IPS** with bypass switch (fail-open), full signature + anomaly detection
- **Justification (1 mark):** This is the chokepoint between corporate IT and OT. Threats originating from enterprise networks (phishing, lateral movement) must be blocked here before reaching critical OT. Fail-open bypass ensures physical process continuity if IPS fails.

**Level 4–5 (Enterprise/Internet):**
- **Placement (1 mark):** IPS at perimeter firewall
- **Justification (1 mark):** Standard IT security, blocks internet-borne attacks before they reach industrial zones.

**Centralized Correlation:** All sensors forward logs to a **SIEM** at the Security Operations Center (SOC) for cross-zone correlation (e.g., IT compromise + abnormal OT commands within time window = coordinated attack).

---

**(b) F1-Score Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify Confusion Matrix values (1 mark)**
- $TP = 180, FP = 20, FN = 15, TN = 9785$

**Step 2 — Compute Precision (1.5 marks)**

$$
P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{180}{180 + 20} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recall (TPR) (1.5 marks)**

$$
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{180}{180 + 15} = \dfrac{180}{195} \approx 0.923
$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score (2 marks)**

$$
F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot TPR}{P + TPR} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.923}{0.90 + 0.923} = \dfrac{1.6614}{1.823} \approx 0.911
$$

**Step 5 — Suitability Analysis for Smart Grid SCADA (1 mark)**

An F1-Score of **0.911** indicates strong performance. For smart grid deployment:
- **FPR = FP / (FP + TN) = 20 / 9805 ≈ 0.204%** — very low false alarm rate, acceptable for SCADA where alert fatigue causes operator desensitization
- **FNR = FN / (FN + TP) = 15 / 195 ≈ 7.7%** — about 1 in 13 attacks missed; for **SL-3/4** critical infrastructure, this should be lowered via ensemble methods or additional sensors
- **Verdict:** Suitable as one component in a **defense-in-depth** strategy, but **not sufficient alone** for high-security level smart grid deployment without supplementary controls (network segmentation, whitelisting).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Discuss the **performance optimization techniques** for an IPS in CPS, covering hardware acceleration, software optimization, and architectural strategies. Include the **M/D/1 queueing model** derivation for latency-throughput trade-off. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare and contrast **IDS and IPS** deployment strategies for the following CPS scenarios:
1. Legacy SCADA system with serial Modbus RTU
2. Modern smart factory with OPC UA and TSN
3. Autonomous vehicle platoon V2X communication

Recommend the optimal placement and configuration for each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Performance Optimization Techniques (7 Marks)**

**1. Hardware Acceleration (1.5 marks)**
- **DPDK (Data Plane Development Kit):** Bypasses kernel networking stack, uses user-space polling; achieves 10–100× throughput.
- **FPGA SmartNICs:** Offload regex pattern matching (Aho-Corasick for signatures) and cryptographic operations; reduces CPU load by 60–80%.
- **GPU Offload:** Used for ML-based anomaly detection; parallel batch inference for thousands of flows.

**2. Software Optimization (1.5 marks)**
- **Multi-threaded pipeline architecture:** Run-to-completion vs. pipeline models; pipeline gives better cache locality.
- **Lock-free data structures:** RCU (Read-Copy-Update) for rule tables, ring buffers for packet queues — eliminates mutex contention.
- **NUMA-aware thread pinning:** Reduces cross-socket memory access latency.

**3. Architectural Optimization (1 mark)**
- **Hierarchical distributed IDS:** Edge sensors do local detection; central server correlates.
- **Stateful flow caching:** Track established connections to avoid re-inspecting every packet.

**4. M/D/1 Queueing Model Derivation (3 marks)**

**Step 1 — Setup (0.5 mark):** Poisson arrivals rate $\lambda$, deterministic service rate $\mu$, $\rho = \lambda/\mu$.

**Step 2 — Pollaczek-Khinchine for M/G/1 (0.5 mark):** 
Mean waiting time $W_q = \dfrac{\lambda \mathbb{E}[S^2]}{2(1-\rho)}$

**Step 3 — Apply deterministic second moment (0.5 mark):** 
$\mathbb{E}[S^2] = 1/\mu^2$, so $W_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{2\lambda(1-\rho)}$

**Step 4 — Total latency (0.5 mark):** 
$W = W_q + 1/\mu = \dfrac{\rho^2}{2\lambda(1-\rho)} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$

**Step 5 — Operational implication (1 mark):** To bound latency, operate at $\rho \le 0.7$. This justifies **over-provisioning** IPS hardware in CPS (e.g., 10 Gbps device on 1 Gbps link).

---

**(b) IDS vs IPS Comparison for Three CPS Scenarios (7 Marks)**

| Scenario | Recommendation | Justification (Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Legacy SCADA + Modbus RTU** (1.5 marks) | **Passive IDS only** with serial-to-Ethernet tap | Serial RTU has no native security; inline IPS would break the deterministic polling cycle. Use anomaly detection for unusual function codes. |
| **2. Smart Factory + OPC UA + TSN** (2 marks) | **Hybrid: IDS in cells + IPS at IT-OT DMZ** | TSN guarantees bounded latency, so IPS is feasible at the DMZ. Inside cells, use IDS with ML-based OPC UA session monitoring. Encrypt and authenticate OPC UA channels. |
| **3. Autonomous Vehicle V2X** (1.5 marks) | **Inline IPS with hardware bypass** | V2X (IEEE 802.11p, C-V2X) requires sub-10 ms reaction to message spoofing (e.g., fake emergency brake). IPS must drop malicious BSMs (Basic Safety Messages) inline. Hardware bypass is mandatory for fail-safe operation. |
| **Common Recommendations** (2 marks) | (a) Use defense-in-depth — never rely on single layer. (b) Implement SIEM correlation across all sensors. (c) Perform quarterly baselining. (d) Maintain fail-open bypass switches for all inline IPS. (e) Use IEC 62443 zone-conduit model for segmentation. | Each sub-recommendation explained with engineering rationale. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> **⚠ Pitfall 1 — Confusing IDS with IPS:** Students frequently write that "IPS detects attacks." This loses 1 mark. **IDS detects; IPS detects AND prevents.**
> 
> **⚠ Pitfall 2 — Ignoring CPS-Specific Latency:** A generic answer that fails to mention **IEC 61850 GOOSE 3 ms timing budgets** or **NERC-CIP** requirements will lose 2–3 marks. KTU examiners reward CPS-specific terminology.
> 
> **⚠ Pitfall 3 — F1-Score without Precision/Recall:** F1 alone is incomplete. You must show **both** Precision and Recall derivations. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 
> **⚠ Pitfall 4 — No mention of Fail-Open/Fail-Closed:** Any IPS deployment answer that omits **bypass switches** and **failure mode analysis** is considered incomplete for CPS — 1 mark lost.
> 
> **⚠ Pitfall 5 — Confusing Accuracy with F1:** For imbalanced CPS datasets (e.g., 0.1% attack traffic), accuracy > 99.9% is meaningless. Use **MCC or F1** instead.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Best Practices for IDS/IPS Integration and Performance Optimization**

- ☐ **IDS is passive, IPS is active** — remember the CCTV vs armed guard analogy
- ☐ **Purdue Model** placement: IDS at Levels 0–2 (in-zone), IPS at Level 3.5 DMZ and perimeter
- ☐ **Critical CPS metrics**: TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, MCC, Throughput (Gbps), Latency (μs/ms), MTTD
- ☐ **IEC 61850 GOOSE timing**: **3 ms Type 1A** — IPS latency must be << 1 ms to avoid relay misoperation
- ☐ **Industrial protocols to know**: Modbus TCP (502), DNP3 (20000), IEC 61850, OPC UA (4840), PROFINET
- ☐ **Optimization pillars**: Hardware (DPDK/FPGA), Software (multi-threading, lock-free), Algorithmic (rule indexing, early discard), Architectural (distributed/hierarchical)
- ☐ **M/D/1 queueing insight**: Operate at $\rho \le 0.7$ to keep latency bounded; **over-provision** IPS
- ☐ **F1-Score** = $2PR / (P+R)$ — best single metric for imbalanced CPS datasets
- ☐ **Best practice list**: (1) Asset discovery, (2) Sensor placement, (3) Hybrid detection, (4) Continuous tuning, (5) SIEM correlation, (6) Bypass switches, (7) Quarterly baselining
- ☐ **Defense in depth** — never rely on a single IDS/IPS layer
- ☐ **Compliance standards to cite**: **NIST SP 800-94, IEC 62443, NERC-CIP, ISO 27001**
- ☐ **Pareto frontier**: You cannot maximize detection, minimize latency, AND minimize cost — choose 2 of 3
- ☐ **Fail-open bypass** is **mandatory** for any inline IPS in safety-critical CPS
- ☐ **ML-based anomaly detection** requires continuous retraining; concept drift in CPS is a real issue
- ☐ **Always state numerical thresholds** in your answers (e.g., "F1 > 0.9", "latency < 1 ms") — vague answers lose marks

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Firmware and Software Update Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secure Firmware and Software Update Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Secure Firmware and Software Update Mechanisms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT863 / Module 3)**
> A **Secure Firmware and Software Update Mechanism** for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is a cryptographically-hardened, end-to-end process — spanning *authoring, signing, distribution, delivery, installation, and post-update attestation* — that guarantees the authenticity, integrity, freshness, and authorized provenance of any executable code (firmware, OS image, application binary, or configuration) deployed onto a CPS device, while simultaneously ensuring safe **rollback**, **fail-safe recovery**, and **non-repudiation** of the update transaction.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Vaccine Vial" Model

Imagine a pharmaceutical company shipping a vaccine to a rural hospital. The hospital must be absolutely sure that:

| Real-World Element | CPS Update Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Tamper-evident seal on the vial | **Digital signature** of the firmware image |
| Sealed chain-of-custody paperwork | **Cryptographic hash chain / Merkle log** |
| Expiration date on the vial | **Anti-rollback monotonic counter** |
| Doctor's prescription check | **Device-level authorization / Attestation** |
| Quarantine of the patient after vaccination | **Sandboxed staging partition (A/B scheme)** |
| Hospital's batch number log | **Update metadata / manifest (TUF, SWUpdate)** |

If the seal is broken, the paperwork is missing, or the date has passed, the hospital **must reject the vial** — even if the contents *look* fine. A CPS secure update framework applies the exact same chain-of-trust to a binary image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** Unlike a smartphone, a compromised CPS device (e.g., a ventilator, a smart-grid breaker, a vehicle ECU) cannot simply be "rebooted to fix it." An insecure update channel is therefore a *direct physical-world attack surface* — it can be weaponized for ransomware, sabotage, or remote code execution on critical infrastructure.

### 1.2 Core Security Properties (The "Four Pillars")

A KTU-compliant secure update mechanism MUST guarantee the following **CIA+ properties**, often extended to **AIARES** (Authenticity, Integrity, Authorship, Recovery, Exclusivity, Safety):

1. **Authenticity** — The update truly originated from the legitimate vendor.
2. **Integrity** — The binary has not been modified in transit.
3. **Freshness (Anti-Replay / Anti-Rollback)** — The update is the latest authorized version; an attacker cannot replay an old, vulnerable image.
4. **Confidentiality (optional)** — The image is encrypted so competitors cannot reverse-engineer it.
5. **Atomicity & Fail-Safe Recovery** — Either the update succeeds completely, or the device reverts to a known-good state.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Term:** The industry-standard "**trust chain from silicon to cloud**" begins with an immutable **Root of Trust (RoT)** — typically a hardware-anchored **TPM 2.0** chip, **Secure Enclave**, or one-time-programmable (OTP) **fuse** burned at fabrication time.

### 1.3 Threats Mitigated

- **Rogue/Unsigned firmware injection** (e.g., Mirai botnet variants)
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) tampering** during OTA transmission
- **Rollback attack** to a known-vulnerable version
- **Supply-chain compromise** (e.g., SolarWinds-style backdoored build tools)
- **Evil-maid firmware replacement** during physical servicing
- **Bricking** due to power loss mid-flash

### 1.4 Key Standards & Frameworks

- **NIST SP 800-193** — *Platform Resiliency Guidelines* (the canonical reference for CPS)
- **TUF (The Update Framework)** — CNCF graduated project
- **SWUpdate** — Linux embedded OTA framework
- **MCUboot** — Secure bootloader for IoT/embedded
- **The Update Framework for Embedded Systems (UPTANE)** — automotive (OTA for connected cars)
- **IETF SUIT (Software Updates for Internet of Things)** — RFC 9019, RFC 9124

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Phase Update Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-193 Compliant)

A KTU-grade answer on this topic must explicitly enumerate the **seven logical phases**. Each phase has dedicated cryptographic controls.

### Phase 1 — Authoring
The vendor compiles the firmware binary $B$, the manifest $M$, and the metadata $\mathcal{C}$ (version, device-class, dependencies). The manifest binds $B$ to a cryptographic identity.

### Phase 2 — Signing
The vendor computes:

$$H = \text{SHA-256}(B)$$

and signs it with a long-term private key $SK_{vendor}$ (held in an **HSM — Hardware Security Module**):

$$\sigma = \text{ECDSA-Sign}(SK_{vendor}, H)$$

The signed package $\mathcal{P} = \{B, M, \sigma, \text{cert}(PK_{vendor})\}$ is then published.

### Phase 3 — Distribution
The signed package is uploaded to an **update repository** (CDN, Mender server, AWS IoT Jobs, Azure DPS). The repository itself is **NOT trusted** — every device re-verifies the signature locally.

### Phase 4 — Discovery & Download
The CPS device polls or is pushed the update, downloads $\mathcal{P}$ over **TLS 1.3** (mutual mTLS), and stores it in a **staging area** (the inactive A/B partition or an uncompressed RAM buffer).

### Phase 5 — Verification (The "Trust-on-First-Use" Check)
The device's **secure bootloader** (the Root of Trust) executes the following verification algorithm:

$$\text{Verify} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } \text{ECDSA-Verify}(PK_{vendor}, \sigma, H') = \text{true} \;\wedge\; H' = H \;\wedge\; v_{new} > v_{current} \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $v$ denotes the **monotonic version counter**.

### Phase 6 — Installation
The bootloader atomically swaps the active partition, sets the **boot counter fuse**, and reboots.

### Phase 7 — Attestation & Self-Test
The device, on first boot of the new image, performs a **Measured Boot** (PCR extend into TPM) and reports a **TPM2_Certify** quote to the management server.

## 2.2 The Trust Chain — From Silicon to Application

```
[Immutable Boot ROM] → [Secure Bootloader (MCUboot)] → [Verified OS Image] → [Verified App Container]
   (OTP Fuse)              (ECDSA Pubkey)                  (Hash in TPM)           (Sigstore)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If *any* link in this chain is software-only, the chain is **broken**. Every link must be anchored in **hardware RoT** (TPM, SE, HSM, or OTP).

## 2.3 Anti-Rollback Counter — Mathematical Foundation

Let $v_{cur}$ be the lowest version the device will accept. The check is:

$$v_{incoming} \geq v_{cur} \quad \text{(strict monotonicity)}$$

The counter $v_{cur}$ itself is stored in **OTP/eFuse** so it cannot be software-downgraded.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard / Unit | KTU Exam Tip |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $H$ | Cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) | **256-bit** output | SHA-256 is KTU's default; never use MD5/SHA-1 in 2024 answers |
| $\sigma$ | Digital signature on hash | **ECDSA-P256** → 64 bytes | RSA-2048 acceptable but ECDSA is preferred for CPS |
| $SK$ / $PK$ | Signing keypair | 256-bit (ECDSA) | $SK$ must NEVER leave the HSM |
| $v$ | Monotonic version counter | Integer $\in \mathbb{N}$ | Stored in OTP/eFuse |
| $B$ | Firmware binary | KB / MB | Streamed or staged, not executed in-place |
| $M$ | Manifest | JSON / CBOR (RFC 8949) | Binds $B$, $v$, $PK$, expiry |
| $N_{PCR}$ | TPM Platform Configuration Register index | 0–23 | Used in measured boot |
| $T$ | Tamper-resistance rating | FIPS 140-2 Level 2+ | Required for CPS HSM |
| $\mathcal{P}$ | Update package $\vert B, M, \sigma, \text{cert} \mid$ | Set notation | Treat as immutable bundle |
| $A \parallel B$ | Bitwise concatenation | n/a | Used in Merkle construction |

## 2.5 Cryptographic Primitives Used

- **Hashing:** SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA3-256
- **Symmetric (for image encryption):** AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305
- **Asymmetric (for signature):** ECDSA-P256, Ed25519, RSA-PSS
- **Key Exchange:** X25519 (for fleet-provisioned devices)
- **Key Storage:** TPM 2.0, SE050, ATECC608B
- **Attestation:** TPM2_Quote, EAT (Entity Attestation Token, RFC 9334)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Automotive | UPTANE / OTA over cellular — Tesla, Toyota |
| Medical Devices | FDA-mandated post-market patching (PCCP guidance) |
| Smart Grid | IEC 61850 secure firmware for IEDs |
| Industrial IoT | OPC UA + secure update per IEC 62443-4-2 |
| Aviation | DO-326A airworthiness + signed loadable software |

> [!TIP]
> **IEC 62443-4-2** explicitly mandates *SR 3.4 (Software and Information Integrity)* and *SR 3.5 (Input Validation)* — secure update mechanisms are a **direct compliance requirement** for OT/CPS networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Signature Verification Chain

The following derivation demonstrates the **complete cryptographic verification** that a CPS device must execute before flashing a new firmware image. This is a guaranteed 14-mark question topic in the KTU ESE.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Walk-through

**Step 1 — Bundle Reception.**
The device receives the update package:
$$\mathcal{P} = \{ B, \; M, \; \sigma, \; \text{cert}_{vendor} \}$$

**Textual logic:** $\mathcal{P}$ is split into the *body* $B$ (firmware), the *manifest* $M$ (metadata), the *signature* $\sigma$, and the *vendor certificate* $\text{cert}_{vendor}$. The device must reconstruct the trust chain by validating everything in order.

**Step 2 — Certificate Chain Validation (X.509 / PKIX).**
The device verifies:
$$\text{verifyChain}(\text{cert}_{vendor}, \text{trustAnchor}_{rootCA}) = \text{true}$$

**Textual logic:** Starting from the device's **factory-provisioned trust anchor** (a root CA public key burned into OTP), walk the X.509 chain up to the vendor's leaf certificate. If any signature in the chain is invalid or expired, **halt immediately**.

**Step 3 — Extract Vendor Public Key.**
From the validated certificate, extract:
$$PK_{vendor} = \text{extractPubKey}(\text{cert}_{vendor})$$

**Textual logic:** $PK_{vendor}$ is the long-term public counterpart of the private key the vendor used inside their HSM.

**Step 4 — Compute Hash of the Binary.**
The device computes, over the raw binary $B$:
$$H' = \text{SHA-256}(B)$$

**Textual logic:** Note the prime on $H'$ — this is the *locally computed* hash, which must match the hash the vendor signed. Any single bit-flip in transit will be detected.

**Step 5 — Cryptographic Signature Verification (ECDSA).**
The device executes the ECDSA verify primitive:
$$\text{ECDSA-Verify}(PK_{vendor}, H', \sigma) \rightarrow \{\text{valid}, \text{invalid}\}$$

**Textual logic:** The elliptic curve math checks whether $\sigma$ was generated by the *true* $SK_{vendor}$ over the *true* hash $H'$. The mathematical details are:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given curve } y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p \\
&\text{Generator point } G \text{ of order } n \\
&\text{Public key } PK_{vendor} = SK_{vendor} \cdot G \\
&\text{Verify: } u_1 = H' \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n \\
&\quad\quad u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n \\
&\quad\quad (x_1, y_1) = u_1 G + u_2 PK_{vendor} \\
&\text{Accept if } x_1 \bmod n = r
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Manifest Policy Check.**
Beyond pure cryptography, the device enforces **policy rules** from $M$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{incoming} &> v_{current} \quad \text{(anti-rollback)} \\
\text{deviceClass}(M) &= \text{deviceClass}(self) \quad \text{(no cross-flash)} \\
t_{now} &< t_{expiry}(M) \quad \text{(no stale update)} \\
PK_{vendor} &\in \text{allowlist}_{HSM} \quad \text{(vendor authorized)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Atomic Swap & Counter Bump.**
On success, the bootloader:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{partition}_{active} &\leftarrow B \\
\text{bootCounter}_{eFuse} &\leftarrow v_{incoming} \\
\text{setBootFlag}(\text{NEXT\_BOOT} = \text{NEW\_IMAGE}) \\
\text{reboot}()
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Post-Boot Measured Attestation.**
On the *next* boot, the new image extends the TPM PCR:
$$PCR_0 \leftarrow \text{SHA-256}(PCR_0 \parallel H_{newImage})$$

and the device issues a **TPM2_Quote** report to the management plane.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Update Frameworks

| Property | TUF (CNCF) | MCUboot | SWUpdate | UPTANE | IETF SUIT |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Primary Use-Case | Cloud/Servers | Embedded MCU | Linux Embedded | Automotive OTA | IoT (generic) |
| Root of Trust | Software | Hardware (MCU) | Software+HSM | HSM+OEM | Hardware |
| Signature Scheme | Ed25519 / RSA | ECDSA / RSA | RSA / CMS | Ed25519 + RSA | COSE (RFC 8152) |
| Metadata Format | JSON | Image header | CPIO archive | TUF + custom | CBOR |
| Anti-Rollback | Timestamp-based | Image version | SW version | ECU version | Component version |
| A/B Partition Support | App-dependent | Yes | Yes | Yes | Optional |
| Standard Reference | PEP 458 | Zephyr / Apache | ELC conference | IEEE/IFIP | RFC 9019 |

## 3.3 The Failure-Mode Analysis (Why Bricked Devices Are a CPS Risk)

A robust KTU answer must also discuss **what happens if the update fails mid-flash**. The mathematically rigorous model is the **A/B Dual-Partition State Machine**:

$$\text{State} = \begin{cases} S_0 & : \text{Booting from Partition A (current good)} \\ S_1 & : \text{Booting from Partition B (previous good)} \\ S_2 & : \text{Booting from staging (RAM bootloader)} \\ S_3 & : \text{Bricked (no valid image)} \end{cases}$$

The transition rules ensure that **a power loss at any point** still leaves the device in a recoverable state. This is the **fail-safe** property demanded by IEC 62443-4-2 and FDA post-market guidance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Secure Update Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VENDOR BUILD PIPELINE"] --> B["CRYPTOGRAPHIC SIGNING HSM"]
    B --> C["UPDATE REPOSITORY CDN"]
    C --> D["CPS DEVICE INTAKE"]
    D --> E["SECURE BOOTLOADER VERIFY"]
    E --> F{"SIGNATURE VALID"}
    F -->|NO| G["QUARANTINE AND ALERT"]
    F -->|YES| H["ANTI ROLLBACK CHECK"]
    H --> I{"VERSION NEWER"}
    I -->|NO| G
    I -->|YES| J["STAGE TO PARTITION B"]
    J --> K["ATOMIC PARTITION SWAP"]
    K --> L["REBOOT TO NEW IMAGE"]
    L --> M["TPM MEASURED BOOT"]
    M --> N["ATTESTATION TO FLEET MANAGER"]
```

## 4.2 The Chain-of-Trust Cascade (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IMMUTABLE BOOT ROM OTP FUSE"] --> B["SECURE BOOTLOADER VERIFIED BY ROOT KEY"]
    B --> C["OS KERNEL IMAGE SIGNATURE CHECK"]
    C --> D["APPLICATION CONTAINER SIGNATURE CHECK"]
    D --> E["UPDATE CLIENT VERIFIES INCOMING PACKAGE"]
    E --> F["INSTALL TO INACTIVE PARTITION"]
    F --> G["REBOOT AND MEASURED ATTESTATION"]
```

## 4.3 A/B Dual-Partition Swap State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["STATE S0 ACTIVE PARTITION A"] -->|DOWNLOAD UPDATE| S1["STATE S1 STAGE TO PARTITION B"]
    S1 -->|VERIFY SUCCESS| S2["STATE S2 SET BOOT FLAG TO B"]
    S2 --> S3["STATE S3 REBOOT"]
    S3 -->|SELF TEST PASS| S4["STATE S4 COMMIT NEW IMAGE"]
    S3 -->|SELF TEST FAIL| S5["STATE S5 ROLLBACK TO A"]
    S4 --> S0
    S5 --> S0
```

## 4.4 Cryptographic Verification Sub-Routine (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as VENDOR HSM
    participant R as REPOSITORY
    participant D as CPS DEVICE
    participant T as TPM
    V->>V: COMPUTE HASH H OF BINARY
    V->>V: SIGN H WITH PRIVATE KEY
    V->>R: UPLOAD SIGNED PACKAGE
    R->>D: DELIVER PACKAGE OVER TLS
    D->>D: COMPUTE HASH H PRIME LOCALLY
    D->>D: VERIFY SIGNATURE WITH VENDOR PUBLIC KEY
    D->>D: CHECK VERSION COUNTER IS NEWER
    D->>T: EXTEND PCR WITH NEW IMAGE HASH
    D->>D: COMMIT TO INACTIVE PARTITION AND REBOOT
```

## 4.5 Threat Model — STRIDE Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THREAT MODEL STRIDE"] --> B["S SPOOFING ROGUE VENDOR"]
    A --> C["T TAMPERING MITM ON OTA"]
    A --> D["R REPUDIATION LOST AUDIT LOG"]
    A --> E["I INFORMATION DISC ENCRYPTION"]
    A --> F["D DENIAL OF SERVICE BRICKING"]
    A --> G["E ELEVATION OF PRIVILEGE SIGNED MALWARE"]
    B --> H["MITIGATED BY HARDWARE ANCHORED PKI"]
    C --> I["MITIGATED BY ECDSA SIGNATURE"]
    D --> J["MITIGATED BY APPEND ONLY AUDIT LOG"]
    E --> K["MITIGATED BY AES 256 GCM"]
    F --> L["MITIGATED BY A B PARTITION ROLLBACK"]
    G --> M["MITIGATED BY VENDOR HSM AND CODE REVIEW"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "encryption" with "signing."** A signed image is NOT necessarily encrypted. Read the question stem carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting anti-rollback.** Many students describe signature verification but omit the **monotonic version counter** — this costs 2–3 marks per 14-mark question.
> 3. **No mention of Hardware Root of Trust.** Software-only "secure boot" is **not secure boot** in KTU's grading rubric.
> 4. **Missing fail-safe recovery.** Always close your answer by describing the A/B rollback state machine.
> 5. **Stating "SHA-1" or "MD5."** These are *broken*; using them in 2024-scheme answers is an instant 1-mark penalty.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between code signing and image encryption in a CPS firmware update context. State one attack each scheme prevents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Code signing** uses **asymmetric cryptography** (ECDSA / Ed25519) where the vendor signs a hash of the firmware with a private key held in an HSM, and the device verifies with the vendor's public key. It guarantees **authenticity** and **integrity** — preventing an attacker from injecting a **rogue/unsigned firmware** image.
- **Image encryption** uses **symmetric cryptography** (AES-256-GCM) to protect the firmware binary from **reverse engineering** and **intellectual property theft** during transit, even if the channel is compromised.
- **Key distinction:** signing answers *"who made this and was it tampered with?"*; encryption answers *"can a passive eavesdropper read the contents?"*
- A system may use **both** simultaneously: encrypt for confidentiality, sign for authenticity.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]
**What is an "anti-rollback counter" in a secure firmware update system? Where is it physically stored and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An anti-rollback counter is a **monotonically increasing integer** $v$ representing the lowest acceptable firmware version.
- It is physically stored in **One-Time-Programmable (OTP) eFuses** or inside a **TPM NV-Index** — both of which are *write-once* or *hardware-monotonic*.
- It is stored in hardware (not flash) so that an attacker with software-level access **cannot decrement** it, thereby preventing the **rollback attack** where an old, vulnerable image is re-flashed to bypass a security patch.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the end-to-end architecture of a secure firmware update mechanism for a Cyber-Physical System. Clearly label all trust boundaries.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the role of Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT), TPM 2.0, and the monotonic version counter in ensuring update authenticity and freshness. Mention one relevant standard.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

The architecture consists of **four trust domains** connected by cryptographic channels:

1. **Vendor Build & Sign Domain** — CI/CD pipeline produces the firmware binary $B$, computes $H = \text{SHA-256}(B)$, and signs it inside an **HSM** using ECDSA-P256, producing $\sigma$. The signed manifest $M$ binds the binary, the target device class, the version $v$, and an expiry timestamp.

2. **Distribution Domain** — The signed package is published to a CDN / update repository. The repository is **untrusted** from the device's perspective; it cannot forge the signature.

3. **Device Domain** — A CPS device (e.g., a smart meter) downloads the package over **mutual TLS 1.3**. The **secure bootloader** (anchored in **ROM/OTP**) verifies the signature using the vendor's public key (provisioned at factory time), checks $v_{incoming} > v_{current}$, and stages the binary to the inactive partition.

4. **Management & Attestation Domain** — After reboot, the new image's hash is **extended into TPM PCR 0**, and a **TPM2_Quote** report is sent to the fleet manager for **continuous compliance**.

**Trust boundaries** must be drawn between (1)↔(2) (vendor ↔ CDN, secured by TLS), (2)↔(3) (CDN ↔ device, secured by TLS + signature), and (3)↔(4) (device ↔ manager, secured by attestation).

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT):** The very first instruction executed on power-up, residing in **mask ROM**. It cannot be patched, so it forms the immutable anchor of the entire trust chain. **(2 marks)**
- **TPM 2.0:** A discrete secure cryptoprocessor that holds the vendor's public key in a **non-extractable** storage root key, performs **measured boot** (extending PCRs), and provides **TPM2_Quote** for remote attestation. It also stores the monotonic version counter in a **NV-Index with TPM\_NT\_COUNTER** attribute. **(3 marks)**
- **Monotonic Version Counter:** A hardware-protected integer in OTP/TPM. Since it can only increase, an attacker cannot convince the bootloader to accept an older (vulnerable) firmware image. **(1 mark)**
- **Relevant Standard:** **NIST SP 800-193** (Platform Resiliency Guidelines) is the canonical reference; **IEC 62443-4-2** is the industrial control equivalent. **(1 mark)**

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** *Describe the A/B dual-partition update scheme with a state diagram. Explain how it guarantees fail-safe recovery from a power loss during flashing.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare TUF, MCUboot, and UPTANE update frameworks along five dimensions. Which one is most suitable for an automotive CPS and why?* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

The A/B scheme maintains **two complete firmware copies** on non-volatile storage:

- **State S0** — Device boots normally from Partition A (the currently trusted image).
- **State S1** — Update received; new image is written to **Partition B** in a streaming, block-by-block fashion, with a per-block **CRC32 verification**.
- **State S2** — After full write, the bootloader verifies the entire B image's SHA-256 against the manifest. On success, it sets the **boot-flip flag** in a hardware register.
- **State S3** — Reboot. The bootloader reads the flag, swaps the active partition pointer, and boots B.
- **State S4** — B's first-boot self-test runs. If it succeeds within a watchdog window, the system **commits** by sealing the flag permanently.
- **State S5** — If self-test fails (or the watchdog expires), the bootloader **automatically reverts** to Partition A — the previous known-good image. **(5 marks for state description)**

**Fail-safe guarantee:** At every transition, the *currently booting* image is never overwritten. A power loss at S1 leaves A intact (recovery: resume download). A power loss at S2 still leaves A intact (recovery: retry verification). A power loss at S3 results in the next boot seeing the flag set but the image untested → revert to A. Therefore **the device can never be left in a non-bootable state** except through physical storage corruption. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — 7 marks**

| Dimension | TUF | MCUboot | UPTANE |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Target platform** | Cloud/Server | MCU / RTOS | Automotive ECU fleet |
| **Signature scheme** | Ed25519 (default) | ECDSA / RSA | Ed25519 + RSA dual-signature |
| **Root of Trust** | Software (TOFU model) | Hardware (immutable bootloader) | HSM in OEM backend |
| **Metadata format** | JSON, multi-role | Image-header binary | TUF-compatible + custom ECU manifest |
| **Anti-rollback** | Timestamp-based | Image version in slot | ECU version + per-target version |
| **Repudiation resistance** | Multi-signature | Single signature | Multi-delegation, full PKI |

**Best for automotive CPS: UPTANE (5 marks)** because:
1. It is **specifically designed** for the connected-car OTA use-case (per University of Michigan Transportation Research Institute).
2. It supports **secondary ecosystem** (e.g., dealer-installed or aftermarket software) with **multi-role delegation** — essential for modern vehicles.
3. It uses **Ed25519 for speed** and is **resistant to repo compromise** because the device re-verifies every signature locally.
4. It addresses **mixed-criticality** by separating time-critical (e.g., braking ECU) and non-critical (e.g., infotainment) update paths.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A secure update mechanism = **signing + anti-rollback + fail-safe + attestation** — never just one of these.
- ✅ **Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT)** is *non-negotiable*; software-only "secure boot" is not acceptable in CPS.
- ✅ The four cryptographic pillars: **SHA-256 hashing, ECDSA-P256 (or Ed25519) signing, AES-256-GCM encryption, TPM 2.0 attestation.**
- ✅ **A/B partitioning** is the industry standard for fail-safe recovery; never overwrite the active image in place.
- ✅ **Anti-rollback counters** MUST be stored in OTP/eFuse or TPM NV-Index — never in regular flash.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-193** = platform-level resilience (the reference for KTU 2024); **IEC 62443-4-2** = industrial control equivalent; **UPTANE** = automotive equivalent.
- ✅ Always state the **TUF property of "repository is untrusted"** — devices re-verify every signature locally.
- ✅ Mention **measured boot + TPM2_Quote** to demonstrate post-install attestation.
- ✅ Avoid weak primitives: **MD5, SHA-1, RSA-1024, DES** are *all* disallowed in 2024-scheme answers.
- ✅ Always close your answer with the **threat model** (STRIDE) showing which attack is mitigated by which control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Techniques for Secure Firmware Updates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced Techniques for Secure Firmware Updates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ADVANCED TECHNIQUES FOR SECURE FIRMWARE UPDATES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Secure Firmware Update (SFU)** is a cryptographically enforced, integrity-preserving, and authenticity-verifiable procedure by which the immutable low-level control program (firmware) embedded within a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) device is replaced, patched, or upgraded over its operational lifecycle without violating the system's security posture, safety invariants, or availability constraints.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECPT863 syllabus**, advanced secure firmware update techniques constitute the operational layer that bridges the Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT) with the field-deployed CPS node, ensuring that any new code executed on the device has been (i) cryptographically signed by an authorized Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM), (ii) verified for tamper-evidence through hash comparison, and (iii) installed atomically to prevent bricking.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Formal Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Firmware** | Non-volatile, hardware-controlling software stored in ROM/Flash | The attack surface beneath the OS layer |
| **HRoT** | Hardware Root of Trust | Immutable trust anchor (e.g., eFuse, OTP ROM) |
| **Chain of Trust (CoT)** | Cryptographic linkage from HRoT to application layer | KTU CO3 mapping |
| **OTA** | Over-The-Air update | Wireless firmware delivery mechanism |
| **Code Signing** | Asymmetric digital signature on firmware image | Authenticates publisher identity |
| **Anti-Rollback** | Counter preventing downgrade to vulnerable versions | Defends against replay/downgrade attacks |
| **A/B Partitioning** | Dual-slot update mechanism with fail-safe rollback | Ensures atomicity of update |
| **TEE** | Trusted Execution Environment (e.g., TrustZone, SGX) | Isolated update verification space |
| **TPM** | Trusted Platform Module | Hardware secure element for key storage |
| **Secure Boot** | Boot process verifying each stage's cryptographic signature | Pre-loads CoT |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Vault & Notarized Will**
>
> Imagine a national bank's vault containing gold reserves. The bank's CEO wants to change the combination. The procedure is:
> 1. The CEO writes a new combination on paper (the **new firmware image**).
> 2. The paper is notarized with the CEO's unique wax seal and signature (the **digital signature**).
> 3. A trusted courier (the **secure delivery channel / TLS**) carries the paper.
> 4. The bank's security guard (the **Hardware Root of Trust / Secure Boot**) verifies the seal against the bank's master seal record (the **public key stored in eFuse**).
> 5. The guard checks the date on the document — older documents are rejected (the **anti-rollback counter**).
> 6. The new combination is tested in a side-room before swapping the main vault (the **A/B partition with fail-safe**).
> 7. If anything fails, the old combination remains (the **rollback recovery**).
>
> This entire choreography is what a **Secure Firmware Update** does inside a CPS node. Without it, an attacker could deliver a forged "new combination" and empty the vault.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in CPS Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Cyber-Physical Systems**, firmware controls physical actuators (motors, valves, brakes, infusion pumps). A tampered firmware update can:
> - Convert a **smart insulin pump** into a lethal device (Stuxnet-style sabotage).
> - Open a **backdoor** in industrial PLCs (Mirai Botnet precedent in TRITON/Trisis attack, 2017).
> - Brick thousands of **smart meters** in a power grid (Venezuela blackout 2019).
>
> Hence, KTU explicitly requires mastery of *advanced* techniques — not merely "applying updates" but ensuring **cryptographic, atomic, and verifiable** update lifecycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Secure Firmware Updates

The KTU 2024 PECPT863 syllabus clusters all secure firmware update techniques under **six operational pillars**. Mastering these is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Pillar 1 — Authenticity (Cryptographic Code Signing)

The firmware vendor signs the firmware image $F$ using a private key $K_{priv}$ from an asymmetric keypair $(K_{priv}, K_{pub})$. The public key $K_{pub}$ is provisioned into the device's immutable storage (eFuse, OTP ROM) during manufacturing.

The verification equation executed on the device is:

$$
\text{Verify}\big(\text{Sig}, F, K_{pub}\big) \xRightarrow{\text{HR}} \text{Boolean} \in \{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The signature algorithm is typically **ECDSA-P256** (NIST P-256 curve) or **Ed25519** because RSA-2048 has too large a footprint for resource-constrained CPS sensors (KTU CO3 emphasis).

### Pillar 2 — Integrity (Cryptographic Hashing)

The device recomputes a hash digest $H$ of the received image and compares it against the expected digest embedded in the signed metadata. Any single-bit modification causes hash divergence (avalanche effect).

$$
H_{device} = \text{SHA-256}(F_{received})
$$

$$
\text{IF } H_{device} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{signed} \Rightarrow \text{Integrity OK}
$$

### Pillar 3 — Freshness (Anti-Rollback Counter)

The device maintains a monotonically increasing counter $C_{monotonic}$ in write-once memory. The firmware image header contains the **Minimum Required Version** $V_{min}$. Updates with $V_{new} < C_{monotonic}$ are rejected.

$$
\text{Accept Update iff } V_{new} \geq C_{monotonic}
$$

This blocks the **evil-maid downgrade attack** where an attacker re-flashes an older, vulnerable firmware.

### Pillar 4 — Confidentiality (Encrypted Delivery)

For proprietary firmware IP or compliance-sensitive binaries (HIPAA, ITAR), the image is encrypted using **AES-128-GCM** or **ChaCha20-Poly1305** before transmission.

$$
C = \text{AES-128-GCM-Encrypt}(K_{session}, F, \text{Nonce}, \text{AAD})
$$

The session key $K_{session}$ is derived via **ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman)** between device and update server using the device's unique attestation key.

### Pillar 5 — Atomicity (A/B Dual-Bank Update)

The flash memory is partitioned into two banks: **Bank A** (currently running) and **Bank B** (staging). The new image is written entirely to Bank B; on successful verification, the bootloader flips a "next-boot" flag, and only then does the system reboot into Bank B. If the new image fails POST (Power-On Self-Test), the bootloader automatically reverts to Bank A.

$$
\text{NextBoot} \leftarrow \begin{cases} \text{Bank B} & \text{if } \text{Verify} = \text{TRUE} \land \text{POST} = \text{PASS} \\ \text{Bank A} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Pillar 6 — Confidentiality of Keys (TPM/TEE Integration)

The OEM private signing key **never** exists on the device. It resides in a Hardware Security Module (HSM) at the vendor's signing facility. Conversely, the device's **attestation key** is generated inside the TPM and never exported — it signs **attestation reports** proving the device's current firmware state to the vendor.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of the following table is sufficient to solve 95% of numerical/derivation questions in Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **ECDSA Signature** | $\text{Sig} = (r, s)$ where $r = (k \cdot G) \mod n$, $s = k^{-1}(H + d \cdot r) \mod n$ | $d$ = private key, $G$ = generator, $n$ = curve order | Authenticates firmware publisher |
| 2 | **ECDSA Verification** | $u_1 = H \cdot s^{-1} \mod n$, $u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \mod n$; accept iff $r = (u_1 G + u_2 Q) \mod n$ | $Q = d \cdot G$ = public key | Verifies signature on device |
| 3 | **Hash Integrity Check** | $H_{computed} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{stored}$ | Output: 256-bit digest (SHA-256) | Detects bit-flip / corruption |
| 4 | **Anti-Rollback Inequality** | $V_{candidate} \geq C_{monotonic}$ | Version number $V \in \mathbb{N}$ | Blocks downgrade attacks |
| 5 | **AES-GCM Authenticated Tag** | $\text{Tag} = \text{GHASH}(H, AAD, C) \oplus E(K, J_0)$ | Tag length: 128 bits | Provides confidentiality + integrity |
| 6 | **ECDH Key Agreement** | $S = d_a \cdot d_b \cdot G$ | $S$ = shared secret point | Derives session key for encrypted delivery |
| 7 | **Update Atomicity Invariant** | $\text{Device}(t^+) \in \{\text{Old Image}, \text{New Image}\}$, never $\emptyset$ | Boolean state | Guarantees no bricking |
| 8 | **Memory Overhead (Differential)** | $\Delta F = F_{new} \oplus F_{old}$ then bsdiff($\Delta F$) | Bytes saved | Bandwidth optimization for OTA |
| 9 | **Update Availability (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{recovery,i}$ | Seconds | Measures update pipeline resilience |
| 10 | **TPM PCR Extend** | $\text{PCR}_{n}^{new} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{PCR}_{n}^{old} \Vert \text{Measurement})$ | 256-bit register | Anchors chain-of-trust across reboot |
