| # | Design Principle | How It Prevents Stuxnet-Style Attacks |
|---|------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Hardware Security Modules (HSM) for signing keys** | Stolen software keys cannot sign code; HSMs are tamper-resistant and FIPS 140-2 Level 3+ certified. |
| 2 | **Strict certificate hierarchy and cross-signing controls** | Vendors should not share signing certificates with third parties; CAs must enforce domain constraints. |
| 3 | **Code provenance attestation** | Each firmware module should be signed by the *device OEM*, not a third-party driver vendor. |
| 4 | **PLC code-signing separation** | Ladder logic / control code should be signed with keys separate from OS/driver keys, following least-privilege. |
| 5 | **Behavior-based anomaly detection at the PLC** | Even a signed update that causes abnormal actuator behavior should be halted by a safety PLC. |
| 6 | **Cryptographic binding of update to target hardware** | Stuxnet-style broad-spectrum drivers cannot be installed on unrelated devices. |
| 7 | **Regular key rotation and CRL/OCSP deployment** | Compromise of any key in the chain must be detectable and revocable. |
| 8 | **Defense in depth — independent safety controller** | IEC 61508 SIL-rated hardware must enforce physical limits independent of any software update. |

**Conclusion for KTU 2024:**

Stuxnet taught the CPS community that *cryptographic authenticity alone is insufficient* — the entire **supply chain of trust** (CA, OEM, signing infrastructure, PLC, safety controller) must be hardened. Modern IEC 62443-4-2 certified components embed multiple of these principles by mandate.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for Stuxnet recap, 3 marks for the design principles table, 2 marks for IEC 62443 mapping.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hash with signature.** Writing "the firmware is verified by hashing it" is **wrong**. Hashing alone proves integrity but not authenticity. Always say: *hash + digital signature*.
> 2. **Forgetting the monotonic counter.** Many students describe signature verification but completely omit the anti-rollback mechanism. This costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring CPS context.** A generic "software update" answer without reference to physical safety, real-time constraints, or IEC 62443 will be marked down. Always tie the answer to *why* CPS is different from IT.
> 4. **Skipping the A/B partition rationale.** Just naming the technique is insufficient — the examiner wants the *fail-safe recovery* justification.
> 5. **Writing only definitions in Part B.** Part B (14 marks) requires diagrams, sub-stages, and worked examples. A text-only answer is capped at 8–9 marks maximum.
> 6. **Omitting standards.** Citing NIST SP 800-193, IEC 62443-3-3 SR 7.6, or ISO/SAE 21434 instantly elevates the answer to the top grade band.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- [x] Secure update = **authenticity + integrity + freshness + non-repudiation + recoverability** (AIFNR)
- [x] **SHA-256** produces a **256-bit (32-byte)** digest
- [x] **ECDSA-P256** signature is **512 bits** (r, s each 256 bits)
- [x] **RSA-2048** signature is **2048 bits** (256 bytes)
- [x] Digital signature flow: $h = \text{H}(B)$; $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(h)$; verify: $\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(\sigma, h) = \text{True}$
- [x] PCR extend: $PCR_{new} = \text{SHA-256}(PCR_{old} \Vert \text{new\_hash})$
- [x] Monotonic counter: accept iff $c_{bundle} \geq c_{current} + 1$
- [x] A/B partitioning provides **atomic**, **reversible**, **fail-safe** updates
- [x] HSMs / TPMs / Secure Elements store signing keys in **tamper-resistant** hardware
- [x] TLS 1.3 + mutual authentication = secure update **channel**
- [x] AES-256-GCM = secure update **confidentiality** + authenticated encryption
- [x] Stuxnet = textbook case of stolen code-signing certificates
- [x] Standards: **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 7.6**, **NIST SP 800-193**, **ISO/SAE 21434**, **IEC 62443-4-2**
- [x] OTA = Over-The-Air update (industry term)
- [x] Delta / differential updates save bandwidth in low-bandwidth CPS deployments
- [x] Anti-bricking = A/B partition + watchdog timer + automatic rollback
- [x] Attestation = TPM-signed PCR quote verified by OEM
- [x] Least-privilege signing hierarchy = separate keys for OS, drivers, control code
- [x] CPS update failures have **physical-world consequences** — not just data loss
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Assessment and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Assessment and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability Assessment (VA)** is the systematic, methodical, and documented process of identifying, quantifying, prioritizing, and classifying security weaknesses (vulnerabilities) in an information system, application, network, or Cyber-Physical System (CPS). It is a proactive, *diagnostic* exercise focused on discovery and cataloguing of flaws before they can be exploited by an adversary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability Management (VM)** is the continuous, closed-loop, and governance-driven *operational discipline* that encompasses the full lifecycle of vulnerabilities — from discovery, analysis, prioritization, remediation, verification, and reporting. Per NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4, VM is defined as the *"process of identifying, evaluating, treating, and reporting on security vulnerabilities in systems and the software that runs on them."*

**Distinction (Vital for KTU):**
- **Assessment** is a *point-in-time* measurement (a *snapshot*).
- **Management** is a *continuous program* (a *movie*).

## 1.2 Key Standardized Identifiers (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> - **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** A unique identifier (e.g., `CVE-2024-12345`) assigned by MITRE to a *specific instance* of a vulnerability in a specific product. There is **one CVE per vulnerability per affected product version**.
> - **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration):** A *category* or *class* of weakness (e.g., `CWE-89` for SQL Injection, `CWE-79` for XSS). One CWE maps to many CVEs.
> - **NVD (National Vulnerability Database):** The U.S. government repository (nvd.nist.gov) that *enriches* CVEs with CVSS scores, CPE matches, and reference data.
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** A **0.0 to 10.0** numerical score reflecting severity (None, Low, Medium, High, Critical).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Check-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A city wants to keep its water pipeline network safe.
> - **Vulnerability Assessment** is the *annual health check-up* of the city. Engineers walk the pipeline, use X-ray scanners, listen for leaks, run pressure tests, and produce a written report: *"Pipe A-17 has 2mm corrosion; Pipe C-03 has loose bolts."* The check-up ends with a report.
> - **Vulnerability Management** is the *entire municipal maintenance department*. It not only finds the leaks but **schedules the repair crew**, **orders replacement parts**, **fixes Pipe A-17 within 7 days**, **retests it**, **closes the ticket**, and **schedules the next inspection in 6 months**. The department *never sleeps*.
>
> In a **Cyber-Physical System** (e.g., a smart power grid, autonomous vehicle, or industrial PLC), vulnerabilities are not just data leaks — they can cause **physical damage, loss of life, or environmental disaster** (think Stuxnet, 2010). This makes VM in CPS a *safety-critical* function, not merely an IT hygiene task.

## 1.4 Why This Topic in CPS (Context for KTU 2024 Scheme)

CPS components — **sensors, actuators, RTUs, PLCs, SCADA masters, IoT gateways, embedded firmware** — have very different attack surfaces than traditional IT:
- Long product lifecycles (20–30 years for industrial controllers).
- Inability to patch frequently (downtime cost is astronomical).
- Proprietary, undocumented protocols (Modbus, DNP3, PROFINET, EtherCAT).
- Real-time, deterministic constraints where security scanning itself can disrupt operations.

This makes **risk-prioritized, non-intrusive** vulnerability management mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (NIST Aligned)

The discipline follows an **infinite loop** (not a one-shot project). The seven canonical phases are:

1. **Discover / Inventory:** Catalogue every asset — hardware, firmware version, OS, application, protocol, network port. *Without an accurate asset inventory, vulnerability management is impossible.* For CPS, this includes every PLC, RTU, sensor, and engineering workstation.
2. **Scan / Identify:** Use automated scanners (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, ICS-specific tools like Claroty or Nozomi Networks) to detect known CVEs. **Active scanning on OT networks is often forbidden** because probe traffic can crash legacy equipment. Passive discovery (monitoring SPAN traffic) is preferred.
3. **Analyze / Prioritize:** Triage raw scanner output. A scanner may report 10,000 findings — most are noise. Engineers must deduplicate, filter false positives, and apply business context.
4. **Assess / Score:** Assign a severity score (CVSS) and a contextual *risk* score.
5. **Treat / Remediate:** Choose one of four actions — **Remediate** (patch), **Mitigate** (compensating control like firewall rule), **Accept** (documented risk acceptance by a CISO/factory manager), or **Avoid** (decommission the asset).
6. **Verify:** Re-scan or manually confirm the fix worked and did not break functionality. In CPS, this often requires a *maintenance window* and *safety integrity checks*.
7. **Report / Monitor:** Feed KPIs (Mean Time to Remediate, vulnerability density, SLA compliance) to governance dashboards.

## 2.2 The CVSS v3.1 Severity Scoring Framework

CVSS has three metric groups, producing a score between **0.0** and **10.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Base Score Formula (CVSS v3.1)** — the *industry-standard* severity metric.

$$
\text{Impact}(I) = 1 - \big( (1 - C) \times (1 - I_c) \times (1 - A_c) \big)
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability}(E) = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min(I + S,\; 10) \right)
$$

where for *Scope Unchanged*:
$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min\!\left( I + S_{\text{unchanged}},\; 10 \right) \right)
$$
and for *Scope Changed*:
$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min\!\left( 1.08 \times (I + S_{\text{changed}}),\; 10 \right) \right)
$$

with $S = 6.42 \times I$ and the *Impact Sub-Score* $I$ itself depending on $C, I_c, A_c$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

| Term | Meaning | Range / Domain | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $CVSS_{\text{Base}}$ | Inherent severity of a flaw | $0.0 - 10.0$ | Score produced by NVD analysts |
| $CVSS_{\text{Temporal}}$ | Severity over time (with exploit availability) | $0.0 - 10.0$ | Considers EBM, RC, RL metrics |
| $CVSS_{\text{Environmental}$ | Severity in *your* specific context | $0.0 - 10.0$ | Allows custom CR/IR/AR/MAV etc. |
| $\text{Risk}$ | $\text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Categorical | Classic NIST risk equation |
| $AV$ | Attack Vector (Network/Adjacent/Local/Physical) | $0.20, 0.55, 0.62, 0.85$ | Physical is worst for CPS |
| $AC$ | Attack Complexity (Low/High) | $0.44, 0.77$ | Low = worse for defender |
| $PR$ | Privileges Required (None/Low/High) | $0.85, 0.62, 0.27$ | None = worst |
| $UI$ | User Interaction (None/Required) | $0.85, 0.62$ | None = worst |
| $C$ | Confidentiality impact | $0.0, 0.22, 0.56$ | None/Low/High |
| $I_c$ | Integrity impact | $0.0, 0.22, 0.56$ | Critical for CPS safety |
| $A_c$ | Availability impact | $0.0, 0.22, 0.56$ | Critical for CPS uptime |
| $S$ | Scope (Unchanged/Changed) | multiplier $1$ or $1.08$ | Changed = escalates score |
| $\text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time To Remediate | Days/Hours | KPI for VM programs |
| $\text{EPSS}$ | Exploit Prediction Scoring System | $0.0 - 1.0$ | Probability of *exploitation in next 30 days* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In a power plant, a CVSS 9.8 RCE in an engineering workstation may be downgraded to *Medium* in the *Environmental* score because the workstation sits on an air-gapped VLAN, but the same CVE in an internet-facing HMI (Human-Machine Interface) would remain *Critical*. Context is everything.

## 2.4 Types of Vulnerability Assessment (Examiner Favourite)

- **Network-based scan:** Scans the network perimeter and internal segments. (Tools: Nmap, Nessus).
- **Host-based scan:** Inspects configuration, registry, file integrity on a single host. (Tools: OpenSCAP, Microsoft Defender).
- **Wireless scan:** Validates Wi-Fi, Zigbee, LoRa, BLE (very relevant for IoT-CPS).
- **Application scan:** DAST/SAST on web front-ends of CPS dashboards.
- **Passive / Agent-less OT scan:** Listens to industrial protocol traffic (Modbus, EtherNet/IP) without sending probe packets. *The* standard for SCADA networks.
- **Credentialed vs Non-credentialed:** Credentialed gives deeper visibility; non-credentialed simulates an external attacker.

## 2.5 CPS-Specific Vulnerability Categories (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Firmware vulnerabilities** — outdated controller firmware with hard-coded credentials (e.g., `CWE-798`).
> 2. **Protocol weaknesses** — lack of authentication in Modbus/TCP, plaintext in DNP3.
> 3. **Configuration drift** — devices deployed with default passwords (Mirai botnet, 2016).
> 4. **Supply-chain / Component CVEs** — a vulnerable open-source library embedded in HMI software.
> 5. **Side-channel vulnerabilities** — timing, power, or acoustic emissions that leak cryptographic keys from PLCs.
> 6. **Undocumented backdoors** — vendor maintenance accounts (Schneider, Siemens, GE have had this issue).
> 7. **Network topology exposures** — flat L2 OT networks, exposed engineering ports (port 502, 2222, 44818).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: CVSS v3.1 Base Score Calculation

**Problem:** A PLC firmware has a vulnerability where an attacker on the *adjacent network* can trigger a stack buffer overflow with *low complexity*, *no privileges*, and *no user interaction*, leading to *high* impact on **Integrity** and **Availability**, but *none* on **Confidentiality**. The exploit affects the PLC *and* crashes the engineering workstation (Scope = Changed). Compute the Base Score.

**Step 1: Identify the metric values from the CVSS v3.1 vector.**
- `AV:X:A` → Adjacent Network → $AV = 0.55$
- `AC:L` → Low complexity → $AC = 0.44$
- `PR:N` → None → $PR = 0.85$
- `UI:N` → None → $UI = 0.62$
- `C:N` → None → $C = 0.0$
- `I:H` → High → $I_c = 0.56$
- `A:H` → High → $A_c = 0.56$
- `S:C` → Changed → multiplier of $1.08$

**Step 2: Compute the Impact Sub-Score $I$.**

$$
I = 1 - \big( (1 - C) \times (1 - I_c) \times (1 - A_c) \big)
$$

Substituting:

$$
I = 1 - \big( (1 - 0.0) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \big)
$$

$$
I = 1 - \big( 1.0 \times 0.44 \times 0.44 \big)
$$

$$
I = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Impact value passed into the final formula.** For Scope Changed, the inner quantity is $I_{\text{adj}} = I$.

**Step 4: Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score $E$.**

$$
E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

$$
E = 8.22 \times 0.55 \times 0.44 \times 0.85 \times 0.62
$$

$$
E = 8.22 \times 0.12759040 = 1.0487
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Scope-Changed Base Score formula.**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min(1.08 \times (I + E),\; 10) \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min(1.08 \times (0.8064 + 1.0487),\; 10) \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min(1.08 \times 1.8551,\; 10) \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\!\left( \min(2.00351,\; 10) \right)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(2.00351) = 2.1
$$

**Result:** Base Score = **2.1 (Low)**. Note how *Scope Changed* does **not** always elevate a score; with $C=0$, the final score is low even though the flaw affects two components.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — VA Methodologies in CPS

| Method | Intrusiveness | CPS Safety Impact | Coverage | Detection Depth | Cost (₹/Effort) | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Active Network Scan (Nessus) | High | **High Risk** (can crash legacy PLCs) | All reachable IPs | Deep CVE check | Low | IT networks, isolated lab replicas |
| Passive Discovery (Nozomi, Claroty) | None | **None** | All visible on SPAN | CVE + protocol anomaly | High | Live OT/ICS production |
| Agent-based (Tenable.ad, Qualys) | Medium | Low | Per-host | Very Deep | Medium | Windows engineering stations |
| Red Team Pen-Test | Variable | Medium | Targeted | Exploit validation | Very High | Annual CPS validation |
| Configuration Audit (CIS Benchmarks) | None | **None** | One host | Misconfigurations | Low | Post-deployment hardening |
| Firmware Reverse Engineering | None | None | One device | Hidden backdoors | Very High | High-assurance audits |

---

## 3.3 Derived Risk Equation (NIST SP 800-30 Adapted)

For a single asset $a$ and threat $t$:

$$
R(a, t) = L(t) \times I(a, t) \times \big( 1 - P_{\text{control}}(a, t) \big)
$$

where $L(t)$ is the *likelihood* of the threat, $I(a,t)$ is the *impact* magnitude, and $P_{\text{control}}$ is the probability that existing controls defeat the threat. When summed over all relevant threat-action pairs:

$$
R_{\text{total}}(a) = \sum_{t \in T} R(a,t)
$$

A CPS engineer uses this to **rank** assets (e.g., turbines, boilers, chillers) and allocate scarce patch-maintenance windows.

---

## 3.4 Worked MTTR Example

> **Question (Frequently asked):** A VM team processed 240 vulnerabilities in a quarter. 200 were remediated within the SLA. Compute the **MTTR Compliance Ratio** and the **MTTR** if the SLA for *Critical* (CVSS ≥ 9.0) is 15 days, *High* (7.0–8.9) is 30 days, and total remediation time recorded was 5,400 days.

$$
\text{Compliance Ratio} = \frac{\text{Findings within SLA}}{\text{Total findings}} = \frac{200}{240} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total remediation time (days)}}{\text{Number of closed tickets}} = \frac{5400}{200} = 27 \text{ days}
$$

> This indicates the program is meeting only the *High* SLA but missing the *Critical* 15-day SLA — a typical KTU board-question insight.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (Continuous Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[DISCOVER: Asset and Configuration Inventory]
    nodeB[SCAN: Active and Passive Vulnerability Detection]
    nodeC[ANALYZE: Triage, Deduplicate, Filter False Positives]
    nodeD[PRIORITIZE: CVSS plus EPSS plus Business Context]
    nodeE[TREAT: Remediate or Mitigate or Accept or Avoid]
    nodeF[VERIFY: Re-scan plus Functional plus Safety Check]
    nodeG[REPORT: KPIs to Governance plus Audit Trail]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeA
    classDef phase fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG phase
```

## 4.2 CPS Vulnerability Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[CPS Vulnerability Surface]
    n1[Firmware Level: Hardcoded creds, Outdated RTOS, Backdoors]
    n2[Protocol Level: Modbus, DNP3, PROFINET weaknesses]
    n3[Network Level: Flat L2, Exposed 502, Default VLANs]
    n4[Application Level: HMI web flaws, SQLi, XSS]
    n5[Physical Level: Tamper, Side-channel, JTAG exposure]
    n6[Supply Chain Level: Vulnerable OSS libs, Vendor CVE]
    n7[Human Level: Phishing of operators, Weak passwords]
    root --> n1
    root --> n2
    root --> n3
    root --> n4
    root --> n5
    root --> n6
    root --> n7
    classDef lvl1 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#202124
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#202124
    class root lvl1
    class n1,n2,n3,n4,n5,n6,n7 lvl2
```

## 4.3 Scoring & Prioritization Flow (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[New CVE published in NVD] --> s2{CVSS Base Score}
    s2 -->|0.0 to 3.9| s3[Low: Add to backlog, review monthly]
    s2 -->|4.0 to 6.9| s4[Medium: Schedule within 90 days]
    s2 -->|7.0 to 8.9| s5[High: Schedule within 30 days]
    s2 -->|9.0 to 10.0| s6{Critical: Apply EPSS plus CISA KEV}
    s6 -->|Known exploited| s7[Emergency: Patch in 24 to 72 hours]
    s6 -->|No known exploit| s8[Plan patch within 14 days]
    s3 --> s9[Continuous monitoring]
    s4 --> s9
    s5 --> s9
    s7 --> s9
    s8 --> s9
    classDef low fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#202124
    classDef med fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#202124
    classDef high fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#202124
    classDef crit fill:#D93025,stroke:#7A0E0B,color:#FFFFFF
    class s3,s9 low
    class s4 med
    class s5 high
    class s6,s7,s8 crit
```

## 4.4 CPS-Aware Assessment Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[IT Zone: Corporate Network]
        a1[IT Asset Inventory CMDB]
        a2[Active VA Scanner: Nessus or Qualys]
    end
    subgraph P2[DMZ Zone: Industrial Demilitarized Zone]
        a3[Firewall plus ICS Protocol Filter]
        a4[Jump Host for Engineers]
    end
    subgraph P3[OT Zone: CPS Field Network]
        a5[Passive OT Sensors: Nozomi or Claroty]
        a6[SPAN Port Tap]
        a7[PLC plus RTU plus HMI Fleet]
    end
    subgraph P4[Analytics Zone]
        a8[SIEM plus VM Platform]
        a9[Risk Scoring Engine: CVSS plus EPSS plus Asset Criticality]
        a10[Patch and Change Management System]
    end
    a1 --> a8
    a2 --> a8
    a6 --> a5
    a5 --> a8
    a7 --- a6
    a8 --> a9
    a9 --> a10
    a10 --> a4
    a4 --> a3
    a3 --> a7
    classDef zone fill:#F1F3F4,stroke:#5F6368,color:#202124
    classDef sys fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 zone
    class a1,a2,a3,a4,a5,a6,a7,a8,a9,a10 sys
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Differentiate between Vulnerability Assessment and Vulnerability Management. Why is the distinction critical in Cyber-Physical Systems?**

**Model Answer:**
Vulnerability Assessment is a *point-in-time* diagnostic exercise that identifies, classifies, and reports weaknesses using scanners and manual tests. Vulnerability Management is a *continuous, governance-driven program* that *additionally* triages, prioritizes, remediates, verifies, and reports on the vulnerabilities across their entire lifecycle. In CPS, the distinction is critical because an assessment alone leaves a SCADA system exposed between audits; only a management program provides the *always-on* monitoring required when vulnerabilities in PLC firmware can be weaponized in *zero-days* and may cause physical harm (equipment damage, environmental release, or loss of life). CPS environments also forbid frequent active scans, making the continuous passive-discovery component of VM indispensable.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Style]`
**Explain the role of CVE, CWE, NVD, and CVSS in vulnerability management.**

**Model Answer:**
The four terms form a coordinated ecosystem maintained by MITRE and NIST. A **CWE** (Common Weakness Enumeration) defines a *class* of flaw (e.g., CWE-787 Out-of-bounds Write). A **CVE** (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) is a unique, product-specific *instance* of a flaw (e.g., CVE-2024-3094, the XZ Utils backdoor). The **NVD** (National Vulnerability Database) is the U.S. government repository that *enriches* each CVE with metadata, affected product enumeration (CPE), references, and severity scores. **CVSS** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) provides the *quantitative* 0.0–10.0 severity scoring framework, with three metric groups: Base, Temporal, and Environmental. Together they allow engineers worldwide to communicate unambiguously about the *same* vulnerability, which is essential for global CPS supply-chain coordination.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. When asked to *list* phases, students often write 3–4 phases and miss 1–2 marks. Use the *canonical 7-phase NIST lifecycle* (Discover, Scan, Analyze, Prioritize, Treat, Verify, Report) and *number them* clearly.
> 2. CVSS questions: Students frequently *forget the roundUp* function and submit raw decimals, losing 2 marks. Always apply $\text{roundUp}(\text{value}, 1)$ per the official spec.
> 3. On CPS-specific questions, students answer in pure IT terms (e.g., "install antivirus"). Examiners *deduct marks* if you do not address **safety, determinism, and air-gap** constraints. Always state the *CPS limitation* explicitly.
> 4. Diagram questions demand *labelled* boxes with arrows showing *direction*; an unlabelled flowchart loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. Avoid confusion between **CVE vs CWE**: examiners often award a *bonus mark* for correct, crisp distinction.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **NIST-aligned Vulnerability Management Lifecycle**. With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain how the lifecycle is *modified* when applied to an **Industrial Control System / SCADA network**, justifying the modifications with specific CPS constraints.

**Model Solution:**

The NIST-aligned vulnerability management lifecycle is a **seven-phase continuous loop**:
1. **Discover / Inventory** — Catalogue every asset: PLC model, firmware version, RTU IP, HMI software, engineering workstation OS, switch firmware, and even cable runs.
2. **Scan** — Identify known vulnerabilities using automated tools.
3. **Analyze** — Triage, deduplicate, and filter false positives.
4. **Prioritize** — Score using CVSS + EPSS + business context.
5. **Treat** — Choose one of {Remediate, Mitigate, Accept, Avoid}.
6. **Verify** — Re-test the fix in a *lab replica* before production.
7. **Report** — Feed KPIs to a SIEM and audit trail.

**CPS Modifications (with labelled diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    c1[Discover: Passive Asset Discovery via SPAN]
    c2[Scan: ONLY Passive ICS-aware Tools]
    c3[Analyze: Map to Purdue Model Levels]
    c4[Prioritize: Safety plus Availability impact first]
    c5[Treat: Patching within Maintenance Window]
    c6[Verify: Safety Integrity Test plus Lab Replica]
    c7[Report: Integrate with Safety Instrumented System]
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3
    c3 --> c4
    c4 --> c5
    c5 --> c6
    c6 --> c7
    c7 --> c1
```

**Justifications for the CPS modifications:**
- Active scanning is replaced by **passive SPAN-based monitoring** because probe packets (e.g., SYN scans) can crash legacy PLCs running Modbus/TCP. *This addresses the real-time determinism constraint.*
- Prioritization is dominated by **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** because a vulnerability that can cause a *physical actuator* to misbehave outranks an IT data-leak CVE.
- Treatment is *scheduled* within a maintenance window because unplanned downtime in a refinery can cost ₹1 crore per hour.
- Verification involves *safety integrity checks* and a *lab replica* (digital twin), not just a re-scan.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the CVSS v3.1 **Base Score** for the following vulnerability and interpret the severity band. A vulnerability in a hospital infusion pump allows an attacker on the **physical** device to press a hidden button combo and rewrite the **firmware**. The attack requires **low complexity**, **no privileges**, and **no user interaction**. Impact: Confidentiality = *High*, Integrity = *High*, Availability = *High*. The vulnerability affects only the pump itself (Scope = Unchanged).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Vector and metric values**
- `AV:P` → Physical → $AV = 0.20$
- `AC:L` → Low → $AC = 0.77$
- `PR:N` → None → $PR = 0.85$
- `UI:N` → None → $UI = 0.85$
- `C:H` → $0.56$
- `I:H` → $0.56$
- `A:H` → $0.56$
- `S:U` → Unchanged

**Step 2: Impact Sub-Score $I$**

$$
I = 1 - \big( (1 - 0.56)^3 \big) = 1 - (0.44)^3 = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816
$$

**Step 3: Exploitability $E$**

$$
E = 8.22 \times 0.20 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
E = 8.22 \times 0.1112885 = 0.9148
$$

**Step 4: Base Score (Scope Unchanged)**

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\big( \min(I + 6.42 \times I,\; 10) \big)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\big( \min(0.914816 \times 7.42,\; 10) \big)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}\big( \min(6.78793,\; 10) \big)
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(6.78793) = 6.9
$$

**Interpretation:** **6.9 = Medium severity.** Despite being a *physical* attack with full impact, the *Physical* attack vector and *Low* attack complexity dampen the score because an attacker must already have physical proximity. In a real *Environmental* score, if the pump is in a public ward, the *Modified Attack Vector* may be re-rated to *Network* and the score would jump to **Critical**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **active network scanning, passive OT discovery, and agent-based host scanning** in the context of CPS vulnerability assessment. Construct a comparative table and recommend the optimal hybrid approach for a **smart-grid substation** with 200 PLCs from two different vendors.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (already covered in SECTION 3.2, summarized here for marks):**

| Dimension | Active Scan | Passive Discovery | Agent-based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intrusiveness | High | None | Medium |
| Coverage of OT | Limited (risky) | Excellent | Limited to host-OS devices |
| Detection Depth | CVE signatures | CVE + protocol anomaly | Deep host config + CVE |
| CPS Safety Risk | High | Negligible | Low |

**Recommended Hybrid Approach for Smart-Grid Substation:**
1. **Layer 1 (Continuous):** Deploy a **passive ICS-aware sensor** (e.g., Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos) on a SPAN port of the substation switch. It will discover all 200 PLCs, map Modbus/DNP3 traffic, and flag known CVEs *without sending any probe packets* — *zero risk of crashing protective relays.*
2. **Layer 2 (Quarterly):** Run an **active scan inside a *substation replica lab* (digital twin)** using Nessus or Tenable.ot. This validates exploitability without affecting the live grid.
3. **Layer 3 (Per-host):** Install lightweight **agents on engineering workstations and HMIs** (Windows/Linux hosts) for deep configuration assessment. *Do not* install agents on the PLCs themselves (vendor lockout and warranty issues).
4. **Layer 4 (Annual):** Conduct a *red-team* exercise targeting the substation firewall and jump host to test *human* and *procedural* controls.

This four-layer hybrid satisfies IEC 62443 and NERC-CIP requirements while protecting real-time control loops.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing plant's VM program recorded the following data for Q1: Total vulnerabilities closed = 180; Total vulnerabilities within SLA = 144; Total remediation time = 4,320 days; Number of critical CVE patches deployed = 24. The *Critical* SLA is 7 days, *High* SLA is 30 days. Calculate the **MTTR**, the **SLA Compliance Ratio**, and discuss — with CPS reasoning — two improvements the CISO should implement next quarter.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: MTTR**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{4320}{180} = 24 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2: SLA Compliance Ratio**

$$
\text{SLA Compliance} = \frac{144}{180} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3: Critical CVE mean remediation time**

If 24 critical CVEs were deployed in 4,320 days, the *implied average* per critical CVE is:

$$
\text{AvgTime(Critical)} = \frac{4320}{24} = 180 \text{ days}
$$

This is **massively non-compliant** with the 7-day SLA.

**Two CPS-aware Improvements (with justification):**

1. **Establish a *hot-spare* digital twin for emergency patching.** Currently, every critical patch is likely tested on the *live* line, requiring a production halt. A digital twin of the assembly line allows 24/7 patch validation *without* scheduling downtime, reducing critical MTTR from 180 days to under 7 days.
2. **Adopt a *risk-based* SLA tiering using CVSS + EPSS + Safety-Impact-Score.** Many "High" CVEs may never be exploited (low EPSS), so over-investing in them delays critical work. A combined scoring model that includes *Safety Integrity Level* (per IEC 61508) ensures that the *Critical* band always gets engineer attention first, raising the SLA compliance ratio from 80% to a target of 95%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **VA = Snapshot; VM = Continuous Program.** Always state this distinction first in any definition question.
- The **7-phase VM lifecycle** is: **Discover → Scan → Analyze → Prioritize → Treat → Verify → Report** — remember in *clockwise* order.
- **CVE** = specific instance; **CWE** = class; **NVD** = enriched database; **CVSS** = severity score. **CVE-1 : CWE-N**, not the other way.
- CVSS v3.1 score is in **$0.0$ to $10.0$** with bands **None(0.0), Low(0.1–3.9), Medium(4.0–6.9), High(7.0–8.9), Critical(9.0–10.0)**. Always apply **$\text{roundUp}$** to one decimal.
- **CPS forbids active scanning** on production OT networks — *always* prefer **passive** discovery via SPAN.
- The four treatment options are **Remediate, Mitigate, Accept, Avoid** — examiners love this 4-element set.
- Prioritize using **CVSS + EPSS + business context** — never CVSS alone; CVSS ignores *exploit availability* and *your environment*.
- **KPIs to remember:** MTTR (lower is better), SLA Compliance Ratio (higher is better), Vulnerability Density (per asset).
- Always mention **safety** (IEC 61508 SIL) and **standards** (IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-40, NIST SP 800-82) in any CPS answer — they are high-yield keywords for full marks.
- For *embedded* devices, *patching is the exception, not the rule* — favour *compensating controls* (network segmentation, jump host, MFA on engineering access).
- **Side-channel** and **supply-chain** vulnerabilities are *post-2020* high-frequency examiner topics; mention them to score above peer average.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Techniques for Assessing Vulnerabilities in CPS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Tools and Techniques for Assessing Vulnerabilities in CPS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computational and communication core interacting with physical processes. Examples include SCADA-controlled power grids, PLC-driven assembly lines, autonomous vehicles, and smart water-treatment plants.

**Vulnerability Assessment (VA)** in CPS is the systematic, methodical process of identifying, classifying, prioritizing, and quantifying security weaknesses across the cyber, physical, and hybrid interfaces of a CPS. Unlike classical IT vulnerability assessment, CPS-VA must also account for:
- **Safety-coupled failures** (a memory leak in a PLC can cause a physical catastrophe).
- **Real-time constraints** (DoS becomes a kinetic event).
- **Legacy protocols** (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet lack authentication by design).
- **Hardware/firmware trust roots** (tamper-resistant controllers, TPMs, secure boot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT863 – Module 4):** Vulnerability assessment in CPS is the disciplined application of scanning, testing, modeling, and formal-verification techniques to discover, score, and remediate exploitable weaknesses in the cyber, physical, and human-in-the-loop components of a CPS, guided by standards such as **NIST SP 800-82**, **IEC 62443**, and **CVSS v3.1/4.0**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Check-up**
> Imagine a CPS as a **hospital with three wings**: the *Cyber Wing* (computers, networks), the *Physical Wing* (sensors, actuators, machines), and the *Bridge* (the wired/wireless connections between them). A *vulnerability assessment* is like a **full-body medical check-up**:
> - **Blood tests & scans** → *Static & Dynamic code analysis* (looking at the software's internals).
> - **Stress tests on the heart** → *Penetration testing* (deliberately stressing the system with simulated attacks).
> - **Walking on a treadmill with electrodes** → *Fuzz testing* (sending weird, unexpected inputs to see what breaks).
> - **Genetic screening** → *Formal verification & model checking* (proving mathematically that the design has no flaw).
> - **Doctor's prescription slip** → **CVSS score + remediation plan**.
>
> In a CPS, a "disease" (vulnerability) in the *Cyber Wing* can directly damage the *Physical Wing* (e.g., a malware altering a setpoint on a furnace). Hence, the check-up must look at *both* wings and the *bridge*.

### 1.3 Core Vulnerability-Assessment Terminology (Bolded for KTU High-Yield Recall)

| Term | Meaning (KTU Board-Preferred Wording) |
|---|---|
| **CVE** | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a unique identifier (e.g., CVE-2024-3094) for a publicly known security flaw. |
| **CWE** | Common Weakness Enumeration — a community-developed catalog of software/hardware weakness types. |
| **CVSS** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System — an open framework (0.0–10.0) to communicate vulnerability severity. |
| **NVD** | National Vulnerability Database (USA NIST) — the authoritative repository of CVE entries enriched with CVSS scores. |
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all points (network ports, APIs, firmware, physical connectors) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter or extract data. |
| **Attack Vector (AV)** | The context by which a vulnerability can be exploited (Network, Adjacent, Local, Physical). |
| **Threat Model** | A structured representation of assets, adversaries, attack paths, and mitigations. |
| **Zero-Day** | A previously unknown vulnerability for which no patch exists. |
| **ICS / SCADA** | Industrial Control System / Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — the classic CPS class. |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller — the field-level digital computer of a CPS. |
| **HMI** | Human-Machine Interface — operator-facing dashboard. |
| **RTU** | Remote Terminal Unit — field telemetry/control device. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of CPS Vulnerability-Assessment Techniques

Vulnerability-assessment techniques in CPS are best organized along **three orthogonal axes**: *Phase of System Lifecycle*, *Depth of Analysis*, and *Type of Evidence Produced*.

### Axis A — Phase of System Lifecycle
1. **Design-Time Assessment**
   - Threat modeling (STRIDE, PASTA, LINDDUN, VAST).
   - Formal verification (model checking, theorem proving).
   - Attack-tree / attack-graph construction.
2. **Build-Time Assessment**
   - Static Application Security Testing (SAST) — analyzes source/binary without execution.
   - Software Composition Analysis (SCA) — flags known-vulnerable libraries.
   - Firmware image inspection (binwalk, firmware-mod-kit).
3. **Run-Time Assessment**
   - Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST).
   - Network vulnerability scanning (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS).
   - Active/passive network monitoring (Wireshark, Zeek).
   - ICS-aware protocol fuzzing (Peach Fuzzer, Boofuzz, AFL for embedded).
4. **Post-Incident / Continuous Assessment**
   - Red-teaming / Purple-teaming.
   - Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) correlation.
   - Digital-twin continuous evaluation.

### Axis B — Depth of Analysis
| Depth | Technique | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Black-box** | Pen-test from outside, no internal docs | Externally visible flaws |
| **Grey-box** | Partial internal knowledge (creds, schematics) | Hybrid-class flaws |
| **White-box** | Full source, design, schematics, threat model | Logical and structural flaws |
| **Crystal-box** | Source + side-channel + physical access (e.g., chip decapping) | Hardware Trojans, fault injection paths |

### Axis C — Type of Evidence Produced
- **Quantitative** (e.g., CVSS numeric score, CVSS Environmental, Mean-Time-To-Compromise).
- **Qualitative** (e.g., STRIDE classification, DREAD rating, NIST 800-30 risk levels: *Low / Moderate / High / Very High*).
- **Formal** (e.g., temporal-logic proof that an unsafe state is unreachable).

### 2.2 Standard Frameworks Governing CPS Vulnerability Assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Reference Set — Memorize the 5 Acronyms**

1. **NIST SP 800-82 r3** — *Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security*; the baseline for CPS security.
2. **IEC 62443** — International standard for **Industrial Automation & Control System (IACS)** security; defines *Security Levels (SL 0–4)* and *Zones & Conduits*.
3. **NIST CSF 2.0** — *Cybersecurity Framework*; the *Identify–Protect–Detect–Respond–Recover* loop, with 2024 update adding a *Govern* function.
4. **CVSS v4.0** (2023) — Adds *Threat*, *Environmental*, and *Supplemental* metric groups; mandatory to use as per NVD since 2024.
5. **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** — Adversary tactics/techniques catalog for industrial environments (e.g., T0836 *Modify Parameter* on a PLC).

### 2.3 CVSS v3.1 / v4.0 — The Formula Cheat Sheet

CVSS produces three metric-group scores that combine into an overall severity:

| Metric Group | Scope | Use-Case in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Base** | Intrinsic properties of the flaw | Always required |
| **Temporal** | Changes over the flaw's life (exploit availability, patch level) | Re-evaluated quarterly by NVD |
| **Environmental** | Tailored to a specific CPS deployment (safety impact, criticality) | Used by asset owner to prioritize |
| **Threat (v4.0 only)** | Real-world exploit activity from authoritative sources | New in v4.0 |
| **Supplemental (v4.0 only)** | Out-of-band attributes (Safety, Automatable, Recovery) | Critical for CPS |

#### The CVSS Equations (Verbatim from FIRST.org)

**Impact Sub-Score (ISC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC}_{\text{unchanged}} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\bigr] \\
\text{ISC}_{\text{changed}} &= 7.52 \times \bigl[\text{ISC}_{\text{base}} - 0.029\bigr] - 3.25 \times \bigl[\text{ISC}_{\text{base}} - 0.02\bigr]^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C, I, A \in \{0.0,\; 0.22,\; 0.56,\; 0.85\}$ are Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability metric values (None/Low/High).

**Impact (range 0.0 – 10.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact}_{\text{unchanged}} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISC} \\
\text{Impact}_{\text{changed}} &= 7.52 \times (\text{ISC} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISC} - 0.02)^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Exploitability (range 0.0 – 10.0):**

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}
$$

where $\text{AV} \in \{0.20, 0.55, 0.62, 0.85\}$ (Physical/Local/Adjacent/Network), $\text{AC} \in \{0.44, 0.77\}$ (Low/High), $\text{PR} \in \{0.20, 0.50, 0.62, 0.85\}$ (None/Low/High for unchanged), and $\text{UI} \in \{0.52, 0.85, 0.62\}$ (None/Required for unchanged).

**Base Score (range 0.0 – 10.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \text{Impact} \le 0 &: \text{Base} = 0 \\
\text{else if } \text{Exploitability} \le 0 &: \text{Base} = 0 \\
\text{else} &: \text{Base} = \bigl\lceil \min\bigl[(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\; 10\bigr] \bigr\rceil_{1\text{dec}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity Bands (CVSS v3.1):** *None* = 0.0; *Low* = 0.1–3.9; *Medium* = 4.0–6.9; *High* = 7.0–8.9; *Critical* = 9.0–10.0.
> In **CVSS v4.0** the bands become: *None / Low (0.1–3.9) / Medium (4.0–6.9) / High (7.0–8.9) / Critical (9.0–10.0)* — same numbers, but a finer-grained *Threat* metric raises Critical-flaws' real-world priority.

### 2.4 Threat-Modeling Frameworks (KTU 7-Mark Favourite)

| Framework | Full Name | Best Fit in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **STRIDE** | Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege | Per-data-flow-diagram (DFD) analysis of a CPS sub-system. |
| **PASTA** | Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis | Risk-driven, business-impact-aligned (good for safety cases). |
| **LINDDUN** | Linkability, Identifiability, Non-repudiation, Detectability, Disclosure, Unawareness, Non-compliance | CPS with strong privacy (smart meters, medical CPS). |
| **VAST** | Visual, Agile, and Simple Threat modeling | Scales to enterprise CPS deployments. |
| **Attack Trees** | Hierarchical AND/OR decomposition of attack goals | Decomposing a "Damage a turbine" goal into sub-goals. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid operators** run **continuous NVD + EPSS** (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) correlation against their asset inventory (PLC firmware, HMI Windows hosts) to triage patches.
- **Automotive OEMs** combine **STRIDE** with **formal verification (UPPAAL, Simulink Design Verifier)** to argue ISO 21434 (UN R155) compliance for road vehicles.
- **Plant-floor engineers** use **ICS-aware passive scanners** (e.g., Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos) because *active* scanning can crash legacy PLCs — so the technique must be tuned.
- **Certification labs** use **fuzzing against protocol implementations (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC UA)** to satisfy IEC 62443-4-2 component-level requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CVSS Base-Score Calculation (Network-Available Buffer Overflow in a PLC Web Server)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (high-yield KTU 14-mark template):** A vendor discloses **CVE-2024-XXXXX** — a buffer overflow in the embedded HTTP server of a substation PLC. Configuration:
> - Attack Vector = **Network** (0.85)
> - Attack Complexity = **Low** (0.77)
> - Privileges Required = **None** (0.85)
> - User Interaction = **None** (0.85)
> - Confidentiality = **High** (0.56)
> - Integrity = **High** (0.56)
> - Availability = **High** (0.56)
> - Scope = **Changed**

Compute the CVSS v3.1 Base Score.

### Step 1 — Compute ISC
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability all equal 0.56.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC}_{\text{base}} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - (0.44)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Impact (Scope = Changed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 7.52 \times (0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.914816 - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the exponent $0.894816^{15}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.894816)^{2} &= 0.800695 \\
(0.894816)^{4} &= 0.641112 \\
(0.894816)^{8} &= 0.411025 \\
(0.894816)^{15} &= (0.894816)^{8} \times (0.894816)^{4} \times (0.894816)^{2} \times (0.894816)^{1} \\
&\approx 0.411025 \times 0.641112 \times 0.800695 \times 0.894816 \\
&\approx 0.188661
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &\approx 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times 0.188661 \\
&\approx 6.661336 - 0.613148 \\
&\approx 6.048188
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Exploitability

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.466319 \\
&\approx 3.83314
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Base Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base} &= \text{roundup}\bigl(\min[\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundup}\bigl(\min[6.048188 + 3.83314,\; 10]\bigr) \\
&= \text{roundup}\bigl(9.881328\bigr) \\
&= 9.9
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret
A score of **9.9 = Critical** ⇒ the substation PLC must be patched within the IEC 62443 SL-3 remediation window (typically ≤ 30 days), with compensating controls (e.g., network segmentation, Modbus firewall) in the interim.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — STRIDE Threat-Model Walkthrough (Smart Water-Tank CPS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A municipality deploys a CPS: an *ultrasonic level sensor* → *edge gateway* (RTU) → *cellular link* → *cloud dashboard* → *actuator* that opens an inlet valve. Apply **STRIDE** to **each data-flow edge**.

| Data-Flow Edge | STRIDE Threats Identified | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor → RTU (RS-485, Modbus RTU) | **T**ampering, **I**nfo Disclosure (no auth) | Add IEC 62443 zone boundary; signed firmware; tamper-evident seals |
| RTU → Cellular | **S**poofing (rogue base station), **T**ampering, **D**oS | Mutual TLS, private APN, certificate pinning |
| Cloud → Dashboard | **R**epudiation (admin denies action), **E**oP | Append-only audit log, RBAC, MFA |
| Dashboard → Actuator | **T**ampering (set valve to 100 % flooding plant), **D**oS | Command signing, two-person rule, rate limit |

**Why STRIDE works for CPS:** Each letter maps cleanly onto a CPS-safety impact — *Tampering a setpoint* ⇒ physical harm; *DoS on the cellular link* ⇒ missed safety response; *Spoofing the actuator* ⇒ man-in-the-middle leading to kinetic damage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Matrix (often a 7-mark sub-part in ESE):** Given a CPS description, recommend the appropriate VA tool. Use the matrix below as the canonical mapping.

| CPS Context | Recommended VA Tool / Technique | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Legacy Modbus-only PLC plant | **Passive network sensor** (Claroty/Nozomi) + **CVE matching** | Active scans can crash RTUs |
| Pre-deployment automotive ECU | **UPPAAL / Simulink Design Verifier** (model checking) | Proof-based safety argument |
| Source code review of a gateway | **SAST** (Coverity, CodeQL) | Find injection, memory flaws |
| Third-party C library used in firmware | **SCA** (Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check) | Detect Log4j-style transitive flaws |
| Web HMI accessible to vendors | **DAST** (OWASP ZAP, Burp) | Find XSS, SQLi, broken auth |
| OPC UA server with no fuzzing done | **Protocol-aware fuzzer** (Peach, Boofuzz) | Reach unknown states |
| Air-gapped nuclear control room | **Red-team tabletop + side-channel** | No active scanning permitted |
| Pre-production firmware image | **binwalk + FirmAE** emulation + **CWE scan** | Detect backdoor accounts, hard-coded keys |
| Post-incident (suspected supply-chain) | **SBOM analysis (SPDX/CycloneDX)** | Trace tainted components |

---

## 3.4 Penetration-Testing Methodology for CPS (KTU Examiner Loves This Outline)

The standard, defensible methodology aligns with **NIST SP 800-115** and **IEC 62443-2-4**, executed in six steps:

1. **Planning & Rules of Engagement** — define *plant-stoppage policy*, *safety officer approval*, *out-of-band rollback plan*.
2. **Discovery** — passive network mapping, asset identification (no aggressive scanning on ICS segments).
3. **Enumeration** — list Modbus function codes, register ranges, firmware versions, default credentials.
4. **Vulnerability Mapping** — correlate with NVD/CVE; compute CVSS Environmental Score adjusted by **Safety Impact Rating**.
5. **Exploitation** — *safe* exploit of a digital twin first; only the live plant if a *safety witness* is present.
6. **Reporting & Remediation** — executive summary, technical findings, OT-safe patches (e.g., configuration change instead of firmware upgrade if plant cannot be stopped).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical CPS-Specific Distinction:** Active pen-test on an *IT* system is safe and common. On a *CPS*, an unscoped port-scan can shut down a city water pump. The methodology is the same — *the aggressiveness calibration* is different.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CPS Vulnerability-Assessment Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([CPS Asset Inventory]) --> A1[Scope and Rules of Engagement]
    A1 --> A2[Passive Discovery: Asset, Protocol, Firmware]
    A2 --> A3[Threat Modeling: STRIDE / Attack Trees]
    A3 --> A4[Static Analysis: SAST plus SCA]
    A4 --> A5[Dynamic Analysis: DAST plus Fuzzing]
    A5 --> A6[Active Vulnerability Scanning: Nessus OpenVAS]
    A6 --> A7[Penetration Testing: Black Grey White Crystal]
    A7 --> A8[Formal Verification: Model Checking plus Theorem Proving]
    A8 --> A9[CVSS v4 Scoring plus EPSS Prioritization]
    A9 --> A10[Remediation: Patch, Compensating Control, Accept]
    A10 --> A11[Continuous Monitoring: SIEM plus NVD plus ATT&CK ICS]
    A11 --> A0
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a CPS — Zones, Conduits, and Attack Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z0[Zone 0: Enterprise IT]
        E1[ERP / Email Server]
        E2[Engineering Workstation]
    end
    subgraph Z1[Zone 1: DMZ / Industrial Demilitarized Zone]
        D1[Historian Server]
        D2[Remote Access Jump Host]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Zone 2: SCADA Operations]
        S1[SCADA Master HMI]
        S2[Engineering Laptop]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Zone 3: Field Control]
        F1[PLC 1]
        F2[PLC 2]
        F3[RTU]
    end
    subgraph Z4[Zone 4: Physical Process]
        P1[Actuator]
        P2[Sensor]
    end
    E1 -- Conduit 1: HTTPS, Email --> D1
    E2 -- Conduit 2: Vendor VPN --> D2
    D1 -- Conduit 3: Modbus/TCP, DNP3 --> S1
    D2 -- Conduit 4: RDP, SSH --> S1
    S1 -- Conduit 5: Modbus Serial --> F1
    S2 -- Conduit 6: Profinet --> F2
    F3 -- Conduit 7: Wireless 900 MHz --> P2
    F1 -- Conduit 8: 24V digital output --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram for KTU:** Every "Conduit N" arrow is an *attack surface* that the vulnerability-assessment plan must enumerate, score with CVSS, and protect (firewall rule, unidirectional gateway, encrypted tunnel, signed firmware, etc.).

## 4.3 CVSS v3.1 Metric-Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Identify the Flaw] --> B1{Exploitable across trust boundaries?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[AV = Network or Adjacent]
    B1 -- No --> B3{Requires local OS access?}
    B3 -- Yes --> B4[AV = Local]
    B3 -- No --> B5[AV = Physical]
    B2 --> B6{AC = Low if repeatable?}
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7{Any data affected?}
    B7 -- No --> B8[Impact = None → Base = 0]
    B7 -- Yes --> B9[Compute ISC, Impact, Exploitability]
    B9 --> B10[Compute Base Score]
    B10 --> B11[Apply Temporal and Environmental]
    B11 --> B12[Final CVSS plus Severity Band]
```

## 4.4 Penetration-Test Kill-Chain Mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Recon: T0883 Internet Accessible Device] --> K2[Initial Access: T0866 Exploitation of Remote Services]
    K2 --> K3[Execution: T0853 Script Execution on HMI]
    K3 --> K4[Persistence: T0839 Module Firmware]
    K4 --> K5[Evasion: T0846 Masquerading]
    K5 --> K6[Discovery: T0840 Network Connection Enumeration]
    K6 --> K7[Lateral Movement: T0867 Lateral Tool Transfer]
    K7 --> K8[Impair Process Control: T0836 Modify Parameter]
    K8 --> K9[Impact: T0832 Manipulation of View]
    K9 --> K10[Damage to Property or Loss of Life]
```
