# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Differentiate between passive and active vulnerability scanning in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. State why active scanning is often avoided in operational-technology networks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Passive scanning** captures live traffic (e.g., via SPAN port, network TAP) and fingerprints devices by their *protocol behavior* (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet) without sending any packet. It is **stealthy**, **non-disruptive**, and identifies all devices including legacy PLCs that would crash if queried. **(1 mark)**
- **Active scanning** injects probe packets (ICMP, SNMP, Modbus status requests) to elicit responses. It produces richer information (open ports, CVEs) but can **trigger safety interlocks, lock PLCs, or flood serial lines**, especially on legacy ICS segments. **(1 mark)**
- Hence, in **operational-technology (OT)** networks, scanners such as **Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos** default to *passive* mode, with active probing only on segregated lab replicas (digital twins). **(1 mark)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List the six categories of the STRIDE threat-modeling framework and give a one-line CPS-relevant example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — half-mark per row, three rows shown for clarity):**

| STRIDE | CPS Example |
|---|---|
| **S**poofing | An attacker spoofs a Modbus master and sends rogue setpoints to a PLC. |
| **T**ampering | Modifying the firmware of a smart meter to under-report consumption. |
| **R**epudiation | A plant operator denies issuing a valve-open command that caused flooding — no signed log exists. |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Plaintext DNP3 traffic leaking substation telemetry over a wireless link. |
| **D**enial of Service | Flooding an RTU's serial port so it cannot send alarms to SCADA. |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Exploiting a default vendor password on an engineering workstation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal-Choice Questions

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Pattern]*
>
> **(a)** *Explain in detail the architecture of a Cyber-Physical System using the **Zone-and-Conduit** model prescribed by IEC 62443. Use a labeled diagram and identify at least **five attack surfaces** with their associated STRIDE threats. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A water-treatment SCADA vendor releases a security bulletin: a stack-based buffer overflow in the HMI's OPC-UA server. The flaw is exploitable from the corporate LAN (Network attack vector), requires no authentication, needs no user interaction, and gives an attacker full read/write of the historian plus command of all actuators. Compute the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** and recommend a 30-day remediation roadmap aligned with **IEC 62443-3-3 Security Level 3**. (7 marks)*

#### (a) Model Solution

1. **Zone-and-Conduit definition (1 mark):** A *Zone* groups CPS assets with a common security policy; a *Conduit* is any logical or physical communication path between zones.
2. **Architectural diagram (3 marks):** Draw four zones — *Enterprise IT*, *DMZ/IDMZ*, *SCADA Operations*, *Field Control*, *Physical Process* — and conduits labelled with protocols (HTTPS, RDP, Modbus/TCP, Modbus Serial, Profinet, analogue 4–20 mA).
3. **Attack-surface enumeration with STRIDE (2 marks):** (i) Conduit Corporate↔IDMZ (HTTPS) → *T, I, D*; (ii) Conduit IDMZ↔SCADA (RDP/SSH) → *S, E*; (iii) Conduit SCADA↔PLC (Modbus) → *S, T, D*; (iv) PLC firmware field-update path → *T, E*; (v) Field-sensor wireless → *S, T*.
4. **Engineering judgement (1 mark):** The model allows defenders to deploy *defence-in-depth* — e.g., a unidirectional gateway at the IDMZ/SCADA boundary.

#### (b) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Identify CVSS Metrics (1.5 marks):**

| Metric | Value | Numeric |
|---|---|---|
| Attack Vector | Network | 0.85 |
| Attack Complexity | Low | 0.77 |
| Privileges Required | None | 0.85 |
| User Interaction | None | 0.85 |
| Confidentiality | High | 0.56 |
| Integrity | High | 0.56 |
| Availability | High | 0.56 |
| Scope | Changed | — |

**Step 2 — Compute ISC (0.5 mark):**
$\text{ISC} = 1 - (1-0.56)^3 = 1 - 0.44^3 = 0.914816$.

**Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope = Changed) (1.5 marks):**
$\text{Impact} = 7.52\,(0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25\,(0.914816 - 0.02)^{15} \approx 6.0482$.

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability (0.5 mark):**
$\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \approx 3.8331$.

**Step 5 — Compute Base Score (1 mark):**
$\text{Base} = \lceil \min[6.0482 + 3.8331,\;10]\rceil = 9.9 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Critical}$.

**Step 6 — 30-Day Remediation Roadmap aligned with IEC 62443-3-3 SL-3 (2 marks):**

| Day | Action | SL-3 Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 0–3 | Isolate HMI behind IDMZ; enforce application allow-list | SR 1.1 Identification, SR 2.1 Authorization enforcement |
| 3–7 | Apply vendor patch in lab (digital twin), validate with regression test | SR 3.4 Software & Info Integrity |
| 7–14 | Roll patch to production HMIs during planned maintenance window | SR 7.6 Network & Security configuration |
| 14–21 | Deploy compensating controls: write-block rule on historian, command signing on actuators | SR 3.1 Communication integrity, SR 6.2 Continuous monitoring |
| 21–30 | Confirm via pen-test that the flaw is no longer exploitable; update incident-response runbook | SR 4.1 Information-confidentiality + SR 2.4 Mobile-code controls |

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice for the Same Module*
>
> **(a)** *Compare and contrast **STRIDE**, **PASTA**, and **Attack Trees** as threat-modeling techniques for CPS. State, with justification, which is most appropriate for a **safety-critical automotive ECU** under **ISO 21434**. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A city power utility operates a fleet of 12 000 smart meters. Each meter runs an embedded RTOS with a CoAP server. You must perform a vulnerability assessment. Design a methodology covering (i) **discovery**, (ii) **static + dynamic analysis**, (iii) **fuzz testing**, (iv) **firmware analysis**, and (v) **scoring + reporting** using **CVSS v4.0 + EPSS**. (7 marks)*

#### (a) Model Solution

1. **STRIDE (1.5 marks)** — Per-element categorization (Spoofing, Tampering …). *Strength:* easy DFD mapping. *Weakness:* not risk-prioritized.
2. **PASTA (1.5 marks)** — Seven-stage risk-centric process (define objectives, technical scope, app decomposition, threat analysis, vulnerability/weakness analysis, attack modeling, risk/impact analysis). *Strength:* ties threats to business/safety impact. *Weakness:* heavy documentation overhead.
3. **Attack Trees (1 mark)** — Hierarchical AND/OR decomposition of an attacker goal. *Strength:* visual, quantifies attack cost. *Weakness:* becomes unmanageable for large systems.
4. **Justification for ISO 21434 (3 marks):** ISO 21434 mandates *TARA* (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment) with **impact rating, attack feasibility, risk value** computed for every threat. PASTA's risk-driven stages map 1-to-1 onto TARA. STRIDE is *complementary* (used to enumerate the threat catalog) and *Attack Trees* are *complementary* (used to model feasibility per ISO 21434 §15). **Hence PASTA + STRIDE + Attack Trees together is the best fit**, with PASTA as the orchestrating process.

#### (b) Model Solution — Smart-Meter VA Methodology

1. **Discovery (1.5 marks):** Use a **passive network sensor** at the concentrator (before meters) to fingerprint CoAP endpoints, RTOS version, and MAC OUI. Cross-correlate with the **CMDB**. Do not active-scan meters.
2. **Static + Dynamic Analysis (1.5 marks):** Acquire firmware (vendor or via JTAG on a lab meter). Run **binwalk** to extract the file system; **SAST** (e.g., CodeQL) on the extracted C sources. **SCA** to identify vulnerable OSS libraries (e.g., older **mbedTLS**).
3. **Fuzz Testing (1.5 marks):** Build a **digital twin** of the meter (QEMU + OpenCoAP). Use **Boofuzz** to send malformed CoAP messages (oversized options, duplicate tokens, wrong Content-Format). Monitor for crashes; capture via `gdb`.
4. **Firmware Analysis (1 mark):** Use **FirmAE** to dynamically emulate firmware; look for hard-coded credentials, default keys, debug shells. Check **secure-boot bypass** and **rollback protection**.
5. **Scoring + Reporting (1.5 marks):** For each finding, compute **CVSS v4.0 Base + Threat + Environmental**. Use the **Safety** and **Automatable** supplemental metrics. Combine with **EPSS** to predict the probability of exploitation in the next 30 days. The final report ranks the **top-5 exploitable flaws** with **compensating controls** (e.g., transport-layer DTLS, certificate pinning) and a **patch-SLA per IEC 62443-3-3 SL-2**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse CVSS v3.1 rounding with v4.0 rounding** — v3.1 uses *roundup-to-1-decimal*; v4.0 uses *ceiling-to-1-decimal*. Many students lose 1 mark by writing the wrong one.
> 2. **Don't state that STRIDE is a "risk-scoring" framework** — STRIDE *classifies* threats; **DREAD** and **CVSS** *score* them. Examiners deduct a full mark for this mix-up.
> 3. **Don't claim active scanning is safe on a live SCADA network** — even a single malformed *Modbus Read Coils* can lock a legacy PLC. Always mention the **safety officer + digital-twin** caveats.
> 4. **For CVSS, you MUST show the ISC and Exploitability intermediate values** — silent jumps to the final score get only partial credit.
> 5. **For STRIDE, mention the DFD (Data-Flow Diagram)** — the examiner looks for the word *DFD* or *Level-0/Level-1*; omitting it costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 of PECPT863**

- **CPS-VA is *not* IT-VA**: physical consequences, real-time constraints, and legacy protocols change the *aggressiveness* of every test.
- **Know the standard frameworks cold**: **NIST SP 800-82 r3**, **IEC 62443** (Zones & Conduits; SL 0–4), **NIST CSF 2.0** (Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover-Govern), **CVSS v4.0** (Base + Threat + Environmental + Supplemental), **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS**.
- **CVSS v3.1 must-derive steps** in this order: **ISC → Impact → Exploitability → Base**. Use the *Scope-Changed* branch when the exploit can affect components beyond the vulnerable one.
- **Severity Bands**: None (0.0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0). Identical in v3.1 and v4.0.
- **Five core threat-modeling frameworks**: **STRIDE** (categorize), **PASTA** (risk-drive), **LINDDUN** (privacy), **VAST** (scale), **Attack Trees** (decompose). Map them to the CPS phase: STRIDE+DFD in design, PASTA in TARA, Attack Trees in feasibility.
- **VA technique layering** — *SAST + SCA + DAST + Fuzz + Network Scanning + Pen-test + Formal Verification* — every question on tools expects you to mention at least *four* layers.
- **CPS-specific scanning tools**: **Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Tenable.OT, Microsoft Defender for IoT** — passive-first, active only on digital twins.
- **Generic tools you must also know**: **Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Nmap, Wireshark, Metasploit, Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, Boofuzz, Peach Fuzzer, AFL, binwalk, FirmAE, UPPAAL, Simulink Design Verifier**.
- **Critical acronyms** — **CVE / CWE / NVD / CVSS / EPSS / SBOM / SPDX / CycloneDX / STRIDE / DREAD / ICS / SCADA / PLC / RTU / HMI / MTU / IED**.
- **Pen-test on CPS must mention**: *rules of engagement*, *safety officer*, *digital twin first*, *compensating controls*, *out-of-band rollback*.
- **Fuzzing on CPS protocols** — at minimum remember **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC UA, CoAP** as the fuzz-targets.
- **CVSS v4.0 additions** — *Threat* metric (uses real-world exploit data) and *Supplemental* metrics (Safety, Automatable, Recovery, value density, vulnerability response effort) — especially **Safety** in CPS.
- **EPSS** = Exploit Prediction Scoring System (0–1 probability score); combine with CVSS to prioritize in OT environments where patching is hard.
- **Last-line exam tip**: always close an answer with a **standards-anchored mitigation** (cite IEC 62443 SL or NIST 800-82). Examiners reward "anchoring" with a bonus mark.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing and Mitigating Discovered Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing and Mitigating Discovered Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *Managing and Mitigating Discovered Vulnerabilities* refers to the systematic, continuous, and policy-driven process of triaging, prioritizing, remediating, and documenting security weaknesses identified during vulnerability assessments, penetration tests, or runtime monitoring of CPS assets (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA HMI, IoT edge nodes, industrial gateways, and cloud orchestration planes).

According to the **NIST SP 800-40 Guide to Enterprise Patch Management** and **ISO/IEC 30111 Vulnerability Handling Processes**, the management phase encompasses four canonical activities:

1. **Vulnerability Triage** – Classification using CVSS v3.1, CWE, and CPS-specific attributes (safety impact, operational continuity, physical harm potential).
2. **Risk-Based Prioritization** – Mapping vulnerability severity against asset criticality and threat exposure.
3. **Remediation Execution** – Patching, configuration hardening, firmware updates, or network segmentation.
4. **Verification & Closure** – Re-scanning, regression testing, and audit logging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In CPS environments, vulnerability management is fundamentally different from IT environments because *availability* and *safety* outrank *confidentiality*. A patch that crashes a PLC controlling a chemical reactor is worse than the vulnerability it fixes. This is the cornerstone concept for every exam answer in this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of vulnerability management in CPS like **managing medical conditions in a hospital ICU**:

| CPS Concept | Hospital Analogy |
|-------------|------------------|
| Discovered Vulnerability | A diagnosed disease in a patient |
| Asset Criticality | Patient's overall health condition |
| Patch (Remediation) | Surgery / medication |
| Compensating Control (Mitigation) | Lifestyle change / monitoring |
| Patch Window (Downtime) | Surgery scheduling slot |
| Patch Failure Risk | Risk of surgical complications |

Just as a doctor does **not** operate on every patient simultaneously (they triage by severity), a CPS security engineer does **not** patch every device at once. They prioritize based on:
- **How sick is the patient (asset)?**
- **How dangerous is the disease (CVSS)?**
- **How risky is the surgery (patch stability)?**

The **3 standard priorities** in CPS vulnerability management are:
1. **Patient safety (operational continuity)**
2. **Containment of contagion (lateral movement prevention)**
3. **Long-term cure (permanent patch)**

> [!NOTE]
> **SLA Reference (Industry Standard Metrics):**
> - **Critical (CVSS 9.0–10.0):** Patch within **15 days**
> - **High (CVSS 7.0–8.9):** Patch within **30 days**
> - **Medium (CVSS 4.0–6.9):** Patch within **90 days**
> - **Low (CVSS 0.1–3.9):** Patch within **180 days**

## 1.3 CPS-Specific Vulnerability Categories

CPS vulnerabilities span both cyber and physical domains. The management strategy must address:

- **Firmware-level bugs** in embedded RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS, ThreadX)
- **Protocol-level exploits** in Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP
- **Hardware backdoors** in ASIC/FPGA bitstreams
- **Supply-chain vulnerabilities** in third-party components (e.g., SolarWinds-style)
- **Safety system bypasses** in SIS (Safety Instrumented Systems)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

The lifecycle is a **closed-loop, continuous process** with five stages. Each stage feeds the next:

- **Stage 1 — Asset Inventory & Baselining:** Identify every CPS component (sensors, actuators, controllers, gateways, cloud agents). Without an inventory, you cannot manage what you don't know exists. Tag each asset with a criticality rating (e.g., IEC 62443-4-2 Security Level 1–4).
- **Stage 2 — Discovery & Scanning:** Use authenticated scanners (Nessus, Qualys, Tenable.ot) and CPS-aware tools (Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos) to identify CVEs, misconfigurations, and protocol anomalies.
- **Stage 3 — Analysis & Prioritization:** Compute CVSS base, temporal, and environmental scores. Multiply by asset criticality to obtain the *Operational Risk Score (ORS)*.
- **Stage 4 — Remediation / Mitigation:** Apply the most appropriate response (patch, config change, segmentation, or accept-the-risk formally).
- **Stage 5 — Verification & Reporting:** Re-scan to confirm closure. Generate audit logs for compliance (IEC 62443, NERC CIP, NIST CSF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Prioritization:** In CPS, a *Critical* vulnerability on a non-critical asset (e.g., office printer) is **less urgent** than a *Medium* vulnerability on a turbine controller. Pure CVSS is insufficient — it must be contextualized with **Environmental Score** modifiers (CR, IR, AR, MA, AVC).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes every formula, metric, and boundary condition required for solving KTU examination problems on this topic.

### Table 2.1 — Core Vulnerability Management Formulas

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Utility |
|--------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **CVSS Base Score** | $$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10)\bigr) $$ | Standardized 0–10 severity |
| **Impact Sub-score** | $$ \text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \bigl(1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A)\bigr) $$ | C=Confidentiality, I=Integrity, A=Availability |
| **Exploitability Sub-score** | $$ \text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI $$ | AV=AttackVector, AC=AttackComplexity, PR=PrivilegesRequired, UI=UserInteraction |
| **Operational Risk Score (ORS)** | $$ \text{ORS} = \text{CVSS}_{\text{env}} \times \text{AssetCriticality} \times \text{ExposureFactor} $$ | CPS-aware prioritization |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i,\text{remediated}}}{n} $$ | Operational efficiency KPI |
| **Patch Coverage Ratio** | $$ \text{Coverage} = \frac{N_{\text{patched}}}{N_{\text{total\_critical}}} \times 100\% $$ | Compliance metric |
| **Remediation Rate** | $$ R = \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{T_{\text{period}}} $$ | Vulnerabilities closed per unit time |
| **Vulnerability Density** | $$ \text{VDensity} = \frac{N_{\text{critical\_vulns}}}{N_{\text{assets}}} $$ | Asset hygiene indicator |
| **SLA Breach Probability** | $$ P_{\text{breach}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} $$ | λ=vulnerability aging rate, t=elapsed days |
| **Compensating Control Effectiveness** | $$ E_{c} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1-p_{i}) $$ | Series probability model for layered controls |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **ORS (Operational Risk Score)** is the single most important formula for KTU questions on CPS vulnerability management. Unlike pure IT environments where CVSS suffices, CPS mandates environmental scoring because the same CVE on a coffee machine vs. a nuclear cooling pump has drastically different consequences.

## 2.3 Remediation vs. Mitigation — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remediation** = Permanently eliminating the root cause (patch, code fix, component replacement).
> **Mitigation** = Reducing the likelihood or impact without fixing the root cause (firewall rule, network segmentation, disabling unused service).

KTU students frequently lose marks by using these terms interchangeably. **They are not synonyms.**

| Aspect | Remediation | Mitigation |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Permanence | Permanent | Temporary |
| Cost | High (engineer + downtime) | Low (config change) |
| Risk Introduced | Patch failure risk | Bypass risk |
| CPS Use Case | Firmware update after vendor release | VLAN isolation while vendor develops patch |

## 2.4 Patch Management Strategies in CPS

- **Hot-Patching (Online):** Applied without taking the system offline. Rare in CPS due to deterministic timing requirements.
- **Cold-Patching (Offline):** System is shut down, patched, restarted. Safest but most disruptive.
- **Rolling Patch:** Applied across redundant controllers one at a time (e.g., redundant PLC pair).
- **Simulated/Shadow Patch:** Tested in a digital twin environment before production deployment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: CVSS v3.1 Base Score Calculation

The CVSS v3.1 Base Score is derived from Impact and Exploitability sub-scores. Let us derive it step-by-step for a sample CPS vulnerability.

### Given (CPS Scenario):
A vulnerability in a Modbus TCP gateway allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to crash the gateway, causing denial of service to downstream PLCs.

**Metric Values (CVSS v3.1 vectors):**
- Attack Vector (AV) = **Network** → AV = 0.85
- Attack Complexity (AC) = **Low** → AC = 0.77
- Privileges Required (PR) = **None** → PR = 0.85
- User Interaction (UI) = **None** → UI = 0.85
- Confidentiality (C) = **None** → C = 0.0
- Integrity (I) = **None** → I = 0.0
- Availability (A) = **High** → A = 0.56

### Step 1: Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

First compute the **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**:

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1-0.0)(1-0.0)(1-0.56)
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1)(1)(0.44) = 1 - 0.44 = 0.56
$$

### Step 2: Determine the Impact Value

Since C, I, A are all defined (none are "Not Defined" / X), the Impact value is:

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}
$$

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.56 = 3.5952
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.37999
$$

$$
5.37999 \times 0.85 = 4.57299
$$

$$
4.57299 \times 0.85 = 3.88704
$$

So $\text{Exploitability} \approx 3.887$

### Step 4: Compute the Base Score

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability} = 3.5952 + 3.88704 = 7.48224
$$

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(7.48224) = \mathbf{7.5}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity Rating:** 7.5 falls in the **High** band (7.0–8.9). In a CPS context, this triggers a **30-day SLA patch cycle** per industry benchmark.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Operational Risk Score (ORS)

### Given:
- $\text{CVSS}_{\text{env}}$ (with Environmental Score modifiers for safety impact) = **9.2**
- Asset Criticality (IEC 62443-4-2 Security Level Target) = **SL-3** (numeric weight = 1.5)
- Exposure Factor (device is on the corporate network = 0.9; air-gapped = 0.3; here, partially exposed = 0.7)

### Step 1: Apply the ORS Formula

$$
\text{ORS} = \text{CVSS}_{\text{env}} \times \text{AssetCriticality} \times \text{ExposureFactor}
$$

$$
\text{ORS} = 9.2 \times 1.5 \times 0.7
$$

$$
9.2 \times 1.5 = 13.8
$$

$$
13.8 \times 0.7 = 9.66
$$

### Step 2: Normalize to 0–10 Scale

Since ORS can exceed 10 in critical CPS assets, we apply normalization:

$$
\text{ORS}_{\text{norm}} = \min\bigl(\text{ORS},\, 10\bigr) = \min(9.66,\, 10) = \mathbf{9.66}
$$

### Step 3: Map to Remediation Tier

| ORS Range | Tier | Action |
|-----------|------|--------|
| 0.0–3.9 | Tier 4 (Low) | Scheduled maintenance patch |
| 4.0–6.9 | Tier 3 (Medium) | 90-day SLA, monitor |
| 7.0–8.9 | Tier 2 (High) | 30-day SLA, change advisory board review |
| 9.0–10.0 | Tier 1 (Critical) | **Emergency change window**, executive sign-off |

> An ORS of **9.66** places this vulnerability in **Tier 1** — emergency change procedure mandatory.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Response Strategy

The following decision matrix guides the choice between Patch, Mitigate, or Accept.

| Condition | Recommended Response |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Vendor patch available, tested in digital twin, no safety impact | **Remediate (Patch)** |
| Vendor patch unavailable, compensating control reduces exposure by ≥ 80% | **Mitigate** |
| System is legacy / EOL, no patch possible, business-critical | **Mitigate + Document Risk Acceptance** |
| Vulnerability is theoretical (no PoC), low asset criticality | **Accept (monitor)** |
| Patch introduces more risk than the vulnerability (unstable firmware) | **Mitigate until stable patch** |
| Vulnerability is actively exploited in the wild (CISA KEV catalog) | **Emergency Mitigate + Request Vendor ETA** |

## 3.4 MTTR Computation — Worked Example

### Given:
During Q1 2024, a CPS plant closed 12 critical vulnerabilities with the following remediation times (days): 14, 18, 22, 11, 30, 25, 19, 16, 28, 21, 17, 23.

### Step 1: Sum the Remediation Times

$$
\sum t_i = 14+18+22+11+30+25+19+16+28+21+17+23
$$

$$
\sum t_i = 32+22+11 = 65, \quad 65+30 = 95, \quad 95+25 = 120
$$

$$
120+19 = 139, \quad 139+16 = 155, \quad 155+28 = 183
$$

$$
183+21 = 204, \quad 204+17 = 221, \quad 221+23 = 244
$$

### Step 2: Apply the MTTR Formula

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum t_i}{n} = \frac{244}{12}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = 20.33 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An MTTR of **20.33 days** is below the 30-day SLA for *High* vulnerabilities — the plant is **SLA-compliant**. Above 30 days would trigger a governance review.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vulnerability Management Lifecycle in CPS (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage1: Asset Inventory and Baselining] --> A2[Stage2: Discovery and CPS-Aware Scanning]
    A2 --> A3[Stage3: Vulnerability Analysis and CVSS Scoring]
    A3 --> A4{Decision: Risk Threshold Check}
    A4 -->|Above ORS 9.0| A5[Stage4a: Emergency Remediation]
    A4 -->|Between ORS 4 and 9| A6[Stage4b: Scheduled Patch Cycle]
    A4 -->|Below ORS 4| A7[Stage4c: Routine Maintenance]
    A5 --> A8[Stage5: Verification and Re-Scan]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9{Audit Logged and Compliant?}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Closure and Reporting]
    A9 -->|No| A11[Reopen and Escalate]
    A11 --> A3
    A10 --> A2
```

## 4.2 CPS Patch Deployment Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Identified CVE] --> B2[Vendor Patch Available?]
    B2 -->|Yes| B3[Digital Twin Sandbox Test]
    B2 -->|No| B4[Compensating Controls Pathway]
    B3 --> B5{Test Passed?}
    B5 -->|Yes| B6[Production Patch Rollout]
    B5 -->|No| B7[Vendor Engagement Loop]
    B7 --> B2
    B4 --> B8[Apply Network Segmentation or WAF Rule]
    B6 --> B9[Post-Patch Validation]
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[Update Vulnerability Database]
```

## 4.3 CPS Vulnerability Triage Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"]
        C1[Nessus or Qualys Scan]
        C2[CPS Passive Monitor Claroty Dragos Nozomi]
        C3[CISA KEV Catalog Feed]
        C4[Vendor Security Advisories]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_LAYER["PROCESS LAYER - Triage Engine"]
        C5[Normalize and Deduplicate CVEs]
        C6[Apply CVSS v3.1 Base]
        C7[Apply Environmental Modifiers Safety and Impact]
        C8[Compute Operational Risk Score]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER - Decision"]
        C9{Tier 1 Critical}
        C10{Tier 2 High}
        C11{Tier 3 Medium}
        C12{Tier 4 Low}
    end

    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6 --> C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9
    C8 --> C10
    C8 --> C11
    C8 --> C12
```

## 4.4 Mitigation Strategy Selection Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Vulnerability Discovered] --> D2{Asset in Safety Zone?}
    D2 -->|Yes SIL rated| D3[SIS Bypass: NO. Apply IEC 61508 Compliant Patch Only]
    D2 -->|No| D4{Patch Available?}
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[Apply During Scheduled Maintenance Window]
    D4 -->|No| D6[Layered Compensating Controls]
    D6 --> D7[Firewall ACL]
    D6 --> D8[VLAN Isolation]
    D6 --> D9[Disable Unused Protocol Ports]
    D6 --> D10[Intrusion Detection Signatures]
    D5 --> D11[Document and Verify]
    D7 --> D11
    D8 --> D11
    D9 --> D11
    D10 --> D11
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between vulnerability remediation and mitigation in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Give one CPS-specific example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Remediation** is the permanent elimination of the root cause of a vulnerability, typically through a vendor-issued patch, firmware update, or secure code replacement. It addresses the *why* of the exploit.

**Mitigation** is a temporary or compensating action that reduces the likelihood or impact of exploitation without removing the root cause, such as network segmentation, firewall rules, or disabling an unused service.

**CPS-Specific Examples:**
- **Remediation:** Updating the firmware of a Siemens S7-1500 PLC to patch CVE-2022-38465 (RCE in webserver).
- **Mitigation:** Placing unpatchable legacy Modbus RTU devices on an isolated VLAN with strict ACLs while awaiting vendor support.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark each for correct definition + 1 mark for valid CPS example.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List the four stages of the vulnerability management lifecycle and state one CPS-specific challenge in each stage.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Stage | CPS-Specific Challenge |
|-------|------------------------|
| Discovery | Passive-only scanning required (active scans may disrupt deterministic control loops) |
| Analysis | CVSS alone ignores safety/physical impact — environmental scoring mandatory |
| Remediation | Patch windows must align with planned downtime; firmware updates risk bricking legacy devices |
| Verification | Re-scanning live CPS can trigger false-positive safety alarms |

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the CVSS v3.1 framework with its three metric groups (Base, Temporal, Environmental). For a CPS vulnerability on a Schneider M340 PLC that allows remote code execution over Ethernet with no authentication, demonstrate the computation of the CVSS Base Score with the following vector:
**AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a vulnerability prioritization workflow for a smart manufacturing plant with 500 PLCs. Justify the use of an *Operational Risk Score* over plain CVSS in CPS environments. Include a remediation tier mapping.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — CVSS Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Metric Values (1 mark):**
- AV (Network) = 0.85
- AC (Low) = 0.77
- PR (None) = 0.85
- UI (None) = 0.85
- C (High) = 0.56
- I (High) = 0.56
- A (High) = 0.56
- Scope (S:U = Unchanged) → standard formulas apply

**Step 2 — Compute ISS (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - (1-0.56)(1-0.56)(1-0.56) = 1 - (0.44)^3
$$

$$
(0.44)^3 = 0.085184
$$

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.87312
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380
$$

$$
5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573
$$

$$
4.573 \times 0.85 = 3.887
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Base Score (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Sum} = 5.87312 + 3.887 = 9.76012
$$

Since Scope is Unchanged and Impact > 0:

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\min(9.76012, 10)) = \mathbf{9.8}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
A score of **9.8 is Critical** (CVSS 9.0–10.0), triggering immediate emergency remediation under the 15-day SLA and warranting executive-level change approval.

#### Part (b) — CPS Prioritization Workflow Design [7 Marks]

**Workflow Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Asset Inventory & Tagging:** Enumerate all 500 PLCs. Tag each with make, model, firmware version, network segment (Zone per IEC 62443-3-3), and Safety Integrity Level (SIL).
2. **Vulnerability Ingestion:** Aggregate feeds from passive CPS monitors (Claroty/Dragos), active scans (Nessus), and CISA KEV.
3. **Contextual Scoring — Operational Risk Score:**

$$
\text{ORS} = \text{CVSS}_{\text{env}} \times \text{AssetCriticality} \times \text{ExposureFactor}
$$

where $\text{CVSS}_{\text{env}}$ incorporates Safety (S) and Operational Continuity (OC) modifiers from the Environmental metric group.

4. **Remediation Tier Mapping (2 marks):**

| ORS | Tier | Action |
|-----|------|--------|
| 9.0–10.0 | T1 | Emergency change window, executive sign-off |
| 7.0–8.9 | T2 | Change Advisory Board, 30-day SLA |
| 4.0–6.9 | T3 | Scheduled maintenance, 90-day SLA |
| 0.0–3.9 | T4 | Standard hygiene cycle, 180-day SLA |

**Justification (1 mark):** Plain CVSS treats every system uniformly. In CPS, a CVSS 7.0 on a packaging-line PLC differs from CVSS 7.0 on a reactor-controller PLC. ORS incorporates asset criticality and exposure, producing a *contextual* risk picture aligned with IEC 62443-4-2 and NIST SP 800-82.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the challenges of patch management in operational technology (OT) environments. Compare and contrast online patching, cold patching, and rolling patch strategies with one CPS use case for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power-grid SCADA system reports 8 vulnerabilities closed over Q1 with remediation times (in days): 12, 25, 31, 9, 45, 18, 22, 28. The plant's SLA for High-severity vulnerabilities is 30 days. Calculate:
1. Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)
2. Number of SLA breaches
3. Vulnerability Density if total critical assets = 40
4. Remediation Rate (vulnerabilities per week)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Patch Management Challenges & Strategies [7 Marks]

**Challenges in OT/CPS Patching (4 marks):**

1. **Availability Constraints:** CPS systems run 24×7×365. Any unplanned restart may disrupt production or safety.
2. **Determinism Requirements:** Real-time control loops (sub-millisecond) cannot tolerate the jitter introduced by patch installation.
3. **Vendor Lock-in & Certification:** Safety-certified systems (IEC 61508 SIL-2/3) require re-certification after any firmware change.
4. **Legacy & EOL Hardware:** Many PLCs run on EOL firmware with no available patches from the OEM.
5. **Limited Rollback:** If a patch fails in CPS, rollback may be impossible (e.g., bricking an embedded controller).
6. **Cross-Vendor Interoperability:** A patch to one device may break a protocol handshake with another vendor's device.

**Strategy Comparison (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Description | CPS Use Case |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Online (Hot) Patching | Applied without shutdown | Rare; used in redundant controllers with supervisor switching |
| **Cold Patching** | System is taken offline, patched, restarted | Batch-process DCS shutdown during planned turnaround |
| **Rolling Patch** | Sequentially patching redundant pairs | Dual-redundant PLC pair in a turbine control system — one is patched while the other carries the load |

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Metrics [7 Marks]

**Given:** Remediation times = [12, 25, 31, 9, 45, 18, 22, 28] days. SLA = 30 days. Assets = 40.

**Step 1 — MTTR (2 marks):**

$$
\sum t_i = 12+25+31+9+45+18+22+28
$$

$$
= 37+31+9+45+18+22+28 = 190
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{190}{8} = \mathbf{23.75 \text{ days}}
$$

**Step 2 — SLA Breaches (2 marks):**
Items exceeding 30 days: **31 and 45** → **2 SLA breaches**.

**Step 3 — Vulnerability Density (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{VDensity} = \frac{N_{\text{critical\_vulns}}}{N_{\text{assets}}} = \frac{8}{40} = \mathbf{0.20 \text{ vulns/asset}}
$$

**Step 4 — Remediation Rate (1.5 marks):**
Q1 ≈ 13 weeks.

$$
R = \frac{8}{13} \approx \mathbf{0.615 \text{ vulns/week}}
$$

**Conclusion:** MTTR is **below** the 30-day SLA on average, but **2 out of 8** (25%) breaches require governance review. Improvement actions: implement rolling patches and pre-test in digital twin.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Vulnerability Management Questions:**
> 1. **Using Remediation and Mitigation interchangeably** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always state both terms separately with distinct CPS examples.
> 2. **Applying pure CVSS to CPS contexts** — Pure CVSS ignores safety and operational continuity. Always mention *environmental scoring* or *ORS* for CPS scenarios.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply CVSS by Asset Criticality** — In CPS, the same CVE has different risk profiles on different assets. The formula expects multiplication, not addition.
> 4. **Using pipe symbols `\|` in markdown tables** — This breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert` or `OR` symbols instead.
> 5. **Ignoring MTTR formula's denominator `n`** — Examiners commonly check that students have divided by the count, not summed blindly.
> 6. **Confusing MTTR with MTBF** — Mean Time *To* Remediate is a vulnerability lifecycle metric, NOT a reliability metric. Keep them distinct.
> 7. **Recommending "patch everything immediately" in CPS** — This is an IT-centric answer and loses marks. CPS requires *planned change windows* and *safety impact assessment*.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** Vulnerability management in CPS = continuous triage → prioritization → remediation → verification loop, adapted for safety/availability constraints.
- **Lifecycle:** 5 stages — Inventory → Discovery → Analysis → Remediation → Verification (continuous loop).
- **CVSS v3.1:** 0–10 score from Impact + Exploitability. Critical: 9.0+, High: 7.0–8.9, Medium: 4.0–6.9, Low: 0.1–3.9.
- **ORS Formula:** $\text{ORS} = \text{CVSS}_{\text{env}} \times \text{AssetCriticality} \times \text{ExposureFactor}$ — the *CPS-aware* prioritization formula.
- **Remediation vs Mitigation:** Remediation = permanent fix; Mitigation = compensating control. Never use interchangeably.
- **SLA Standards:** Critical = 15 days, High = 30 days, Medium = 90 days, Low = 180 days.
- **Patch Strategies:** Online (hot), Cold (offline), Rolling (redundant pair), Shadow (digital twin). Choose based on availability and safety.
- **CPS-Specific Scanners:** Use Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi (passive) — never active-scan safety-critical loops.
- **Standards Mapping:** IEC 62443-4-2 (component security), IEC 61508/61511 (safety), NIST SP 800-82 (ICS), NIST SP 800-40 (patching).
- **Key Metrics:** MTTR, Patch Coverage Ratio, Remediation Rate, Vulnerability Density, SLA Breach Probability.
- **Decision Rule:** In CPS, **safety and availability outrank confidentiality** — always state this in any prioritization answer.
- **Acceptance Risk:** When patching is impossible, formal risk acceptance with executive sign-off is mandatory (audit requirement).
- **CISA KEV Catalog:** Vulnerabilities on the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list demand *emergency* mitigation regardless of CVSS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conducting Vulnerability Assessments and Applying Remediation Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability Assessment** is a systematic, methodical, and documented process of identifying, classifying, prioritizing, and remediating security weaknesses (vulnerabilities) in an information system, network, or **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**. It involves the use of automated scanners, manual verification techniques, and standardized scoring frameworks to evaluate the exploitability and impact of flaws across the **IT (Information Technology)** and **OT (Operational Technology)** layers.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, a vulnerability assessment differs from a traditional IT assessment because it must account for:
- **Safety implications** (loss of life, physical damage)
- **Operational continuity** (uptime of critical infrastructure)
- **Protocol-specific flaws** (Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA, PROFINET)
- **Purdue Model** boundaries (Levels 0 to 5)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Inspector"

Imagine you are a **structural engineer inspecting a 50-story smart building** before issuing an occupancy permit.

| Real-World Analogy | Cybersecurity Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cracks in the foundation | Unpatched firmware in PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers) |
| Faulty electrical wiring | Misconfigured network firewalls |
| Weak door locks | Default/weak credentials on HMI (Human-Machine Interface) panels |
| Inspector's clipboard checklist | **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) Database** |
| Building code (NBC) | **NIST SP 800-53 / IEC 62443 Standards** |
| Severity of crack (hairline vs. deep) | **CVSS Base Score (0.0 to 10.0)** |
| Repairing the crack | **Remediation (Patching, Hardening, Compensating Control)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a building inspector does not *just* find problems but is also responsible for issuing a **"Certificate of Occupancy"** with a list of mandatory repairs, a vulnerability assessor does not stop at discovery — they must **prioritize, recommend remediation, and verify fixes**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions the Examiner Loves to Ask:**

- **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited to compromise security (CIA Triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- **Threat**: Any circumstance or event with the potential to cause harm (e.g., a hacker, malware, natural disaster).
- **Risk**: The product of *Threat × Vulnerability × Impact*. Expressed as: **Risk = Likelihood × Impact**.
- **Asset**: Anything of value to the organization (PLCs, SCADA servers, engineering workstations, intellectual property).
- **Exploit**: A piece of code, technique, or tool used to take advantage of a vulnerability.
- **Threat Actor**: The entity responsible for the threat (insider, nation-state, hacktivist, script kiddie).
- **Attack Surface**: The sum of all points (network ports, APIs, physical interfaces) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data.
- **Zero-Day**: A previously unknown vulnerability for which no patch exists at the time of discovery.
- **Remediation**: The act of eliminating or mitigating a vulnerability (patch, reconfiguration, removal).
- **Compensating Control**: An alternative safeguard put in place when a primary control cannot be implemented (e.g., isolating a device via network segmentation instead of patching).
- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)**: A globally unique identifier (e.g., **CVE-2021-44228** for Log4Shell) assigned to a publicly known vulnerability.
- **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration)**: A category system for software/hardware weaknesses (e.g., **CWE-79: Cross-Site Scripting**).
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)**: A standardized scoring framework (0.0 to 10.0) maintained by **FIRST.org** to rate vulnerability severity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (NIST-Aligned)

The vulnerability management process is **continuous and cyclical**, never a one-time event. It follows these structured phases:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6-Phase Vulnerability Management Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-40r4):**

1. **Phase 1 — Asset Discovery & Inventory**
   - Identify *all* devices within scope (IT assets, OT endpoints, IoT sensors, cloud workloads).
   - Use active scanning (Nmap, Nessus) and passive discovery (SPAN port taps, ICS-specific tools like **Claroty** or **Nozomi Networks**).
   - *Why it matters*: You cannot protect what you do not know exists. **Shadow OT assets** are a primary attack vector.

2. **Phase 2 — Vulnerability Scanning & Identification**
   - Deploy **active scanners** (authenticated + unauthenticated) to probe for known CVEs.
   - Deploy **passive monitoring** for OT environments to avoid disrupting sensitive processes.
   - Compare findings against the **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)**.

3. **Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis & Prioritization**
   - Score each vulnerability using **CVSS v3.1** (Base, Temporal, Environmental scores).
   - Overlay business context (CPS safety, regulatory compliance, asset criticality).
   - *Formula*: **Effective Risk = CVSS Base × Asset Criticality × Threat Intelligence Weight**

4. **Phase 4 — Remediation Planning**
   - Develop a remediation roadmap with timelines (Critical: 15 days, High: 30 days, Medium: 60 days, Low: 90 days — per typical **PCI-DSS** and **CISA BOD 22-01** guidance).
   - Choose remediation strategy: **Patch, Mitigate, Accept, or Transfer (Risk)**.

5. **Phase 5 — Remediation Implementation & Verification**
   - Apply patches, configuration changes, or compensating controls.
   - **Re-scan** to confirm the vulnerability is eliminated. A vulnerability is *not* closed until verification is complete.

6. **Phase 6 — Reporting & Continuous Monitoring**
   - Generate executive and technical reports.
   - Feed metrics into the organization's **GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance)** platform.
   - Schedule the next scan cycle (continuous monitoring for CPS — **24x7x365** is ideal).

## 2.2 The CVSS Scoring Framework (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVSS v3.1 Formula Structure (Examiner Favourite)**

CVSS produces three metric group scores, each producing a numeric value from **0.0 to 10.0**:

**A. Base Score** — intrinsic properties of the vulnerability
**B. Temporal Score** — properties that change over time (exploit availability, patch level)
**C. Environmental Score** — properties unique to a user's environment (asset criticality)

### Severity Rating Scale

| CVSS Score Range | Severity Rating | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **0.0** | None | Informational; no action required |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | Low | Schedule remediation in next maintenance window |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | Medium | Remediate within 30–60 days |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | High | Remediate within 15–30 days |
| **9.0 – 10.0** | Critical | **Emergency patching** (e.g., Log4Shell, BlueKeep) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Vulnerability & Risk Assessment**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Representation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Risk Score** | $R = L \times I$ | R = Risk, L = Likelihood, I = Impact; dimensionless index |
| **CVSS Base Score (simplified)** | $BS = \text{roundup}(\min(AV+AC+PR+UI+S+C+I, 10))$ | 0.0 to 10.0 scale |
| **Exploitability Sub-score** | $E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | AV, AC, PR, UI are coefficient weights |
| **Impact Sub-score** | $I_S = 1 - [(1-C) \times (1-I) \times (1-A)]$ | C, I, A are 0/0.22/0.56 coefficients |
| **Total CVSS** | $\text{Total} = \text{roundup}(\min((E + I_S), 10))$ | Bounded between 0.0 and 10.0 |
| **Vulnerability Density** | $VD = \frac{V_{count}}{A_{count}}$ | Vulnerabilities per Asset; dimensionless |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum (T_{fixed} - T_{discovered})}{N_{vulns}}$ | Measured in **days** |
| **Patch Coverage Ratio** | $PCR = \frac{P_{applied}}{P_{total}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| **Attack Surface Reduction** | $ASR = \frac{AS_{before} - AS_{after}}{AS_{before}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| **Risk Exposure (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy in **currency (USD/INR)** |
| **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Asset Value × Exposure Factor |
| **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \frac{ALE \times M - C}{C} \times 100$ | M = Mitigation effectiveness, C = Cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid the Pipe Symbol in Tables**: I have used $\vert$ implicitly via LaTeX vertical bars. When handwriting, do not confuse absolute value notation with the markdown table separator.

## 2.4 Remediation Strategies — The Four-Accepted Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite — List the 4 Remediation Approaches:**

1. **Patch (Eliminate)** — Install vendor-issued updates to permanently fix the flaw. **Most preferred method.**
2. **Mitigate (Reduce Risk)** — Apply configuration changes, hardening, or network segmentation to reduce exploitability when patching is impossible.
3. **Compensating Control** — Implement an alternative safeguard (e.g., placing an unpatchable legacy PLC behind a **data diode** or one-way gateway).
4. **Accept / Transfer Risk** — Formally document residual risk when cost of remediation exceeds asset value. Requires **sign-off from senior management**. In CPS, "accept the risk" is rarely acceptable if safety is impacted.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Power Grid Operators** use vulnerability assessments to comply with **NERC CIP** standards.
- **Manufacturing Plants** (Industry 4.0) use them to protect **OPC UA** communication.
- **Healthcare** uses them for **HIPAA** compliance and protecting medical IoT devices.
- **Automotive** uses them for **ISO/SAE 21434** (Automotive Cybersecurity Engineering).
- **Smart Cities** use them to protect SCADA-controlled water treatment and traffic systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: CVSS v3.1 Base Score

The CVSS Base Score is calculated from three sub-components: **Exploitability (E)**, **Impact (I)**, and the **Base Score equation**. We derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Impact Metrics

The **Impact** considers three CIA Triad components:
- **C** = Confidentiality coefficient
- **I** = Integrity coefficient
- **A** = Availability coefficient

Each takes a value from the set: $\{0, 0.22, 0.56\}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Where: } & 0 \rightarrow \text{None impact} \\
& 0.22 \rightarrow \text{Low impact} \\
& 0.56 \rightarrow \text{High impact}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Impact Sub-Score (IS)

The **Impact Sub-Score** $I_S$ is calculated using the probability-of-compromise formula:

$$
I_S = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right]
$$

> **Textual Explanation**: $I_S$ represents the probability that *at least one* of the CIA components will be compromised. The expression $(1 - C)$ is the probability that Confidentiality is **not** compromised, and we take the product of all three "non-compromise" probabilities, then subtract from 1.

### Step 3: Determine Scope (S)

The **Scope** is binary: **Unchanged (U)** or **Changed (C)**.
- Unchanged: vulnerability affects only the vulnerable component.
- Changed: vulnerability impacts components beyond the vulnerable one.

### Step 4: Compute the Final Impact Value (IV)

$$
IV = \begin{cases}
6.42 \times I_S & \text{if } S = U \\
7.52 \times (I_S - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (I_S - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if } S = C
\end{cases}
$$

> **Textual Explanation**: If the scope changes, the impact is amplified because the vulnerability propagates beyond its origin.

### Step 5: Calculate Exploitability (E)

The Exploitability sub-score uses four metrics with coefficient weights:

| Metric | Options & Weights |
|---|---|
| **AV** (Attack Vector) | Network=0.85, Adjacent=0.62, Local=0.55, Physical=0.20 |
| **AC** (Attack Complexity) | Low=0.77, High=0.44 |
| **PR** (Privileges Required) | None=0.85, Low=0.62 (0.68 if Scope=C), High=0.27 (0.50 if Scope=C) |
| **UI** (User Interaction) | None=0.85, Required=0.62 |

$$
E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

### Step 6: Compute Base Score (BS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
BS_{raw} &= \begin{cases}
\text{roundup}(\min(I_V + E, 10)) & \text{if } I_V > 0 \\
0 & \text{if } I_V \leq 0
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example**: A SCADA HMI has a buffer overflow vulnerability accessible over the network, with low attack complexity, no privileges required, no user interaction, and high impact on confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Scope is **Changed**.

**Given values:**
- AV = 0.85 (Network)
- AC = 0.77 (Low)
- PR = 0.85 (None, Scope = Unchanged)
- UI = 0.85 (None)
- C = 0.56, I = 0.56, A = 0.56
- Scope = Changed

**Step 1: Calculate Impact Sub-Score $I_S$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)\right] \\
&= 1 - \left[0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44\right] \\
&= 1 - \left[0.44^3\right] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Impact Value (IV) for Changed Scope**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_V &= 7.52 \times (I_S - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (I_S - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 7.52 \times (0.914816 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.914816 - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.894816)^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

We evaluate $(0.894816)^{15}$:

$$
(0.894816)^{15} \approx 0.1825
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_V &= 6.6613 - 3.25 \times 0.1825 \\
&= 6.6613 - 0.5931 \\
&\approx 6.0682
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Exploitability (E)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.7225 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.556325 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.472876 \\
&\approx 3.887
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Base Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BS &= \text{roundup}(\min(I_V + E, 10)) \\
&= \text{roundup}(\min(6.0682 + 3.887, 10)) \\
&= \text{roundup}(9.9552) \\
&\approx 9.955
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: CVSS Base Score = **9.9 → CRITICAL Severity**. The system must be patched immediately under any CPS risk framework.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Remediation Decision Tree

| Vulnerability Type | Best Remediation | CPS-Specific Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Unpatched firmware (CVE known) | **Patch** | May require **OT maintenance window** (production halt) |
| Legacy EOL (End-of-Life) PLC | **Compensating Control** | Isolate via data diode; cannot be patched |
| Default credentials on HMI | **Mitigate** (change password, enable MFA) | Vendor lockout risk; document procedure |
| Unencrypted Modbus/TCP traffic | **Mitigate** (wrap in TLS via VPN) | Modbus lacks native security — use **Modbus Security** or **DNP3 Secure Authentication** |
| Excessive open ports (22, 80, 502) | **Mitigate** (close ports, firewall rules) | Verify no legitimate OT protocol depends on the port |
| Zero-day exploit in the wild | **Compensating Control** + **Accept partial risk** | Monitor via IDS/IPS (e.g., **Snort**, **Suricata** rules) |
| Physical tamper risk on RTU | **Mitigate** (locks, tamper switches) | Critical for remote field devices |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Vulnerability" as it applies to Cyber-Physical Systems. Differentiate between a vulnerability and a threat with a suitable CPS example.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Vulnerability** is a weakness or flaw in a CPS component (hardware, software, firmware, or configuration) that could be exploited to compromise the system's security or safety.
>
> **Difference**:
>
> | Aspect | Vulnerability | Threat |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Nature** | Passive weakness | Active agent/event |
> | **Example in CPS** | Unpatched Schneider M340 PLC firmware | A nation-state attacker deploying **Triton/TRISIS** malware |
> | **Mitigation** | Patch, harden, or compensate | Detection, deterrence, response |
>
> In the **Triton/TRISIS attack (2017)**, the **vulnerability** was a flaw in the Triconex SIS (Safety Instrumented System) firmware authentication, and the **threat** was the **Xenotime threat group** attempting to disable safety controls at a petrochemical plant. The vulnerability was passive; the threat actor actively exploited it.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]
**List and briefly explain the four accepted remediation strategies in vulnerability management.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four remediation strategies are:
>
> 1. **Patch (Eliminate)** — Permanently fix the vulnerability by applying the vendor-issued software/firmware update. *Example*: Applying a Siemens SIMATIC Step 7 security patch.
>
> 2. **Mitigate (Reduce)** — Reduce the likelihood or impact via configuration changes (e.g., closing open ports, enabling encryption, disabling unused services).
>
> 3. **Compensating Control (Alternative Safeguard)** — Deploy an alternative mechanism when patching is impossible (e.g., placing an EOL Windows XP SCADA workstation behind a network firewall and disabling all internet access).
>
> 4. **Accept / Transfer Risk** — Formally document residual risk when remediation cost exceeds asset value. Requires **CISO + Business Owner sign-off**. In safety-critical CPS, "accept" is rarely an option.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**a)** Explain the **NIST-aligned Vulnerability Management Lifecycle** in detail, with specific emphasis on how each phase applies to a **Cyber-Physical System** environment. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Calculate the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** for the following vulnerability and recommend a remediation strategy:  
*An attacker on the same OT network segment can send specially crafted Modbus TCP packets to a Schneider M340 PLC, causing memory corruption and unauthorized shutdown. The attack requires low complexity, no privileges, no user interaction, and has high impact on availability and integrity, with low impact on confidentiality. The scope remains unchanged.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for **Question A(a)**:

> **Phase 1 — Asset Discovery & Inventory (1 mark)**:  
> Identify all IT, OT, and IoT assets using active scanners (Nessus) and passive ICS sensors (Claroty). Maintain an accurate **CMDB (Configuration Management Database)**. In CPS, this includes PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, engineering workstations, and historian servers.
>
> **Phase 2 — Vulnerability Scanning (1 mark)**:  
> Combine **active authenticated scans** (IT) with **passive deep-packet-inspection sensors** (OT) to avoid disrupting sensitive processes. Map findings to the **NVD/CVE database**.
>
> **Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis (1 mark)**:  
> Apply **CVSS v3.1** scoring. For CPS, override generic CVSS with **environmental scores** that factor in safety implications (e.g., a vulnerability on a Safety Instrumented System scores higher than the same flaw on a non-safety asset).
>
> **Phase 4 — Remediation Planning (1.5 marks)**:  
> Prioritize using the formula: **Effective Risk = CVSS Base × Asset Criticality × Threat Intel Weight**. Develop a remediation roadmap with timelines: **Critical: 15 days, High: 30 days, Medium: 60 days, Low: 90 days**.
>
> **Phase 5 — Remediation & Verification (1.5 marks)**:  
> Apply patches during **scheduled OT maintenance windows**. For legacy systems, deploy **compensating controls** (data diodes, network segmentation). **Re-scan to verify** the vulnerability is closed. A vulnerability is *not* closed until re-scan confirms it.
>
> **Phase 6 — Reporting & Continuous Monitoring (1 mark)**:  
> Generate reports for executives (risk posture) and engineers (technical findings). Feed data into **SIEM/SOAR** platforms. Schedule **24x7x365** continuous monitoring using passive sensors. The process then **loops back to Phase 1**.

#### Model Solution for **Question A(b)**:

**Step 1: Identify CVSS Metric Values**

| Metric | Value | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **AV** (Attack Vector) | Adjacent Network | 0.62 |
| **AC** (Attack Complexity) | Low | 0.77 |
| **PR** (Privileges Required) | None (Scope = Unchanged) | 0.85 |
| **UI** (User Interaction) | None | 0.85 |
| **C** (Confidentiality) | Low | 0.22 |
| **I** (Integrity) | High | 0.56 |
| **A** (Availability) | High | 0.56 |
| **S** (Scope) | Unchanged | — |

**Step 2: Calculate Impact Sub-Score $I_S$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= 1 - [(1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)] \\
&= 1 - [0.78 \times 0.44 \times 0.44] \\
&= 1 - [0.78 \times 0.1936] \\
&= 1 - 0.151008 \\
&= 0.848992
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Impact Value (IV) for Unchanged Scope**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_V &= 6.42 \times I_S \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.848992 \\
&\approx 5.4505
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Exploitability (E)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8.22 \times 0.62 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.62 \times 0.77 \times 0.7225 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.62 \times 0.556325 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.3449215 \\
&\approx 2.8353
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Base Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BS &= \text{roundup}(\min(I_V + E, 10)) \\
&= \text{roundup}(\min(5.4505 + 2.8353, 10)) \\
&= \text{roundup}(8.2858) \\
&\approx 8.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Interpret Result and Recommend Remediation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVSS Base Score = 8.3 → HIGH Severity**.
>
> **Recommended Remediation Strategy**: **Patch** the Schneider M340 PLC firmware (vendor-issued) within **15–30 days**, scheduled during the next OT maintenance window. Additionally, **mitigate** by:
> - Implementing **Modbus firewall rules** to whitelist authorized master devices.
> - Enabling **network segmentation** (separate the PLC into a Level 1 VLAN).
> - Deploying **passive ICS monitoring** (e.g., Nozomi Networks) to detect anomalous Modbus traffic patterns.
> - Verifying with a **post-patch re-scan** that the vulnerability is closed.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**a)** Discuss the **challenges of conducting vulnerability assessments in OT/CPS environments** as compared to traditional IT environments. List at least five distinct challenges. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A manufacturing plant's **legacy S7-300 PLC** has a critical vulnerability (CVSS 9.8) for which **no patch is available** (End-of-Life hardware). The asset is critical to a 24/7 production line with a daily revenue loss of **₹5,00,000 per day** if shut down. The cost of replacing the PLC is **₹50,00,000**. Propose a **compensating control strategy** and compute the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** and **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** for your solution costing **₹12,00,000** with **80% effectiveness**. Assume the ARO (Annual Rate of Occurrence) is **1.0** and Asset Value (AV) is **₹2,00,00,000**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for **Question B(a)**:

> **Five Major Challenges of CPS Vulnerability Assessment (vs. IT):**
>
> 1. **System Availability Constraint (1.5 marks)**: OT systems must run 24/7. **Active scanning can crash legacy PLCs** and disrupt physical processes. Mitigation: Use *passive* ICS-aware sensors instead of active probes.
>
> 2. **Proprietary Protocols (1.5 marks)**: OT uses protocols like **Modbus, DNP3, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, BACnet** that traditional IT scanners (Nessus, Qualys) do not understand. Requires ICS-specific tools (Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Tenable.ot).
>
> 3. **Safety vs. Security Trade-off (1.5 marks)**: Patching a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) may invalidate its **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) certification** (IEC 61508/61511). The assessor must coordinate with safety engineers.
>
> 4. **Legacy & EOL Systems (1.5 marks)**: Many OT devices run **Windows XP, VxWorks, or proprietary RTOS** for which patches no longer exist. The assessor must rely on **compensating controls** rather than patching.
>
> 5. **Air-Gap Myth & Network Convergence (1 mark)**: Modern Industry 4.0 environments connect OT to IT networks (and cloud), breaking the **"air-gap" assumption**. The IT/OT convergence expands the attack surface exponentially, requiring **Purdue Model-aware assessment**.

#### Model Solution for **Question B(b)**:

**Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**

The Exposure Factor (EF) is the percentage of asset value lost if the vulnerability is exploited. For a critical PLC shutdown affecting production, we use the full asset value as a proxy. Given AV = ₹2,00,00,000, we take EF = 1.0 (worst case for a critical PLC).

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Return on Security Investment (ROSI)**

Given:
- Mitigation effectiveness $M = 0.80$
- Cost of control $C = 12{,}00{,}000$ INR

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{(ALE \times M) - C}{C} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80) - 12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}60{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}48{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 12.333 \times 100 \\
&\approx 1233.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Propose Compensating Control Strategy**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended Compensating Controls (since patch is unavailable for EOL PLC):**
>
> 1. **Network Segmentation** — Place the S7-300 PLC on a dedicated Level 1 VLAN isolated from the corporate IT network.
> 2. **Data Diode / Unidirectional Gateway** — Enforce one-way communication from OT to IT to prevent remote exploitation from IT networks.
> 3. **Layered Firewall Rules** — Whitelist only authorized S7 communication peers; block all other traffic.
> 4. **Passive ICS Monitoring** — Deploy **Claroty or Nozomi** sensors to detect anomalous S7 communications in real time.
> 5. **Physical Security** — Lock the control cabinet to prevent local USB-based attacks (e.g., Stuxnet-style vectors).
> 6. **Vendor Engagement** — Negotiate with Siemens for an **extended support contract** or migrate to a supported S7-1500 platform within 12–18 months.
>
> **Conclusion**: With a **ROSI of 1233%**, the compensating control investment is **highly justified** — saving ₹1,48,00,000 in expected annual losses for a ₹12,00,000 investment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Threat" with "Vulnerability"** — A threat is an *actor/event*; a vulnerability is a *weakness*. The examiner deducts 1 mark for every interchange.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the CPS Context** — Generic IT answers (e.g., "patch within 7 days") are insufficient for CPS. Always mention **maintenance windows, safety implications, and ICS-specific tools**.
>
> 3. **CVSS Calculation Errors** — Students often misapply the **Scope coefficient**. If Scope = **Changed**, the PR weight reduces (0.62 → 0.68, 0.27 → 0.50), and a different IV formula applies.
>
> 4. **Skipping Verification Step** — A vulnerability is **not closed** until a **post-remediation re-scan** confirms elimination. Many students stop at "apply patch" and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "Accept Risk" Path** — Some students only mention patching. The examiner expects all 4 strategies: **Patch, Mitigate, Compensate, Accept**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Continuous Monitoring** — A vulnerability assessment is *not* a one-time event. The lifecycle must explicitly loop back to scanning and continuous monitoring.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Vulnerability** = weakness; **Threat** = actor/event; **Risk** = Likelihood × Impact.
- [ ] CVSS scale is **0.0 to 10.0**: Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).
- [ ] CVSS formula structure: $BS = \text{roundup}(\min(I_V + E, 10))$.
- [ ] Exploitability formula: $E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$.
- [ ] Impact sub-score: $I_S = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$.
- [ ] Four remediation strategies: **Patch, Mitigate, Compensate, Accept**.
- [ ] Vulnerability Management Lifecycle has **6 phases** (NIST SP 800-40r4): Discover → Scan → Analyze → Plan → Remediate & Verify → Report & Monitor.
- [ ] In CPS/OT, **active scanning can disrupt physical processes** — use **passive ICS sensors**.
- [ ] **CVE** = unique ID for a specific flaw; **CWE** = category of weakness.
- [ ] **CPS-specific vulnerabilities** include: legacy firmware, default credentials, unencrypted OT protocols (Modbus/TCP), and shadow assets.
- [ ] **Purdue Model** has 6 levels (0–5): physical process → enterprise.
- [ ] **Compensating controls** (e.g., data diode, segmentation) are used when patching is impossible (EOL systems).
- [ ] **ALE = SLE × ARO** and **ROSI = (ALE × M − C) / C × 100** — must be memorized with units.
- [ ] Verification is mandatory: **Re-scan after every remediation**.
- [ ] Vulnerability management is **continuous** — schedule 24/7 monitoring for CPS.
- [ ] For the **Triton/TRISIS** case study, mention the **Xenotime** threat actor and **SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** compromise.
- [ ] Standards to remember: **NIST SP 800-40r4**, **NIST SP 800-53**, **IEC 62443**, **ISO/SAE 21434**, **NERC CIP**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Response for Firmware and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incident Response for Firmware and Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Incident Response (IR) for Firmware** is the coordinated, structured set of procedures used by an organization to prepare for, detect, analyze, contain, eradicate, and recover from cybersecurity incidents that compromise the **firmware layer** of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). Firmware, in the CPS context, refers to the low-level software permanently etched into a device's non-volatile memory (ROM, EEPROM, NOR/NAND flash) that initializes hardware, boots the system, and provides runtime control abstractions to higher-level software (OS, middleware, applications).

According to **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** (Computer Security Incident Handling Guide) and **NIST SP 800-193** (Platform Resiliency), firmware incident response is treated as a distinct sub-discipline of IR because firmware sits *below* the operating system, often outside the visibility of traditional endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**
> Firmware in CPS includes the **Bootloader**, **BIOS/UEFI**, **Board Support Package (BSP)**, **Real-Time OS (RTOS) kernel image**, **Device Drivers**, **FPGA bitstreams**, and **Over-the-Air (OTA) update modules**. A firmware compromise is fundamentally a **root-of-trust violation**.

### 1.2 Common Firmware Vulnerability Classes (CWE Mapping)

| Vulnerability Class | CWE Identifier | CPS-Specific Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardcoded Credentials** | CWE-798 | Default admin passwords in industrial controllers |
| **Improper Authentication** | CWE-287 | Bypassing secure boot signatures |
| **Buffer Overflow** | CWE-120 | Stack smashing in bootloader parsers |
| **Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity** | CWE-345 | Rogue firmware accepted during OTA update |
| **Missing Immutable Root of Trust** | CWE-1326 | SPI flash directly rewritable via JTAG |
| **Race Condition** | CWE-362 | TOCTOU in firmware update daemon |
| **Information Exposure** | CWE-200 | Debug logs leaking cryptographic keys |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Building Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Skyscraper Inspector**
> Imagine a 50-storey skyscraper (your CPS device). Normal cybersecurity guards the *floors* (OS, applications) — they check IDs, monitor visitors, and lock doors. **Firmware is the building's foundation and structural steel.** If a saboteur weakens the foundation (flashes malicious firmware), the entire building can collapse even when every floor's security is perfect, *or worse, the floors themselves can be made to betray the occupants*. A firmware incident responder is the **structural engineer with ground-penetrating radar** — they look where ordinary guards cannot.

### 1.4 Why Firmware IR is *Different* from Conventional IR

1. **Visibility Gap**: Traditional antivirus and EDR agents run *above* the firmware; they have no read access to the UEFI/BIOS, secure boot ROM, or embedded controller firmware.
2. **Persistence Mechanism**: Compromised firmware survives OS reinstallation, disk wiping, and even hardware replacement of upper layers — a property called **"below-the-OS persistence."**
3. **Physical-World Consequence**: In CPS, a firmware compromise can directly manipulate actuators (valves, motors, breakers) — bridging the cyber-physical divide.
4. **Limited Forensics**: Volatile memory of embedded MCUs is often lost on power-off, while non-volatile firmware storage may use proprietary formats without standard acquisition tools.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 The NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle (Adapted for Firmware)

The lifecycle has **four core phases** that loop continuously:

#### Phase 1: Preparation
- Inventory all firmware-bearing assets (asset register with firmware version hashes).
- Maintain **cryptographically signed golden images** in an air-gapped vault.
- Pre-stage forensic tools: **Chip-off readers (e.g., Xeltek), JTAG/SWD debuggers, UEFI variable dumpers, Binwalk, Ghidra, FACT-extractor**.
- Train IR teams on chip-off techniques and chain-of-custody documentation.

#### Phase 2: Detection & Analysis
- **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)** for firmware:
  - Mismatched SHA-256 hash of SPI flash vs. golden image.
  - Unexpected UEFI NVRAM variables (e.g., `Boot####` pointing to unsigned loader).
  - Anomalous power-on self-test (POST) behaviour.
  - Spike in SMM (System Management Mode) handler invocations.
  - Outbound traffic from BMC/IPMI interfaces to unknown IPs.
- Use **TPM measured boot logs (PCR 0–7)** to detect unsigned boot components.

#### Phase 3: Containment, Eradication, & Recovery
- **Containment**: Isolate the device at the network layer (VLAN quarantine, physical unplug). Disable JTAG debug ports via hardware write-protect.
- **Eradication**: Reflash firmware from cryptographically verified golden image using out-of-band programmer (e.g., CH341A, Bus Pirate).
- **Recovery**: Re-enable secure boot, rotate all keys, re-issue certificates, perform full functional & safety validation.

#### Phase 4: Post-Incident Activity
- Root-cause analysis (5-Whys, fishbone diagram).
- Update threat model and firmware security baseline.
- Share IoCs with ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers).

### 2.2 Risk Quantification Metrics

**Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond/Recover (MTTR)** are critical SLAs in firmware IR:

$$\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occur},i})$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{resolved},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{occur}}$ = timestamp of compromise
- $t_{\text{detect}}$ = timestamp of confirmed detection
- $t_{\text{resolved}}$ = timestamp of full eradication and recovery
- $N$ = total number of incidents in the observation window

> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *lower* MTTD and MTTR indicates a more mature firmware IR capability. In CPS, the **Mean Time to Unsafe State (MTTUS)** is a supplementary safety metric — the time from compromise until the CPS reaches a hazardous physical state.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| CVSS v3.1 Base Score | $\text{Base} = \text{roundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10))$ when S:U; $\text{roundUp}(\min(1.08(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\, 10))$ when S:C | Score $\in [0.0,\, 10.0]$ |
| Intrinsic Sub-Score (ISS) | $\text{ISS} = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$ | $C, I, A \in \{0, 0.22, 0.56, 0.85\}$ (None, Low, High) |
| Impact (Scope Unchanged) | $I = 6.42 \times \text{ISS}$ | $I \in [0,\, 6.42]$ |
| Exploitability | $E = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$ | $E \in [0,\, 10]$ |
| Confidentiality Metric | $C \in \{0,\, 0.22,\, 0.56,\, 0.85\}$ | None / Low / High |
| MTTD | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{occur}})$ | Hours / Days |
| MTTR | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (t_{\text{resolved}} - t_{\text{detect}})$ | Hours / Days |
| Severity Rating | 0.0 None; 0.1–3.9 Low; 4.0–6.9 Medium; 7.0–8.9 High; 9.0–10.0 Critical | N/A |
| Hash Verification | $\text{verify}(H_{\text{flash}},\, H_{\text{golden}})$ | Boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note on CVSS:** The above equations are the verbatim published CVSS v3.1 specification (FIRST.org). The roundUp function rounds **to one decimal place** using standard ceiling logic.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**: Used to triage TRITON/TRISIS, Industroyer, and Pipedream firmware-level incidents.
- **Automotive**: UN R155 and ISO/SAE 21434 require OEMs to demonstrate firmware IR capabilities for type approval.
- **Medical Devices**: FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance (2023) mandates firmware vulnerability management workflows.
- **Smart Grid & SCADA**: NERC CIP-008-6 requires documented firmware incident reporting within 1 hour for high/medium impact BES Cyber Systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: CVSS v3.1 Base Score Computation for a Firmware Vulnerability

**Scenario:** A programmable logic controller (PLC) has a firmware update daemon with a stack-based buffer overflow. The vulnerability is exploitable **only with physical access** (e.g., USB at the engineering port), requires **high attack complexity** (specific byte sequence), needs **no privileges**, **no user interaction**, and results in **complete loss of confidentiality, integrity, and availability**. Scope is **unchanged** (only the affected PLC is impacted, not the upstream SCADA HMI).

**CVSS Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:P/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`

**Step 1 — Extract metric values from the CVSS v3.1 specification table:**

| Metric | Value | Numeric Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Physical | $0.20$ |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | High | $0.50$ |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None | $0.85$ |
| User Interaction (UI) | None | $0.85$ |
| Scope (S) | Unchanged | (multiplier decision) |
| Confidentiality (C) | High | $0.56$ |
| Integrity (I) | High | $0.56$ |
| Availability (A) | High | $0.56$ |

**Step 2 — Compute the Intrinsic Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
