\text{ISS} &= 1 - [(1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)] \\
&= 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)] \\
&= 1 - [(0.44)(0.44)(0.44)] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope = Unchanged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISS} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.8731 \text{ (4 d.p.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI} \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.20 \times 0.50 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

Working the multiplication left-to-right:

$$
\begin{aligned}
8.22 \times 0.20 &= 1.644 \\
1.644 \times 0.50 &= 0.8220 \\
0.8220 \times 0.85 &= 0.6987 \\
0.6987 \times 0.85 &= 0.593895
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\text{Exploitability} = 0.5939$ (4 d.p.).

**Step 5 — Compute the Base Score (Scope = Unchanged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability} \\
&= 5.8731 + 0.5939 \\
&= 6.4670
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the ceiling rounding to one decimal place:

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(6.4670) = 6.5
$$

**Step 6 — Severity Rating Lookup:** $6.5 \in [4.0,\, 6.9] \Rightarrow$ **Medium Severity**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Firmware IR Decision Flow

The matrix below maps **observed symptom → probable firmware class → IR action**, designed for rapid field triage:

| Observed Symptom | Probable Firmware Class | Immediate IR Action | Forensic Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| TPM PCR 0–7 mismatch | Bootloader/BIOS tampering | Quarantine, capture TPM event log | **High** — preserve NVRAM |
| Unsigned kernel loaded | Secure Boot bypass | Disable network, dump ACPI tables | **Critical** |
| Anomalous SPI flash CRC | Direct flash write (rogue reflash) | Power down, chip-off acquisition | **Critical** |
| Excessive SMM handler calls | SMM rootkit (e.g., ThinkPwn class) | Disable SMI routing if possible | **High** |
| BMC/IPMI traffic to unknown C2 | Baseboard Management Compromise | VLAN isolate, capture PCAP | **Critical** |
| POST hangs at random stages | Firmware rootkit with anti-forensics | Photograph POST codes, reflash from golden image | **High** |
| Inverted GPIO pin state at boot | Bootkit in MCU bootloader | JTAG halt, dump internal flash | **Critical** |
| HMI shows "firmware up-to-date" while version is rolled back | Downgrade attack (e.g., thinkpwn, Trickboot) | Compare bootloader rollback fuses | **High** |

### 3.3 MTTD / MTTR Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a SCADA operator records the following five firmware incidents over a quarter:

| Incident | $t_{\text{occur}}$ | $t_{\text{detect}}$ | $t_{\text{resolved}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Day 1 00:00 | Day 1 06:00 | Day 2 12:00 |
| 2 | Day 10 14:00 | Day 10 18:00 | Day 11 09:00 |
| 3 | Day 18 03:00 | Day 19 22:00 | Day 22 18:00 |
| 4 | Day 25 09:00 | Day 25 09:30 | Day 25 13:00 |
| 5 | Day 40 16:00 | Day 42 11:00 | Day 44 05:00 |

**Detection deltas (in hours):** 6, 4, 43, 0.5, 43

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTD} &= \frac{6 + 4 + 43 + 0.5 + 43}{5} = \frac{96.5}{5} = 19.3 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resolution deltas (in hours):** 30, 15, 44, 3.5, 42

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{30 + 15 + 44 + 3.5 + 42}{5} = \frac{134.5}{5} = 26.9 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice Incident 4 has a sub-30-minute MTTD — this is achievable because **TPM measured boot** failed immediately on the next reboot. Incidents 3 and 5 (≈43h MTTD) suggest the operator was relying on network-side detection alone, which is blind to firmware-layer attacks. The remediation is to **enable platform integrity telemetry** and **continuous attestation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61 Adapted)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ph1[Phase 1 Preparation] --> ph2[Phase 2 Detection and Analysis]
    ph2 --> ph3[Phase 3 Containment Eradication Recovery]
    ph3 --> ph4[Phase 4 Post Incident Activity]
    ph4 --> ph1
    ph1 -.-> assets[Asset Inventory of Firmware Bearing Devices]
    ph1 -.-> golden[Maintain Signed Golden Firmware Vault]
    ph2 -.-> tpm[Validate TPM PCR 0 to 7]
    ph2 -.-> hash[Compare SPI Flash SHA-256 Hash]
    ph3 -.-> iso[Network and Physical Isolation]
    ph3 -.-> reflash[Out of Band Reflash via Programmer]
    ph4 -.-> rca[Root Cause Analysis 5 Whys]
    ph4 -.-> share[Share IoCs with ISAC Community]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Firmware Attack Surface Map in a CPS

```mermaid
graph LR
    root[Firmware Attack Surface in CPS]
    n1[Bootloader and BIOS Layer]
    n2[RTOS Kernel Image]
    n3[Device Drivers and BSP]
    n4[OTA Update Module]
    n5[Baseboard Management Controller BMC]
    n6[FPGA Bitstream]
    n7[Supply Chain and Third Party Firmware]
    root --> n1
    root --> n2
    root --> n3
    root --> n4
    root --> n5
    root --> n6
    root --> n7
    n1 --> v1[V1 Secure Boot Bypass]
    n1 --> v2[V2 Hardcoded Boot Password]
    n2 --> v3[V3 RTOS Kernel Rootkit]
    n3 --> v4[V4 Driver Buffer Overflow]
    n4 --> v5[V5 Unsigned Firmware Acceptance]
    n5 --> v6[V6 BMC Credential Stuffing]
    n6 --> v7[V7 Bitstream Reverse Engineering]
    n7 --> v8[V8 Malicious Vendor Implant]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Firmware Forensic Acquisition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    st1[Step 1 Identify Affected Device] --> st2[Step 2 Document Chain of Custody]
    st2 --> st3[Step 3 Network and Power Isolate]
    st3 --> st4a[Step 4A Software Acquire via In Band Tool]
    st3 --> st4b[Step 4B Hardware Acquire via Chip Off or JTAG]
    st4a --> st5[Step 5 Compute SHA-256 Hash of Image]
    st4b --> st5
    st5 --> st6[Step 6 Compare Hash to Golden Image]
    st6 --> st7[Step 7 Static Analysis with Binwalk Ghidra]
    st7 --> st8[Step 8 Dynamic Analysis in Emulator QEMU or Unicorn]
    st8 --> st9[Step 9 Root Cause Attribution]
    st9 --> st10[Step 10 Forensic Report and Remediation Plan]
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Firmware Trust Chain (Why Compromise is Catastrophic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rom[Hardware Immutable ROM] --> sb[Secure Boot Verifier]
    sb --> bl[Signed Bootloader]
    bl --> kern[Signed RTOS Kernel]
    kern --> drv[Signed Device Drivers]
    drv --> app[Application Layer]
    app --> user[User Facing Services]
    classA[Class A Tampering] -.-> rom
    classB[Class B Tampering] -.-> sb
    classC[Class C Tampering] -.-> bl
    classD[Class D Tampering] -.-> kern
    classE[Class E Tampering] -.-> drv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The downward arrows represent the *chain of trust* — each layer cryptographically validates the next. An attacker breaking *any* link (dashed class labels A–E) can compromise every layer above it. This is the conceptual reason firmware IR is treated as a **root-of-trust violation** rather than a normal application-layer incident.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Visual-Hallucination Note:** All four diagrams use only alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `ph1`, `n1`, `st1`, `rom`) and plain-text labels — no reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) and no embedded markdown formatting that would break the Mermaid parser.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Define "firmware" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems and explain why incident response at the firmware layer is fundamentally different from application-layer incident response. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Firmware is the low-level software permanently stored in non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash) of a CPS device that initializes hardware and provides runtime control to higher software layers. (1 mark)
Firmware IR differs from application-layer IR in three key ways: (a) it lies below the OS and is therefore invisible to conventional EDR/AV tools, (b) it survives OS reinstallation and disk wiping, providing *below-the-OS persistence*, and (c) in CPS it directly controls physical actuators, so compromise can produce real-world safety consequences. (2 marks — one mark per valid distinguishing point, up to 2)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *List and briefly describe the four phases of the NIST SP 800-61 incident response lifecycle as applied to a firmware compromise. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Preparation (1 m):** Pre-stage forensic tools, maintain cryptographically signed golden firmware images, train responders on chip-off and JTAG techniques.
2. **Detection & Analysis (1 m):** Identify IoCs such as TPM PCR mismatches, SPI flash hash mismatch, and anomalous UEFI NVRAM variables.
3. **Containment, Eradication, & Recovery (1/2 m):** Network/physical isolate, reflash from golden image via out-of-band programmer, re-enable secure boot.
4. **Post-Incident Activity (1/2 m):** Root-cause analysis, update threat model, share IoCs with ISAC community.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the key technical challenges in *detecting* firmware-level attacks in CPS devices. How do platform integrity technologies (TPM, secure boot, measured boot) help overcome these challenges?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** With reference to a real-world CPS firmware incident (e.g., TRITON/TRISIS, Industroyer, or Pipedream), explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting forensic analysis of compromised firmware, including chain-of-custody requirements.

**Model Answer — Q1(a) (7 Marks):**

The technical challenges in detecting firmware attacks in CPS are:

1. **Visibility Gap (1 m):** Standard EDR agents run in the OS user/kernel space and have no read access to SPI flash, UEFI NVRAM, or the BMC. Firmware manipulation is therefore *invisible* to conventional telemetry.

2. **Proprietary and Encrypted Firmware Formats (1 m):** ICS vendors (Siemens, Schneider, ABB) often use signed/encrypted firmware blobs. Even when extracted, the file system inside the blob (e.g., Cramfs, JFFS2, UBI) is non-standard and requires vendor-specific parsers.

3. **Resource Constraints (1 m):** Many CPS embedded MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0/M3) lack MMU, lack sufficient RAM for on-device HIDS agents, and cannot afford the cycles of cryptographic attestation.

4. **Below-the-OS Persistence (1 m):** Firmware implants survive OS reinstall, disk wipe, and upper-layer component replacement, defeating standard remediation playbooks.

5. **False Sense of Secure Boot (1 m):** If the *root of trust itself* (e.g., SPI flash write-protect fuse) is not enforced, an attacker with physical access can simply re-flash a malicious image that the boot ROM will happily load, bypassing every higher layer.

**Platform integrity mitigations (1 m each, up to 2 m):**
- **TPM Measured Boot:** The TPM chip records SHA-1/SHA-256 hashes of each boot stage into Platform Configuration Registers (PCR 0–7). A mismatch on next boot is a definitive tamper indicator.
- **UEFI Secure Boot:** Cryptographically verifies the bootloader and kernel signatures against vendor-trusted keys stored in the BIOS NVRAM. Unsigned code is rejected at pre-OS stage.
- **Intel Boot Guard / AMD Platform Security Processor:** Anchor the root of trust in immutable fuses on the SoC, making SPI flash reflash infeasible without platform vendor co-operation.

**Model Answer — Q1(b) (7 Marks):**

**Case study reference — TRITON/TRISIS (2017, Schneider Triconex SIS):** Attackers deployed custom firmware into the Safety Instrumented System (SIS) controller (Tricon MP3008) by exploiting a zero-day in the engineering workstation's TriStation protocol. The malicious firmware reprogrammed the SIS to a fail-safe state (and ultimately to a dangerous state) — a textbook CPS firmware attack.

**Forensic procedure (1 mark per step, 1 mark for chain-of-custody):**

1. **Identify and Inventory:** Enumerate all affected Tricon controllers by serial number, firmware version, and Triconex SDM hash.
2. **Document Chain of Custody (mandatory):** Every transfer of evidence is logged with timestamp, handler signature, tamper-evident bag ID, and SHA-256 hash. This satisfies admissibility under IEC 62443 and NERC CIP-008-6.
3. **Network and Power Isolate:** Disconnect from process control network; do NOT power off abruptly (volatile forensic evidence in SRAM is lost).
4. **Acquire Firmware Image:** Use either (a) **in-band** TriStation protocol dump, or (b) **out-of-band** chip-off via JTAG to SPI flash (more forensically sound — captures unaltered bytes).
5. **Hash and Verify:** Compute SHA-256 of the acquired image and compare with the vendor's signed golden image hash.
6. **Static Analysis:** Use `binwalk` to extract embedded file systems, then `Ghidra`/`IDA Pro` to disassemble the suspected payload module.
7. **Dynamic Analysis (Sandbox):** Emulate the firmware in `QEMU` or `Unicorn Engine` to observe API calls and library imports; identify the implant's C2 channel.
8. **Root Cause Attribution & Reporting:** Document the implant's functionality (e.g., ladder logic overwrite routine), attribute to a threat actor using YARA rules and MITRE ATT&CK for ICS (T0855: Unauthorized Command Message, T0857: System Firmware).
9. **Remediation Plan:** Reflash from vendor-signed image, rotate keys, push firmware update, deploy HIDS on engineering workstations.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CVSS v3.1 framework** in detail. Using the vector `CVSS:3.1/AV:P/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`, compute the **base score** step by step, and state the resulting severity rating.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **containment, eradication, and recovery** strategies specific to firmware incidents. Distinguish between software-level and hardware-level remediation approaches.

**Model Answer — Q2(a) (7 Marks):**

**CVSS v3.1 Framework (3 marks for explanation):**
The Common Vulnerability Scoring System v3.1, maintained by **FIRST.org**, is the industry-standard metric for communicating the severity of software/firmware vulnerabilities. It produces a numeric score in $[0.0,\, 10.0]$ derived from three metric groups:
- **Base metrics** (intrinsic, immutable): Attack Vector (AV), Attack Complexity (AC), Privileges Required (PR), User Interaction (UI), Scope (S), Confidentiality (C), Integrity (I), Availability (A).
- **Temporal metrics** (change over time): Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence.
- **Environmental metrics** (context-specific): Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability Requirements, plus Modified Base metrics.

The Base Score is derived using the equations in the cheat sheet (Section 2.3). The vector string is a compact representation readable by automated scoring tools.

**Step-by-step computation for the given vector (4 marks — see Section 3.1 for the full derivation):**

- ISS $= 1 - (0.44)^3 = 0.914816$
- Impact $= 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.8731$ (since Scope = Unchanged)
- Exploitability $= 8.22 \times 0.20 \times 0.50 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 0.5939$
- Sum $= 6.4670$
- **Base Score = roundUp(6.4670) = 6.5** (Medium Severity)

**Severity rating:** $6.5 \in [4.0,\, 6.9]$ → **Medium**.

**Model Answer — Q2(b) (7 Marks):**

| Stage | Software-Level Approach | Hardware-Level Approach | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Containment** (2 m) | VLAN quarantine; disable network interfaces; revoke active sessions on engineering workstation; suspend OTA delivery server. | Physically unplug the device; place tamper-evident seal on enclosure; lock equipment room; disable JTAG/SWD test points via hardware switches. | 1 m per row |
| **Eradication** (2 m) | Reflash firmware using vendor's signed update package over the normal update channel; verify signature; replace bootloader if needed. | Use **out-of-band programmer** (CH341A, Xeltek SuperPro) to overwrite SPI/NOR flash from external golden image; set hardware write-protect fuses; remove JTAG test headers. | 1 m per row |
| **Recovery** (2 m) | Re-enable Secure Boot, rotate all keys/certificates, redeploy HIDS/EDR, perform full functional test of CPS process. | Replace compromised SoC/MCU; perform **dimensional/electrical validation**; re-calibrate sensors and actuators; perform **safety integrity test** per IEC 61508 SIL level. | 1 m per row |
| **Validation** (1 m) | Verify TPM PCR 0–7 values match golden reference; check UEFI NVRAM integrity; run automated attestation report. | Visual inspection for hardware tampering (added components, modified traces); continuity test of critical PCB nets. | 1 m for row |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Do not** state the Base Score without showing the **roundUp** step. Many students write "6.467" and stop. Awarding 6.5 (after ceiling) is required.
> 2. **Do not** confuse the **Exploitability** equation's Scope handling. Scope is **NOT** multiplied into Exploitability — it only changes the Base formula's outer multiplier (1.08).
> 3. **Do not** forget to state the **severity rating** (Medium/High/Critical) — it is worth a half-mark separately.
> 4. In the IR procedure answer, examiners specifically look for **chain-of-custody** mention. Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. In Q1(b), generic "scan with antivirus" is **not** a valid firmware forensic step. Examiners expect chip-off / JTAG / vendor-specific dump methods.
> 6. Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets are **not** expected — use labelled flow diagrams drawn with pen. Mermaid is for these notes only.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 High-Yield Bullets — Read Before Exam**

- **Firmware** lives *below* the OS in non-volatile memory and is the **root of trust** of any CPS.
- Firmware attacks are **invisible to EDR/AV** and **survive OS reinstall** — this is the "below-the-OS persistence" property.
- NIST SP 800-61 has **4 phases**: Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident.
- The **firmware IR forensic workflow** is: Identify → Custody Document → Isolate → Acquire (in-band or chip-off) → Hash → Static + Dynamic Analysis → RCA → Report.
- **TPM measured boot** records hashes of each boot stage into PCR 0–7; **mismatch = compromise**.
- **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** uses: ISS $= 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$; Impact $= 6.42 \times$ ISS (when Scope Unchanged); Exploitability $= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$; Base = roundUp(Impact + Exploitability) capped at 10.
- **Severity bands**: 0.0 None, 0.1–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 **Medium**, 7.0–8.9 **High**, 9.0–10.0 **Critical**.
- **MTTD** and **MTTR** are the two key SLAs — aim for low values via continuous attestation and signed golden images.
- **Containment** is two-pronged: software (network quarantine, OTA suspension) **AND** hardware (unplug, disable JTAG, tamper-evident seal).
- **Eradication** requires **cryptographically verified reflash**; software-only remediation is insufficient if the attacker has root-of-trust control.
- **Recovery** must include **safety integrity re-validation** (IEC 61508 SIL, IEC 62443) — never return a CPS to service without it.
- **Chain of custody** is non-negotiable for forensic evidence to be admissible in audit / regulatory proceedings (NERC CIP-008-6, IEC 62443-2-4).
- **Standards to remember**: NIST SP 800-61, NIST SP 800-193, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS (T0855, T0857, T0860), IEC 62443, ISO/SAE 21434 (automotive), UN R155.
- **Never** say firmware IR is "the same as OS IR" in an exam — the examiner is specifically testing whether you understand the *layer-below* distinction.
- For **CPS**, always consider the **physical safety** dimension — a firmware incident can transition to a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) event within seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Response Strategies Specific to Firmware and Vulnerability Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incident Response Strategies Specific to Firmware and Vulnerability Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Firmware Incident Response (FIR)

**Firmware Incident Response (FIR)** is the structured, policy-driven set of procedures used by an organization to **detect, contain, eradicate, recover from, and document** security incidents that compromise the firmware layer of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). Unlike traditional software IR, FIR operates below the operating system at the hardware-software interface (BIOS/UEFI, U-Boot, RTOS bootloaders, MCU firmware blobs, PLC control logic, etc.) and frequently requires physical access, JTAG/SWD debuggers, or vendor-signed update channels to remediate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECPT863 / Module 4**
> Firmware is treated as a **persistent root-of-trust failure surface**. A single successful firmware implant can survive OS reinstallation, disk replacement, and full factory resets, making IR strategy fundamentally different from conventional IT incident handling.

### 1.2 Core Concept — What is a "Firmware Vulnerability"?

A **firmware vulnerability** is a defect, misconfiguration, hardcoded credential, or undocumented backdoor in the low-level code that initialises and controls hardware. Firmware vulnerabilities fall into three broad KTU-defined classes:

| Class | Description | Example Vector |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A — Design Flaw** | Logic defect in firmware source (e.g., unchecked buffer in bootloader) | Stack overflow in U-Boot `do_bootd()` |
| **Class B — Supply-Chain Implant** | Malicious modification introduced before deployment | Stuxnet-style PLC ladder-logic injection |
| **Class C — Configuration Drift** | Default credentials, unsigned update channels, exposed debug ports | Hardcoded UART shell on IoT sensor |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building's Hidden Plumbing

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Firmware = Building Foundation Plumbing)**
> Imagine a 50-storey smart building. The **Operating System** is the lobby reception desk — if compromised, you can replace it in a day. The **Firmware** is the underground plumbing and electrical conduits hidden in the concrete — if tampered with, water poisons every floor, and you cannot fix it without breaking walls. **Firmware Incident Response is the emergency engineering team that locates the poisoned pipe, isolates it, drains the contamination, and rebuilds the conduit — all without collapsing the building.**

This analogy is crucial: firmware IR rarely allows a clean "re-image" because the firmware itself may be the source of the attack. The IR team must operate with **forensic-grade caution** to avoid destroying evidence of the implant.

### 1.4 Why Firmware Incidents Demand a Unique IR Strategy

Five distinguishing properties of firmware-level incidents, as codified in KTU Module 4:

1. **Persistence Beyond Re-imaging** — Implants live in SPI flash, eMMC boot partitions, or secure-boot variables.
2. **Limited Telemetry** — Standard SIEM/EDR agents run *above* the firmware layer and are blind to it.
3. **Hardware-Coupled Recovery** — A bad flash can permanently brick the device.
4. **Supply-Chain Provenance Required** — You must trace the binary back to the OEM signing key.
5. **Safety Implications in CPS** — In ICS/IoMT/automotive, firmware tampering can cause physical harm, not just data loss.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Thresholds Used in FIR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard FIR Metrics (per NIST SP 800-193 & ENISA Firmware Resilience Guidelines):**
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — target **≤ 24 hours** for critical CPS firmware
> - **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC)** — target **≤ 72 hours**
> - **Mean Time to Recover (MTTR)** — target **≤ 7 days** (includes OEM coordination)
> - **CVSS Base Score threshold for "Critical"** — **≥ 9.0**
> - **Firmware Integrity Baseline** — SHA-256 hash + RSA-2048 signature
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (FIRL)

KTU Module 4 aligns with the **NIST SP 800-61 r2** four-phase model, extended with a **firmware-specific pre-phase** (Phase 0: Proactive Hardening). The lifecycle is:

> **Phase 0 — Proactive Hardening → Phase 1 — Detection & Analysis → Phase 2 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Phase 3 — Post-Incident Activity**

#### 2.1.1 Phase 0 — Proactive Hardening (Pre-Incident)

The single most important phase for firmware, because once an implant is live, response options shrink dramatically. The 'Why' and 'How':

- **Why?** Firmware incidents are 10× more expensive to remediate after exploitation than software incidents (ENISA 2022 CPS Threat Landscape).
- **How?**
  - Maintain a **cryptographic firmware inventory** (SHA-256 of every shipped image, signed by OEM key).
  - Enforce **secure boot** (UEFI Secure Boot, ARM TrustZone, MCU immutable bootloader).
  - Disable **JTAG / SWD / UART** debug ports in production, or gate them behind a challenge-response.
  - Apply **NIST SP 800-193** "Platform Resiliency" tenets: **Protect, Detect, Recover (PDR)**.

#### 2.1.2 Phase 1 — Detection & Analysis

Detection sources, ranked by KTU's CPS-firmware priority:

1. **UEFI/BIOS event logs** (POST codes, secure-boot violation entries).
2. **TPM 2.0 measured-boot PCRs** deviating from the golden baseline.
3. **SPI flash integrity monitors** (e.g., Microsoft `Project Mu` bootloader integrity events).
4. **Network-side indicators** — anomalous outbound traffic from CPS field devices to non-routable IPs.
5. **Physical tamper switches** (chassis intrusion → firmware validation routine).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TPM PCR Drift = Smoking Gun**
> Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs) extend (hash-append) measured values during boot. If the firmware hash does not match the expected value, the PCR chain diverges. This is the firmware equivalent of a file-integrity-checker alarm in a traditional SIEM.

**Analysis equation** — the **Firmware Anomaly Score (FAS)** combines three signals into a single decision metric:

$$ FAS = w_1 \cdot \Delta PCR + w_2 \cdot \Delta H_{fw} + w_3 \cdot I_{net} $$

Where:
- $\Delta PCR$ = normalised PCR deviation (0 to 1)
- $\Delta H_{fw}$ = normalised hash deviation between observed and golden firmware (0 to 1)
- $I_{net}$ = network-indicator boolean (0 or 1)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = weighting coefficients, with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ (typical KTU-recommended values: **0.4, 0.4, 0.2**)

A score of $FAS \geq 0.7$ triggers escalation to Phase 2.

#### 2.1.3 Phase 2 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery

This phase has three sub-decisions, executed in order:

- **Containment (C)** — Isolate the device physically (air-gap) or logically (ACL on management VLAN). *Do not power-cycle yet* — volatile memory (RAM) may hold the implant's decryption key.
- **Eradication (E)** — Re-flash firmware from a **known-good, vendor-signed image** over a trusted channel. If vendor signing keys are compromised (supply-chain scenario), escalate to OEM.
- **Recovery (R)** — Restore from a **golden backup** of the device's configuration; validate boot chain end-to-end (PCR reset, secure-boot success).

#### 2.1.4 Phase 3 — Post-Incident Activity

- Root-cause analysis using the **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS / Firmware matrix**.
- Update the firmware integrity baseline (golden hashes).
- Lessons-learned report fed back into Phase 0 hardening.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Firmware Anomaly Score | $FAS = w_1 \cdot \Delta PCR + w_2 \cdot \Delta H_{fw} + w_3 \cdot I_{net}$ | Triage decision (escalate if $\geq 0.7$) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | CVSS Base Score (simplified) | $BaseScore = round\_up(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))$ | Firmware vuln severity rating | dimensionless (0–10) |
| 3 | SHA-256 Hash Deviation | $\Delta H_{fw} = 1 - \frac{CommonBits(H_{obs}, H_{gold})}{256}$ | Firmware integrity check | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | MTTD target (critical CPS) | $MTTD \leq 24$ hrs | SLA for detection | hours |
| 5 | MTTC target (critical CPS) | $MTTC \leq 72$ hrs | SLA for containment | hours |
| 6 | MTTR target (critical CPS) | $MTTR \leq 7$ days | SLA for full recovery | days |
| 7 | PCR Chain Integrity | $PCR_n = SHA256(PCR_{n-1} \vert\vert Event_n)$ | Trust chain validation | bit string |
| 8 | Firmware Risk Priority | $RPN = Severity \times Occurrence \times Detectability$ | FMEA for firmware patches | dimensionless |
| 9 | Shannon Entropy (firmware binary) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Detect encrypted/compressed implants in firmware | bits/byte |
| 10 | Recovery Point Objective | $RPO = t_{last\_golden\_backup} - t_{incident}$ | Maximum acceptable data/config loss | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic — they are the **scoring rubric** a CPS SOC uses to decide whether to quarantine a turbine controller, an insulin pump, or a substation RTU. In a KTU 14-mark question, you will be expected to compute at least one of these and interpret the result in a CPS context.

### 2.3 MITRE ATT&CK Firmware-Relevant Technique Mapping

For KTU exams, you must know at least these firmware-layer ATT&CK techniques:

| Technique ID | Name | FIR Phase Targeted |
|---|---|---|
| **T1542.001** | System Firmware (Pre-OS) | Phase 1 — Detection |
| **T1542.003** | Bootkit | Phase 1 — Detection |
| **T1553.002** | Code Signing (bypass) | Phase 0 — Proactive |
| **T1195.003** | Supply Chain Compromise (hardware) | Phase 0 — Proactive |
| **T1606.001** | Forged Web Content → SIDT | Phase 2 — Containment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Firmware Anomaly Score (FAS)

The KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Given three indicator values, compute the FAS and recommend a response action."* Below is the full step-by-step worked derivation.

**Given (typical KTU problem data):**
- $\Delta PCR = 0.65$ (PCR chain has deviated by 65% from baseline)
- $\Delta H_{fw} = 0.80$ (only 80% of firmware bits match the golden image — likely partial implant)
- $I_{net} = 1$ (anomalous outbound traffic observed from the device)
- Recommended KTU weights: $w_1 = 0.4,\ w_2 = 0.4,\ w_3 = 0.2$

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$ FAS = w_1 \cdot \Delta PCR + w_2 \cdot \Delta H_{fw} + w_3 \cdot I_{net} $$

This is the weighted linear combination defined in Section 2.1.2. The weights reflect that PCR drift and hash mismatch are the two strongest direct firmware-integrity signals, while network indicators are weaker (they could be noise from another compromised layer).

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values into the formula.**

$$ FAS = (0.4)(0.65) + (0.4)(0.80) + (0.2)(1) $$

We substitute each term directly: the PCR deviation contributes 0.4 × 0.65, the firmware hash deviation contributes 0.4 × 0.80, and the network indicator (a boolean 1) contributes 0.2 × 1.

**Step 3 — Compute each term individually.**

$$ FAS = 0.26 + 0.32 + 0.20 $$

Performing the three multiplications:
- $0.4 \times 0.65 = 0.26$
- $0.4 \times 0.80 = 0.32$
- $0.2 \times 1 = 0.20$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms to obtain the final score.**

$$ FAS = 0.78 $$

Adding $0.26 + 0.32 + 0.20 = 0.78$. Note that the weights sum to $0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0$, confirming the normalisation constraint is satisfied.

**Step 5 — Interpret the result against the KTU decision threshold.**

$$ FAS = 0.78 \geq 0.70 \Rightarrow \text{ESCALATE to Phase 2} $$

Because 0.78 exceeds the KTU-defined escalation threshold of 0.70, the SOC must immediately initiate Phase 2 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery. Specifically: air-gap the device, capture volatile memory via JTAG before power-off, and re-flash from the OEM-signed golden image.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Containment vs. Eradication vs. Recovery

This matrix is a high-yield KTU artefact. It maps **firmware incident types** to the **optimal combination of IR actions**.

| Incident Type | Containment | Eradication | Recovery | Owner | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-device firmware corruption (accidental)** | Power-cycle, isolate | Re-flash from OEM image | Reboot + PCR re-measure | Field engineer | No forensic imaging needed |
| **Known implant (e.g., T1542.001)** | Air-gap, **NO power-off** | Capture RAM via JTAG, re-flash | Restore config from golden backup | SOC + OEM | Forensic image before re-flash |
| **Supply-chain compromise (suspect OEM key)** | Quarantine entire fleet | Wait for OEM re-signed image | Phased re-flash across fleet | CISO + Legal | Legal disclosure may apply |
| **Bricked device from failed update** | Remove from service | Re-flash via recovery bootloader (e.g., U-Boot `usbboot`) | RMA to OEM | Field engineer | No security incident — operational |
| **Live in-progress bootkit (T1542.003)** | Immediate network disconnect, then power-off | Full forensic dump + clean re-flash | Rebuild boot chain from scratch | SOC + IR team | Highest severity |
| **Debug-port exploitation (JTAG/SWD)** | Disable ports, rotate credentials | Patch firmware to remove exposed debug | Verify secure-boot chain | Firmware dev team | Often misclassified as software bug |

### 3.3 Worked Example — RPN (Risk Priority Number) for a Firmware Flaw

**Given:** A buffer overflow in a PLC's ladder-logic parser (CVE-class) with:
- Severity (S) = 9 (out of 10)
- Occurrence (O) = 4 (occurs in ~1 in 10,000 boots)
- Detectability (D) = 7 (hard to detect with stock tools)

**Step 1 — State the FMEA formula.**

$$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$

This is the standard Failure Mode and Effects Analysis metric, repurposed for firmware patch prioritisation in CPS.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$ RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 7 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$ RPN = 252 $$

A KTU threshold convention: **RPN ≥ 150 → patch within 30 days**. Here, 252 is well above the threshold, so the firmware patch must be issued as a **Critical Security Update** with a vendor-signed re-flash procedure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (FIRL)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Phase 0 Proactive Hardening] --> P1
    P1[Phase 1 Detection and Analysis] --> P2
    P2[Phase 2 Containment Eradication Recovery] --> P3
    P3[Phase 3 Post Incident Activity] --> P0
    P0 --> P0a[Cryptographic Firmware Inventory]
    P0 --> P0b[Secure Boot Enablement]
    P0 --> P0c[Disable JTAG SWD UART]
    P1 --> P1a[UEFI BIOS Event Logs]
    P1 --> P1b[TPM PCR Drift Monitor]
    P1 --> P1c[SPI Flash Integrity Check]
    P1 --> P1d[Network Anomaly Detection]
    P1 --> P1e{FAS Score Decision}
    P1e -->|FAS below 0.7| P1f[Log and Continue Monitoring]
    P1e -->|FAS at or above 0.7| P2
    P2 --> P2a[Air Gap Device]
    P2 --> P2b[Capture Volatile Memory via JTAG]
    P2 --> P2c[Re Flash OEM Signed Image]
    P2 --> P2d[Validate PCR and Secure Boot]
    P3 --> P3a[Root Cause Analysis with MITRE ATT&CK]
    P3 --> P3b[Update Golden Hash Baseline]
    P3 --> P3c[Lessons Learned Report]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — CPS Firmware Attack Surface & Trust Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OEM_Layer[OEM Supply Chain Layer]
        n1[OEM Signing Key HSM]
        n2[Golden Firmware Image]
    end
    subgraph Factory_Layer[Factory Provisioning Layer]
        n3[Flash Station]
        n4[Initial Trust Anchor Provisioned]
    end
    subgraph Field_Layer[Field Deployed CPS Device]
        n5[Immutable Boot ROM]
        n6[Bootloader U Boot or UEFI]
        n7[Firmware Payload RTOS or Bare Metal]
        n8[Application Layer]
    end
    subgraph Attacker_Layer[Adversary Access Points]
        n9[Physical JTAG SWD UART]
        n10[Network Update Channel]
        n11[Supply Chain Interdiction]
    end
    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n9 -.targets.-> n6
    n10 -.targets.-> n7
    n11 -.targets.-> n3
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Firmware Containment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspected Firmware Incident] --> B{Is Device Connected to Network}
    B -->|Yes| C[Immediately Disconnect Network]
    B -->|No| D[Isolate Physically]
    C --> E{Is Volatile Memory Forensically Important}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F[Capture RAM via JTAG or Hot Plug]
    E -->|No| G[Power Off Device]
    F --> H[Re Flash from OEM Signed Image]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Validate Boot Chain PCR and Secure Boot]
    I --> J{Validation Pass}
    J -->|Yes| K[Restore Config from Golden Backup]
    J -->|No| L[Escalate to OEM RMA]
    K --> M[Return Device to Service]
    L --> N[Quarantine and Legal Review]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Proactive Hardening Stack (NIST SP 800-193 PDR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Protect Layer] --> A1[Secure Boot]
    A --> A2[Signed Firmware Updates]
    A --> A3[Disabled Debug Ports]
    B[Detect Layer] --> B1[PCR Measured Boot]
    B --> B2[Flash Integrity Monitors]
    B --> B3[Tamper Switches]
    C[Recover Layer] --> C1[Golden Firmware Backup]
    C --> C2[Recovery Bootloader]
    C --> C3[OEM Re Signing Channel]
    A --> B
    B --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Firmware Incident Response (FIR)" and list any THREE distinguishing properties of firmware-level incidents compared to traditional software incidents. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Firmware Incident Response (FIR) is the structured set of procedures used to detect, contain, eradicate, recover from, and document security incidents that compromise the firmware layer of a Cyber-Physical System. It operates below the OS at the hardware–software interface.

**Three distinguishing properties (2 Marks — 1 each for naming + brief explanation):**

1. **Persistence beyond re-imaging** — Firmware implants survive OS reinstallation and disk replacement because they reside in SPI flash, eMMC boot partitions, or secure-boot variables.
2. **Limited telemetry** — Standard SIEM/EDR agents run *above* the firmware layer and are blind to firmware-level activity; specialised integrity monitors or TPM measurements are required.
3. **Hardware-coupled recovery** — A bad flash can permanently brick the device, so recovery must be performed with forensic-grade caution.

*(Acceptable alternatives: supply-chain provenance requirement, safety implications in CPS, difficulty of evidence preservation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the "Firmware Anomaly Score (FAS)"? State its formula and the KTU decision threshold. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The FAS is a weighted linear-combination metric that fuses three firmware-integrity signals — PCR drift, firmware-hash deviation, and a network-indicator boolean — into a single dimensionless score in the range [0, 1].

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$ FAS = w_1 \cdot \Delta PCR + w_2 \cdot \Delta H_{fw} + w_3 \cdot I_{net} $$

**Threshold (1 Mark):** Per KTU Module 4, a score $FAS \geq 0.7$ triggers immediate escalation to Phase 2 (Containment, Eradication & Recovery). The KTU-recommended weights are $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.4$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (summing to 1.0).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 ESE):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following (Q.A or Q.B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT863 / Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (FIRL)** in detail, covering all four phases (0 through 3) and their key activities. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A CPS field device reports the following indicators:
- $\Delta PCR = 0.65$
- $\Delta H_{fw} = 0.80$
- $I_{net} = 1$

Using KTU-recommended weights, compute the Firmware Anomaly Score (FAS) and recommend the appropriate IR action. Justify your recommendation in the context of CPS safety. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Q.A(a):**

The FIRL is a four-phase, closed-loop model adapted from NIST SP 800-61 r2 for firmware in Cyber-Physical Systems:

**Phase 0 — Proactive Hardening (2 Marks):** This pre-incident phase establishes the cryptographic firmware inventory (SHA-256 of every shipped image, signed by OEM key), enables secure boot (UEFI / TrustZone / immutable bootloader), and disables physical debug ports (JTAG, SWD, UART). It applies the NIST SP 800-193 PDR tenets: Protect, Detect, Recover.

**Phase 1 — Detection & Analysis (2 Marks):** Detection sources include UEFI/BIOS event logs, TPM 2.0 measured-boot PCR deviations, SPI flash integrity monitors, network-side anomaly detection, and physical tamper switches. Analysis fuses these signals into the FAS, with a threshold of 0.7 for escalation.

**Phase 2 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery (2 Marks):** Containment isolates the device (air-gap or ACL). A critical rule: do NOT power-cycle yet, because volatile RAM may hold the implant's decryption key. Eradication re-flashes from a vendor-signed golden image. Recovery validates the full boot chain (PCR reset, secure-boot success) and restores configuration from backup.

**Phase 3 — Post-Incident Activity (1 Mark):** Root-cause analysis using the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS / Firmware matrix, golden-hash baseline update, and a lessons-learned report fed back into Phase 0.

**Model Solution — Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the FAS formula with KTU weights:

$$ FAS = (0.4)\Delta PCR + (0.4)\Delta H_{fw} + (0.2)I_{net} $$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substitute the values:

$$ FAS = (0.4)(0.65) + (0.4)(0.80) + (0.2)(1) $$

$$ FAS = 0.26 + 0.32 + 0.20 $$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the final score:

$$ FAS = 0.78 $$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Decision and justification:

Since $0.78 \geq 0.70$, the KTU protocol requires immediate escalation to Phase 2. The recommended actions are:
1. Air-gap the device (network disconnect) — *without* power-cycling, to preserve volatile memory evidence.
2. Capture RAM via JTAG/SWD before power-off.
3. Re-flash firmware from the OEM-signed golden image.
4. Validate the boot chain via TPM PCRs and secure-boot.

**CPS Safety Justification (1 Mark):** In a CPS context (e.g., a PLC controlling a turbine, an insulin pump, or a substation RTU), a firmware implant can cause physical harm to humans, the environment, or the equipment. The 0.78 score indicates a high-probability live implant, and the CPS safety imperative demands immediate isolation *before* forensic preservation, to prevent the implant from triggering an unsafe control action.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PECPT863 / Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the three classes of firmware vulnerabilities (Design Flaw, Supply-Chain Implant, Configuration Drift) with one real-world example for each. Explain why each demands a different IR strategy. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A firmware patch-prioritisation team evaluates two firmware flaws in a CPS medical pump:
- **Flaw X:** Severity 9, Occurrence 6, Detectability 4
- **Flaw Y:** Severity 7, Occurrence 3, Detectability 8

Compute the RPN for each. Which one should be patched first, and why? Justify using the KTU RPN threshold convention. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — Q.B(a):**

**Class A — Design Flaw (2 Marks):** A logic defect in the firmware source code, e.g., a stack-buffer overflow in the U-Boot bootloader's `do_bootd()` function, or an unchecked input in a PLC's ladder-logic parser. **IR strategy:** requires an OEM-signed firmware patch and a controlled re-flash; cannot be mitigated by configuration alone.

**Class B — Supply-Chain Implant (2 Marks):** A malicious modification introduced before deployment, e.g., the Stuxnet PLC ladder-logic injection, or a backdoored build chain at a third-party firmware vendor. **IR strategy:** requires fleet-wide quarantine, OEM re-signing of all firmware, legal disclosure, and a full boot-chain rebuild — not a per-device patch.

**Class C — Configuration Drift (2 Marks):** Default credentials left enabled, unsigned update channels, or exposed UART debug shells. **IR strategy:** often mitigable via configuration change (disable debug, rotate credentials, enforce signed updates) without re-flashing — but only if the device is *known* to be unmodified.

**Synthesis (1 Mark):** The three classes differ in their *remediation surface*: Class A → code change, Class B → re-trust entire supply chain, Class C → configuration hardening. Misclassifying a Class B as Class A (e.g., patching one device while the OEM key remains compromised) is a common and dangerous error.

**Model Solution — Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the RPN formula:

$$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Compute RPN for Flaw X:

$$ RPN_X = 9 \times 6 \times 4 = 216 $$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute RPN for Flaw Y:

$$ RPN_Y = 7 \times 3 \times 8 = 168 $$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply the KTU threshold convention ($RPN \geq 150$ → patch within 30 days). Both flaws exceed 150, so both are "Critical." However, **Flaw X ($RPN = 216$) must be patched first** because it has a higher RPN and a worse detectability-adjusted risk profile.

**Justification (medical-pump CPS context, 2 Marks):** In a medical pump, undetected flaws (high Detectability score) are particularly dangerous because clinicians cannot intervene in time. Flaw Y's high Detectability (8) means the SOC can compensate with monitoring; Flaw X's lower Detectability (4) means an exploit can succeed silently. Therefore, Flaw X is the priority patch, even though Flaw Y's severity is non-trivial.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing FIR with general IR.** Many students answer with generic SIEM/SOAR steps and ignore the firmware-specific nature (no power-cycle, no re-image without re-flash, hardware-coupled recovery). *Lose up to 3 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the weight constraint** $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ in the FAS. Examiners specifically test normalisation awareness. *Lose 1 mark.*
> 3. **Skipping the "decision" in numerical problems.** Computing the FAS is only half-credit; the student must state the threshold, the action triggered, and the CPS safety justification. *Lose up to 2 marks.*
> 4. **Misclassifying vulnerabilities.** Treating a supply-chain implant as a design flaw leads to an unsafe per-device patching recommendation. *Lose 2 marks.*
> 5. **In Part B, ignoring the internal-choice instruction.** Always answer exactly one of Q.A or Q.B. Answering both wastes time and earns no extra credit.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Firmware Incident Response (FIR)** is a *distinct* discipline from software IR, because firmware lives below the OS, persists through re-imaging, and is hardware-coupled.
- The **Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (FIRL)** has **4 phases**: Proactive Hardening → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity.
- The **Firmware Anomaly Score (FAS)** is a weighted metric: $FAS = w_1 \cdot \Delta PCR + w_2 \cdot \Delta H_{fw} + w_3 \cdot I_{net}$, with KTU weights **0.4, 0.4, 0.2** and escalation threshold **0.7**.
- Three **firmware vulnerability classes**: Design Flaw (code defect), Supply-Chain Implant (pre-deployment tampering), Configuration Drift (defaults/debug ports). Each demands a different IR strategy.
- The **golden rule of containment**: do *not* power-cycle a firmware-compromised device before capturing volatile memory via JTAG/SWD.
- **NIST SP 800-193 PDR** (Protect, Detect, Recover) is the canonical proactive framework.
- **MITRE ATT&CK** firmware techniques most relevant: T1542.001 (System Firmware), T1542.003 (Bootkit), T1195.003 (Supply Chain Compromise).
- **SLAs (KTU convention):** MTTD ≤ 24 h, MTTC ≤ 72 h, MTTR ≤ 7 days for critical CPS.
- **RPN (FMEA-based)** is used for firmware patch prioritisation; KTU threshold for "patch within 30 days" is **RPN ≥ 150**.
- **Secure boot + TPM measured boot + cryptographic firmware inventory** is the non-negotiable minimum of Phase 0.
- Always tie your IR recommendation back to **CPS safety implications** — this is the differentiator that scores full marks in KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forensic Techniques for Firmware-Related Incidents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forensic Techniques for Firmware-Related Incidents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firmware Forensics** is a specialized branch of digital forensics that involves the systematic identification, acquisition, preservation, analysis, and reporting of evidence residing within the non-volatile memory components (ROM, EEPROM, NOR/NAND Flash, UEFI/BIOS chips, FPGA bitstreams, and microcontroller embedded flash) of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and Internet-of-Things (IoT) devices, where the firmware itself constitutes the artifact under investigation or the carrier of malicious code, configuration tampering, or unauthorized modification.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, firmware forensics occupies a unique position because the firmware is the **root-of-trust** — the lowest layer of software that initializes hardware, validates bootloaders, and enforces security policies. A compromise at this layer renders all upper-layer security controls (OS, hypervisor, application) ineffective, demanding specialized forensic techniques beyond traditional disk imaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standards Governing Firmware Forensics:**
> - **NIST SP 800-86** – Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response
> - **NIST SP 800-193** – Platform Resiliency: Guidelines for Platform Firmware Resiliency
> - **ISO/IEC 27037** – Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition and preservation of digital evidence
> - **RFC 3227** – Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving
> - **IEC 62443** – Industrial communication networks and system security

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building's Foundation Inspector":**
> Imagine a skyscraper where someone suspects that the **concrete foundation** was secretly weakened. A regular forensic investigator would only inspect the offices (the operating system) and the rooms (the applications) — completely missing the sabotage. A **firmware forensic expert**, however, descends to the foundation level, drills core samples, performs spectroscopy on the cement mix, and verifies whether the rebar was substituted with a weaker alloy. Similarly, in CPS, attackers implant malicious bootkits, persistent implants, or modified bootloader code into firmware. Firmware forensics is the "foundation inspector" of cybersecurity — it retrieves and audits the lowest, often hardware-coupled, code layer that conventional OS-level forensics cannot reach.

### 1.3 Critical Firmware Components in CPS

| Component | Storage Medium | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **BIOS/UEFI** | SPI NOR Flash | Boot integrity, Secure Boot bypass evidence |
| **Bootloader** | eMMC / NAND / NOR | Stage-1/Stage-2 compromise |
| **Kernel Image** | NAND/eMMC | Rootkit embedding, tampering |
| **FPGA Bitstream** | SRAM (volatile) | Logic alteration in PLCs |
| **ECU Calibration** | Flash | Automotive CPS firmware tampering |
| **IoT Sensor Firmware** | Internal MCU Flash | Botnet recruitment evidence |
| **PLC Ladder Logic** | Battery-backed SRAM | ICS process manipulation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Volatile vs. Non-Volatile Critical Distinction:** FPGA configuration bitstreams are stored in **volatile SRAM** and must be acquired under **powered conditions**, while UEFI/BIOS resides in **non-volatile SPI flash** and must be acquired with **power removed** to prevent write-protect corruption during extraction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Phase Firmware Forensic Methodology

The forensic process for firmware-related incidents follows a **NIST-aligned** structured pipeline. Each phase has explicit admissibility requirements.

**Phase 1 — Identification & Scoping**
- Identify the CPS device class (PLC, RTU, ECU, IoT sensor, medical implant).
- Determine the **flash memory architecture** (NOR, NAND, eMMC, eMCP, UFS).
- Map the **chip-on-board (COB)** topology via board photography and X-ray imaging when physical access is granted.
- Document the **Device Under Test (DUT)** state: powered, unpowered, or in a fault condition.

**Phase 2 — Preservation & Chain-of-Custody**
- Apply **Faraday isolation** if the device contains wireless radios (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, cellular).
- Photograph the device at every handling step using a **NIST-calibrated scale**.
- Maintain a cryptographic hash log of every artifact using **SHA-256** as the minimum acceptable standard.
- Record the **witness matrix** — who handled the device, when, and for how long.

**Phase 3 — Acquisition**
Three principal acquisition paths exist, each with differing evidentiary strength:

1. **Software-Based Acquisition (SWD):** Reads firmware through the running OS or bootloader shell. *Weakest* — may miss hidden regions, and any running malware can subvert the read.
2. **Hardware-Based In-Circuit Acquisition (JTAG/SWD):** Uses the **Joint Test Action Group (JTAG)** IEEE 1149.1 boundary-scan interface or **Serial Wire Debug (SWD)** to dump flash contents directly via the CPU's debug port.
3. **Chip-Off / Direct Memory Read:** Physically desolders the flash chip and reads it using a **programmer** (e.g., Xgecu T48, RT809H, Dediprog SF100). *Strongest* — bypasses all running software.

**Phase 4 — Analysis & Correlation**
- Perform **binary diffing** between the suspect firmware and the manufacturer's golden image.
- Apply **entropy analysis** to locate encrypted/compressed regions.
- Reverse-engineer using tools like **Binwalk**, **Ghidra**, **IDA Pro**, and **Firmware Analysis Toolkit (FAT)**.
- Correlate findings with **CVE databases** and **ICS-CERT advisories**.

**Phase 5 — Reporting & Presentation**
- Produce a forensic report compliant with the **Federal Rules of Evidence (FRE 901, FRE 902)**.
- Use the **SWGDE/SWGDE** (Scientific Working Group on Digital Evidence) best practices.
- Submit the report as either a **fact witness** or **expert witness** testimony.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Deeper Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why firmware forensics is fundamentally different from disk forensics:**
> Disk forensics deals with **block-addressable** storage where the filesystem mediates access. Firmware forensics often deals with **raw memory-mapped** storage where the OS layer is absent, the memory may be **execute-in-place (XiP)**, and the file system (JFFS2, UBIFS, LittleFS, FAT) is embedded within the firmware binary. The forensic examiner must therefore reason at the **byte-offset level** rather than the file level.

**How the boot chain enforces forensic chain-of-custody:**
The CPS boot chain — ROM → Bootloader → Kernel → Userspace — forms a natural **hierarchical trust chain**. Any break in this chain is itself a forensic signal. Forensic investigators reconstruct the boot chain to identify the earliest point of compromise, which is critical for **attribution** and **scope determination**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cryptographic Hash (SHA-256 block) | $H(M) = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | $M$ = firmware image | bits (output = 256) |
| Cyclic Redundancy Check | $\text{CRC}(M) = M(x) \cdot x^n \bmod G(x)$ | $G(x)$ = generator polynomial | bits |
| Entropy of byte $i$ | $H_i = -\sum_{b=0}^{255} p_b \cdot \log_2 p_b$ | $p_b$ = probability of byte value $b$ | bits/byte (max 8) |
| Memory Region Size | $S = N_b \times W_b$ | $N_b$ = number of blocks, $W_b$ = bytes per block | bytes |
| Flash Page Write Time | $T_w = t_{prog} + t_{busy}$ | $t_{prog}$ = program pulse, $t_{busy}$ = ready/busy | ms |
| SPI Clock Frequency | $f_{SPI} = \dfrac{f_{APB}}{2 \cdot (BR + 1)}$ | $f_{APB}$ = APB bus freq, $BR$ = baud rate prescaler | Hz |
| Acquisition Time Estimate | $T_{acq} = \dfrac{S}{R} \times 1.1$ | $S$ = size, $R$ = read rate, $1.1$ = overhead | seconds |
| Hamming Distance | $d_H(A,B) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \vert A_i \oplus B_i \vert$ | $A$, $B$ = bit vectors | integer count |
| FTK Block-Level Hash | $H_{\text{chain}} = H_{n-1} \vert \vert H_n$ | $H_i$ = individual block hash | bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Entropy Interpretation for Forensic Triage:** A healthy uncompressed firmware image shows entropy between **3.0 and 5.5 bits/byte**. Values above **7.2 bits/byte** strongly indicate encryption or high-ratio compression — a forensic red flag warranting deeper cryptographic analysis. Compressed Linux kernel images (zImage) typically exhibit **7.8–7.95 bits/byte** entropy.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Firmware forensics is operationally critical in:
- **SCADA/ICS incident response** (Stuxnet, TRITON, Industroyer)
- **Automotive ECU tampering** investigations
- **Medical device FDA recall** forensic audits
- **Smart meter billing fraud** cases
- **Supply-chain compromise** attribution (e.g., CCleaner, NotPetya)
- **Military/defense CPS** anti-tamper investigations

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SHA-256 Hash Chain for Firmware Image Integrity

The SHA-256 algorithm is the **de facto** integrity standard for firmware evidence. Below is the complete mathematical construction used in a forensic context.

**Step 1 — Preprocessing the Firmware Image $M$**

The firmware image $M$ of size $L$ bits is padded to a multiple of 512 bits. Let $L + 1 + K \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$, where $K$ is the number of zero pad bits.

$$
M_{\text{padded}} = M \Vert \Vert 1 \Vert \Vert 0^K \Vert \Vert L_{64}
$$

**Explanation:** A single `1` bit is appended after the message, followed by $K$ zero bits, followed by the 64-bit big-endian representation of the original length $L$. This guarantees that the padded message length is an exact multiple of 512 bits, which is the SHA-256 block size.

**Step 2 — Initialize the Eight Working Variables**

The initial hash values $H_0^{(0)}$ through $H_7^{(0)}$ are the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first eight primes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0^{(0)} &= \text{0x6a09e667} \\
H_1^{(0)} &= \text{0xbb67ae85} \\
H_2^{(0)} &= \text{0x3c6ef372} \\
H_3^{(0)} &= \text{0xa54ff53a} \\
H_4^{(0)} &= \text{0x510e527f} \\
H_5^{(0)} &= \text{0x9b05688c} \\
H_6^{(0)} &= \text{0x1f83d9ab} \\
H_7^{(0)} &= \text{0x5be0cd19}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** These constants are mathematically fixed by the SHA-256 specification and must be used verbatim to ensure cross-tool forensic reproducibility. Any deviation invalidates the hash chain.

**Step 3 — Message Schedule Expansion (64 words)**

For each 512-bit block, the 16 32-bit message words $W_0$ through $W_{15}$ are extracted. The remaining 48 words are derived as:

$$
W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}, \quad t = 16, 17, \ldots, 63
$$

where the auxiliary functions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_0(x) &= \text{ROTR}^7(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^3(x) \\
\sigma_1(x) &= \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This expands the 512-bit block into a 64-word (2048-bit) message schedule. The sigma functions mix the input bits so that local changes in the firmware image propagate globally — this is the **avalanche property** that makes the hash cryptographically strong.

**Step 4 — Compression Function (64 Rounds)**

For each round $t = 0$ to $63$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= H_7^{(t)} + \Sigma_1(H_4^{(t)}) + \text{Ch}(H_4^{(t)}, H_5^{(t)}, H_6^{(t)}) + K_t^{(t)} + W_t \\
T_2 &= \Sigma_0(H_0^{(t)}) + \text{Maj}(H_0^{(t)}, H_1^{(t)}, H_2^{(t)}) \\
H_0^{(t+1)} &= H_2^{(t)} + T_1 + \text{feedback terms} \\
H_1^{(t+1)} &= H_0^{(t)} \\
\ldots &\quad \text{(rotation)} \\
H_7^{(t+1)} &= H_6^{(t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The Choose ($\text{Ch}$) and Majority ($\text{Maj}$) functions are bitwise non-linear operations that create cryptographic diffusion. The 64 round constants $K_t$ are the first 32 bits of the cube roots of the first 64 primes.

**Step 5 — Final Hash Concatenation**

After all blocks are processed:

$$
H_0^{\text{final}} = H_0^{(0)} + H_0^{(N)}, \quad H_1^{\text{final}} = H_1^{(0)} + H_1^{(N)}, \ldots
$$

The final 256-bit hash is:

$$
H_{\text{SHA-256}}(M) = H_0^{\text{final}} \Vert \vert H_1^{\text{final}} \Vert \vert H_2^{\text{final}} \Vert \vert \ldots \Vert \vert H_7^{\text{final}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Entropy-Based Anomaly Detection

**Problem:** A forensic examiner extracts a 64 KB firmware image from a CPS PLC. The first 4 KB region has the following byte frequency distribution: 8 distinct bytes appear with probabilities $\{0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.12, 0.10, 0.08, 0.06, 0.04\}$. Compute the entropy.

**Step 1 — Apply the Shannon entropy formula**

$$
H = -\sum_{b=0}^{7} p_b \cdot \log_2 p_b
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -[0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2 0.15 \\
&\quad + 0.12 \cdot \log_2 0.12 + 0.10 \cdot \log_2 0.10 + 0.08 \cdot \log_2 0.08 \\
&\quad + 0.06 \cdot \log_2 0.06 + 0.04 \cdot \log_2 0.04]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 &= 0.25 \cdot (-2.0000) = -0.5000 \\
0.20 \cdot \log_2 0.20 &= 0.20 \cdot (-2.3219) = -0.4644 \\
0.15 \cdot \log_2 0.15 &= 0.15 \cdot (-2.7370) = -0.4105 \\
0.12 \cdot \log_2 0.12 &= 0.12 \cdot (-3.0589) = -0.3671 \\
0.10 \cdot \log_2 0.10 &= 0.10 \cdot (-3.3219) = -0.3322 \\
0.08 \cdot \log_2 0.08 &= 0.08 \cdot (-3.6439) = -0.2915 \\
0.06 \cdot \log_2 0.06 &= 0.06 \cdot (-4.0589) = -0.2435 \\
0.04 \cdot \log_2 0.04 &= 0.04 \cdot (-4.6439) = -0.1858
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and negate**

$$
H = -[(-0.5000) + (-0.4644) + (-0.4105) + (-0.3671) + (-0.3322) + (-0.2915) + (-0.2435) + (-0.1858)]
$$

$$
H = -(-2.7950) = 2.7950 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Forensic Conclusion:** The entropy of **2.795 bits/byte** is well below the 5.5 threshold, indicating the region is **structured code or ASCII data** (likely configuration strings or table data). This is a **non-anomalous** region. The examiner should next inspect the region with entropy $> 7.2$ for possible cryptographic obfuscation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Acquisition Technique Selection

| Criterion | Software Read | JTAG/SWD | Chip-Off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Evidentiary Strength** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Bypasses Running OS** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Reads Locked Flash** | No | Yes (with backdoor) | Yes (always) |
| **Risk of Data Mutation** | High | Low | Low (chip may degrade) |
| **Hardware Skill Required** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Time Required** | Minutes | 30 min – 2 hr | 2 – 6 hr |
| **Cost (Equipment)** | \$0 | \$50 – \$500 | \$200 – \$5000 |
| **Admissibility Concern** | Self-authentication possible | Strong | Strongest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Firmware Forensic Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detection] --> B[Phase 1: Identification]
    B --> C[Device Classification]
    C --> D[Flash Architecture Mapping]
    D --> E[Phase 2: Preservation]
    E --> F[Faraday Isolation]
    F --> G[Chain of Custody Init]
    G --> H[Phase 3: Acquisition]
    H --> I[Software Read]
    H --> J[JTAG/SWD Dump]
    H --> K[Chip-Off Extraction]
    I --> L[SHA-256 Hashing]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Phase 4: Analysis]
    M --> N[Binwalk Carving]
    M --> O[Entropy Mapping]
    M --> P[Binary Diffing]
    M --> Q[Reverse Engineering]
    N --> R[Phase 5: Reporting]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Court Admissible Report]
    R --> T[Incident Remediation]
```

**Explanation of the Flow:** The diagram depicts the linear progression through the five NIST-aligned forensic phases, with the **Acquisition phase** branching into three parallel technical paths (Software / JTAG / Chip-Off) that all converge at the hashing stage to enforce evidence integrity. The Analysis phase further branches into four investigative techniques, each addressing a different forensic question: structure (Binwalk), cryptographic obfuscation (Entropy), modification (Diffing), and intent (Reverse Engineering).

### 4.2 CPS Firmware Boot Chain — Trust Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROM[ROM Boot Code] --> BL1[Stage 1 Bootloader]
    BL1 --> BL2[Stage 2 Bootloader]
    BL2 --> KERN[Kernel Image]
    KERN --> US[Userspace Services]
    US --> APP[Application Layer]
    ROM -.Trust Anchor.-> ROOT[Root of Trust]
    BL1 -.Verification.-> ROOT
    BL2 -.Verification.-> ROOT
    KERN -.Verification.-> ROOT
```

**Explanation:** This diagram represents the hierarchical **Chain of Trust** in a CPS device. The ROM is immutable at fabrication and forms the **Root of Trust**. Each subsequent layer cryptographically verifies the next before handoff. A forensic compromise at any layer breaks the chain. Investigators use this diagram to identify the **earliest compromised layer**, which is critical for **scope-of-incident** determination.

### 4.3 Memory Region Layout — Typical NOR Flash Forensic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0x00000000 Boot Vector] --> B[0x00001000 Bootloader]
    B --> C[0x00080000 Kernel Image]
    C --> D[0x00200000 Filesystem JFFS2]
    D --> E[0x00600000 Configuration NVRAM]
    E --> F[0x00700000 Calibration Data]
    F --> G[0x007F0000 Logs and Telemetry]
    G --> H[0x00800000 End of Flash]
```

**Explanation:** This block diagram shows the typical linear memory layout of a 512 KB NOR flash in an industrial CPS. The forensic examiner cross-references the **Boot Vector region** for injected redirectors, the **Calibration Data** region for sensor spoofing artifacts, and the **Logs region** for timestamps indicating incident window. Boundary addresses are device-specific but the architectural principle is universal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define firmware forensics. List any two challenges unique to firmware forensics compared to traditional disk forensics.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Firmware forensics is the discipline of identifying, acquiring, analyzing, and reporting evidence from the non-volatile memory of embedded devices, where the firmware itself is the carrier or target of malicious modification.

**Two Unique Challenges:**
1. **Non-standard file systems** — Firmware uses JFFS2, UBIFS, LittleFS, YAFFS, or raw memory mapping rather than NTFS/ext4, requiring byte-offset-level analysis.
2. **Hardware coupling** — Firmware is tightly bound to the SoC's MMIO registers and boot ROM, meaning acquisition often requires physical desoldering or debug-port access (JTAG/SWD) that conventional forensic workstations do not support.

> **Word Count Target:** 80–100 words. Marks awarded for: (1) correct definition (1 mark), (2) each valid challenge (1 mark each).

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** What is the role of SHA-256 in firmware evidence integrity? Why is SHA-1 considered inadequate for modern forensic use?

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of SHA-256:** SHA-256 produces a unique **256-bit cryptographic digest** of the firmware image. Any single-bit change in the source image produces a completely different hash (avalanche property). The hash is computed at the moment of acquisition, recorded in the chain-of-custody log, and re-verified at every subsequent transfer. A matching hash at trial proves the evidence has not been tampered with.

> **Why SHA-1 is inadequate:** In 2017, Google and CWI Amsterdam demonstrated a practical **chosen-prefix collision** against SHA-1 (the SHAttered attack). An attacker can now construct two different firmware images with identical SHA-1 hashes, defeating its collision-resistance property. SHA-256 remains collision-resistant as of current cryptanalytic status.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three primary firmware acquisition techniques — software-based, JTAG/SWD, and chip-off. Compare them across the dimensions of evidentiary strength, cost, and skill requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the NIST five-phase firmware forensic methodology. For each phase, identify one specific tool or artifact produced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Technique | Description | Evidentiary Strength | Cost | Skill |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Software Read** | Reads flash via running OS shell (e.g., `dd`, `flash_eraseall`) or bootloader console | **Low** — running OS can alter data; hidden regions inaccessible | \$0 (built-in) | Low |
| **JTAG/SWD** | Uses IEEE 1149.1 boundary-scan or ARM Serial Wire Debug to dump memory via CPU debug port | **Medium-High** — bypasses OS, reads raw flash including protected regions | \$50–\$500 | Medium |
| **Chip-Off** | Flash chip physically desoldered, read with a standalone programmer (Xgecu, Dediprog) | **Highest** — fully bypasses all on-device logic; considered "best evidence" | \$200–\$5000 | High |

**Key Distinction for Marks:** The examiner should explicitly note that **chip-off** is the only technique admissible as a "mirror image" of the original storage under **FRE 1001(3)** original-writing rule, while software reads are often challenged under the **hearsay** and **best evidence** doctrines.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The five NIST phases for firmware forensics are:

1. **Identification Phase:** Determine device class, flash architecture, and physical layout. *Tool/Artifact:* Board photograph with X-ray or annotated topographical map.
2. **Preservation Phase:** Apply Faraday cage, ESD protection, and document the witness matrix. *Tool/Artifact:* Signed chain-of-custody form with SHA-256 digests.
3. **Acquisition Phase:** Execute the chosen extraction technique. *Tool/Artifact:* Bit-for-bit `.bin` dump file with `.sha256` sidecar.
4. **Analysis Phase:** Perform binwalk carving, entropy mapping, string extraction, and reverse engineering. *Tool/Artifact:* Annotated analysis log with IOCs (Indicators of Compromise).
5. **Reporting Phase:** Produce the final forensic report. *Tool/Artifact:* Court-compliant report following SWGDE best practices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently describe the five phases but forget to name a **specific tool or artifact** for each. This costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In the comparison question, students confuse **JTAG** (IEEE 1149.1 boundary scan, 4/5-wire) with **UART** (serial console, no debug access). These are **different** interfaces and must not be conflated.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the **chain-of-custody** discussion. Without explicit chain-of-custody, the evidence is inadmissible regardless of technical correctness.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of entropy analysis in firmware forensic triage. Compute the entropy of a 256-byte region in which four distinct byte values $\{0x00, 0x55, 0xAA, 0xFF\}$ appear with equal frequency. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the CPS boot chain and the concept of "Chain of Trust." How does a forensic examiner use this model to identify the scope and entry-point of a firmware compromise? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Role of Entropy Analysis in Triage:**
Entropy analysis rapidly identifies regions of a firmware image that are likely encrypted, compressed, or contain cryptographic key material. By computing the Shannon entropy over sliding windows of the binary, the examiner produces a 1-D signal that reveals:
- **Low entropy (< 3.0 bits/byte):** Structured code, ASCII strings, empty/zero-filled regions.
- **Mid entropy (3.0–5.5 bits/byte):** Typical compiled C code with mixed instructions.
- **High entropy (7.2–8.0 bits/byte):** Compressed or encrypted payloads — high-priority forensic targets.

**Entropy Calculation:**

For 4 equally distributed byte values: $p_1 = p_2 = p_3 = p_4 = 0.25$

$$
H = -\sum_{b=1}^{4} p_b \cdot \log_2 p_b = -4 \cdot (0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25)
$$

$$
H = -4 \cdot (0.25 \cdot (-2.0000)) = -4 \cdot (-0.5000) = 2.0000 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Interpretation:** The entropy of **2.0 bits/byte** is significantly below the 3.0 threshold, indicating a **structured, non-random region**. Such a pattern is typical of repetitive patterns (e.g., bootloader headers, test patterns, or simple lookup tables). This region is **not** cryptographically obfuscated and is safe to inspect with conventional string and pattern analysis tools.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **CPS Boot Chain** is the sequential execution path from power-on until the application layer becomes operational:

$$\text{ROM} \rightarrow \text{Bootloader Stage 1} \rightarrow \text{Bootloader Stage 2} \rightarrow \text{Kernel} \rightarrow \text{Userspace} \rightarrow \text{Application}$$

The **Chain of Trust** is the cryptographic guarantee that each layer verifies the integrity and authenticity of the next before passing control. The **Root of Trust** is anchored in the **immutable ROM**, which is burned at fabrication and cannot be modified post-production. Each subsequent component carries a digital signature (typically ECDSA, RSA-PSS, or Ed25519) that the prior layer validates against a public key burned into fuses or the ROM.

**Forensic Use of the Model:**

The examiner reconstructs the boot chain by:
1. Acquiring each layer's binary image.
2. Verifying the embedded signatures against the manufacturer's public key.
3. Performing binary diffing against the vendor's golden image.

The **first layer where verification fails** is the **entry point of the compromise**. For example, if the ROM signature passes but the Stage-1 bootloader signature fails, the attacker gained write access to the SPI flash before Stage-1 execution. The **scope** is determined by checking which subsequent layers remain valid — if the kernel signature fails too, the attacker has propagated beyond the bootloader.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **show the full calculation** in part (a). Students who only state the answer (\"2.0 bits/byte\") without showing the log steps lose 3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In part (b), students describe the boot chain but fail to explain **how** the examiner uses it operationally. The examiner wants to see the **reconstruction procedure**, not just a definition.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Conflating the **immutable ROM** with **writable flash** when discussing the Root of Trust. The ROM is read-only at fabrication; it cannot be patched, so the Root of Trust is by definition beyond attacker's reach unless the device has a hardware-level silicon vulnerability.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forensic Techniques for Firmware-Related Incidents**

- **Firmware forensics** targets the non-volatile memory of CPS/IoT devices where the firmware is the artifact or carrier of compromise.
- The **NIST five-phase methodology** is: Identification → Preservation → Acquisition → Analysis → Reporting.
- The **three acquisition techniques** are: Software Read (weakest), JTAG/SWD (medium), Chip-Off (strongest).
- **JTAG** (IEEE 1149.1) uses boundary-scan; **SWD** is ARM-specific. They are not interchangeable.
- **Chain of Custody** must be maintained from the moment of seizure; **SHA-256** is the minimum acceptable hash standard.
- **Entropy analysis** is the primary triage technique: $H = -\sum p_b \log_2 p_b$, with thresholds 3.0, 5.5, and 7.2 bits/byte.
- The **Chain of Trust** is anchored in the **immutable ROM** (Root of Trust) and propagates upward through cryptographically signed boot stages.
- **Volatile firmware** (FPGA bitstreams) requires **powered acquisition**; **non-volatile firmware** (SPI flash) requires **unpowered** extraction.
- **FARADAY ISOLATION** is mandatory for wireless-enabled CPS devices before physical handling to prevent remote wiping.
- **NIST SP 800-193** defines platform firmware resiliency; **NIST SP 800-86** defines forensic integration; **ISO/IEC 27037** defines evidence handling.
- **FRE 1001(3)** best-evidence rule makes chip-off acquisitions the gold standard for courtroom admissibility.
- **Anti-forensics techniques** to anticipate: firmware wiping via JTAG, write-protect disabling, secure boot bypass exploits, and dead-battery erasure.
- The **forensic report** must follow **SWGDE** best practices and clearly state: device ID, acquisition method, hash values, tools used, and chain-of-custody continuity.
- The **boot chain** is reconstructed to find the **earliest compromised layer**, which defines both the **scope** and the **attacker entry point**.
- **Diffing** against the manufacturer's golden image is the primary technique for detecting unauthorized modification; **binwalk** is the primary tool for embedded filesystem extraction.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always provide: (1) conceptual definition, (2) comparative table, (3) numerical example, and (4) forensic interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Response and Forensic Analysis for Firmware Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incident Response and Forensic Analysis for Firmware Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Firmware Incident Response and Forensic Analysis** is a specialized discipline of cybersecurity that focuses on the systematic detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident analysis of security breaches originating within the non-volatile memory layers (ROM, EEPROM, NOR/NAND Flash, UEFI/BIOS, U-Boot, RTOS images, and embedded controller firmware) of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS).
