It combines the rigour of digital forensics (Chain of Custody, evidence integrity via cryptographic hashing) with the procedural discipline of Incident Response (IR) frameworks such as **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2**.

In the context of CPS — where firmware directly controls physical actuators, sensors, and industrial control loops (PLCs, RTUs, smart meters, medical devices, automotive ECUs) — firmware-level incidents differ fundamentally from traditional IT incidents. A compromised firmware image can persist across reboots, evade host-based antivirus, manipulate sensor telemetry, and induce unsafe physical states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT863 / Module 4):**
> *"Incident Response for firmware vulnerabilities encompasses the orchestrated set of procedures for identifying, isolating, and analysing malicious artefacts embedded within the firmware layer of CPS, while Forensic Analysis applies scientifically validated methods to extract, preserve, and interpret volatile and non-volatile firmware evidence in a manner admissible under evidentiary standards."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a modern hospital patient monitored by a sophisticated life-support machine:

- **The Patient (CPS device)** is alive and running critical processes.
- **The Doctor (IR Team)** is called when something goes wrong — but the symptoms (erratic readings) are confusing.
- **The X-Ray Machine (Forensic Tools)** lets the doctor see inside the body without cutting it open.
- **The Biopsy (Chip-off / JTAG Extraction)** is needed when the doctor must physically remove a tissue sample (firmware chip) for deep lab analysis.
- **The Medical Report (Chain of Custody & Forensic Report)** is the legally admissible document that explains what was found and why.

Just as a doctor cannot treat a patient based on guesswork, a CPS security engineer cannot remediate a firmware compromise without **verifiable forensic evidence** of the root cause, the persistence mechanism, and the lateral blast radius.

## 1.3 Why Firmware Forensics is a Distinct Discipline

| Property | IT Forensics | Firmware Forensics (CPS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Evidence medium** | HDD, SSD, RAM dumps | Flash chips, EPROM, embedded controllers |
| **Acquisition method** | Software imaging, live response | JTAG, ISP, SPI programmers, chip-off |
| **Volatility** | Seconds (RAM) | Decades (Flash) |
| **Attack surface** | OS, applications, network | Bootloaders, secure boot bypass, supply chain |
| **Impact of compromise** | Data loss, exfiltration | Physical damage, safety hazards, kinetic risk |
| **Recovery complexity** | OS reinstall | Hardware-level reprogramming or replacement |
| **Evidentiary standard** | Admissible in civil/criminal court | Often requires expert witness testimony on embedded systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Statistic (2024 ENISA Threat Landscape):** Approximately **70% of CPS/IoT devices** have at least one known firmware vulnerability at the time of deployment, and the **mean dwell time** for firmware-resident malware exceeds **180 days** — compared to 10–21 days for traditional IT malware.

## 1.4 Core Terminology — KTU Quick Reference

- **Firmware**: Non-volatile, low-level software programmed into read-only or re-writable memory that initialises hardware and provides runtime services to higher-level OS/software.
- **Incident**: An occurrence that actually or potentially jeopardizes the Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA) of a CPS.
- **Chain of Custody**: A documented, unbroken chronological record tracing the seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of physical/digital evidence.
- **Acquisition**: The process of creating a forensically sound copy of volatile or non-volatile storage.
- **Bit-stream Image**: A sector-by-sector, uncompressed forensic duplicate of a storage device.
- **Write-Blocker**: A hardware or software tool that prevents any write operations to the source evidence medium during acquisition.
- **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)**: An observable artefact (hash, IP, registry key, anomaly) that indicates a system has been breached.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61)

The NIST framework defines **four core phases** of incident response. When applied to firmware in CPS, each phase has unique technical considerations.

### Phase 1 — Preparation

This pre-incident phase establishes the capability to respond.

- Maintain an **authoritative firmware inventory** (hardware revision, firmware version, cryptographic signatures, OEM vendor).
- Pre-position **acquisition tools** (Bus Pirate, Shikra, Xilinx JTAG programmers, CH341A SPI programmers).
- Build a **golden image repository** of verified, OEM-signed firmware for each CPS device class.
- Develop **IR playbooks** specific to firmware compromise (e.g., PLC bricking, smart meter false-data injection).
- Conduct **tabletop exercises** simulating firmware supply-chain attacks (e.g., compromise of vendor build pipeline).

### Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis

Identification of an active or historical firmware incident relies on:

- **Anomaly-based detection**: Deviations in CPS process telemetry (e.g., unexpected PLC ladder-logic transitions).
- **Integrity monitoring**: TPM/PCR (Platform Configuration Register) measurements vs. expected baseline hashes.
- **Behavioural heuristics**: Unusual SPI/I²C bus traffic, bootloader re-entry, or secure-boot failures.
- **IoC matching**: YARA rules applied to extracted firmware images.
- **Side-channel observation**: Power analysis, EM emissions revealing anomalous code execution.

### Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery

Firmware-specific containment actions include:

1. **Network isolation** of the affected CPS segment (industrial DMZ segmentation).
2. **Physical disconnection** of suspect devices from actuator lines.
3. **Switching to a redundant/golden device** to maintain operational continuity.
4. **Avoidance of reboot** (which may trigger destructive malware payloads).
5. **Re-flashing** from a verified OEM-signed image, then applying updated firmware with OEM PGP signature verification.
6. **Hardware-level re-keying**: Updating device certificates, TPM endorsement keys, and HSM-bound credentials.

### Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity

- **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**: Determine the initial access vector (supply chain, insider, exposed JTAG, weak signing key).
- **Lessons Learned report**: Document detection gaps, IR timeline (MTTD, MTTR), and procedural improvements.
- **Threat intelligence sharing**: Submit IoCs to ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers) such as ICS-CERT, ENISA, AIVD.
- **Forensic report preparation**: Court-admissible documentation for potential legal proceedings.

## 2.2 Firmware Acquisition Methodologies — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 In-System Programming (ISP) / In-Circuit Programming (ICP)

The target device is **not removed** from the PCB. Acquisition is performed through a test-pad, debug header, or unpopulated connector using protocols such as **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)**, **I²C**, **UART**, or **JTAG**.

**Advantages:** Non-destructive, repeatable, lower skill floor.
**Disadvantages:** May be disabled by blown fuses or locked debug ports; slower than direct chip access.

### 2.2.2 Joint Test Action Group (JTAG) Acquisition

JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) provides boundary-scan access to a device's pins and internal registers. Through the Test Access Port (TAP), a forensic examiner can:

- Halt CPU execution at a known state.
- Dump memory contents via the EXTEST/BYPASS instructions.
- Use OpenOCD, UrJTAG, or Segger J-Link to extract firmware.

**JTAG Pinout (Standard 20-pin ARM Cortex):**

| Pin | Signal | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | VTREF | Target voltage reference |
| 2 | TMS | Test Mode Select |
| 3 | TDI | Test Data In |
| 4 | TCK | Test Clock |
| 5 | TDO | Test Data Out |
| 6 | nSRST | System Reset |
| 7-8 | NC / TRST | Not connected / Test Reset |
| 9 | GND | Ground |
| 15 | nTRST | TAP reset |
| 17 | DBGRQ | Debug request |
| 19 | DBGACK | Debug acknowledge |

### 2.2.3 Chip-Off Forensics

The flash memory IC is physically **desoldered** using hot-air rework stations or infrared BGA rework systems, then read in a dedicated programmer (e.g., Xeltek SuperPro, RT809H).

**Process flow:**
1. **Pre-heating** PCB to 80–100°C to prevent warping.
2. **Localized heating** of target IC to 220–250°C (lead-free SAC305 solder).
3. **Clean-up** of residual solder with wick and flux.
4. **Reading** the bare IC in a programmer.
5. **Soldering** a replacement (sourced from golden firmware) back onto the PCB.

**Risk:** Heat-induced damage to adjacent components; potential evidence degradation.

### 2.2.4 Cold Boot Attack

A class of attack (and forensic technique) exploiting the **data remanence** of DRAM. Cooling the memory module with compressed air or liquid nitrogen preserves bit-cell charge for seconds to minutes, allowing direct memory image capture during or after a controlled reboot.

**Persistence formula (typical DDR3 at room temperature):**

$$t_{retain} \approx e^{\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B}\right)\left(\frac{1}{T_{room}} - \frac{1}{T_{cold}}\right)} \times t_{0}$$

Where:
- $E_a$ is the activation energy of charge loss (~0.6 eV for typical DRAM cells).
- $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**.
- $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.
- $t_0$ is the baseline retention time at room temperature.

## 2.3 Forensic Hashing & Integrity Verification

Every firmware image must be cryptographically hashed at the moment of acquisition. The two principal algorithms used in KTU 2024-aligned forensic practice are:

**SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm, 256-bit output):**

$$H_{SHA256}(M) = \text{SHA-256}(M) \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

**Collision Resistance Property:** Finding $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ requires approximately $2^{128}$ operations (birthday-bound).

## 2.4 Shannon Entropy for Firmware Anomaly Detection

Firmware sections containing valid code or data exhibit entropy in well-defined ranges. Compressed or encrypted regions exhibit uniformly high entropy (~8.0 bits/byte). The Shannon entropy of a byte stream is:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \quad \text{[bits/byte]}$$

| Entropy Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $0.0 - 1.0$ | Sparse data, all-zeros, ASCII text |
| $1.0 - 3.0$ | Low-density structured data |
| $3.0 - 5.0$ | Source code, headers, structured records |
| $5.0 - 7.0$ | Mixed code/data, typical firmware regions |
| $7.0 - 7.5$ | Compressed blocks (gzip, LZMA) |
| $7.5 - 8.0$ | **Encrypted or random** — likely obfuscation/encryption |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to identify "suspicious regions" in a firmware image, computing the entropy histogram (often visualised as a 256-bin per-block chart) and locating entropy spikes near 8.0 is the standard forensic answer.

## 2.5 Firmware Analysis Workflow — Binwalk & Reverse Engineering

The canonical Linux-based firmware analysis pipeline:

1. **Acquire** firmware bit-stream image.
2. **Hash** (SHA-256) the image; record acquisition timestamp and examiner ID.
3. **Binwalk scan** to identify embedded filesystems (SquashFS, JFFS2, CramFS, UBIFS, YAFFS).
4. **Entropy analysis** to flag encrypted/obfuscated regions.
5. **Carve** filesystems using `binwalk -e firmware.bin`.
6. **Static analysis** of extracted binaries using `file`, `strings`, `readelf`, and disassembly in **Ghidra** or **IDA Pro**.
7. **Dynamic analysis** in emulators (QEMU, Unicorn Engine, Renode) or sandboxed hardware.
8. **YARA scan** against known malware rule sets.
9. **Correlation** with CPE/CVE databases (NVD, ICS-CERT advisories).
10. **Report generation** with full chain of custody.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Purpose | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i)\log_2 p(x_i)$ | Shannon entropy for anomaly detection | bits/byte |
| 2 | $H_{SHA256}(M) \in \{0,1\}^{256}$ | Forensic integrity hash | 256 bits |
| 3 | $t_{retain} = t_0 \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_{room}}-\frac{1}{T_{cold}}\right)}$ | DRAM remanence vs. temperature | seconds |
| 4 | $P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/(2\cdot 2^b)}$ | Birthday attack probability | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{image} = \frac{S}{R_{acq}} \times (1 + \alpha)$ | Acquisition time with overhead | seconds |
| 6 | $C_{hash} = \frac{N_{blocks} \cdot t_{block}}{T_{wall}}$ | Hash throughput | blocks/second |
| 7 | $MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{detect,i} - t_{occur,i})$ | Mean Time To Detect | hours/days |
| 8 | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{resolve,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | Mean Time To Respond | hours/days |
| 9 | $R_{SPI} = f_{clk} \times \frac{1}{N_{div}}$ | SPI acquisition throughput | bits/second |
| 10 | $E_a(\text{DRAM}) \approx 0.6 \text{ eV}$ | Charge-loss activation energy | electron-volts |
| 11 | $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | Boltzmann constant | J/K |
| 12 | $N_{AV} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | Avogadro number | mol$^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin **secure boot validation**, **TPM PCR measurement**, **evidence admissibility** under Daubert/Frye standards, and **forensic timeline reconstruction** used in industrial control incident investigations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acquisition Time Estimation

**Problem:** A forensic examiner must acquire a 16 MB NOR flash chip over an SPI bus clocked at 25 MHz, with a clock divider of 4. The acquisition software has a 15% protocol overhead (command/response turnaround, CRC checks). How long will the acquisition take, and how much faster would JTAG at 10 MHz with 25% overhead be?

### Step 1 — Identify the variables

We define:
- $S$ = total image size in bytes = $16 \text{ MB} = 16 \times 2^{20}$ bytes.
- $f_{clk}$ = bus clock frequency = $25 \text{ MHz} = 25 \times 10^6$ Hz.
- $N_{div}$ = clock divider = $4$.
- $\alpha$ = protocol overhead fraction = $0.15$.
- $T_{acq}$ = total acquisition time in seconds.

### Step 2 — Compute the effective SPI throughput

The SPI bus transmits 1 bit per clock cycle (single I/O mode), so the bit rate $R_{SPI}$ is:

$$R_{SPI} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N_{div}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/second}$$

### Step 3 — Convert bit rate to byte rate

Since $1$ byte $= 8$ bits:

$$R_{byte} = \frac{R_{SPI}}{8} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 781{,}250 \text{ bytes/second}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the raw transfer time

$$T_{raw} = \frac{S}{R_{byte}} = \frac{16 \times 2^{20}}{781{,}250} = \frac{16{,}777{,}216}{781{,}250} \approx 21.475 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 5 — Apply protocol overhead

The total wall-clock acquisition time $T_{acq}$ is inflated by the overhead factor $(1 + \alpha)$:

$$T_{acq} = T_{raw} \times (1 + \alpha) = 21.475 \times 1.15 \approx 24.696 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 6 — Repeat for JTAG at 10 MHz with 25% overhead

For JTAG single-wire (TDI), the bit rate is the same as the JTAG clock (TCK) since one bit is shifted per TCK cycle:

$$R_{JTAG} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/second} = 1.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/second}$$

$$T_{raw,JTAG} = \frac{16 \times 2^{20}}{1.25 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{16{,}777{,}216}{1{,}250{,}000} \approx 13.422 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{acq,JTAG} = 13.422 \times 1.25 \approx 16.777 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 7 — Comparative result

$$\text{Speedup}_{JTAG \to SPI} = \frac{T_{acq,SPI}}{T_{acq,JTAG}} = \frac{24.696}{16.777} \approx 1.472$$

**Conclusion:** Despite JTAG's higher protocol overhead (25% vs 15%), its higher raw clock rate makes it ~1.47× faster than the divided SPI interface in this scenario.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Collision Probability for Forensic Hashes

**Problem:** A firm has been issued $N$ = 10,000 firmware images. Each is hashed with SHA-256 (256-bit output). What is the probability that two hashes collide (i.e., a malicious or accidental hash collision could compromise the integrity chain)?

### Step 1 — Define the birthday problem

We apply the standard birthday-bound approximation. The probability of at least one collision in $N$ samples drawn from a space of $2^b$ possible hash values is:

$$P_{collision} \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{b}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

- $N = 10{,}000$.
- $N(N-1) = 10{,}000 \times 9{,}999 = 99{,}990{,}000$.
- $b = 256$, so $2^{256} \approx 1.158 \times 10^{77}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the exponent

$$\frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{b}} = \frac{99{,}990{,}000}{2 \times 1.158 \times 10^{77}} = \frac{99{,}990{,}000}{2.316 \times 10^{77}} \approx 4.318 \times 10^{-71}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the exponential

Since the exponent is so small, we use the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for tiny $x$:

$$P_{collision} \approx 1 - (1 - 4.318 \times 10^{-71}) = 4.318 \times 10^{-71}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

The collision probability is approximately **$4.3 \times 10^{-71}$** — effectively zero for any practical forensic purpose. This justifies SHA-256 as a viable forensic integrity primitive for the foreseeable future.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Even with $N = 2^{128}$ images (an astronomical number), the collision probability remains below $10^{-38}$. SHA-256 remains secure against birthday attacks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Entropy-Based Detection of Encrypted Firmware Blocks

**Problem:** A 64 KB firmware image is divided into 256 blocks of 256 bytes each. For one suspicious block, the byte frequency distribution is approximately uniform with $p(x_i) \approx 1/256$ for each $i \in [0, 255]$. Compute the Shannon entropy and interpret the result.

### Step 1 — Apply the Shannon entropy formula

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $p(x_i) = 1/256$

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \log_2\left(\frac{1}{256}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the logarithm

$$\log_2\left(\frac{1}{256}\right) = \log_2(2^{-8}) = -8$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \times (-8) = -\frac{1}{256} \times (-8) \times 256 = 8 \text{ bits/byte}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

The block has maximum entropy (8.0 bits/byte), meaning it is **indistinguishable from random data** — the strongest possible indicator of encryption, strong compression, or cryptographic key material. In a firmware forensic context, this is a high-confidence IoC that warrants further investigation (potential cryptographic implant, key storage, or obfuscated malware payload).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cold-Boot DRAM Remanence Time

**Problem:** A DRAM module has a baseline room-temperature (293 K) retention of $t_0 = 10$ seconds. The forensic team cools it to $-40°C$ (233 K) using compressed air. Compute the new retention time, given $E_a = 0.6$ eV.

### Step 1 — Convert $E_a$ to Joules

$$E_a = 0.6 \text{ eV} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV} = 9.612 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the temperature ratio

$$\frac{1}{T_{room}} - \frac{1}{T_{cold}} = \frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{233} = 0.003413 - 0.004292 = -0.000879 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the exponent

$$\frac{E_a}{k_B} \times \left(\frac{1}{T_{room}} - \frac{1}{T_{cold}}\right) = \frac{9.612 \times 10^{-20}}{1.381 \times 10^{-23}} \times (-0.000879)$$

$$= 6962.49 \times (-0.000879) \approx -6.122$$

### Step 4 — Apply the exponential

$$e^{-6.122} \approx 0.00219$$

### Step 5 — Compute the new retention time

$$t_{retain} = t_0 \times e^{6.122} = 10 \times 456.6 \approx 4566 \text{ seconds} \approx 76 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

Cooling the DRAM from 20°C to -40°C extends data retention from **10 seconds to ~76 minutes** — a ~456× increase, providing ample window for a forensic memory image capture.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Forensic Decision Flow

| Stage | Tool / Method | Output Artefact | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| Preparation | IR Playbook, Asset Inventory | Playbook PDF, CVE feed | "Describe the 4 NIST phases" |
| Acquisition | JTAG / SPI / Chip-off | Bit-stream image (`.bin`) | "Compare acquisition methods" |
| Hashing | `sha256sum`, FTK Imager | Hash digest file | "Why SHA-256 over MD5?" |
| Entropy analysis | `binwalk -E`, custom script | Entropy graph | "Identify encrypted blocks" |
| Filesystem extraction | `binwalk -e`, `unsquashfs` | Decompressed tree | "Find embedded filesystems" |
| Static analysis | Ghidra, IDA, `strings` | Disassembly, IoCs | "Locate hard-coded credentials" |
| Dynamic analysis | QEMU, Renode, hardware | Behaviour trace | "Trace malware execution" |
| Reporting | FTK, EnCase, custom | Court-admissible report | "Explain chain of custody" |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Firmware Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST Aligned)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete NIST SP 800-61 firmware IR lifecycle, mapped to CPS-specific forensic actions at each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prep1[Asset Inventory and Firmware Baseline] --> prep2[CPS Network Segmentation Map]
    prep2 --> prep3[Pre-positioned JTAG and SPI Tools]
    prep3 --> prep4[Golden Firmware Image Repository]
    prep4 --> prep5[IR Playbook for Firmware]
    prep5 --> det1[Anomaly in CPS Telemetry]
    det1 --> det2[Secure Boot Verification Failure]
    det2 --> det3[TPM PCR Mismatch Alert]
    det3 --> det4[Indicator of Compromise Correlation]
    det4 --> con1[Isolate Affected CPS Segment]
    con1 --> con2[Disconnect Actuator Lines]
    con2 --> con3[Switch to Redundant Device]
    con3 --> era1[Forensically Image Suspect Firmware]
    era1 --> era2[Hash and Verify Chain of Custody]
    era2 --> era3[Static and Dynamic Malware Analysis]
    era3 --> era4[Re-flash from Verified Golden Image]
    era4 --> era5[Re-key Device Certificates]
    era5 --> rec1[Restore CPS Operational State]
    rec1 --> rec2[Validate Sensor Actuator Behaviour]
    rec2 --> pos1[Root Cause Analysis Report]
    pos1 --> pos2[Lessons Learned and Playbook Update]
    pos2 --> pos3[Share IoC with ISAC and ENISA]
    pos3 --> prep1
```

## 4.2 Firmware Acquisition Methodology Comparison Flow

This diagram renders the decision tree a forensic examiner follows when selecting an acquisition method for a suspect CPS device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Suspect CPS Device Identified] --> q1{Debug Ports Accessible?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Which Protocol Exposed?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Bootloader Shell Available?}
    q3 -- Yes --> boot[Acquire via U-Boot or Bootloader]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Flash Chip Removable?}
    q4 -- Yes --> chip[Chip-off Desoldering and Read]
    q4 -- No --> live[Acquire via Live Memory Forensics]
    q2 --> spi[SPI ISP Acquisition]
    q2 --> jtag[JTAG Boundary Scan Acquisition]
    q2 --> uart[UART Console Dump]
    q2 --> i2c[I2C Bus Sniff]
    spi --> hash1[SHA-256 Hash Source and Image]
    jtag --> hash1
    uart --> hash1
    i2c --> hash1
    boot --> hash1
    chip --> hash1
    live --> hash1
    hash1 --> store[Evidence Locker with Chain of Custody]
```

## 4.3 Forensic Analysis Pipeline (Static and Dynamic)

The complete analytical pipeline from raw image to admissible evidence, rendered as a Mermaid block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Firmware Bit-stream Image] --> h1[SHA-256 Hash Computation]
    h1 --> ent[Entropy Histogram Analysis]
    h1 --> bw[Binwalk Signature Scan]
    ent --> sus[Flag High Entropy Regions]
    bw --> fs[Extract Embedded Filesystems]
    sus --> man[Manual Reverse Engineering in Ghidra]
    fs --> man
    man --> yara[YARA Scan Against CPS Malware Rules]
    yara --> cve[CVE and CPE Database Correlation]
    cve --> dyn[Dynamic Analysis in QEMU Sandbox]
    dyn --> corr[Timeline and IoC Correlation]
    corr --> rpt[Generate Forensic Report]
    rpt --> court[Admissible Evidence for Legal Proceedings]
```

## 4.4 Chain of Custody Block Architecture

The mandatory evidence-handling structure that makes firmware forensic output legally admissible.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    acq[Examiner Acquires Firmware Image] --> id[Assign Unique Evidence ID]
    id --> hash[Compute SHA-256 at Time of Seizure]
    hash --> doc[Document Date Time Examiner Location]
    doc --> loc[Store in Tamper-evident Evidence Bag]
    loc --> log1[Log Entry in Chain of Custody Record]
    log1 --> xfer[Transfer to Forensic Lab with Sign-off]
    xfer --> verify[Re-verify Hash at Receipt]
    verify --> an[Analysis Under Write-blocked Conditions]
    an --> log2[Log All Analysis Actions with Timestamps]
    log2 --> ret[Return or Destroy per Policy]
    ret --> arch[Archive with Full Custody History]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**
*Define firmware forensics. List any two challenges unique to firmware forensics in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Firmware forensics is the disciplined application of scientifically validated methods to the acquisition, preservation, analysis, and interpretation of digital evidence residing within the non-volatile firmware storage (ROM, Flash, EEPROM) of computing devices, with the goal of producing findings admissible in legal or administrative proceedings.

**Two CPS-Specific Challenges (1 Mark — 0.5 each):**

1. **Hardware-level acquisition complexity:** Unlike HDD/SSD imaging, firmware extraction often requires physical interventions such as JTAG tap access, SPI bus sniffing, or chip-off desoldering.
2. **Safety-critical impact:** Tampering with firmware in CPS can induce unsafe physical states (e.g., a compromised PLC may drive an actuator beyond mechanical limits), making live acquisition dangerous and complicating evidence handling.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the Shannon entropy of a firmware block that contains only the byte value 0x00? What does this signify in a forensic context?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Computation (2 Marks):** With $p(0x00) = 1$ and all other $p(x_i) = 0$:

$$H(X) = -1 \cdot \log_2(1) - \sum_{i \neq 0} 0 \cdot \log_2(0) = 0 \text{ bits/byte}$$

**Forensic Interpretation (1 Mark):** A zero-entropy block represents a fully erased or never-programmed region of flash memory. In a forensic context, this may indicate (a) unallocated flash space, (b) a deliberately wiped region following a secure erase, or (c) a partially overwritten file. It warrants further investigation if located where valid firmware code is expected.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper / PECPT863]**

**(a)** Explain the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response lifecycle. Map each of the four phases to a specific firmware-level action that an IR team would execute in a CPS environment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forensic team must acquire a 32 MB firmware image from a PLC using an SPI bus clocked at 50 MHz with a clock divider of 8. The protocol overhead is 20%. Calculate the total acquisition time. Compare this with a JTAG-based acquisition at 20 MHz TCK with 30% overhead, and recommend the preferred method. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Preparation Phase (2 Marks):**

- Build and maintain an authoritative **firmware inventory** for every CPS asset, including hardware revision, current firmware version, vendor PGP signature, and the SHA-256 of the OEM golden image.
- **Pre-position** acquisition hardware (JTAG probes, SPI programmers, hot-air rework station) and software (OpenOCD, Binwalk, Ghidra, FTK Imager).
- Draft **firmware-specific IR playbooks** that cover common attack scenarios (e.g., Stuxnet-style PLC ladder-logic rewriting, supply-chain implant, secure-boot bypass).

**2. Detection & Analysis Phase (2 Marks):**

- Deploy **TPM-based integrity monitoring** to detect PCR mismatches between expected and measured boot hashes.
- Use **anomaly-based detection** on CPS process telemetry (sudden deviation in actuator current, sensor drift, or PLC scan-time anomalies).
- Perform **YARA scanning** of any recovered firmware images against the latest ICS-CERT and Dragos malware rulesets.

**3. Containment, Eradication & Recovery Phase (2 Marks):**

- **Isolate** the affected CPS segment by closing industrial firewall rules and disconnecting the physical network cable.
- **Power-down** the compromised device (only after consulting the playbook — some firmware malware triggers destructive payloads on shutdown).
- **Re-flash** the device from a verified OEM-signed golden image, then re-key all device certificates and HSM-bound credentials.

**4. Post-Incident Activity Phase (1 Mark):**

- Conduct a **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** to determine the initial access vector and produce a **Lessons Learned** report.
- Submit the Indicators of Compromise (hashes, IPs, exploit signatures) to relevant **ISACs** (e.g., AIVD, ENISA, ICS-CERT) and update the IR playbook.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $S = 32 \text{ MB} = 32 \times 2^{20} = 33{,}554{,}432$ bytes.
- $f_{clk,SPI} = 50 \text{ MHz}$, $N_{div} = 8$, $\alpha_{SPI} = 0.20$.
- $f_{JTAG} = 20 \text{ MHz}$, $\alpha_{JTAG} = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — SPI throughput (1 Mark):**

$$R_{SPI} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{8} = 6.25 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/second}$$

$$R_{byte,SPI} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^6}{8} = 781{,}250 \text{ bytes/second}$$

**Step 2 — SPI raw time (1 Mark):**

$$T_{raw,SPI} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{781{,}250} \approx 42.95 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — SPI with overhead (1 Mark):**

$$T_{acq,SPI} = 42.95 \times 1.20 \approx 51.54 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — JTAG throughput (1 Mark):**

$$R_{byte,JTAG} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{8} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/second}$$

**Step 5 — JTAG raw time + overhead (1 Mark):**

$$T_{raw,JTAG} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{2.5 \times 10^6} \approx 13.42 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{acq,JTAG} = 13.42 \times 1.30 \approx 17.45 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and Recommendation (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Speedup}_{JTAG/SPI} = \frac{51.54}{17.45} \approx 2.95$$

**Recommendation:** **JTAG is the preferred method** because it is ~2.95× faster despite its higher protocol overhead, and it provides CPU halt control for reliable, deterministic dumps. JTAG is also less sensitive to flash-chip-specific SPI command-set quirks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper / PECPT863]**

**(a)** Describe three firmware acquisition methodologies (JTAG, ISP/SPI, and Chip-off). Compare their advantages and limitations in a CPS forensic context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A suspect firmware image is found to have a uniformly distributed byte frequency in a 4 KB block. Compute its Shannon entropy. What does this indicate, and what investigative steps should the forensic team take next? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. JTAG Acquisition (2.5 Marks):**

- **Principle:** Uses the IEEE 1149.1 boundary-scan standard. The Test Access Port (TAP) provides shift-register access to device pins and internal memory buses.
- **Procedure:** Connect a JTAG probe (J-Link, OpenOCD-compatible adapter) to the target's TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, and nTRST lines. Halt the CPU and use the EXTEST or custom debug instructions to dump memory.
- **Advantages:** CPU-controllable, deterministic, repeatable without physical damage.
- **Limitations:** Requires un-fused debug port; many production CPS devices have JTAG locked at OEM level.

**2. ISP / SPI Acquisition (2.5 Marks):**

- **Principle:** The flash memory is read in-circuit via the SPI bus using commands like Read (0x03), JEDEC ID (0x9F), and Read Status Register (0x05).
- **Procedure:** Solder fly-wires to the SPI test pads (CS, SCK, MOSI, MISO, GND, VCC), then use a hardware programmer (CH341A, Bus Pirate) and `flashrom` software.
- **Advantages:** Low-cost, accessible, works on locked JTAG if the SPI pads remain exposed.
- **Limitations:** Requires knowledge of the flash part number; can be disabled by status-register write-protect bits.

**3. Chip-Off Acquisition (2 Marks):**

- **Principle:** The flash IC is physically desoldered and read in a standalone programmer.
- **Procedure:** Pre-heat PCB, desolder the target flash with hot air at 220–250°C, clean pads, read in Xeltek/Wellon programmer, and either re-solder a known-good replacement or program the original chip with a golden image.
- **Advantages:** Works when all other methods fail; provides the most direct access to raw flash contents.
- **Limitations:** Risk of PCB damage, IC destruction, and evidence degradation; highly skill-intensive.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Uniform distribution $p(x_i) = 1/256$ for $i \in [0, 255]$.

**Step 1 — Entropy computation (2 Marks):**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \log_2\left(\frac{1}{256}\right) = -256 \times \frac{1}{256} \times (-8) = 8 \text{ bits/byte}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**

This is the **maximum possible entropy** for a byte stream. The block is statistically indistinguishable from a cryptographically secure random source. In firmware, this is a strong indicator of:
- **Encrypted firmware region** (e.g., a read-protected code section)
- **Compressed data** (gzip, LZMA, LZO)
- **Embedded cryptographic key material** (private key, certificate store)
- **Obfuscated / packed malware payload**

**Step 3 — Investigative next steps (3 Marks):**

1. **Identify the surrounding context** — what comes before/after the high-entropy block? Headers, file signatures, or strings may reveal the data type.
2. **Check for known signatures** with Binwalk, looking for patterns such as a U-Boot uImage header, a PKCS#12 envelope, or an OpenSSL encrypted blob (`Salted__` magic).
3. **Attempt cryptographic identification** — try AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 decryption with any keys recovered from related memory regions.
4. **Decompile surrounding code** in Ghidra to find the decryption routine and its key derivation function.
5. **If the block is unexpected** in a known firmware region, treat it as an **IoC** — it may be a malicious implant or backdoor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Part A marks with Part B marks.** Part A questions are capped at 3 marks; do not write paragraphs that earn 0 extra marks and waste time.
> 2. **Show all numerical units in Part B derivations.** A correct final answer without intermediate units (MB/s, seconds) loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mention Chain of Custody explicitly** in any question on forensic procedure — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this phrase, even if the rest of the answer is sound.
> 4. **In entropy questions, state the max value (8.0 bits/byte) explicitly** and then interpret — do not just write "high entropy" without quantification.
> 5. **In acquisition method questions, name the tools** (e.g., OpenOCD, flashrom, Xeltek) — generic answers like "use a hardware tool" lose marks.
> 6. **Always relate firmware forensics back to CPS safety** — examiners reward the connection to physical-world impact, not just digital evidence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Firmware Incident Response & Forensics**

- **NIST 4 Phases** — Preparation → Detection/Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident Activity. Memorise the order and the CPS-specific action in each.
- **Chain of Custody** is **non-negotiable** for court admissibility. Always hash (SHA-256) at the moment of seizure; re-verify at receipt; document every transfer.
- **Acquisition Hierarchy (most to least invasive):** Chip-off → JTAG → SPI/ISP → Bootloader shell → Live memory. Choose the least invasive method that yields a complete image.
- **JTAG Pinout (ARM 20-pin):** Pin 1 = VTREF, Pin 4 = TCK, Pin 7 = TMS, Pin 9 = TDI, Pin 13 = TDO, Pin 15 = nSRST.
- **SPI Acquisition Rate:** $R_{byte} = f_{clk} / (N_{div} \times 8)$. Always include protocol overhead $(1 + \alpha)$ in the final time estimate.
- **Shannon Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i)\log_2 p(x_i)$; range $0$ to $8$ bits/byte. Max entropy ($8.0$) ⇒ encryption/compression/randomness.
- **SHA-256 Collision Probability** for $N$ images: $P \approx 1 - e^{-N(N-1)/(2 \cdot 2^{256})}$ — practically zero.
- **Cold-Boot Remanence:** Cooling DRAM from 293 K to 233 K extends retention from ~10 s to ~76 minutes ($E_a \approx 0.6$ eV).
- **Boltzmann Constant** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — required in cold-boot derivations.
- **YARA Rules** are the de-facto standard for IoC matching in recovered firmware — keep them updated.
- **TPM PCR Mismatches** are the strongest in-band indicator of firmware tampering in modern CPS.
- **Forensic Report Must Contain:** Acquisition method, hash digests, chain of custody, tool versions, examiner qualifications, findings, and supporting artefact references.
- **CPS-Specific Hazard:** Some firmware implants (e.g., Stuxnet's 417 payload) trigger on specific process conditions — coordinate with safety engineers before reboot or re-flash.
- **Golden Image Repository** is the cornerstone of recovery — without it, eradication is impossible.
- **Supply Chain Risk** (e.g., SolarWinds, xZ Utils backdoor) means IR must consider OEM build pipeline as an attack surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** under the KTU 2024 scheme, **Emerging Trends and Technologies** refer to the next-generation computational, cryptographic, and architectural paradigms that fundamentally reshape how confidentiality, integrity, availability, and safety are enforced in tightly coupled cyber-physical environments. These technologies are not merely "future concepts" but are increasingly mandated in critical infrastructure sectors (power grids, autonomous vehicles, smart healthcare, industrial automation, smart cities) and include:

1. **Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) for Threat Intelligence** — Data-driven detection, prediction, and response in CPS networks.
2. **Blockchain and Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** — Tamper-evident, decentralized trust for industrial control systems.
3. **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** — The "never trust, always verify" security model replacing perimeter-based defenses.
4. **Edge and Fog Computing Security** — Distributed security enforcement at the network periphery.
5. **5G/6G and Beyond Network Security** — Securing ultra-low-latency, massive-machine CPS communications.
6. **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) and Quantum Key Distribution (QKD)** — Cryptographic resilience against quantum adversaries.
7. **Digital Twins for Security** — Virtual replicas of CPS used for attack simulation, vulnerability discovery, and predictive defense.
8. **Homomorphic Encryption and Privacy-Preserving Computation** — Performing analytics on encrypted CPS telemetry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECPT863 / Module 4):** Students must be able to *compare, contrast, and architecturally justify* the deployment of at least four of the above technologies in CPS contexts, with specific reference to **Course Outcome CO4** (Evaluate emerging security paradigms for safety-critical CPS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Intuition

Imagine a **modern multi-specialty hospital** (the CPS):
- **AI/ML** is like an AI radiologist that reads every X-ray (sensor stream) in milliseconds and flags anomalies (intrusions) the human eye misses.
- **Blockchain** is the tamper-proof shared ledger every doctor, pharmacy, and insurance desk trusts — no one can secretly alter a prescription.
- **Zero Trust** means the surgeon must re-verify identity at *every* door of the OT, even though they are already inside the building.
- **Edge Computing** is the bedside monitoring unit that analyses vitals *locally* rather than sending them to a distant cloud — faster response, less exposure.
- **5G/6G** is the hospital's private ultra-fast internal network carrying robotic surgery traffic.
- **Quantum-Resistant Cryptography** is the new unbreakable lock installed on the pharmacy door because someone invented a master key (quantum computer) that breaks the old one.
- **Digital Twin** is a virtual simulation of the entire hospital used to test a new virus outbreak response *without endangering real patients*.
- **Homomorphic Encryption** lets the insurance company calculate your bill *without ever seeing your actual diagnosis*.

This layered, intelligent, and verifiable approach is exactly what next-generation CPS security demands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exam:** When asked to "discuss emerging trends," always anchor your answer to a **specific CPS domain** (e.g., smart grid, autonomous vehicle, IIoT factory) — generic answers lose marks. The examiner expects *applied* knowledge, not just textbook definitions.

## 1.3 Why These Trends Matter Now (2024–2026 Context)

Three converging forces make this module's content *not optional*:

- **Force 1 — Convergence of IT/OT:** The historic isolation of Operational Technology (OT) is gone. CPS now ride on enterprise IP networks, inheriting IT threats.
- **Force 2 — Quantum Threat Horizon:** NIST finalized its first PQC standards (FIPS 203 ML-KEM, FIPS 204 ML-DSA, FIPS 205 SLH-DSA) in **August 2024**, making PQC migration an immediate, board-level concern.
- **Force 3 — Regulatory Pressure:** India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**, NIS2 (EU), and IEC 62443 updates force CPS operators to adopt verifiable, auditable security architectures.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Technology-by-Technology Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 AI / ML for CPS Security

**Core Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Data Acquisition:** CPS sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, voltage) produce high-frequency, multivariate time-series. This forms the *feature space* $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$.
- **Step 2 — Feature Engineering:** Extract statistical signatures — mean $\mu$, standard deviation $\sigma$, skewness, kurtosis, and spectral features (FFT components).
- **Step 3 — Model Training:** Use supervised (Random Forest, SVM, Deep Neural Networks) or unsupervised (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, One-Class SVM) models to learn the *nominal* CPS behaviour baseline.
- **Step 4 — Anomaly Scoring:** For each new observation, compute an *anomaly score* $s(x) \in [0, 1]$. A threshold $\tau$ triggers an alert.
- **Step 5 — Response:** Trigger actuator lockdown, isolate the compromised controller, alert SOC.

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why:** CPS operate in *non-stationary* environments. A traditional signature-based IDS (like Snort) cannot detect *zero-day* attacks. ML learns the *invariant* physical laws (e.g., Kirchhoff's, Newton's) embedded in the data.
- **How:** The model's loss function penalizes the difference between predicted and observed behaviour, e.g., Mean Squared Error (MSE) for regression-based anomaly detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adversarial ML Warning (KTU favorite):** Attackers can craft *adversarial perturbations* $\delta$ such that $x + \delta$ is misclassified, even though $||\delta||_p$ is imperceptibly small. In CPS, this is *physical-world dangerous* (e.g., fooling a vision-based autonomous vehicle). Defenses include **adversarial training**, **defensive distillation**, and **input sanitization**.

### 2.1.2 Blockchain for CPS Security

**Core Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Transaction Creation:** A CPS event (e.g., "Valve V-102 opened at timestamp T") is hashed.
- **Step 2 — Block Formation:** Multiple events are Merkle-tree aggregated into a block.
- **Step 3 — Consensus:** Nodes (gateways, controllers) validate the block via Proof of Authority (PoA) or Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) — *not* energy-intensive PoW, which is unsuitable for CPS.
- **Step 4 — Chaining:** Each new block contains the hash of the previous block, forming an immutable chain.
- **Step 5 — Smart Contract Enforcement:** Access control policies execute automatically on the chain (e.g., Solidity-based rules).

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why:** CPS historically suffer from *single-point-of-trust* failures (one compromised SCADA server = total collapse). Blockchain distributes trust across all participating nodes.
- **How:** Cryptographic chaining plus consensus makes retroactive tampering computationally infeasible — to alter block $i$, an attacker must redo the work for all $i, i+1, \ldots, n$ blocks *and* outpace honest nodes.

### 2.1.3 Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)

**Core Logic Steps (NIST SP 800-207):**
- **Step 1 — Continuous Verification:** Every request, internal or external, must be authenticated, authorized, and encrypted.
- **Step 2 — Micro-segmentation:** The CPS is divided into small security zones (e.g., per PLC, per sensor cluster) with independent access policies.
- **Step 3 — Least Privilege Access:** A device gets *only* the minimum permissions required for its function.
- **Step 4 — Assume Breach Mentality:** The architecture is designed as if the attacker is *already inside* the network.
- **Step 5 — Policy Decision Point (PDP) / Policy Enforcement Point (PEP):** Centralized PDP issues time-bounded, context-aware access decisions enforced by PEPs at every junction.

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why:** Traditional "castle-and-moat" perimeters fail because once an attacker breaches the firewall (e.g., via a phishing email on an engineering workstation), lateral movement is unrestricted.
- **How:** ZTA replaces implicit trust with **continuous risk scoring** $R = f(\text{identity}, \text{device health}, \text{location}, \text{behaviour}, \text{time})$ evaluated on every transaction.

### 2.1.4 Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

**Core Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Threat Assessment:** Identify cryptographic primitives vulnerable to Shor's algorithm (RSA, ECC, DH) and Grover's algorithm (symmetric, hash).
- **Step 2 — Algorithm Selection:** Migrate to NIST-standardized PQC: **ML-KEM** (key encapsulation, lattice-based), **ML-DSA** (signatures, lattice-based), **SLH-DSA** (hash-based signatures).
- **Step 3 — Hybrid Deployment:** Run classical + PQC in parallel during transition to maintain compatibility.
- **Step 4 — Crypto-Agility:** Design systems so the algorithm can be swapped without re-architecting the application.

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why:** A Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC) breaks RSA-2048 in polynomial time. Adversaries are already executing *Harvest Now, Decrypt Later* attacks — collecting encrypted CPS traffic to decrypt when quantum capability arrives.
- **How:** PQC is based on hard mathematical problems believed quantum-resistant: **Learning With Errors (LWE)** for lattices, **code decoding** for code-based crypto, **multivariate polynomial** systems.

### 2.1.5 Digital Twins for CPS Security

**Core Logic Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Mirror Construction:** Build a real-time virtual replica fed by live sensor data.
- **Step 2 — State Synchronization:** Use Kalman filters or particle filters to align virtual and physical state.
- **Step 3 — Attack Simulation:** Run *red-team* attacks on the twin (replay, DoS, false-data injection) without risk to the physical system.
- **Step 4 — Predictive Defense:** Use the twin to forecast the *consequences* of an ongoing attack and pre-position mitigations.
- **Step 5 — Forensic Reconstruction:** Post-incident, replay the attack in the twin to understand the kill chain.

### 2.1.6 Homomorphic Encryption (HE) and Federated Learning (FL)

- **HE** allows computation $f(x)$ on ciphertext $\text{Enc}(x)$, yielding $\text{Enc}(f(x))$. Useful for *privacy-preserving CPS analytics* where a cloud AI analyses encrypted sensor data.
- **FL** trains a shared ML model across distributed CPS edge nodes *without* centralizing raw data — each node trains locally, only gradients are aggregated. Critical for healthcare CPS and multi-factory industrial deployments.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Entropy of a CPS Sensor Stream

**Problem Context:** A smart-grid substation has a current sensor that takes four discrete states during normal operation: $\{0\text{A}, 10\text{A}, 50\text{A}, 100\text{A}\}$ with measured probabilities $\{0.50, 0.30, 0.15, 0.05\}$. Compute the Shannon entropy.

**Step 1 — Define the entropy formula.**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_{2} p(x_i)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the four probability terms.**

$$
H(X) = -\Big[ p_1 \log_2 p_1 + p_2 \log_2 p_2 + p_3 \log_2 p_3 + p_4 \log_2 p_4 \Big]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values $p_1 = 0.50$, $p_2 = 0.30$, $p_3 = 0.15$, $p_4 = 0.05$.**

$$
H(X) = -\Big[ 0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05 \Big]
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each logarithm in base 2.**

| Term | Value | $\log_2$ | Product $p \log_2 p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0.50 \log_2 0.50$ | $0.50$ | $-1.0000$ | $-0.5000$ |
| $0.30 \log_2 0.30$ | $0.30$ | $-1.7370$ | $-0.5211$ |
| $0.15 \log_2 0.15$ | $0.15$ | $-2.7370$ | $-0.4105$ |
| $0.05 \log_2 0.05$ | $0.05$ | $-4.3219$ | $-0.2161$ |

**Step 5 — Sum the products and negate.**

$$
H(X) = -\big[ -0.5000 - 0.5211 - 0.4105 - 0.2161 \big]
$$

$$
H(X) = 0.5000 + 0.5211 + 0.4105 + 0.2161
$$

$$
H(X) = 1.6477 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.**

The maximum possible entropy for a 4-symbol alphabet is $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits. The observed entropy $1.6477$ bits is **82.4% of maximum**, indicating *moderate* predictability — consistent with a normally operating substation (low current most of the time, occasional peaks).

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Security Application:** A sudden entropy **drop** (e.g., to $0.5$ bits) signals a *fault injection* attack — the attacker is forcing the sensor into a single repetitive state, suppressing the natural variability that an ML IDS would otherwise flag as anomalous.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — F1-Score and ROC Analysis for a CPS Intrusion Detection System

**Problem Context:** An Autoencoder-based IDS deployed on a water-treatment CPS produces the following confusion matrix after 24 hours of monitoring 10,000 sensor packets:

| | **Predicted Normal** | **Predicted Attack** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Normal** | 9,750 (TN) | 50 (FP) |
| **Actual Attack** | 30 (FN) | 170 (TP) |

Compute the F1-Score and analyse the IDS suitability.

**Step 1 — Compute True Positives, False Positives, False Negatives, True Negatives from the matrix.**

$$
TP = 170, \quad FP = 50, \quad FN = 30, \quad TN = 9{,}750
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{170 + 9{,}750}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}920}{10{,}000} = 0.9920
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{170}{170 + 50} = \frac{170}{220} = 0.7727
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall (also called True Positive Rate, TPR).**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{170}{170 + 30} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.8500
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.7727 \times 0.8500}{0.7727 + 0.8500}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.6568}{1.6227} = \frac{1.3136}{1.6227} = 0.8095
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the False Positive Rate (FPR).**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 9{,}750} = \frac{50}{9{,}800} = 0.0051
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the False Negative Rate (FNR) — the most critical metric in safety-CPS.**

$$
FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{30}{30 + 170} = \frac{30}{200} = 0.1500
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering Interpretation.**

The IDS has **high accuracy (99.20%)** but a **15% miss rate (FNR)**. In a safety-critical CPS (e.g., a water-treatment plant), a 15% chance of *missing* a chemical injection attack is *unacceptable*. Recommended action: **lower the detection threshold** $\tau$ to increase recall at the cost of more false alarms, then add a *secondary verification layer* (e.g., physical-law consistency check) to filter the increased FPs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Never quote *only accuracy* in CPS IDS evaluation. Always pair it with **FNR (miss rate)** and **FPR (false alarm rate)**. Accuracy alone is misleading in imbalanced datasets (where attacks are rare), which is the *default* in CPS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Symmetric Key Size Post-Quantum (Grover's Algorithm)

**Problem Context:** A CPS uses AES-128 for encrypting PLC-to-SCADA communication. Determine the new key size required to maintain equivalent security against a quantum adversary running Grover's algorithm.

**Step 1 — State the classical brute-force work factor.**

For an $n$-bit key, classical exhaustive search requires $W_{classical} = 2^n$ operations.

**Step 2 — State Grover's quantum speedup.**

$$
W_{quantum} = O(\sqrt{2^n}) = 2^{n/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the quantum work factor to the desired classical security level.**

The CPS requires security equivalent to **AES-256** (i.e., $2^{256}$ classical operations) — a common requirement for safety-critical infrastructure (NIST Security Level 5).

$$
2^{n/2} = 2^{256}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$.**

$$
\frac{n}{2} = 256 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 512
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.**

To maintain **post-quantum AES-256-equivalent security**, the CPS must migrate to **AES-512** (or alternatively, accept AES-128 and acknowledge a *halving* of effective security to $2^{64}$ operations against a quantum attacker, which is *not* acceptable for safety-critical CPS).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Grover's algorithm provides a *square-root* speedup, NOT a complete break. AES-128 is *not* immediately broken by quantum computers — its effective security drops to 64 bits, which is still beyond near-term capability, but is *future-unsafe* and *harvest-now-decrypt-later vulnerable*.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blockchain Block Confirmation Time for CPS Use Case

**Problem Context:** A consortium blockchain (PBFT consensus) is deployed across 5 industrial gateways in a factory. The network hash rate is $H = 10^4$ hashes/sec, target block time $T_{target} = 10$ sec, and the current difficulty $D = 2^{20}$. Verify whether the parameters yield the desired block time and recompute difficulty for $T_{target} = 5$ sec.

**Step 1 — Expected number of hashes per block.**

$$
E[\text{hashes per block}] = D = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576
$$

**Step 2 — Expected time per block.**

$$
T_{block} = \frac{E[\text{hashes per block}]}{H} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{10^4} = 104.86 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with target.**

$T_{block} = 104.86$ sec $\gg$ $T_{target} = 10$ sec. **The difficulty is 10× too high.** The factory needs to *reduce* $D$ by a factor of ~10.48.

**Step 4 — Recompute $D$ for $T_{target} = 5$ sec.**

$$
D_{new} = T_{target} \times H = 5 \times 10^4 = 50{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to nearest power of 2 for implementation.**

$$
D_{new} = 2^{15.61} \approx 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.**

The factory's CPS-grade blockchain should run with **$D = 2^{16}$**, giving an actual block time of:

$$
T_{block}^{actual} = \frac{2^{16}}{10^4} = \frac{65{,}536}{10{,}000} = 6.55 \text{ sec}
$$

This is within the 5–10 second latency budget acceptable for industrial control message audit logging.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Zero Trust Architecture for a Cyber-Physical System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates a NIST SP 800-207 aligned Zero Trust implementation layered over a smart-factory CPS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDGE_ZONE["EDGE ZONE - FIELD DEVICES"]
        S1["SENSOR 1 - TEMPERATURE"]
        S2["SENSOR 2 - PRESSURE"]
        S3["SENSOR 3 - FLOW RATE"]
        A1["ACTUATOR 1 - VALVE"]
        A2["ACTUATOR 2 - PUMP"]
    end

    subgraph PEP_LAYER["PEP LAYER - POLICY ENFORCEMENT POINTS"]
        PEP1["PEP EDGE GATEWAY"]
        PEP2["PEP PLC FIREWALL"]
    end

    subgraph CORE_ZONE["CORE ZONE - CONTROL SERVERS"]
        SCADA["SCADA SERVER"]
        HIST["HISTORIAN DATABASE"]
        HMI["HMI WORKSTATION"]
    end

    subgraph ZTA_PLANE["ZTA CONTROL PLANE"]
        PDP["PDP POLICY DECISION POINT"]
        PA["PA POLICY ADMINISTRATION"]
        IDP["IDP IDENTITY PROVIDER"]
        DHR["DHR DEVICE HEALTH REGISTRY"]
        SIEM["SIEM AND ANALYTICS"]
    end

    S1 --> PEP1
    S2 --> PEP1
    S3 --> PEP1
    PEP1 --> PEP2
    A1 --> PEP2
    A2 --> PEP2
    PEP2 --> SCADA
    PEP2 --> HMI
    SCADA --> HIST
    HMI --> HIST

    PEP1 -. CONTINUOUS VERIFICATION .-> PDP
    PEP2 -. CONTINUOUS VERIFICATION .-> PDP
    PDP --> PA
    PDP --> IDP
    PDP --> DHR
    SCADA --> SIEM
    HIST --> SIEM
    SIEM -. RISK FEEDBACK .-> PDP
    PA -. POLICY UPDATES .-> PEP1
    PA -. POLICY UPDATES .-> PEP2
```

**Key Architectural Properties:**
- **No implicit trust** between any two nodes, even within the same subnet.
- **Continuous risk scoring** at every PEP based on identity, device posture, behavioural baseline, and time-of-day.
- **Bidirectional feedback** from SIEM to PDP closes the adaptive loop.

- **Smart contracts automate** compliance with IEC 62443 and sectoral regulations.
