```mermaid
    subgraph SENS_NET["SENSOR NETWORK"]
        TS["TELEMETRY STREAM"]
        HS["HEALTH STREAM"]
        CS["COMMAND STREAM"]
    end

    subgraph TWIN_CORE["DIGITAL TWIN CORE"]
        MIR["MIRROR STATE REPOSITORY"]
        SIM["PHYSICS SIMULATOR"]
        KAL["KALMAN STATE ESTIMATOR"]
        PRED["PREDICTIVE ENGINE"]
    end

    subgraph SEC_USE["SECURITY USE CASES"]
        REDT["RED TEAM ATTACK SIMULATION"]
        FORENSIC["FORENSIC REPLAY"]
        PATCH["PATCH VALIDATION"]
        WHATIF["WHAT IF ANALYSIS"]
    end

    EQ1 --> TS
    EQ2 --> TS
    EQ3 --> HS
    TS --> MIR
    HS --> MIR
    CS --> MIR
    MIR --> KAL
    KAL --> SIM
    SIM --> PRED
    PRED --> REDT
    PRED --> FORENSIC
    PRED --> PATCH
    PRED --> WHATIF
    REDT --> EQ1
    PATCH --> EQ2
```

**Security Value Proposition:**
- **Zero-risk vulnerability discovery** — attacks are executed on the virtual replica.
- **Predictive defense** — the predictive engine forecasts the *propagation path* of a real attack 30–60 seconds before it spreads, allowing proactive isolation.
- **Compliance by demonstration** — auditors can replay any incident on the twin to verify the operator's response.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)*. List any **four** core principles of ZTA as per NIST SP 800-207 and state why ZTA is more suitable than perimeter-based security for modern CPS.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) is a security model defined by NIST SP 800-207 (2020) in which no entity — user, device, or service — is trusted by default, regardless of whether it resides inside or outside the traditional network perimeter. Trust must be *continuously earned* through explicit verification.

**Four Core Principles (1 Mark):**
1. **Verify explicitly** — Authenticate and authorize *every* transaction using identity, device posture, location, and behavioural context.
2. **Least-privilege access** — Grant minimum permissions via Just-In-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA).
3. **Assume breach** — Architect the system as if the attacker is *already inside*; minimize blast radius via micro-segmentation.
4. **Continuous validation** — Risk is re-evaluated on every request, not once at session login.

**Suitability for CPS (1 Mark):** Modern CPS integrate IT and OT networks, eroding the traditional perimeter. A single compromised engineering workstation on the OT side can give an attacker lateral access to all PLCs. ZTA's micro-segmentation and continuous verification *contain* lateral movement at the device level, which is essential for safety-critical CPS where a single compromised actuator can cause physical harm.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between **Federated Learning (FL)** and **Centralized Machine Learning** in the context of CPS security analytics. Mention **one advantage** and **one limitation** of FL.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Differentiation (2 Marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Centralized ML** | **Federated Learning** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Location | All raw sensor data sent to a central server | Raw data stays at the local edge node |
| Privacy Exposure | High — raw CPS telemetry leaves the device | Low — only model gradients/weights are shared |
| Bandwidth Requirement | Very high (continuous raw data upload) | Low (only periodic gradient transmission) |
| Attack Surface | Central server is a single high-value target | Distributed; no single point of compromise |
| Suitability for CPS | Poor for bandwidth-constrained IIoT | Excellent for distributed CPS edge nodes |

**Advantage of FL (0.5 Mark):** Preserves data sovereignty and confidentiality — critical for healthcare CPS subject to DPDPA 2023 and for cross-organization industrial collaborations.

**Limitation of FL (0.5 Mark):** Susceptible to *poisoning attacks* where a malicious edge node submits corrupted gradients to bias the global model. Defenses (Krum, Multi-Krum, robust aggregation) add computational overhead.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-internal choice. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and working of a Post-Quantum Cryptographic (PQC) migration strategy** for a Cyber-Physical System. In your answer:
- (i) Identify *two* classical cryptographic primitives that are vulnerable to quantum attacks and the *specific quantum algorithm* that breaks each. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Describe the *three* NIST-standardized PQC algorithms finalized in 2024 along with the *hard mathematical problem* each is based on. *(3 marks)*
- (iii) Explain the concept of *crypto-agility* and *hybrid deployment* during PQC migration. *(2 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A smart-grid CPS uses ML-based anomaly detection. After deployment, the confusion matrix for one week is:

|  | **Predicted Normal** | **Predicted Attack** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Normal** | 49,200 | 300 |
| **Actual Attack** | 100 | 400 |

- (i) Calculate the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and FPR**. Show all steps. *(4 marks)*
- (ii) Is this IDS suitable for *safety-critical* deployment? Justify with at least two reasons. Suggest one specific improvement. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Blockchain Technology** in securing Cyber-Physical Systems.
- (i) Explain *why* a *permissioned* blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) is preferred over a public one (e.g., Bitcoin) for CPS. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Describe the **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)** consensus mechanism and compute the maximum number of faulty nodes it can tolerate in a network of **10 validator nodes**. *(3 marks)*
- (iii) Illustrate, with a use case, how **smart contracts** automate access control in a CPS. *(2 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **Digital Twin** as a security tool for CPS.
- (i) Define *digital twin* and state *three* security use cases. *(3 marks)*
- (ii) Describe the *state synchronization* mechanism using a **Kalman filter**, with the prediction and update equations. *(3 marks)*
- (iii) List *two* limitations of digital-twin-based security analysis. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Model Solutions — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — PQC Migration**

**(i) Quantum-Vulnerable Primitives (2 Marks):**

| **Primitive** | **Function** | **Quantum Algorithm** | **Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|
| RSA-2048 | Public-key encryption, digital signatures | Shor's Algorithm | Broken in polynomial time $O((\log N)^3)$ |
| ECDSA (secp256r1) | Digital signatures, key exchange | Shor's Algorithm | Broken in polynomial time |
| AES-128 | Symmetric encryption | Grover's Algorithm | Effective security halved: $2^{128} \to 2^{64}$ |
| SHA-256 | Hash function | Grover (brute-force pre-image) | Effective pre-image resistance halved |

**(ii) NIST 2024 PQC Standards (3 Marks):**

| **Standard** | **FIPS Number** | **Type** | **Underlying Hard Problem** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ML-KEM** (formerly CRYSTALS-Kyber) | FIPS 203 | Key Encapsulation Mechanism | Module Learning With Errors (M-LWE) over lattices |
| **ML-DSA** (formerly CRYSTALS-Dilithium) | FIPS 204 | Digital Signature | Module Learning With Errors (M-LWE) + Module Short Integer Solution (M-SIS) |
| **SLH-DSA** (formerly SPHINCS+) | FIPS 205 | Stateless Hash-Based Signature | Security of underlying hash function (collision/preimage resistance) |

*Note:* **FALCON** (also lattice-based) is expected as a future FIPS standard for compact signatures.

**(iii) Crypto-Agility and Hybrid Deployment (2 Marks):**

- **Crypto-agility:** A system design principle where cryptographic algorithms are *abstracted* behind interfaces (e.g., a `CryptoProvider` class) so that algorithms can be swapped (RSA → ML-KEM) *without* rewriting the application logic. This is a mandatory pre-requisite for PQC migration in long-lifecycle CPS (e.g., power-grid PLCs with 20-year operational lifetimes).
- **Hybrid deployment:** During the transition period, both classical (e.g., ECDH) and PQC (e.g., ML-KEM) key exchanges are performed in parallel, and the derived shared secrets are *concatenated* and hashed to produce the final session key. This ensures that the system remains secure if *either* the classical or PQC algorithm is broken (defense in depth against both classical and quantum adversaries).

#### **Part (b) — IDS Confusion Matrix**

**Given values:** $TP = 400$, $FP = 300$, $FN = 100$, $TN = 49{,}200$.

**(i) Calculations (4 Marks):**

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{400}{400 + 300} = \frac{400}{700} = 0.5714
$$

**Recall (TPR):**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{400}{400 + 100} = \frac{400}{500} = 0.8000
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.5714 \times 0.8000}{0.5714 + 0.8000} = \frac{0.9143}{1.3714} = 0.6667
$$

**False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{300}{300 + 49{,}200} = \frac{300}{49{,}500} = 0.00606
$$

**(ii) Safety-Critical Suitability (3 Marks):**

- **Not suitable as-is.** Justifications:
  1. **High FNR (False Negative Rate):** $FNR = FN / (FN + TP) = 100 / 500 = 0.20$ (20%). One in five attacks is *missed*. In a smart grid, a missed false-data-injection attack on the Automatic Generation Control (AGC) loop could destabilize the frequency of an entire regional grid.
  2. **Low Precision (0.57):** 43% of alerts are false alarms. In a control-room with 300 false alarms per week, operator *alarm fatigue* develops, leading to genuine alerts being ignored — a documented cause of major industrial incidents (e.g., the 2003 Northeast blackout).
- **Specific improvement:** Implement a **two-stage detection cascade** — Stage 1: low-threshold ML for high recall; Stage 2: physics-based *consistency check* (e.g., verify that reported power flow obeys Kirchhoff's Current Law) to filter false positives. This *ensemble* approach is shown to lift F1 above 0.90 in the SWaT and WADI datasets.

---

### **Model Solutions — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Blockchain in CPS**

**(i) Permissioned vs Public Blockchain (2 Marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Public (Bitcoin/Ethereum)** | **Permissioned (Hyperledger Fabric)** |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | 7–30 TPS (Bitcoin), ~15 TPS (Ethereum PoW) | 1,000–20,000 TPS |
| Latency | ~10 min (Bitcoin) to 13 sec (Ethereum) | Sub-second finality |
| Energy | Prohibitively high (PoW) | Negligible (PBFT) |
| Membership | Permissionless — anyone can join | Permissioned — KYC/identity verified |
| Consensus | PoW/PoS | PBFT, Raft, PoA |
| Suitability for CPS | Unsuitable | Highly suitable |

**(ii) PBFT Tolerance (3 Marks):**

PBFT tolerates $f$ Byzantine (malicious or faulty) nodes where:

$$
f = \left\lfloor \frac{n - 1}{3} \right\rfloor
$$

For $n = 10$:

$$
f = \left\lfloor \frac{10 - 1}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3.0 \right\rfloor = 3
$$

The system remains safe and live as long as **no more than 3 out of 10** nodes are compromised or malicious.

**Working of PBFT:** The protocol proceeds in three phases — *Pre-prepare*, *Prepare*, and *Commit*. A client request is broadcast by the primary; replicas exchange prepare messages; upon receiving $2f$ matching prepare messages, replicas broadcast commit messages; upon $2f + 1$ matching commit messages, the request is executed. The view-change protocol rotates the primary if it is suspected faulty.

**(iii) Smart Contract Use Case (2 Marks):**

**Use Case — Automated Access Control in a Substation:** A smart contract deployed on the consortium blockchain encodes the rule *"Only an operator with role = 'Senior_Engineer' AND device = 'PLC_Substation_7' AND time = 09:00–17:00 IST AND has passed MFA in the last 60 seconds may issue a 'close breaker' command."* When a PLC requests authorization, the contract atomically verifies the four conditions against on-chain identity registry and off-chain SAML/MFA service, then returns *Allow* or *Deny* in <100 ms. Every authorization decision is immutably logged on the chain, providing a regulator-ready audit trail. This eliminates the single point of trust failure inherent in a centralized RADIUS/LDAP server.

#### **Part (b) — Digital Twin for Security**

**(i) Definition and Use Cases (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** A **Digital Twin** is a continuously synchronized, high-fidelity virtual replica of a physical CPS asset or system, fed by real-time sensor data, that can be used for simulation, prediction, and analysis *without affecting* the physical system.

**Three Security Use Cases:**
1. **Red-Team Attack Simulation** — Execute replay, false-data injection, and DoS attacks on the twin to discover vulnerabilities safely.
2. **Predictive Attack Propagation** — Forecast the *spread* of an ongoing attack across coupled subsystems 30–60 seconds before it actually propagates.
3. **Patch Validation** — Test a firmware patch or new firewall rule on the twin *first*, ensuring it does not destabilize the control loop before deploying to production.

**(ii) Kalman Filter for State Synchronization (3 Marks):**

The Kalman filter estimates the state $x_k$ of the CPS in two steps:

**Prediction Step (time update):**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A_k \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B_k u_k
$$

$$
P_{k \mid k-1} = A_k P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A_k^T + Q_k
$$

**Update Step (measurement update):**

$$
K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H_k^T \big( H_k P_{k \mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k \big)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \big( z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} \big)
$$

$$
P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \mid k-1}
$$

Where $A_k$ is the state transition matrix (physical model), $B_k$ is the control input matrix, $H_k$ is the observation matrix, $Q_k$ and $R_k$ are process and measurement noise covariances, and $K_k$ is the Kalman gain. When the residual $z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ deviates significantly, this signals a *sensor anomaly* or an active *false-data-injection attack*, which can be detected by the IDS.

**(iii) Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. **Model fidelity requirement:** If the underlying physical model $A_k$ does not capture non-linearities (e.g., fluid hammer, phase transitions), the twin diverges from reality, producing misleading security conclusions. An Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) or Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) is required for non-linear systems, increasing complexity.
2. **Initial synchronization cost:** Constructing the twin from scratch for a legacy plant requires extensive historical data and reverse-engineering of undocumented control logic — often infeasible within project timelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Diagrams are mandatory in 14-mark questions.** A well-drawn Zero Trust or Blockchain architecture diagram can earn up to 2 marks even if the prose is weak. *Always* include a labelled block diagram.
> 2. **Don't confuse F1-Score with Accuracy.** Many students mistakenly quote accuracy (0.992) for imbalanced CPS datasets where the attack class is <1%. Always pair with FNR.
> 3. **PBFT formula is $\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$, NOT $(n-1)/2$** as in Raft/Paxos. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Cite the standard.** In PQC answers, mention *FIPS 203, 204, 205* explicitly. In ZTA answers, cite *NIST SP 800-207*. Examiners reward "standard-aware" answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Emerging Trends and Technologies**

- **AI/ML for CPS:** Use *unsupervised* models (Autoencoder, Isolation Forest) for zero-day detection; *supervised* models (RF, XGBoost) for known attack classes; *ensemble* for production deployment. Always report **F1 + FNR**, never accuracy alone.
- **Adversarial ML:** Attackers craft perturbations $||\delta||_p < \epsilon$ to fool CPS vision and control systems. Defenses: adversarial training, defensive distillation, randomized smoothing.
- **Blockchain:** Use *permissioned* (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum) with *PBFT* consensus for CPS. PBFT tolerates $\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ Byzantine nodes. Smart contracts encode access-control policies.
- **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207):** *Verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach, continuous validation.* Replaces perimeter-based security. Micro-segmentation is the operational cornerstone.
- **Edge/Fog Computing Security:** Move security enforcement to the network edge. Reduces latency, bandwidth, and attack surface. Enables *Edge AI* for real-time CPS threat detection.
- **5G/6G for CPS:** Network slicing enables isolated, customized security policies per CPS use case (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC). Slice isolation index $\eta = 1 - C_{cross}/C_{total}$ measures security.
- **Post-Quantum Cryptography:** NIST 2024 standards — **ML-KEM (FIPS 203)**, **ML-DSA (FIPS 204)**, **SLH-DSA (FIPS 205)**. Shor's breaks RSA/ECC; Grover halves symmetric-key security. *Crypto-agility* is mandatory; *hybrid deployment* during migration.
- **Digital Twins:** Real-time virtual replicas for *red-team simulation*, *predictive defense*, *patch validation*. State sync via **Kalman / Extended Kalman filter**. Limitations: model fidelity, initial calibration cost.
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Compute on ciphertext. Enables privacy-preserving CPS analytics. Computationally expensive; partially homomorphic (Paillier, ElGamal) is practical today.
- **Federated Learning:** Train shared model *without* centralizing raw CPS data. Reduces privacy risk and bandwidth. Vulnerable to *gradient poisoning*; defend with Krum, robust aggregation.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Shannon Entropy: $H(X) = -\sum p \log_2 p$
  - F1: $F_1 = 2PR / (P + R)$
  - PBFT: $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$
  - Grover: $T_{quantum} = 2^{n/2}$
  - MTBF-based Availability: $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$
- **Standards to Quote:** NIST SP 800-207 (ZTA), FIPS 203/204/205 (PQC), IEC 62443 (Industrial CPS), NIST IR 8259 (IoT), DPDPA 2023 (India Privacy).
- **Domain Anchors (use in answers):** Smart grid, autonomous vehicles, smart healthcare, Industry 4.0 factory, water-treatment SCADA, smart-city infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latest Advancements in CPS Security Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Latest Advancements in CPS Security Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are the "Latest Advancements" in CPS Security?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT863)** and **NEP 2020** outcome-based framework, *Latest Advancements in CPS Security Technologies* refers to the **frontier research and deployed engineering solutions that have emerged post-2020 to counter sophisticated, multi-vector attacks against Cyber-Physical Systems**. These are not legacy firewalls or signature-based antivirus tools — they represent **paradigm shifts** where security is *intrinsic, intelligent, decentralized, and mathematically provable*.

The technology stack typically covers six major pillars:

| # | Pillar | One-line Definition |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **AI/ML-Driven Intrusion Detection** | Self-learning models (LSTM, Transformers, GANs) that detect zero-day attacks in OT/ICS traffic. |
| 2 | **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** | "Never trust, always verify" — identity, device, and context-based micro-segmentation. |
| 3 | **Blockchain & Distributed Ledger Tech (DLT)** | Immutable, consensus-driven audit trails for CPS data and firmware. |
| 4 | **Quantum-Resistant Cryptography (PQC)** | Lattice, hash, and code-based algorithms resilient to Shor/Grover quantum attacks. |
| 5 | **Digital Twins for Security** | A virtual replica of the CPS used for *what-if* attack simulation and predictive defense. |
| 6 | **Confidential Computing (TEE / PUF)** | Hardware-isolated enclaves (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) and Physically Unclonable Functions for root-of-trust. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to demonstrate awareness of *emerging defense paradigms* — not just the names, but the **threat model each technology solves**, and **why legacy solutions fail against modern APTs (Advanced Persistent Threats)** targeting CPS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Metaphor

Imagine a **smart hospital** where life-support ventilators, MRI machines, infusion pumps, and EHR databases are all interconnected (the *Cyber-Physical System*).

- **Old Security (Perimeter-based):** A big locked front door. Once a hacker (e.g., ransomware) sneaks in via a contractor's infected laptop, the entire hospital is compromised. This is the *castle-and-moat* model — and it has failed repeatedly (e.g., the 2017 *WannaCry* and 2021 *Colonial Pipeline* attacks).
- **New Advancements:**
  - **AI/ML IDS** = A 24×7 AI radiologist that scans every patient-vital data stream for *anomalous patterns* the human eye cannot see in real time.
  - **Zero Trust** = Even the *senior cardiologist* must re-authenticate via fingerprint + OTP before touching a specific ventilator.
  - **Blockchain** = An immutable, tamper-proof logbook where every drug-dosage change is signed and time-stamped — *no one can secretly alter a record*.
  - **PQC** = Future-proofing the hospital's locks so that even when *quantum thieves* arrive, the medical records remain unintelligible.
  - **Digital Twin** = A *virtual replica* of the hospital's network running in parallel, where the security team stages ransomware attacks to find weaknesses *before* real attackers exploit them.
  - **TEE / PUF** = A *biometric safe inside the MRI machine itself* — the firmware decryption key is born from the silicon's microscopic imperfections and never leaves the chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Modern CPS security is **defense-in-depth plus defense-in-intelligence**. The single-pillar "firewall" era is over; the new model is *layered, adaptive, and cryptographically agile*.

## 1.3 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Examiner-Ready)

> **Latest Advancements in CPS Security Technologies** constitute the **convergence of artificial intelligence, post-quantum cryptography, distributed ledgers, hardware-rooted trust, and digital twin simulation** — engineered to provide *provable security guarantees* (rather than probabilistic ones) for safety-critical CPS deployments in Industry 4.0, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, and medical IoT.

The **standard security metrics** used to evaluate these technologies (highlighted in bold for KTU 14-mark answers):

- **P_d (Probability of Detection)** — typically targeted at ≥ **0.99** for CPS-grade IDS.
- **P_fa (Probability of False Alarm)** — must be ≤ **0.001** to avoid operator fatigue.
- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — modern AI-based systems target **< 1 second** for OT traffic.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond)** — Zero Trust micro-segmentation targets **< 30 seconds** for automated containment.
- **NIST CSF 2.0 Tiers** — Advancements must move organizations from Tier 1 (*Reactive*) to Tier 4 (*Adaptive*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Each Advancement Exists — The "Threat → Defense" Logic Chain

### 2.1.1 AI/ML-Driven Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)

- **Why it exists:** Signature-based IDS (Snort, Suricata with old rulesets) **cannot detect zero-day attacks** because no signature exists yet. CPS traffic is also *highly imbalanced* (98% normal, 2% attack).
- **How it works (operational logic):**
  1. **Data Acquisition:** Passive tapping of OT protocols (Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP) using TAPs or SPAN ports.
  2. **Feature Engineering:** Extracts temporal features (packet inter-arrival time), statistical features (entropy of register addresses), and protocol features (function codes).
  3. **Model Training:** Supervised (Random Forest, XGBoost) for known attacks; unsupervised (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders) for zero-days; deep learning (LSTM, Transformer) for sequence attacks.
  4. **Inference:** Real-time scoring on edge devices (NVIDIA Jetson, Google Coral).
  5. **Response:** SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, Response) integration for automated PLC shutdown or VLAN isolation.

- **The "Why" — Engineering Rationale:** CPS environments cannot tolerate high false positives because a false alarm on a nuclear reactor SCRAM signal could trigger a multi-million-dollar shutdown. Hence, modern systems use **hybrid models (semi-supervised + reinforcement learning)** that *learn from operator feedback* — closing the loop.

### 2.1.2 Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) — NIST SP 800-207

- **Why it exists:** Perimeter security assumed internal networks were safe. Once an attacker breaches the perimeter (e.g., via phishing, supply chain), lateral movement is trivial.
- **How it works (operational logic):**
  1. **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** evaluates every access request.
  2. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** is the gateway/device that grants or denies.
  3. **Continuous Authentication:** Re-verifies identity, device posture, location, and behavior.
  4. **Micro-segmentation:** Divides the CPS into tiny network zones (e.g., one PLC per zone).
  5. **Least-Privilege Access:** Engineers get access only to specific HMI screens for limited durations.

- **The "Why" — Engineering Rationale:** A compromise of an engineering laptop in the *Enterprise Zone* cannot pivot to the *Safety Zone* (where SIS — Safety Instrumented Systems — live). The blast radius is contained to one segment.

### 2.1.3 Blockchain for CPS

- **Why it exists:** Centralized log servers are single points of failure. An attacker who compromises the SIEM can *erase evidence* of intrusion.
- **How it works:** Each PLC event is hashed, signed, and added to a tamper-evident chain. Consensus (PBFT, PoA — Proof of Authority in permissioned chains like Hyperledger Fabric) ensures no single node can rewrite history.
- **The "Why":** Provides *non-repudiation* and *forensic integrity* — critical for incident response in regulated industries (IEC 62443, NERC CIP).

### 2.1.4 Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

- **Why it exists:** **Shor's algorithm** running on a sufficiently large quantum computer (≈ 4,000 logical qubits) can break RSA-2048 and ECC in polynomial time. "Harvest now, decrypt later" attacks mean adversaries are *already stealing* today's CPS data to decrypt in 2030+.
- **How it works:** NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203 ML-KEM, FIPS 204 ML-DSA, FIPS 205 SLH-DSA) replace integer-factorization and discrete-log hardness assumptions with **lattice problems** (LWE, Ring-LWE), **hash-based signatures**, and **code-based schemes (McEliece)**.

### 2.1.5 Digital Twin for Security (DT-S)

- **Why it exists:** Testing security patches on a *live* power grid or refinery is infeasible. Staging attacks on production systems violates IEC 62443-3-3 SL-3.
- **How it works:** A high-fidelity simulation (using Ansys, MATLAB/Simulink, NS-3, or specialized tools like **Claroty xDome**, **Nozomi Networks Guardian**) mirrors the real CPS. Red teams launch attacks; blue teams defend; AI trains on the results.
- **The "Why":** Enables *predictive defense* and *safe validation* of zero-day patches.

### 2.1.6 Confidential Computing & Hardware Roots of Trust

- **TEE (Trusted Execution Environment):** Intel SGX, AMD SEV, ARM TrustZone — creates an encrypted memory region (*enclave*) where code + data are invisible to the OS/hypervisor.
- **PUF (Physically Unclonable Function):** Exploits manufacturing variations in SRAM power-up states to derive a unique, unclonable key. **No key is stored** — it is *born* from silicon.
- **Why:** Protects against insider threats, malicious firmware updates, and supply-chain implants.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Boundary / Standard Value | Real-World Utility |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **IDS Detection Performance** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $F_1 \geq 0.95$ target for CPS IDS | Balances precision (P) and recall (R) on imbalanced data |
| 2 | **Bayes Optimal Detection** | $P_d = 1 - \beta$ where $\beta = P(\text{accept } H_0 \vert H_1)$ | $\beta \leq 0.01$ (Neyman-Pearson) | Minimizes missed attacks at fixed false-alarm rate |
| 3 | **Shannon Entropy (CPS Anomaly)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $H < 0.5$ bits ⇒ anomalous | Detects encrypted C2 beacons in OT traffic |
| 4 | **Zero Trust Risk Score** | $R = w_1 \cdot I + w_2 \cdot D + w_3 \cdot C$ | $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ | Identity (I), Device (D), Context (C) weighted decision |
| 5 | **Blockchain Block Time** | $T_{finality} = k \cdot T_{block}$ | Hyperledger Fabric: $k=2$, $T_{block} \approx 1\text{s}$ | Latency budget for CPS audit logs |
| 6 | **Lattice Hardness (LWE)** | $\Vert A \vec{s} + \vec{e} - \vec{b} \Vert \geq q/4$ with prob. $\leq 2^{-\lambda}$ | $\lambda = 128$ bits (NIST Level 1) | Underpins CRYSTALS-Kyber (ML-KEM) |
| 7 | **Quantum Bit Cost (Shor)** | $Q = 2n + 2\lceil \log_2 n \rceil + 9$ | RSA-2048 ⇒ ~$4096$ logical qubits | Justifies PQC migration timeline |
| 8 | **PUF Uniqueness** | $U = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \text{HD}(R_i, R_j)$ | $U \in [49\%, 51\%]$ | Verifies each PUF instance is unique |
| 9 | **PUF Reliability** | $Rel = 1 - \frac{\#\text{bit-flips across temps}}{N \cdot T}$ | $Rel \geq 0.95$ | Stability across temperature/voltage |
| 10 | **TEE Attestation Latency** | $T_{attest} = T_{quote} + T_{verify}$ | $T_{attest} \leq 100$ ms | CPS real-time budget |
| 11 | **MTTD Improvement (AI)** | $MTTD_{new} = MTTD_{legacy} \cdot (1 - \eta)$ | $\eta \in [0.7, 0.9]$ typical | Quantifies AI ROI for CPS security |
| 12 | **Crypto-agility Cost** | $C_{agility} = \frac{T_{rekey} + T_{retest}}{T_{system}}$ | $\leq 5\%$ of system uptime | Cost of swapping RSA→PQC algorithms |
| 13 | **Federated Learning Convergence** | $\Vert w^{(t)} - w^{*} \Vert^2 \leq \frac{\eta}{t} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \sigma_k^2$ | Non-IID data increases $\sigma_k$ | Privacy-preserving IDS across multiple CPS sites |
| 14 | **Moving Target Defense (MTD) Entropy** | $H_{MTD} = -\sum_{i=1}^{M} \frac{1}{M} \log_2 \frac{1}{M} = \log_2 M$ | $M \geq 2^{10}$ IP addresses | Attacker's scan cost grows exponentially |
| 15 | **Digital Twin Fidelity Error** | $E_{fidelity} = \frac{\Vert y_{real} - y_{twin} \Vert_2}{\Vert y_{real} \Vert_2}$ | $E_{fidelity} \leq 0.05$ | Validates twin usability for security testing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The metrics above are the *lingua franca* of CPS security literature. KTU 14-mark answers that reference these equations (with units) consistently earn full credit because they demonstrate the student can quantify — not just describe — security claims.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes-Optimal Threshold for CPS Intrusion Detection

The CPS anomaly-detection problem is a classical **binary hypothesis testing** problem:

- $H_0$ (null hypothesis): the OT traffic sample is *normal*.
- $H_1$ (alternate hypothesis): the traffic contains an *attack* signature (e.g., unauthorized Modbus write).

Let the test statistic be $T(x)$ — e.g., the reconstruction error of an autoencoder on packet features. We seek a threshold $\tau$ such that the **Neyman-Pearson criterion** is satisfied at a fixed false-alarm probability $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Set the false-alarm constraint:**

$$
P(T(x) \geq \tau \vert H_0) = \alpha
$$

**Step 2 — Express the detection probability:**

$$
P_d = P(T(x) \geq \tau \vert H_1) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{T \vert H_1}(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT):**

The Bayes-optimal test (by the Neyman-Pearson lemma) is the **likelihood ratio test**:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{f(x \vert H_1)}{f(x \vert H_0)} \underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}}{\gtrless}} \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the threshold chosen to satisfy $P(\Lambda(x) \geq \eta \vert H_0) = \alpha$.

**Step 4 — Specialize to Gaussian assumptions (e.g., for a one-dimensional feature $x$):**

$$
f(x \vert H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_0} \exp\left( -\frac{(x - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right)
$$

$$
f(x \vert H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_1} \exp\left( -\frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Form the log-likelihood ratio:**

$$
\ln \Lambda(x) = \ln \frac{\sigma_0}{\sigma_1} - \frac{(x - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} + \frac{(x - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate $x$ (assuming $\sigma_0 = \sigma_1 = \sigma$ for simplicity — the homoscedastic case common in CPS baseline traffic):**

$$
\ln \Lambda(x) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \left[ (x - \mu_0)^2 - (x - \mu_1)^2 \right] = \frac{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)}{\sigma^2} \cdot x + \frac{\mu_0^2 - \mu_1^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the optimal decision threshold $x_{\tau}$:**

The test $\Lambda(x) \geq \eta$ is monotonic in $x$ (for $\mu_1 > \mu_0$). Thus:

$$
x \geq \tau \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\sigma^2 \ln \eta + \frac{\mu_0^2 - \mu_1^2}{2}}{\mu_1 - \mu_0}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the achieved detection probability:**

$$
P_d = Q\!\left( \frac{\tau - \mu_1}{\sigma_1} \right) = 1 - \Phi\!\left( \frac{\tau - \mu_1}{\sigma} \right)
$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian tail function and $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

**Step 9 — Express ROC via deflection coefficient:**

The *deflection* $d = \frac{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2}{\sigma^2}$ summarizes separability. A KTU-grade IDS should achieve $d \geq 9$ (i.e., 3-$\sigma$ separation) for the chosen feature.

**Final Result (KTU-Ready):**

$$
\boxed{\; P_d = 1 - \Phi\!\left( \frac{\sigma^2 \ln \eta + \frac{\mu_0^2 - \mu_1^2}{2} - \mu_1(\mu_1 - \mu_0)}{\sigma(\mu_1 - \mu_0)} \right)\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU answers:** The threshold $\tau$ is *not* chosen arbitrarily — it is set by the **Neyman-Pearson lemma** to maximize $P_d$ subject to a fixed $\alpha$. In CPS, $\alpha$ is typically $\leq 10^{-3}$ because operator alarm fatigue is a safety hazard.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lattice-Based Security (LWE Problem → PQC Foundation)

**Step 1 — Define the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem:**

Given $A \in \mathbb{Z}_q^{n \times m}$, $\vec{s} \in \mathbb{Z}_q^n$, and a small error vector $\vec{e} \in \mathbb{Z}_q^m$ drawn from a narrow Gaussian $\chi$:

$$
\vec{b} = A\vec{s} + \vec{e} \pmod{q}
$$

The **decision-LWE** problem: distinguish $(A, \vec{b})$ from a uniformly random pair.

**Step 2 — Why it is hard (worst-case reduction):**

By the **Regev reduction** (2005, JACM), breaking *average-case* LWE is at least as hard as solving *worst-case* lattice problems (GapSVP, SIVP) on $n$-dimensional lattices in $\mathbb{Z}^n$. Best known algorithms run in time:

$$
T = 2^{O(n)}
$$

For NIST Level 1 security ($\lambda = 128$ bits), $n = 512$ is typical for Kyber-512.

**Step 3 — Build a public-key encryption scheme (Kyber / ML-KEM, FIPS 203):**

- **Key Generation:** Sample $A$, $\vec{s}, \vec{e}$ (small). Public key: $(A, \vec{b} = A\vec{s} + \vec{e})$.
- **Encryption:** To encrypt message $m \in \{0,1\}^{256}$:
  - Sample $\vec{r}, \vec{e_1}, e_2$.
  - Compute $\vec{u} = A^T \vec{r} + \vec{e_1}$.
  - Compute $v = \vec{b}^T \vec{r} + e_2 + \frac{q}{2} m$.
- **Decryption:** Compute $m' = v - \vec{s}^T \vec{u} = \frac{q}{2} m + (\text{small noise})$. Round to nearest multiple of $q/2$ to recover $m$.

**Step 4 — Security bound:**

$$
\lambda \approx n \cdot \log_2 q - \log_2(\sigma)
$$

For Kyber-512: $n = 256$ (in module form), $q = 3329$, $\sigma \approx 1.5$, giving $\lambda \approx 118$–$128$ bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When asked "Why is PQC needed?", the correct answer chains: *Shor's algorithm → breaks RSA/ECC → 'harvest now, decrypt later' → lattice problems are quantum-safe because the best quantum algorithm (Grover) only gives a square-root speedup, not polynomial*. LWE remains $2^{O(n)}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Zero Trust Risk Score Computation

**Step 1 — Define the input signals:**

- $I \in [0,1]$: Identity confidence (MFA = 1.0, password only = 0.4).
- $D \in [0,1]$: Device posture (patched + EDR = 1.0, unpatched = 0.0).
- $C \in [0,1]$: Context anomaly (normal hours + location = 1.0, 3 AM from foreign IP = 0.0).

**Step 2 — Compute the composite risk score:**

$$
R = w_I I + w_D D + w_C C
$$

For a CPS environment, typical weights (per NIST SP 800-207):

$$
w_I = 0.40, \quad w_D = 0.40, \quad w_C = 0.20
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the policy decision:**

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Allow} & \text{if } R \geq 0.80 \\
\text{Step-up MFA} & \text{if } 0.50 \leq R < 0.80 \\
\text{Deny} & \text{if } R < 0.50
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Continuous re-evaluation:**

The PDP refreshes $R$ every $\Delta t$ (typically 60 s) and on every new telemetry event.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often describe Zero Trust as a *product* (e.g., "Okta ZTA"). It is **not** — it is an *architectural philosophy* and a set of guiding principles (NIST SP 800-207). Mentioning a specific vendor tool without the framework is a 3-mark deduction.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) as per NIST SP 800-207. List its three core principles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Zero Trust Architecture is a security model that assumes no implicit trust is granted to assets or user accounts based solely on their physical or network location (i.e., inside or outside the network perimeter). Every access request is fully authenticated, authorized, and encrypted.
- **Three core principles (2 marks — 1 each, name + brief):**
  1. **Never trust, always verify** — Continuous validation of identity, device, and context.
  2. **Least-privilege access (just-in-time, just-enough)** — Minimal access for the minimal duration.
  3. **Assume breach** — Design the network to limit blast radius through micro-segmentation.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Physically Unclonable Function (PUF)? How does it differ from a traditional key stored in flash memory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **PUF Definition (2 marks):** A PUF is a hardware security primitive that exploits the **unavoidable, random manufacturing variations** in semiconductor devices (e.g., SRAM power-up states, ring oscillator frequencies) to produce a unique, unpredictable, and unclonable response when queried with a challenge (CRPs — Challenge-Response Pairs).
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** A traditional key is *stored* in non-volatile memory and can be *extracted* by physical attacks (chip decapping, probing). A PUF *derives* the key on-demand from silicon physics — **the key never exists in storage**, making it inherently tamper-resistant.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as 7 + 7. You must answer either Option A OR Option B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A: [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of an **AI/ML-driven Intrusion Detection System (IDS) for CPS**. Discuss the challenges of deploying supervised learning in OT environments and how **federated learning** addresses them.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **Digital Twin-based security validation framework** for a Cyber-Physical System. Highlight its advantages over testing on live production systems.

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (a) — 7 marks

**1. Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Layer 1 — Data Acquisition:** Passive tapping of OT networks (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104) via industrial TAPs. Data is pre-processed: parsed into transaction records, normalized, and feature-engineered (temporal: inter-arrival times; statistical: entropy of function codes; protocol: read/write ratios).
- **Layer 2 — Model Layer:** Hybrid AI model:
  - **Supervised component (XGBoost/Random Forest):** Trained on labeled datasets (e.g., ICS-x dataset, SWaT, WADI) to detect *known* attacks.
  - **Unsupervised component (Autoencoder / Isolation Forest):** Trained *only* on normal traffic baselines to detect *zero-day* deviations via reconstruction error.
  - **Sequential component (LSTM / Transformer):** Captures temporal dependencies in multi-step attacks (e.g., reconnaissance → exploitation → lateral movement).
- **Layer 3 — Decision & Response:** Threshold-based alarm generation → SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, Response) for automated PLC-mode changes, VLAN isolation, or engineer notification.

**2. Supervised Learning Challenges in OT (2 marks):**
- **Class imbalance:** Normal traffic is ~98%, attack traffic ~2%. Vanilla classifiers are biased toward the majority class. Solutions: **SMOTE**, **class-weighted loss**, **F1-score optimization**.
- **Label scarcity:** Labeling CPS attacks requires expensive SCADA experts. New attack types emerge continuously.
- **Concept drift:** CPS behavior changes with firmware updates, new production recipes, or seasonal loads. Static models decay.
- **Operational risk:** False positives can trigger unnecessary plant shutdowns (e.g., spurious SCRAM in a nuclear plant).

**3. Federated Learning Solution (2 marks):**
- Multiple CPS sites (e.g., 10 substations) collaboratively train a shared global model **without sharing raw OT data** (preserving data privacy and regulatory compliance).
- **Algorithm:** FedAvg — each site trains locally for $E$ epochs → sends only model weights $\Delta w$ to a central server → server aggregates via $w_{global} = \sum_k \frac{n_k}{N} w_k$ → distributes updated weights.
- **Benefit:** Leverages collective attack intelligence (rare attacks at one site improve all sites) while keeping site-specific sensitive traffic local.
- **Challenge:** Non-IID data (each site has unique load profiles) requires **FedProx** or **personalized FL**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (b) — 7 marks

**1. Digital Twin Definition (1 mark):** A digital twin is a high-fidelity, real-time virtual replica of a physical CPS — including its OT network, PLC logic, and HMI screens — used for safe experimentation.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw the diagram from Section 4.5 above — the student should reproduce the three-layer architecture: Real CPS | Digital Twin Layer | Defense Layer.)*

**3. Framework Components (2 marks):**
- **Simulation Engine:** Tools like MATLAB/Simulink, Ansys Twin Builder, or NS-3 for network emulation. Must replicate Modbus register maps, PLC scan times, and process dynamics.
- **Attack Injection Module:** Red-team toolkit (Caldera, Atomic Red Team adapted for ICS) that injects MITM, replay, DoS, and false-data injection attacks.
- **Vulnerability Scanner:** ICS-specific tools (e.g., Claroty, Nozomi, Tenable.ot) probing the twin.
- **Patch Validation Sandbox:** Tests firmware patches, configuration changes, and rule updates before live deployment.

**4. Advantages over Live Testing (2 marks):**
- **Zero production risk:** Attacks cannot cause real safety incidents or financial loss.
- **Reproducibility:** Attack scenarios can be replayed with deterministic seeds for regression testing.
- **Speed:** A year of real traffic can be simulated in hours via time-scaling.
- **Compliance:** Aligns with **IEC 62443-3-3 SL-3** which mandates testing of changes in a non-production environment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B: [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **threat of quantum computing to classical cryptography** in CPS. Explain the **Learning With Errors (LWE) problem** and how it forms the basis of NIST's post-quantum standard **ML-KEM (FIPS 203)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)** and **Physically Unclonable Function (PUF)** as hardware roots of trust. How do they jointly secure a CPS firmware update?

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (a) — 7 marks

**1. Quantum Threat (2 marks):**
- **Shor's algorithm (1994):** Runs in polynomial time $O((\log N)^3)$ on a quantum computer and breaks:
  - **RSA** (integer factorization) — $N = p \cdot q$.
  - **ECC** (discrete logarithm) — basis of ECDSA, ECDH.
- **Threat to CPS:** A large-scale quantum computer (estimated 20M physical qubits, 4K logical qubits) breaks RSA-2048 in hours. The *harvest-now, decrypt-later* attack means adversaries (e.g., nation-state APTs) are already archiving encrypted CPS telemetry, firmware updates, and grid commands for future decryption.
- **Grover's algorithm:** Gives a quadratic speedup on symmetric keys — AES-128 effective strength drops to 64 bits. **Mitigation:** Double key size to AES-256.

**2. LWE Problem (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Given $A \in \mathbb{Z}_q^{n \times m}$, $\vec{s} \in \mathbb{Z}_q^n$, $\vec{e} \leftarrow \chi$ (small), distinguish $(A, A\vec{s} + \vec{e} \mod q)$ from uniform random.
- **Hardness:** Average-case LWE $\equiv$ worst-case lattice problems (GapSVP$_\gamma$, SIVP$_\gamma$) on $n$-dim lattices. Best known algorithms: BKZ lattice reduction, running in $2^{O(n)}$.
- **Post-quantum security:** No known quantum algorithm does better than classical (unlike Shor's polynomial break of RSA). Hence LWE is the foundation of PQC.

**3. ML-KEM (Kyber) Construction (2 marks):**
- **Public key:** $(A, \vec{b} = A\vec{s} + \vec{e})$.
- **Encryption:** Sample $\vec{r}, \vec{e_1}, e_2$; compute $\vec{u} = A^T \vec{r} + \vec{e_1}$, $v = \vec{b}^T \vec{r} + e_2 + \frac{q}{2} m$.
- **Decryption:** $m \approx v - \vec{s}^T \vec{u}$ (mod $q$, rounded).
- **NIST Status:** FIPS 203 (August 2024) — primary PQC KEM.

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (b) — 7 marks

**1. TEE Definition (2 marks):**
- A TEE is an isolated, hardware-enforced execution environment within a processor (e.g., **Intel SGX**, **AMD SEV**, **ARM TrustZone**) that protects code and data from the host OS, hypervisor, and physical attackers.
- **Key properties:** Encrypted memory regions (*enclaves*), *attestation* (remote verification of enclave integrity), and *sealed storage* (binding secrets to the device).

**2. PUF Definition (2 marks):**
- A PUF derives a unique, unpredictable binary response from inherent manufacturing randomness in silicon (e.g., SRAM power-up, arbiter delays, optical scatter).
- **Properties:** Uniqueness (~50% Hamming Distance between chips), Reliability (>95% across temperatures), Unclonability (infeasible to physically duplicate).
- **Use:** Generates a device-specific root key on boot — never stored, never transmitted in plaintext.

**3. Joint CPS Firmware Update Flow (3 marks):**
*(Student should draw: Manufacturer → Signed Firmware + PQC Key Encapsulation → CPS Device → TEE Verifies Attestation → PUF Unwraps Key → Secure Boot Loads Firmware in Enclave → Hash Anchored on Blockchain Audit Log.)*

**Process steps:**
1. **Manufacturer side:** Signs firmware with ML-DSA (FIPS 204); encapsulates AES-256 key with ML-KEM.
2. **Device side — PUF bootstrap:** On power-up, PUF generates a unique seed → feeds a fuzzy extractor to derive a stable 256-bit key.
3. **TEE attestation:** Device's TEE generates a quote (signed hash of enclave state + PUF-derived identity) → manufacturer verifies.
4. **Key unwrap:** TEE decrypts the ML-KEM ciphertext using the device's PQC private key (stored in the enclave) → recovers AES key.
5. **Firmware verification:** TEE verifies ML-DSA signature, computes SHA-384 hash, and writes the firmware to a sealed memory region.
6. **Audit:** Firmware hash and attestation record are published to the blockchain audit ledger for non-repudiation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PQC with Quantum Cryptography (QKD):** PQC is *mathematical* (runs on classical computers, resists quantum attacks). QKD is *physics-based* (requires quantum hardware). They are **not the same**. Mentioning QKD when asked about PQC costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Treating Zero Trust as a product:** ZTA is an *architecture* (NIST SP 800-207), not a software purchase. Generic brand names without the framework = 0 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the threat model:** Every advancement must be justified by the *attack* it prevents. "AI helps security" without naming the specific threat = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting CPS-specific constraints:** CPS cannot tolerate the same network downtime as IT. Answers proposing "blockchain on every PLC" without addressing latency/throughput are penalized.
> 5. **Hand-waving PUF:** A common error is calling PUF a "secure storage" — it is a **key generation** primitive, not storage. The key is *derived*, not *stored*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Tape to Your Study Wall.**

- ✅ **Module 4 is about *what's coming next*, not legacy tools.** Examiners expect awareness of post-2020 paradigms.
- ✅ **Six pillars to memorize:** AI/ML IDS, ZTA, Blockchain, PQC, Digital Twin, TEE+PUF.
- ✅ **AI/ML IDS metrics:** $F_1 \geq 0.95$, $P_{fa} \leq 0.001$, MTTD < 1 s.
- ✅ **Zero Trust = NIST SP 800-207** — three principles: never trust, least privilege, assume breach.
- ✅ **Blockchain for CPS = permissioned** (Hyperledger Fabric, IOTA) — *not* public chains like Bitcoin (energy/throughput unfit for CPS).
- ✅ **PQC standards (finalized by NIST, Aug 2024):**
  - FIPS 203 = **ML-KEM** (Kyber) — key encapsulation.
  - FIPS 204 = **ML-DSA** (Dilithium) — digital signatures.
  - FIPS 205 = **SLH-DSA** (SPHINCS+) — hash-based backup signatures.
- ✅ **LWE hardness:** Lattice problems — quantum-safe because no polynomial-time quantum algorithm is known.
- ✅ **Shor's algorithm cost:** $\approx 4n$ logical qubits to break RSA-$n$ bits. RSA-2048 needs ~$4096$ logical qubits.
- ✅ **Digital Twin fidelity target:** Relative error $E_{fidelity} \leq 0.05$ (5%).
- ✅ **PUF is a *generator*, not a *storage*.** The key is derived from silicon randomness, never stored.
- ✅ **TEE + PUF = hardware root of trust** — the strongest layer against firmware tampering and insider threats.
- ✅ **Federated Learning** solves OT data scarcity and privacy — via FedAvg, $w_{global} = \sum_k \frac{n_k}{N} w_k$.
- ✅ **Crypto-agility** is the *practical* enabler of PQC migration — systems must swap algorithms without re-architecting.
- ✅ **IEC 62443, NIST CSF 2.0, and NIST SP 800-207** are the *compliance anchors* examiners love to cite.
- ✅ **Be ready to compare:** AI/ML IDS vs signature IDS, ZTA vs perimeter, PQC vs QKD, Digital Twin vs live testing.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU PECPT863 | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Emerging Technologies Such as Quantum Computing and Blockchain on CPS Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT863 - ADVANCED CYBERSECURITY STRATEGIES FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Emerging Technologies Such as Quantum Computing and Blockchain on CPS Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) — Refresher

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered mechanism whose operations are monitored, coordinated, controlled, and integrated by a computing and communication core interacting with physical processes. Sensors, actuators, and control loops are deeply intertwined with computation, networking, and data analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT863):** A CPS is a tightly integrated system combining sensing, computation, communication, and actuation with physical processes, where the embedded software directly influences the physical world. Examples include **Smart Grids**, **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**, **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, **SCADA**, **IoMT** (Internet of Medical Things), and **UAV swarms**.

## 1.2 Emerging Technologies: The Two Pillars

### 🔹 Quantum Computing
**Quantum Computing (QC)** is a paradigm of computation that exploits quantum-mechanical phenomena — **superposition**, **entanglement**, and **quantum interference** — to process information encoded in **qubits** (quantum bits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Quantum computing is the use of quantum-mechanical phenomena such as superposition and entanglement to perform computation. A qubit can exist in a linear combination of basis states $\vert 0 \rangle$ and $\vert 1 \rangle$, enabling **2ⁿ parallel state exploration** with just **n qubits** through *quantum parallelism*.

### 🔹 Blockchain
**Blockchain** is a distributed, append-only, tamper-evident digital ledger that records transactions in a cryptographically chained sequence of *blocks*, replicated across a peer-to-peer network and secured by **consensus protocols** (PoW, PoS, PBFT, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (NIST SP 800-183-aligned):** Blockchain is a distributed digital ledger technology in which transactions are grouped into blocks, each block cryptographically linked to its predecessor via a hash pointer, and validated by a decentralized consensus algorithm — ensuring **integrity**, **immutability**, **non-repudiation**, and **fault tolerance**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library of Secrets"

Imagine a **CPS as a fortress**:

- The **walls** = Classical cryptography (RSA, AES, ECC).
- The **guards** = Firewalls, IDS, access control.

### ⚡ Quantum Computing = The "Master Key-Maker"
Think of a classical computer as a librarian who must check **one book at a time** to find a hidden key. A **quantum computer**, by contrast, uses a magical search spell (Grover's algorithm) to scan **all books simultaneously**, and another spell (Shor's algorithm) to instantly reverse-engineer the lock's mathematical blueprint. In the wrong hands, this demolishes the fortress walls.

### 🔗 Blockchain = The "Indestructible Public Notary"
Imagine a fortress where every transaction (every door opened, every weapon fired) is written in **permanent ink** in a ledger. The ledger is copied across **thousands of independent scribes** (nodes). To alter a single entry, a malicious actor would have to forge the ink in every scribe's copy *and* rewrite the cryptographic seal — practically impossible. This is the **immutability guarantee** of blockchain.

## 1.4 The Convergence: Why It Matters for CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Emerging technologies create a **dual-impact security paradigm**:
> 1. **Disruptive Threat Vector** — Quantum breaks classical cryptography, threatening CPS that rely on RSA/ECC (e.g., V2X authentication, smart meter signing).
> 2. **Defensive Enabler** — Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and blockchain (ledger integrity, decentralized trust) offer novel defense primitives for CPS.

**Standard Metrics at a Glance:**
- **RSA-2048 classical security:** ≈ **112 bits** of security.
- **Shor's algorithm (Quantum):** reduces to **polynomial time** $O((\log N)^3)$.
- **Blockchain finality (Bitcoin):** ≈ **6 blocks × 10 min = 1 hour** (probabilistic finality).
- **Block time (Ethereum PoS):** ≈ **12 seconds** with **deterministic finality** after ~12.8 min.
- **Grover's speedup:** Quadratic — search in $\sqrt{N}$ vs classical $N$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Quantum Computing — Threat Anatomy for CPS

### 2.1.1 The Two Killer Algorithms

**A) Shor's Algorithm (1994) — The Cryptographic Nemesis**
- Solves **Integer Factorization** and **Discrete Logarithm** in polynomial time.
- **Target cryptosystems (widely used in CPS):**
  * **RSA** (smart meters, firmware signing in ICS)
  * **ECC / ECDSA** (V2X communication, IoT authentication)
  * **Diffie-Hellman** (TLS handshake in SCADA remote access)

**B) Grover's Algorithm (1996) — The Symmetric Brute-Forcer**
- Provides **quadratic speedup** for unstructured search.
- Affects **symmetric ciphers (AES-128, AES-256)** and **hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3)**.
- Effectively **halves the key length** ⇒ AES-128 → 64-bit quantum security ⇒ **migration to AES-256 recommended**.

### 2.1.2 Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) for CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST PQC Standards (FIPS 203, 204, 205 — August 2024):**
> - **ML-KEM (Kyber)** — Module-Lattice KEM → for key exchange in CPS.
> - **ML-DSA (Dilithium)** — Module-Lattice signature → for firmware / code signing in ICS.
> - **SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+)** — Hash-based signature → conservative backup.

CPS-specific challenges: **constrained devices** (sensors, RTUs), **real-time control loops**, **legacy protocols** (Modbus, DNP3, CAN bus) that cannot easily migrate.

## 2.2 Blockchain — Defensive Anatomy for CPS

### 2.2.1 Core Properties Mapping to CPS Security Goals

| Blockchain Property | CPS Security Goal | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Immutability** (via hash chain) | Integrity of logs/audit trails | Tamper-proof SCADA logs |
| **Decentralization** | Removes single point of failure | Resilient V2X PKI |
| **Transparency & auditability** | Accountability, non-repudiation | Firmware update provenance |
| **Smart contracts** | Automated policy enforcement | Access control in smart grid |
| **Consensus** | Byzantine fault tolerance | Trustless IoT coordination |

### 2.2.2 Hash Chain Mathematics

Each block $B_i$ contains:
- **Previous hash:** $H(B_{i-1})$
- **Merkle root:** $M_i = H(H(t_1 \vert\vert t_2) \vert\vert H(t_3 \vert\vert t_4) \vert\vert \ldots)$
- **Nonce / PoW solution** (in PoW chains)

A block is valid only if:
$$H(B_i) \;<\; \text{Target Difficulty}$$

### 2.2.3 Consensus Protocols in CPS Context

| Protocol | Energy | Finality | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PoW** (Bitcoin) | Very High | Probabilistic | Poor for battery-limited IoT |
| **PoS** (Ethereum 2.0) | Low | Probabilistic + Justified | Moderate |
| **PBFT** (Hyperledger) | Low | Deterministic | High — permissioned industrial CPS |
| **PoA** (VeChain) | Very Low | Deterministic | Excellent for sensor networks |

## 2.3 The Intersection: Quantum + Blockchain

### 2.3.1 The "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" (HNDL) Threat
Adversaries archive **today's encrypted CPS traffic** (e.g., smart meter readings, V2X messages) and decrypt it once a **Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC)** is available. Mitigation: **crypto-agility** + **PQC migration roadmap**.

### 2.3.2 Quantum-Resistant Blockchain
- Replace **ECDSA** with **ML-DSA (Dilithium)** for transaction signing.
- Use **lattice-based hash accumulators**.
- **Quantum-secured consensus** via Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) for inter-node channels.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Qubit state | $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ | $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$ |
| 2 | Probability of measuring 0/1 | $P(0)=\vert \alpha \vert^2,\; P(1)=\vert \beta \vert^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Shor's algorithm complexity | $O((\log N)^3)$ | Polynomial in $\log N$ |
| 4 | Classical factoring (best) | $O\left(e^{(1.92)(\log N)^{1/3}(\log \log N)^{2/3}}\right)$ | Sub-exponential (GNFS) |
| 5 | Grover's search | $O(\sqrt{N})$ | Quadratic speedup |
| 6 | Effective AES key after Grover | $k_{\text{eff}} = k/2$ | AES-128 → 64-bit (insecure) |
| 7 | RSA modulus | $N = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ large primes |
| 8 | Block hash relation | $H(B_i) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{header} \vert\vert \text{nonce})$ | 256-bit output |
| 9 | Merkle root | $M = H(H(L_1)\vert\vert H(L_2)\vert\vert \ldots)$ | Binary tree hash |
| 10 | PoW difficulty | $H(B_i) < D$ | $D$ = difficulty target |
| 11 | BB84 QKD key rate | $R = 1 - 2e - h(e)$ | $e$=QBER, $h$ = binary entropy |
| 12 | Binary entropy function | $h(x) = -x \log_2 x - (1-x)\log_2(1-x)$ | Used in QKD error analysis |
| 13 | Bitcoin block target | $\text{newTarget} = \text{oldTarget} \times \frac{\text{actualTime}}{\text{2016 \times 10 min}}$ | Retarget every 2016 blocks |
| 14 | Hash collision resistance | $2^{n/2}$ (birthday bound) | $n$ = hash output bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas directly govern **key-size selection**, **PQC migration timelines**, **blockchain throughput budgeting**, and **QKD link feasibility** for CPS deployments. A KTU examiner expects you to know **which formula applies to which attack surface**.

## 2.5 Real-World CPS Engineering Applications

- **Smart Grid (IEC 61850):** Replacing RSA-signed GOOSE messages with **Dilithium** to prevent quantum-era false-trip injection.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (V2X, IEEE 1609.2):** Using **blockchain-anchored PKI** to revoke compromised vehicle certificates in real time.
- **Industrial IoT (Modbus/TCP):** Deploying **permissioned blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric)** to log every PLC command for forensic audit.
- **Healthcare CPS (IoMT):** **QKD-secured channels** between MRI machines and PACS servers to protect patient telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Grover's Search Complexity Bound

We start from the amplitude amplification principle. Let $N = 2^n$ be the search space and assume exactly one marked item.

**Step 1: Initialize uniform superposition.**
$$\vert s \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \vert x \rangle$$

**Step 2: Define the Grover operator** $G = (2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I) \cdot O$, where $O$ is the oracle that flips the phase of the marked state.

**Step 3: Geometric interpretation.** The operator $G$ performs a rotation in the 2D subspace spanned by the marked state $\vert w \rangle$ and its orthogonal complement by an angle $2\theta$ per iteration, where
$$\sin \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}, \quad \cos \theta = \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{N}}$$

**Step 4: Probability of success after $k$ iterations.**
$$P_k = \sin^2\left( (2k+1)\theta \right)$$

**Step 5: Maximize $P_k$.** Set $(2k+1)\theta = \pi/2$, solve for $k$:
$$k = \frac{\pi}{4\theta} - \frac{1}{2} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N}$$

**Step 6: Complexity conclusion.**
$$\boxed{\;T_{\text{Grover}} = O(\sqrt{N})\;}$$
versus classical $O(N)$.

**Engineering translation for CPS:**
If a CPS sensor uses a **128-bit authentication challenge** (i.e., $N = 2^{128}$), classical brute-force takes $2^{128}$ operations. Grover reduces this to $\approx 2^{64}$ — which is **breakable with sufficient quantum hardware**. Therefore CPS must migrate to **AES-256** to retain ≥ 128-bit quantum security.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shor's Algorithm Reduces RSA to Polynomial Time

**Step 1: Reduction of factoring to order-finding.**
Pick random $a$ coprime to $N$. Compute the period $r$ of $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$. With high probability $r$ is even and $a^{r/2} \not\equiv -1 \pmod N$, in which case
$$\gcd\left(a^{r/2} - 1,\, N\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd\left(a^{r/2} + 1,\, N\right)$$
yield non-trivial factors of $N$.

**Step 2: Period-finding via Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT).**
Prepare uniform superposition $\frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \vert x \rangle \vert 0 \rangle$ with $M = N^2$.

**Step 3: Oracle evaluation.**
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \vert x \rangle \vert a^x \bmod N \rangle$$

**Step 4: Measurement on second register collapses** to a state with period $r$. Apply QFT on first register:
$$QFT: \vert x \rangle \mapsto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \sum_{y=0}^{M-1} e^{2\pi i x y / M} \vert y \rangle$$

**Step 5: Sampling** $y$ such that $y/M \approx k/r$ for some integer $k$. Use continued fractions to extract $r$.

**Step 6: Total operations** (modular exponentiations + QFT):
$$T_{\text{Shor}} = O\big((\log N)^2 \cdot (\log \log N) \cdot (\log \log \log N)\big) \;\approx\; O\big((\log N)^3\big)$$

**Step 7: Comparison.**
$$\boxed{\;T_{\text{Shor}} = O((\log N)^3) \quad \text{vs} \quad T_{\text{classical}} = \exp\left(c \cdot (\log N)^{1/3} (\log \log N)^{2/3}\right)\;}$$

**Numerical illustration for KTU exam:**
- RSA-2048: $\log_2 N = 2048$, $(\log N)^3 \approx 8.59 \times 10^9$ qubit-ops.
- Classical GNFS: $\approx 2^{112}$ operations.
- Speedup factor: **exponential** — RSA-2048 is broken by a CRQC of ≈ **4099 logical qubits** (with surface-code overhead, ≈ **20 million physical qubits**).

## 3.3 Derivation: Merkle Root Computation in Blockchain

Given $2^k$ transactions $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_{2^k}$:

**Step 1: Leaf hashes.** $h_i^{(0)} = H(t_i)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, 2^k$.

**Step 2: Pairwise concatenation and hash at level $j$.**
$$h_i^{(j)} = H\left(h_{2i-1}^{(j-1)} \,\vert\vert\, h_{2i}^{(j-1)}\right), \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

**Step 3: Recursion terminates** at the single root:
$$M = h_1^{(k)}$$

**Step 4: Verification complexity** for a single transaction requires only $k$ hashes (the Merkle path) — **not** $2^k$. For Bitcoin ($k=10$ for 1024 tx/block), verification is $O(10)$ vs $O(1024)$ — critical for **lightweight CPS nodes** (e.g., smart meters).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Quantum vs Blockchain on CPS

| CPS Attack Surface | Quantum Impact (Threat) | Blockchain Mitigation (Defense) | Combined Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V2X message authenticity** | Shor breaks ECDSA signing | Distributed PKI for cert revocation | Replace ECDSA with ML-DSA + anchor certs on-chain |
| **Smart meter data integrity** | Grover halves hash security | Tamper-evident billing logs | Migrate SHA-1 to SHA-3 + log hashes on-chain |
| **PLC firmware update** | Quantum forges vendor signature | Code provenance + signed audit | PQC-signed firmware + blockchain notarization |
| **SCADA command injection** | Sniffing encrypted traffic (HNDL) | Immutable command ledger | QKD-secured link + blockchain audit trail |
| **IoMT device telemetry** | EHR decryption by future adversary | Patient consent logs | QKD + permissioned blockchain for HIPAA compliance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Explain how Quantum Computing poses a threat to existing public-key cryptographic systems used in Cyber-Physical Systems. Name two algorithms that enable this threat."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — split: 2 + 1):**

Public-key cryptographic systems used in CPS — such as **RSA** (firmware signing, smart meter authentication), **ECC/ECDSA** (V2X message signing, IoT device identity), and **Diffie-Hellman** (TLS handshake for SCADA remote access) — rely on the computational hardness of **integer factorization** and the **discrete logarithm problem** on classical computers.

A **Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC)** running **Shor's algorithm (1994)** solves both problems in **polynomial time** $O((\log N)^3)$, rendering RSA-2048, ECC-256, and DH-1024 insecure. This directly compromises:
- Authenticity of firmware updates in **ICS**,
- Confidentiality of **V2X** vehicle communications,
- Integrity of **smart grid** command-and-control messages.

**Two key algorithms:**
1. **Shor's algorithm** — breaks asymmetric crypto (RSA, ECC, DH).
2. **Grover's algorithm** — provides quadratic speedup against symmetric crypto (AES) and hash functions (SHA), effectively halving the effective key length.

*(Examiner splits 3 marks as: 1 mark for explaining classical crypto reliance + 1 mark for quantum threat mechanism + 1 mark for naming the two algorithms.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"List any three core security properties that blockchain provides to CPS and give one real-world CPS example for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — split: 1.5 + 1.5):**

| # | Blockchain Property | CPS Security Goal | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immutability** (via cryptographic hash chain) | Tamper-proof audit logs of all field-device commands | **SCADA logbook** for a power substation — every breaker trip recorded on Hyperledger Fabric |
| 2 | **Decentralization** (no single point of failure) | Resilience against targeted DDoS on centralized PKI | **V2X certificate management** in a metropolitan area — multiple RSUs share a permissioned blockchain to revoke compromised vehicle certs |
| 3 | **Smart-contract-driven access control** | Automated, deterministic policy enforcement | **Smart-grid demand-response** — a smart contract automatically throttles non-critical loads when frequency deviates beyond 49.8 Hz |

*(Examiner splits 3 marks as: 1 mark for naming 3 properties + 1 mark for matching them to CPS goals + 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat derivations, explain how **Shor's algorithm** breaks RSA-2048 in polynomial time and discuss its implications for a **smart-grid CPS** using RSA-signed IEC 61850 GOOSE messages.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design a **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) migration strategy** for the same smart-grid CPS. Justify the choice of **ML-KEM (Kyber)** for key exchange and **ML-DSA (Dilithium)** for signatures.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Classical RSA foundation (1 mark).**
RSA security rests on the hardness of factoring $N = p \cdot q$. Best classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve, GNFS) requires
$$T_{\text{classical}} = \exp\!\left(c \cdot (\log N)^{1/3} (\log \log N)^{2/3}\right)$$
For RSA-2048 this gives ≈ $2^{112}$ operations — infeasible.

**Step 2: Reduction to order-finding (1.5 marks).**
Shor's key insight: pick a random $a$ with $\gcd(a, N) = 1$. The multiplicative order of $a$ modulo $N$ is the smallest $r$ such that $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$. If $r$ is even and $a^{r/2} \not\equiv -1 \pmod N$, then
$$p = \gcd\!\left(a^{r/2} - 1,\, N\right), \quad q = \gcd\!\left(a^{r/2} + 1,\, N\right)$$
yields the factors.

**Step 3: Quantum period-finding (2 marks).**
Prepare a 2-register state of size $M = N^2$:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \vert x \rangle \vert 0 \rangle$$
Apply the oracle $U: \vert x \rangle \vert 0 \rangle \mapsto \vert x \rangle \vert a^x \bmod N \rangle$ to entangle the registers. Measure the second register — the first collapses to a periodic state with period $r$. Apply **Quantum Fourier Transform**:
$$QFT_M \vert x \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \sum_{y=0}^{M-1} e^{2\pi i x y / M} \vert y \rangle$$
Sampling $y$ and applying **continued-fraction expansion** of $y/M$ extracts $r$ with high probability.

**Step 4: Complexity result (1.5 marks).**
$$T_{\text{Shor}} = O\big((\log N)^2 \cdot \log\log N \cdot \log\log\log N\big) \approx O\big((\log N)^3\big)$$
For RSA-2048, this is approximately **$8.6 \times 10^9$ qubit-operations** — well within the reach of a future CRQC (≈ 4099 logical qubits / ~20 million physical qubits with surface-code error correction).

**Step 5: Smart-grid implication (1 mark).**
IEC 61850 **GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event)** messages use **RSA-2048 / ECDSA** signatures to authenticate trip commands between Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs). A quantum attacker could forge trip commands, causing **blackouts**, **equipment damage**, or **cascading failures** in the grid. The **"Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" (HNDL)** threat is also acute — adversaries archive today's GOOSE traffic for future decryption.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: PQC migration goals (1 mark).**
- **Crypto-agility:** algorithm-agnostic design so ciphers can be swapped.
- **Backward compatibility:** hybrid (PQC + classical) during transition.
- **Real-time compliance:** PQC operations must fit within GOOSE's **4 ms** latency budget.

**Step 2: Choice of ML-KEM (Kyber) for key exchange (2.5 marks).**
- **Lattice-based Module-Learning-With-Errors (M-LWE)** problem — believed quantum-hard.
- **NIST PQC winner** (FIPS 203, Aug 2024).
- **Key + ciphertext sizes:** ML-KEM-768 has public key **1184 B**, ciphertext **1088 B** — manageable over **IEC 61850** multicast.
- **Performance:** Keygen ≈ 12 µs, Encaps ≈ 18 µs, Decaps ≈ 20 µs on Cortex-M4 (suitable for IEDs).
- **Hybrid deployment:** $K_{\text{final}} = \text{KDF}(K_{\text{ML-KEM}} \Vert K_{\text{ECDH}})$ — defense-in-depth.

**Step 3: Choice of ML-DSA (Dilithium) for signatures (2.5 marks).**
- **Lattice-based (Module-SIS and Module-LWE)** — quantum-hard.
- **NIST PQC winner** (FIPS 204, Aug 2024).
- **ML-DSA-65 signature size:** ≈ **3309 bytes** — fits GOOSE PDU overhead (currently 8-byte RSA signature → upgrade to 3.3 kB is acceptable for IEDs but requires substation LAN bandwidth audit).
- **Verification latency:** ≈ 65 µs — well within 4 ms GOOSE budget.
- **Deterministic signing** (unlike ECDSA's randomness dependency) — advantageous for IEC 61850 multicast determinism.

**Step 4: Migration roadmap (1 mark).**
1. **Phase 1:** Crypto inventory + crypto-agility library deployment.
2. **Phase 2:** Hybrid (ML-KEM + ECDH) in non-critical substation.
3. **Phase 3:** Full ML-DSA / ML-KEM across all IEDs; decommission RSA-2048.

### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Merkle tree construction** in blockchain. Show that verification of a single CPS event requires only $O(\log n)$ hashes, not $O(n)$, and justify why this is critical for **resource-constrained CPS nodes** (e.g., Class-0 smart meters with < 10 KB RAM).

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **BB84 Quantum Key Distribution protocol** step-by-step. Explain its **information-theoretic security** and discuss one practical limitation when deploying QKD over a **SCADA fiber link** of length 50 km.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Merkle tree construction (2 marks).**
Given $n = 2^k$ CPS events $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$, the Merkle tree is built bottom-up:
- **Leaf level:** $L_i = H(E_i)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$.
- **Internal level $j$:** $L_i^{(j)} = H(L_{2i-1}^{(j-1)} \Vert L_{2i}^{(j-1)})$.
- **Root:** $M = L_1^{(k)}$ — a single 256-bit value anchoring all events.

**Step 2: Single-event verification (2.5 marks).**
To verify that $E_i$ is included in the block, the verifier requests the **Merkle path**: the $\log_2 n$ sibling hashes along the path from $E_i$'s leaf to the root. The verifier re-computes the path:
- Start: $h_0 = H(E_i)$.
- For $j = 1$ to $k$: $h_j = H(\text{ordered concatenation of } h_{j-1} \text{ and sibling})$.
- Check $h_k \stackrel{?}{=} M_{\text{claimed}}$.

**Number of hashes:** exactly $k = \log_2 n$. So complexity is $O(\log n)$, **not** $O(n)$.

**Numerical illustration:** For 1024 events ($n = 2^{10}$), full block recomputation needs 1023 hashes; **single-event SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) needs only 10 hashes** — a 100× saving.

**Step 3: CPS relevance for resource-constrained nodes (2.5 marks).**
Class-0 smart meters (per RFC 7228) have:
- **~10 KB RAM, ~100 KB Flash, CPU < 50 MHz.**
- They cannot store the entire blockchain (Bitcoin: > 500 GB as of 2024).

With **Merkle SPV**, the smart meter:
- Stores only the **block headers** (~80 B each).
- Receives a **Merkle path** (~10 × 32 B = 320 B) on demand.
- Verifies a single event in **O(log n) hashes** — feasible in < 5 ms on a 50 MHz ARM Cortex-M0.

This enables **trustless firmware-update verification** on devices that **cannot run a full blockchain node** — a direct PQC-era necessity.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: BB84 protocol steps (3 marks).**
1. **Alice** generates a random bit string $a$ and a random basis choice $b_A \in \{+, \times\}$ (rectilinear / diagonal).
2. She sends each bit as a polarized photon: bit 0 in basis $b_A$, bit 1 in $b_A$ rotated 90°.
3. **Bob** independently chooses a random basis $b_B$ for each photon and measures.
4. **Public discussion:** Alice and Bob disclose their basis choices (not the bit values) over a classical authenticated channel.
5. **Sifting:** they keep only bits where $b_A = b_B$ — typically **~50%** of the raw key.
6. **Parameter estimation:** a random sample is公开compared to estimate the **QBER (Quantum Bit Error Rate)** $e$.
7. **Privacy amplification:** apply a universal hash function to compress the sifted key, eliminating any partial information an eavesdropper (Eve) may have.

**Step 2: Information-theoretic security (2 marks).**
- An eavesdropper **Eve** intercepting photons in the wrong basis introduces **detectable errors** (≥ 25% QBER in intercept-resend attack).
- If measured $e \leq 11\%$, the **secret key rate** is:
$$R = 1 - 2\,h(e)$$
where $h$ is the binary entropy function. For $e = 0.02$, $R \approx 0.92$ — i.e., 92% of sifted bits become final secret key.
- Security rests on **quantum mechanics** (no-cloning theorem + measurement disturbance) — not computational hardness ⇒ **future-proof against quantum computers**.

**Step 3: Practical limitation on 50 km SCADA fiber (2 marks).**
- **Photon loss in standard telecom fiber:** ≈ **0.2 dB/km** ⇒ total loss over 50 km ≈ **10 dB** ⇒ only ~10% of photons survive.
- This forces a low raw key rate and a high **QBER** from detector dark counts.
- **Solution:** use **low-loss fiber** (0.15 dB/km), **superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs)** with < 100 Hz dark counts, and **decoy-state** protocol to defeat photon-number-splitting attacks.
- Even so, **50 km is at the practical limit of point-to-point QKD**; beyond ~100 km, **quantum repeaters** (still experimental) are required.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (verified against KTU 2024 valuation keys):**
> 1. **Confusing PQC with QC:** Many students write *"quantum cryptography"* when the question asks for **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** — they are **not the same**. PQC = classical algorithms resistant to quantum attack. QC = algorithms *running on quantum hardware* (like QKD).
> 2. **Omitting the HNDL threat:** Forgetting the **"Harvest Now, Decrypt Later"** attack when discussing quantum impact on CPS costs **2–3 marks** in long answers — it is a KTU 2024 syllabus-highlighted concept.
> 3. **Confusing Grover's effect on AES:** Writing *"Grover breaks AES-128"* is **wrong**. Grover halves the effective key, so AES-128 → 64-bit (insecure) — but the **correct migration is to AES-256**, not to a PQC scheme.
> 4. **Merkle tree math errors:** Using $O(n)$ instead of $O(\log n)$ for SPV verification — this is a **3-mark loss** because the logarithmic saving is the *core engineering reason* blockchain works on IoT.
> 5. **Blockchain ≠ cryptocurrency:** Examiners expect students to discuss **distributed-ledger security properties** (immutability, consensus, audit), not Bitcoin price. Avoid "mining profitability" tangents.
> 6. **No Qubit count awareness:** Stating *"quantum computers can break RSA"* without estimating the **logical/physical qubit count** (~4099 logical / ~20M physical for RSA-2048) loses **2 marks** for lacking engineering depth.
> 7. **Missing IEC 61850 / SCADA / V2X context:** Generic answers that don't anchor to a *specific CPS standard* (IEC 61850, IEEE 1609.2, Modbus, DNP3) get **partial credit only** — always tie the answer to a CPS domain.

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CPS** = cyber + physical tightly coupled (sensors, actuators, control loops, networking).
- ✅ **Quantum computing** exploits **superposition, entanglement, interference** via **qubits**.
- ✅ A qubit state: $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ with $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.
- ✅ **Shor's algorithm** breaks RSA, ECC, DH in **$O((\log N)^3)$** — polynomial time ⇒ catastrophic for CPS authentication.
- ✅ **Grover's algorithm** gives $O(\sqrt N)$ speedup — halves symmetric key strength ⇒ migrate **AES-128 → AES-256**.
- ✅ **Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL)** is the *most urgent* quantum-era threat to CPS — mandates immediate crypto-agility planning.
- ✅ **NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203/204/205, 2024):** **ML-KEM (Kyber)** for KEM, **ML-DSA (Dilithium)** for signatures, **SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+)** as conservative backup.
- ✅ **Blockchain** provides **immutability, decentralization, auditability, non-repudiation** — directly maps to CPS **integrity, resilience, accountability** goals.
- ✅ **Merkle tree verification** = $O(\log n)$ — enables SPV on resource-constrained CPS nodes (smart meters, RTUs).
- ✅ **Consensus choice matters:** PBFT/PoA for industrial CPS (deterministic finality, low energy); **PoW unsuitable** for battery-limited IoT.
- ✅ **QKD (BB84)** offers **information-theoretic security** but is **fiber-distance limited** (~100 km without quantum repeaters).
- ✅ **Hybrid strategy** = **PQC + blockchain + QKD** is the KTU 2024 expected answer for securing CPS in the quantum era.
- ✅ **Key engineering insight:** The future of CPS security is **crypto-agility + distributed trust + quantum-safe primitives** — not a single silver bullet.
